The leaves in pteridophyta are small (microphylls) as in *Selaginella* or large (macrophylls) as in ferns. The sporophytes bear sporangia that are subtended by leaf-like appendages called **sporophylls**. In some cases sporophylls may form distinct compact structures called strobili or cones (*Selaginella*, *Equisetum*). The sporangia produce spores by meiosis in spore mother cells. The spores germinate to give rise to inconspicuous, small but multicellular,

Diagram of a *Selaginella* plant showing its structure. The plant has a creeping stem with small, narrow leaves. A root system is visible at the base. Labels indicate: Leaves, Stem, and Roots.

Diagram of an *Equisetum* plant showing its structure. The plant has a central stem with whorled branches at nodes. The top of the stem bears a strobilus. The base of the plant shows a rhizome with roots. Labels indicate: Strobilus, Node, Internode, Branch, and Rhizome.

Photograph of a Fern plant, showing dense, feathery fronds.

Diagram of a *Salvinia* plant showing its structure. The plant has large, rounded leaves and a dense, fibrous root system. Labels indicate: (d).

**Figure 3.3** Pteridophytes : (a) *Selaginella* (b) *Equisetum* (c) Fern (d) *Salvinia*

free-living, mostly photosynthetic thalloid gametophytes called **prothallus**. These gametophytes require cool, damp, shady places to grow. Because of this specific restricted requirement and the need for water for fertilisation, the spread of living pteridophytes is limited and restricted to narrow geographical regions. The gametophytes bear male and female sex organs called antheridia and archegonia, respectively. Water is required for transfer of antherozoids – the male gametes released from the antheridia, to the mouth of archegonium. Fusion of male gamete with the egg present in the archegonium result in the formation of zygote. Zygote thereafter produces a multicellular well-differentiated sporophyte which is the dominant phase of the pteridophytes. In majority of the pteridophytes all the spores are of similar kinds; such plants are called **homosporous**. Genera like *Selaginella* and *Salvinia* which produce two kinds of spores, macro (large) and micro (small) spores, are known as **heterosporous**. The megaspores and microspores germinate and give rise to female and male gametophytes, respectively. The female gametophytes in these plants are retained on the parent sporophytes for variable periods. The development of the zygotes into young embryos take place within the female gametophytes. This event is a precursor to the **seed habit** considered an important step in evolution.

The pteridophytes are further classified into four classes: Psilopsida (*Psilotum*); Lycopside (*Selaginella*, *Lycopodium*), Sphenopsida (*Equisetum*) and Pteropsida (*Dryopteris*, *Pteris*, *Adiantum*).

kebo103-s15.content:
The gymnosperms (*gymnos* : naked, *sperma* : seeds) are plants in which the ovules are not enclosed by any ovary wall and remain exposed, both before and after fertilisation. The seeds that develop post-fertilisation, are not covered, i.e., are naked. Gymnosperms include medium-sized trees or tall trees and shrubs (Figure 3.4). One of the gymnosperms, the giant redwood tree *Sequoia* is one of the tallest tree species. The roots are generally tap roots. Roots in some genera have fungal association in the form of **mycorrhiza** (*Pinus*), while in some others (*Cycas*) small specialised roots called coralloid roots are associated with  $N_2$ - fixing cyanobacteria. The stems are unbranched (*Cycas*) or branched (*Pinus*, *Cedrus*). The leaves may be simple or compound. In *Cycas* the pinnate leaves persist for a few years. The leaves in gymnosperms are well-adapted to withstand extremes of temperature, humidity and wind. In conifers, the needle-like leaves reduce the surface area. Their thick cuticle and sunken stomata also help to reduce water loss.

The gymnosperms are heterosporous; they produce haploid microspores and megaspores. The two kinds of spores are produced within sporangia that are borne on sporophylls which are arranged spirally along an axis to form lax or compact strobili or **cones**. The strobili bearing **microsporophylls** and **microsporangia** are called microsporangiate or **male strobili**. The microspores develop into a male gametophytic generation which is highly reduced and is confined to only a limited number of cells. This reduced gametophyte is called a **pollen grain**. The development of pollen grains take place within the microsporangia. The cones bearing megasporophylls with ovules or **megaspore** are called macrosporangiate or **female strobili**. The male or female cones or strobili may be borne on the same tree (*Pinus*). However, in *cycas* male cones and megasporophylls are borne on different trees. The megaspore mother cell is differentiated from one of the cells of the nucellus. The nucellus is protected by envelopes and the composite structure is called an **ovule**. The ovules are borne on megasporophylls which may be clustered to form the female cones. The megaspore mother cell divides meiotically to form four megaspores. One of the megaspores enclosed within the **megaspore** develops into a multicellular female gametophyte that bears two or more **archegonia** or female sex organs. The multicellular female gametophyte is also retained within megaspore.

Unlike bryophytes and pteridophytes, in gymnosperms, the male and the female gametophytes do not have an independent free-living existence. They remain within the sporangia retained on the sporophytes. The pollen grain is released from the microsporangium. They are carried in air currents and come in contact with the opening of the ovules borne on megasporophylls. The pollen tube carrying the male gametes grows towards archegonia in the ovules and discharge their contents near the mouth of the archegonia. Following fertilisation, zygote develops into an embryo and the ovules into seeds. These seeds are not covered.

**Figure 3.4** Gymnosperms: (a) *Cycas* (b) *Pinus* (c) *Ginkgo*

kebo103-s16.content:
Unlike the gymnosperms where the ovules are naked, in the angiosperms or flowering plants, the pollen grains and ovules are developed in specialised structures called **flowers**. In angiosperms, the seeds are enclosed in fruits. The angiosperms are an exceptionally large group of plants occurring in wide range of habitats. They range in size from the smallest *Wolffia* to tall trees of *Eucalyptus* (over 100 metres). They provide us with food, fodder, fuel, medicines and several other commercially important products. They are divided into two classes : the **dicotyledons** and the **monocotyledons** (Figure 3.5).

Figure 3.5 consists of two side-by-side photographs. Photograph (a) shows a potted plant with several bright yellow flowers and green leaves, characteristic of a dicotyledon. Photograph (b) shows a plant with long, narrow, parallel leaves and small white flowers, characteristic of a monocotyledon.

Figure 3.5 Angiosperms : (a) A dicotyledon (b) A monocotyledon

kebo103-s17.content:
Plant kingdom includes algae, bryophytes, pteridophytes, gymnosperms and angiosperms. Algae are chlorophyll-bearing simple, thalloid, autotrophic and largely aquatic organisms. Depending on the type of pigment possessed and the type of stored food, algae are classified into three classes, namely Chlorophyceae, Phaeophyceae and Rhodophyceae. Algae usually reproduce vegetatively by fragmentation, asexually by formation of different types of spores and sexually by formation of gametes which may show isogamy, anisogamy or oogamy.

Bryophytes are plants which can live in soil but are dependent on water for sexual reproduction. Their plant body is more differentiated than that of algae. It is thallus-like and prostrate or erect and attached to the substratum by rhizoids. They possess root-like, leaf-like and stem-

like structures. The bryophytes are divided into liverworts and mosses. The plant body of liverworts is thalloid and dorsiventral whereas mosses have upright, slender axes bearing spirally arranged leaves. The main plant body of a bryophyte is gamete-producing and is called a gametophyte. It bears the male sex organs called antheridia and female sex organs called archegonia. The male and female gametes produced fuse to form zygote which produces a multicellular body called a sporophyte. It produces haploid spores. The spores germinate to form gametophytes.

In pteridophytes the main plant is a sporophyte which is differentiated into true root, stem and leaves. These organs possess well-differentiated vascular tissues. The sporophytes bear sporangia which produce spores. The spores germinate to form gametophytes which require cool, damp places to grow. The gametophytes bear male and female sex organs called antheridia and archegonia, respectively. Water is required for transfer of male gametes to archegonium where zygote is formed after fertilisation. The zygote produces a sporophyte.

The gymnosperms are the plants in which ovules are not enclosed by any ovary wall. After fertilisation the seeds remain exposed and therefore these plants are called naked-seeded plants. The gymnosperms produce microspores and megaspores which are produced in microsporangia and megasporangia borne on the sporophylls. The sporophylls – microsporophylls and megasporophylls – are arranged spirally on axis to form male and female cones, respectively. The pollen grain germinates and pollen tube releases the male gamete into the ovule, where it fuses with the egg cell in archegonia. Following fertilisation, the zygote develops into embryo and the ovules into seeds.

The angiosperms are divided into two classes – the dicotyledons and the monocotyledons.

kebo103-d000.content: Diagram: Structure of a Selaginella plant (diagram). Caption: Diagram of a *Selaginella* plant showing its structure. The plant has a creeping stem with small, narrow leaves. A root system is visible at the base. Labels indicate: Leaves, Stem, and Roots. Description: The diagram should represent the external morphology of a Selaginella plant, a pteridophyte with a creeping, dorsiventral stem that branches dichotomously and bears small microphyllous leaves (often arranged in four rows, with two sizes in many species). The stem is the main axis and may show rhizophores arising from branch points or lower nodes; these are leafless, root-bearing organs that grow downward and give rise to adventitious roots. The true roots are thin, branched, and arise from the rhizophores, not directly from the stem. The leaves are small, simple, scale-like microphylls with a single vein, attached along the stem and branches. Overall, the plant body is differentiated into stem, leaves, rhizophores, and roots, with the creeping stem spreading horizontally and the roots descending into the substrate for anchorage and absorption. Labels: Stem, Leaves, Rhizophore, Roots. NEET relevance: Identification of Selaginella as a pteridophyte with microphylls and rhizophores; Distinction between rhizophore and root; Characteristic creeping stem and dichotomous branching. Board relevance: Draw and label the external structure of Selaginella; Differentiate stem, leaves, rhizophore, and roots; Explain why Selaginella is a pteridophyte and mention its vascular, seedless nature. Exam-critical labels: Stem, Leaves, Rhizophore, Roots. Common mistakes: Calling the downward brown axis directly a root (correct: It should be a rhizophore if it is the leafless downward organ connecting stem to roots); Labeling all small structures on the stem as leaves without noting their microphyll nature (correct: They are microphyllous leaves (microphylls), small simple leaves with a single vein); Showing roots as hanging from the stem surface (correct: Roots should arise from rhizophores at the lower region).

kebo103-d001.content: Diagram: Bryophytes showing Marchantia, Funaria, and Sphagnum structures (diagram). Caption: The figure consists of four diagrams labeled (a) through (d). (a) and (b) show the thallus of *Marchantia*. (a) is a female thallus with a long stalk topped by a flower-like Archegoniophore. It also features Gemma cups and Rhizoids. (b) is a male thallus with a long stalk topped by Antheridiophores. It also features Gemma cups and Rhizoids. (c) shows *Funaria*, divided into a Sporophyte (consisting of a Capsule on a Seta) and a Gametophyte (consisting of Leaves, a Main axis, and Rhizoids). (d) shows a *Sphagnum* gametophyte, which is a branched plant with Antheridial branches and Archegonial branches. Description: This figure represents four classic bryophyte examples from NCERT: (a) the female gametophyte of Marchantia, a dorsiventral thalloid liverwort with a flattened, lobed thallus bearing cup-like gemma cups on the dorsal surface and multicellular rhizoids on the ventral surface; the female reproductive stalk is the archegoniophore, a long stalk ending in an umbrella-like receptacle that bears archegonia on its underside. (b) the male gametophyte of Marchantia, similarly thalloid and dorsiventral, with gemma cups and rhizoids, but bearing an antheridiophore, a stalked disc-like receptacle that carries antheridia on its upper surface. (c) Funaria, a moss, showing the dominant leafy gametophyte with a central main axis, spirally arranged leaves, and rhizoids at the base; attached to it is the dependent sporophyte consisting of a foot embedded in the gametophyte, a seta, and a terminal capsule where meiosis produces spores. (d) Sphagnum, a peat moss, showing the gametophyte with a main axis and clustered lateral branches arranged into an antheridial branch and an archegonial branch; the plant body is highly branched and lacks true roots, with rhizoids present only in the juvenile stage or absent in the mature plant. The diagram emphasizes the alternation of generations in bryophytes, with the gametophyte as the dominant, photosynthetic phase and the sporophyte remaining nutritionally dependent on it. Labels: Female thallus of Marchantia, Archegoniophore, Gemma cup, Rhizoids, Male thallus of Marchantia, Antheridiophore, Gemma cup, Rhizoids, Sporophyte, Capsule, Seta, Gametophyte, Leaves, Main axis, Rhizoids. NEET relevance: Identify the bryophyte examples Marchantia, Funaria, and Sphagnum from diagrams.; Distinguish gametophyte and sporophyte in mosses.; Recognize archegoniophore vs antheridiophore in Marchantia.. Board relevance: Draw and label Marchantia female and male thalli.; Draw and label Funaria showing gametophyte and sporophyte.; Draw and label Sphagnum gametophyte with reproductive branches.. Exam-critical labels: Archegoniophore, Antheridiophore, Gemma cup, Rhizoids, Sporophyte, Capsule, Seta, Gametophyte. Common mistakes: Calling the Marchantia stalked disc a sporophyte (correct: It is a gametophytic reproductive structure: archegoniophore or antheridiophore); Labeling gemma cups as reproductive organs for sexual reproduction (correct: Gemma cups are asexual vegetative propagules); Treating rhizoids as true roots (correct: Rhizoids are root-like anchoring structures, not true roots).

kebo103-d002.content: Diagram: Two angiosperm plants shown as examples of a dicotyledon and a monocotyledon (photograph). Caption: Figure 3.5 consists of two side-by-side photographs. Photograph (a) shows a potted plant with several bright yellow flowers and green leaves, characteristic of a dicotyledon. Photograph (b) shows a plant with long, narrow, parallel leaves and small white flowers, characteristic of a monocotyledon. Description: The figure should show two whole angiosperm plants used to illustrate the distinction between dicotyledons and monocotyledons. In the dicotyledon example, the plant should have a taproot system, broad leaves with reticulate venation, and floral parts typically arranged in multiples of four or five; the visible yellow flower heads represent the reproductive structures of a dicot plant, and the overall habit is usually more branched. In the monocotyledon example, the plant should have a fibrous root system, long narrow leaves with parallel venation, and floral parts typically arranged in multiples of three; the plant is generally less branched and shows the characteristic monocot leaf form. The diagram is not meant to identify a specific species with certainty, but to demonstrate the contrasting vegetative and floral features that define dicots and monocots in angiosperms. Labels: Dicotyledon, Monocotyledon, Flower, Leaves, Stem. NEET relevance: Difference between dicotyledons and monocotyledons; Examples of angiosperms; Diagnostic characters: cotyledon number, leaf venation, root system, floral part number. Board relevance: Draw and label a dicot and a monocot plant; Differentiate dicot and monocot on the basis of leaves, roots, and flowers; Explain why a given plant is dicot or monocot from visible features. Exam-critical labels: Dicotyledon, Monocotyledon, Flower, Leaves, Stem, Taproot, Fibrous root, Reticulate venation. Common mistakes: Calling the left plant a monocot because it has flowers (correct: It is a dicotyledon example); Calling the right plant a dicot because it is a flowering plant (correct: It is a monocotyledon example); Identifying the right plant as having reticulate venation (correct: It should show parallel venation in the leaves).

kebo103-d003.content: Diagram: different types of algae and their body forms/structures (diagram). Caption: The image contains seven diagrams of different algae types, labeled as follows: Description: This diagram represents the major thallus/body forms of representative algae from three groups: green algae, brown algae, and red algae. In the green algae panel, Volvox is shown as a hollow spherical colonial organism composed of many small biflagellate cells arranged on the colony surface, with internal daughter colonies developing inside the parent colony; Ulothrix is shown as an unbranched filament of rectangular cells arranged end to end. In the brown algae panel, Laminaria is depicted as a large differentiated thallus with a blade-like frond, a stalk-like stipe, and a basal holdfast for attachment; Fucus is shown as a flattened, dichotomously branched thallus with a prominent midrib, paired air bladders (pneumatocysts) that aid buoyancy, and a holdfast; Dictyota is shown as a flattened, dichotomously branched frond lacking a distinct midrib and air bladders, attached by a holdfast. In the red algae panel, Porphyra is represented by a thin, sheet-like, leaf-like blade (foliose thallus), while Polysiphonia is shown as a delicate branched filamentous thallus with a central axis and lateral branches. The diagram emphasizes the diversity of algal organization from colonial and filamentous forms to differentiated parenchymatous thalli with specialized attachment and buoyancy structures. Labels: Volvox, Daughter colony, Parent colony, Ulothrix, Laminaria, Frond, Stipe, Holdfast, Fucus, Air bladder, Midrib, Frond, Holdfast, Dictyota, Frond. NEET relevance: Identification of algal thallus types and representative genera; Distinguishing colonial, filamentous, foliose, and differentiated thallus forms; Recognizing brown algae structures such as holdfast, stipe, frond, midrib, and air bladders. Board relevance: Draw and label Volvox, Ulothrix, Laminaria, Fucus, Dictyota, Porphyra, and Polysiphonia; Short notes on thallus organization in algae; Labeling holdfast, stipe, frond, midrib, air bladder, main axis, and branches. Exam-critical labels: Daughter colony, Parent colony, Ulothrix, Holdfast, Stipe, Frond, Midrib, Air bladder. Common mistakes: Calling Volvox a single cell or a filament (correct: Volvox is a spherical colonial alga); Labeling Ulothrix as a branched filament (correct: Ulothrix is an unbranched filament); Calling the basal attachment of brown algae a root (correct: It is a holdfast).

kebo103-d004.content: Diagram: A botanical illustration of a Ginkgo branch with fan-shaped leaves and seeds (structural). Caption: A botanical illustration of a Ginkgo branch, showing the fan-shaped leaves and the clusters of seeds. Description: The diagram should depict a Ginkgo biloba branch, a gymnosperm shoot system bearing dimorphic shoots: long shoots (elongated, indeterminate axes) and dwarf shoots or spur shoots (short, condensed lateral shoots) that carry clusters of fan-shaped leaves. The leaves are simple, leathery, and distinctly bilobed to deeply notched in many cases, with dichotomously branching veins radiating from the petiole toward the margin, a hallmark of Ginkgo. Reproductive structures are shown as naked ovules/seeds borne on stalks, not enclosed in an ovary, reflecting the gymnosperm condition. In a mature female branch, paired ovules develop into fleshy, yellowish seeds hanging from slender peduncles near the leaf axils. The diagram should emphasize the arrangement of leaves on both long and dwarf shoots and the position of the seeds on separate stalks, illustrating the primitive seed-plant features of Ginkgo. Labels: Long shoot, Dwarf shoot, Leaves, Seeds. NEET relevance: Identification of Ginkgo as a gymnosperm with fan-shaped leaves and dichotomous venation; Recognition of dimorphic shoots: long shoot and dwarf shoot; Understanding that seeds are naked and not enclosed in fruit. Board relevance: Draw and label the Ginkgo branch showing long shoot, dwarf shoot, leaves, and seeds; Explain why Ginkgo is called a living fossil; Mention the characteristic fan-shaped leaves and dichotomous venation. Exam-critical labels: Long shoot, Dwarf shoot, Leaves, Seeds. Common mistakes: Calling the seeds a fruit or cone (correct: They are naked seeds/ovules borne on stalks); Labeling the short leafy spur as a branchlet or twig without specifying dwarf shoot (correct: It is a dwarf shoot (spur shoot)); Describing the leaves as needle-like or scale-like (correct: They are fan-shaped leaves with dichotomous venation).

kebo103-d005.content: Diagram: Salvinia plant structure (diagram). Caption: Diagram of a *Salvinia* plant showing its structure. The plant has large, rounded leaves and a dense, fibrous root system. Labels indicate: (d). Description: The diagram should represent the floating aquatic fern Salvinia, a heterosporous pteridophyte with a slender, horizontally creeping stem (rhizome) that floats at or just below the water surface. From the nodes of the rhizome arise whorls of three leaves: two are green, floating or emergent leaves that are oval to oblong, entire, and often covered with water-repellent hairs, while the third is a submerged, finely dissected leaf that functions as a root-like organ for absorption and anchorage. True roots are absent in Salvinia; the submerged leaf is highly modified and root-like in appearance, which is a key diagnostic feature. The plant is adapted for aquatic life, with buoyant leaves and specialized hairs that trap air and repel water, helping it remain afloat. Labels: rhizome, floating leaves, submerged modified leaf, leaf hairs (trichomes), node. NEET relevance: Identification of Salvinia as a heterosporous aquatic fern; Recognition that the root-like hanging structure is a modified leaf, not a root; Adaptations for floating life: buoyant leaves and hydrophobic hairs. Board relevance: Draw and label the Salvinia plant structure; Label the rhizome and the three leaves at a node; Explain why the submerged structure is not a root. Exam-critical labels: rhizome, floating leaves, submerged modified leaf, node. Common mistakes: roots (correct: submerged modified leaf); stem (correct: rhizome); single leaf (correct: whorl of three leaves).

kebo103-d006.content: Diagram: Structure of an Equisetum plant (diagram). Caption: Diagram of an *Equisetum* plant showing its structure. The plant has a central stem with whorled branches at nodes. The top of the stem bears a strobilus. The base of the plant shows a rhizome with roots. Labels indicate: Strobilus, Node, Internode, Branch, and Rhizome. Description: The diagram should represent the external morphology of Equisetum (horsetail), a pteridophyte with a distinct, jointed, green aerial shoot arising from a creeping underground rhizome. The aerial stem is clearly segmented into nodes and internodes: nodes are the swollen joints that bear whorls of slender, highly reduced lateral branches, while internodes are the elongated stem regions between successive nodes. At the apex of the fertile shoot, a terminal strobilus (cone) is present; it consists of tightly packed sporangiophores that bear sporangia and is the site of spore production. The underground rhizome is horizontal, creeping, and bears adventitious roots from its lower surface; it also gives rise to aerial shoots from its nodes. In a scientifically accurate diagram, the stem is ribbed and jointed, branches arise only in whorls at nodes, and the strobilus is terminal on the fertile axis. The figure should emphasize the relationship between the subterranean rhizome, the erect aerial shoot, and the reproductive cone at the top. Labels: Strobilus, Node, Internode, Branch, Rhizome, Adventitious roots. NEET relevance: Identification of Equisetum as a pteridophyte with jointed stem and terminal strobilus; Distinguishing nodes, internodes, rhizome, and whorled branches; Recognizing that the reproductive structure is a strobilus bearing sporangia. Board relevance: Draw and label the structure of Equisetum; Differentiate node and internode; Show whorled branches and terminal strobilus. Exam-critical labels: Strobilus, Node, Internode, Branch, Rhizome, Adventitious roots. Common mistakes: Calling the whorled lateral structures 'leaves' instead of branches (correct: They are whorled branches; leaves in Equisetum are highly reduced and form a sheath at the node); Labeling the cone as a 'flower' or 'fruit' (correct: It is a strobilus (spore-bearing cone)); Placing branches on internodes (correct: Branches arise in whorls from nodes).

kebo104-s00.content:
# CHAPTER 4ANIMAL KINGDOM

- 4.1 Basis of Classification
- 4.2 Classification of Animals

When you look around, you will observe different animals with different structures and forms. As over a million species of animals have been described till now, the need for classification becomes all the more important. The classification also helps in assigning a systematic position to newly described species.

kebo104-s01.section_title: 4.1 BASIS OF CLASSIFICATION + 4.1.1 Levels of Organisation
kebo104-s01.content:
In spite of differences in structure and form of different animals, there are fundamental features common to various individuals in relation to the arrangement of cells, body symmetry, nature of coelom, patterns of digestive, circulatory or reproductive systems. These features are used as the basis of animal classification and some of them are discussed here.

Though all members of Animalia are multicellular, all of them do not exhibit the same pattern of organisation of cells. For example, in sponges, the cells are arranged as loose cell aggregates, i.e., they exhibit **cellular level** of organisation. Some division of labour (activities) occur among the cells. In coelenterates, the arrangement of cells is more complex. Here the cells performing the same function are arranged into tissues, hence is called **tissue level** of organisation. A still higher level of organisation, i.e., **organ level** is exhibited by members of Platyhelminthes and other higher phyla where tissues are grouped together to form organs, each specialised for a particular function. In animals like Annelids, Arthropods, Molluscs,

**Figure 4.1 (a)** Radial symmetry

**Figure 4.1 (b)** Bilateral symmetry

**Figure 4.2** Showing germinal layers :  
(a) Diploblastic (b) Triploblastic

Echinoderms and Chordates, organs have associated to form functional systems, each system concerned with a specific physiological function. This pattern is called **organ system** level of organisation. Organ systems in different groups of animals exhibit various patterns of complexities. For example, the digestive system in Platyhelminthes has only a single opening to the outside of the body that serves as both mouth and anus, and is hence called incomplete. A complete digestive system has two openings, mouth and anus. Similarly, the circulatory system may be of two types:

- (i) **open type** in which the blood is pumped out of the heart and the cells and tissues are directly bathed in it and
- (ii) **closed type** in which the blood is circulated through a series of vessels of varying diameters (arteries, veins and capillaries).

kebo104-s03.content: Animals can be categorised on the basis of their symmetry. Sponges are mostly **asymmetrical**, i.e., any plane that passes through the centre does not divide them into equal halves. When any plane passing through the central axis of the body divides the organism into two identical halves, it is called **radial symmetry**. Coelenterates, ctenophores and echinoderms have this kind of body plan (Figure 4.1a). Animals like annelids, arthropods, etc., where the body can be divided into identical left and right halves in only one plane, exhibit **bilateral symmetry** (Figure 4.1b).

kebo104-s04.content:
Animals in which the cells are arranged in two embryonic layers, an external **ectoderm** and an internal **endoderm**, are called **diploblastic** animals, e.g., coelenterates. An undifferentiated layer, mesoglea, is present in between the ectoderm and the endoderm (Figure 4.2a).

Those animals in which the developing embryo has a third germinal layer, **mesoderm**, in between the ectoderm and endoderm, are called **triploblastic** animals (platyhelminthes to chordates, Figure 4.2b).

kebo104-s05.content:
Presence or absence of a cavity between the body wall and the gut wall is very important in classification. The body cavity, which is lined by mesoderm is called **coelom**. Animals possessing coelom are called **coelomates**, e.g., annelids, molluscs, arthropods, echinoderms, hemichordates and chordates (Figure 4.3a). In some animals, the body cavity is not lined by mesoderm, instead, the mesoderm is present as scattered pouches in between the ectoderm and endoderm. Such a body cavity is called **pseudocoelom** and the animals possessing them are called **pseudocoelomates**, e.g., aschelminthes (Figure 4.3b). The animals in which the body cavity is absent are called **acoelomates**, e.g., platyhelminthes (Figure 4.3c).

Figure 4.3 Diagrammatic sectional view of :  
(a) Coelomate (b) Pseudocoelomate  
(c) Acoelomate

kebo104-s06.content:
In some animals, the body is externally and internally divided into segments with a serial repetition of at least some organs. For example, in earthworm, the body shows this pattern called **metameric segmentation** and the phenomenon is known as **metamerism**.

Notochord is a mesodermally derived rod-like structure formed on the dorsal side during embryonic development in some animals. Animals with notochord are called **chordates** and those animals which do not form this structure are called **non-chordates**, e.g., porifera to echinoderms.

kebo104-s08.content:
The broad classification of Animalia, based on common fundamental features as mentioned in the preceding sections, is given in Figure 4.4.

\*Echinodermata exhibits radial or bilateral symmetry depending on the stage.

**Figure 4.4** Broad classification of Kingdom Animalia based on common fundamental features

The important characteristic features of the different phyla are described.

Members of this phylum are commonly known as sponges. They are generally marine and mostly asymmetrical animals (Figure 4.5). These are primitive multicellular animals and have cellular level of organisation. Sponges have a water transport or canal system. Water enters through minute pores (**ostia**) in the body wall into a central cavity, **spongocoel**, from where it goes out through the **osculum**. This pathway of water transport is helpful in food gathering, respiratory exchange and removal of waste. **Choanocytes** or collar cells line the spongocoel and the canals. Digestion is intracellular. The body is supported by a skeleton made up of **spicules** or **spongin fibres**. Sexes are not separate (**hermaphrodite**), i.e., eggs and sperms are produced by the same individual. Sponges reproduce asexually by fragmentation and sexually by formation of gametes. Fertilisation is internal and development is indirect having a larval stage which is morphologically distinct from the adult.

**Figure 4.5** Examples of Porifera : (a) *Sycon* (b) *Euspongia* (c) *Spongilla*

Examples: *Sycon* (Scypha), *Spongilla* (Fresh water sponge) and *Euspongia* (Bath sponge).

kebo104-s10.content:
They are aquatic, mostly marine, sessile or free-swimming, radially symmetrical animals (Figure 4.6). The name cnidaria is derived from the

Figure 4.6 Examples of Coelenterata indicating outline of their body form :  
(a) *Aurelia* (Medusa) (b) *Adamsia* (Polyp)

cnidoblasts or cnidocytes (which contain the stinging capsules or nematocysts) present on the tentacles and the body. Cnidoblasts are used for anchorage, defense and for the capture of prey (Figure 4.7). Cnidarians exhibit tissue level of organisation and are diploblastic. They have a central gastro-vascular cavity with a single opening, mouth on **hypostome**. Digestion is extracellular and intracellular. Some of the cnidarians, e.g., **corals** have a skeleton composed of calcium carbonate. Cnidarians exhibit two basic body forms called **polyp** and **medusa** (Figure 4.6). The former is a sessile and cylindrical form like *Hydra*, *Adamsia*, etc. whereas, the latter is umbrella-shaped and free-swimming like *Aurelia* or jelly fish. Figure 4.7 Diagrammatic view of Cnidoblast

Examples: *Physalia* (Portuguese man-of-war), *Adamsia* (Sea anemone), *Pennatula* (Sea-pen), *Gorgonia* (Sea-fan) and *Meandrina* (Brain coral).

**Figure 4.8** Example of Ctenophora (*Pleurobrachia*)

kebo104-s11.content:
Ctenophores, commonly known as **sea walnuts** or **comb jellies** are exclusively marine, radially symmetrical, diploblastic organisms with tissue level of organisation. The body bears eight external rows of ciliated **comb plates**, which help in locomotion (Figure 4.8). Digestion is both extracellular and intracellular. **Bioluminescence** (the property of a living organism to emit light) is well-marked in ctenophores. Sexes are not separate. Reproduction takes place only by sexual means. Fertilisation is external with indirect development.

Examples: *Pleurobrachia* and *Ctenoplana*.

kebo104-s12.content:
They have dorso-ventrally flattened body, hence are called **flatworms** (Figure 4.9). These are mostly endoparasites found in animals including human beings. Flatworms are bilaterally symmetrical, triploblastic and acoelomate animals with organ level of organisation. Hooks and suckers are present in the parasitic forms. Some of them absorb nutrients from the host directly through their body surface. Specialised cells called flame cells help in osmoregulation and excretion. Sexes are not separate. Fertilisation is internal and development is through many larval stages. Some members like *Planaria* possess high regeneration capacity.

Examples: *Taenia* (Tapeworm), *Fasciola* (Liver fluke).

**Figure 4.9** Examples of Platyhelminthes : (a) Tape worm (b) Liver fluke

kebo104-s13.content:
The body of the aschelminthes is circular in cross-section, hence, the name **roundworms** (Figure 4.10). They may be freeliving, aquatic and terrestrial or parasitic in plants and animals. Roundworms have organ-system level of body organisation. They are bilaterally symmetrical, triploblastic and pseudocoelomate animals. Alimentary canal is complete with a well-developed **muscular pharynx**. An excretory tube removes body wastes from the body cavity through the excretory pore. Sexes are separate (**dioecious**), i.e., males and females are distinct. Often females are longer than males. Fertilisation is internal and development may be direct (the young ones resemble the adult) or indirect.

Examples : *Ascaris* (Roundworm), *Wuchereria* (Filaria worm), *Ancylostoma* (Hookworm).

kebo104-s14.content:
They may be aquatic (marine and fresh water) or terrestrial; free-living, and sometimes parasitic. They exhibit organ-system level of body organisation and bilateral symmetry. They are triploblastic, metameric segmented and coelomate animals. Their body surface is distinctly marked out into **segments** or **metameres** and, hence, the phylum name Annelida (Latin, *annulus* : little ring) (Figure 4.11). They possess longitudinal and circular muscles which help in locomotion. Aquatic annelids like *Nereis* possess lateral appendages, **parapodia**, which help in swimming. A closed circulatory system is present. **Nephridia** (sing. nephridium) help in osmoregulation and excretion. Neural system consists of paired ganglia (sing. ganglion) connected by lateral nerves to a double ventral nerve cord. *Nereis*, an aquatic form, is dioecious, but earthworms and leeches are monoecious. Reproduction is sexual.

Examples : *Nereis*, *Pheretima* (Earthworm) and *Hirudinaria* (Blood sucking leech).

**Figure 4.10** Example of Aschelminthes: Roundworm

**Figure 4.11** Examples of Annelida : (a) *Nereis* (b) *Hirudinaria*

**Figure 4.12** Examples of Arthropoda :  
(a) Locust (b) Butterfly  
(c) Scorpion (d) Prawn

**Figure 4.13** Examples of Mollusca :  
(a) *Pila* (b) *Octopus*

kebo104-s15.content:
This is the **largest phylum** of Animalia which includes insects. Over two-thirds of all named species on earth are arthropods (Figure 4.12). They have organ-system level of organisation. They are bilaterally symmetrical, triploblastic, segmented and coelomate animals. The body of arthropods is covered by chitinous exoskeleton. The body consists of **head, thorax and abdomen**. They have **jointed appendages** (arthros-joint, poda-appendages). Respiratory organs are gills, book gills, book lungs or tracheal system. Circulatory system is of open type. Sensory organs like antennae, eyes (compound and simple), statocysts or balancing organs are present. Excretion takes place through **malpighian tubules**. They are mostly dioecious. Fertilisation is usually internal. They are mostly oviparous. Development may be direct or indirect.

Examples: Economically important insects – *Apis* (Honey bee), *Bombyx* (Silkworm), *Laccifer* (Lac insect)

Vectors – *Anopheles*, *Culex* and *Aedes* (Mosquitoes)

Gregarious pest – *Locusta* (Locust)

Living fossil – *Limulus* (King crab).

kebo104-s16.content:
This is the **second largest** animal phylum (Figure 4.13). Molluscs are terrestrial or aquatic (marine or fresh water) having an organ-system level of organisation. They are bilaterally symmetrical, triploblastic and coelomate animals. Body is covered by a calcareous shell and is unsegmented with a distinct **head, muscular foot and visceral hump**. A soft and spongy layer of skin forms a mantle over the visceral hump. The space between the hump and the mantle is called the mantle cavity in which feather like gills are present. They have respiratory and excretory functions. The anterior head region has sensory tentacles. The mouth contains a file-like rasping organ for feeding, called **radula**.

They are usually dioecious and oviparous with indirect development.

Examples: *Pila* (Apple snail), *Pinctada* (Pearl oyster), *Sepia* (Cuttlefish), *Loligo* (Squid), *Octopus* (Devil fish), *Aplysia* (Sea-hare), *Dentalium* (Tusk shell) and *Chaetopleura* (Chiton).

kebo104-s17.content:
These animals have an endoskeleton of calcareous ossicles and, hence, the name Echinodermata (Spiny bodied, Figure 4.14). All are marine with organ-system level of organisation. The adult echinoderms are radially symmetrical but larvae are bilaterally symmetrical. They are triploblastic and coelomate animals. Digestive system is complete with mouth on the lower (ventral) side and anus on the upper (dorsal) side. The most distinctive feature of echinoderms is the presence of **water vascular system** which helps in locomotion, capture and transport of food and respiration. An excretory system is absent. Sexes are separate. Reproduction is sexual. Fertilisation is usually external. Development is indirect with free-swimming larva.

Examples: *Asterias* (Star fish), *Echinus* (Sea urchin), *Antedon* (Sea lily), *Cucumaria* (Sea cucumber) and *Ophiura* (Brittle star).

The image shows two examples of echinoderms. (a) is a starfish, specifically Asterias, with five arms radiating from a central disk. (b) is a brittle star, specifically Ophiura, which has a central disk and five long, thin, whip-like arms.

**Figure 4.14** Examples of Echinodermata :  
(a) *Asterias*  
(b) *Ophiura*

kebo104-s18.content:
Hemichordata was earlier considered as a sub-phylum under phylum Chordata. But now it is placed as a separate phylum under non-chordata. Hemichordates have a rudimentary structure in the collar region called stomochord, a structure similar to notochord.

This phylum consists of a small group of **worm-like** marine animals with organ-system level of organisation. They are bilaterally symmetrical, triploblastic and coelomate animals. The body is cylindrical and is composed of an anterior **proboscis**, a **collar** and a long **trunk** (Figure 4.15). Circulatory system is of open type. Respiration takes place through gills. Excretory organ is proboscis gland. Sexes are separate. Fertilisation is external. Development is indirect.

Examples: *Balanoglossus* and *Saccoglossus*.

kebo104-s19.content:
Animals belonging to phylum Chordata are fundamentally characterised by the presence of a **notochord**, a **dorsal**

The diagram illustrates the external anatomy of a Balanoglossus. It is an elongated, worm-like organism. The anterior end is divided into a large, bulbous proboscis and a narrower collar region. The rest of the body is a long, tapering trunk. Labels with leader lines point to each of these three regions: Proboscis, Collar, and Trunk.

**Figure 4.15** *Balanoglossus*

graph TD
      NC[Nerve cord] --- GS[Gill slits]
      Notochord --- GS
      Notochord --- PAP[Post-anal part]

**Figure 4.16** Chordata characteristics

**hollow nerve cord** and **paired pharyngeal gill slits** (Figure 4.16). These are bilaterally symmetrical, triploblastic, coelomate with organ-system level of organisation. They possess a post anal tail and a closed circulatory system.

Table 4.1 presents a comparison of salient features of chordates and non-chordates.

**TABLE 4.1 Comparison of Chordates and Non-chordates**

**Figure 4.17** *Ascidia*

Phylum Chordata is divided into three subphyla: **Urochordata** or **Tunicata**, **Cephalochordata** and **Vertebrata**.

Subphyla Urochordata and Cephalochordata are often referred to as **protochordates** (Figure 4.17) and are exclusively marine. In Urochordata, notochord is present only in larval tail, while in Cephalochordata, it extends from head to tail region and is persistent throughout their life.

Examples: Urochordata – *Ascidia*, *Salpa*, *Doliolum*; Cephalochordata – *Branchiostoma* (Amphioxus or Lancelet).

The members of subphylum Vertebrata possess notochord during the embryonic period. The notochord is replaced by a cartilaginous or bony **vertebral column** in the adult. Thus all vertebrates are chordates but all chordates are not vertebrates. Besides the basic chordate characters, vertebrates have a ventral muscular heart with two, three or four chambers, kidneys for excretion and osmoregulation and paired appendages which may be fins or limbs.

The subphylum Vertebrata is further divided as follows:

graph TD

(lacks jaw)]
    Vertebrata -- Division --> Gnathostomata[Gnathostomata  
(bears jaw)]

(bear fins)]

kebo104-s20.content:
All living members of the class Cyclostomata are ectoparasites on some fishes. They have an elongated body bearing 6-15 pairs of **gill slits** for respiration. Cyclostomes have a sucking and circular mouth without jaws (Fig. 4.18). Their body is devoid of scales and paired fins. Cranium and vertebral column are cartilaginous. Circulation is of closed type. Cyclostomes are marine but migrate for spawning to fresh water. After spawning, within a few days, they die. Their larvae, after metamorphosis, return to the ocean.

Examples: *Petromyzon* (Lamprey) and *Myxine* (Hagfish).

kebo104-s21.content:
They are marine animals with streamlined body and have cartilaginous endoskeleton (Figure 4.19). Mouth is located ventrally. **Notochord is persistent** throughout life. Gill slits are separate and without **operculum** (gill cover). The skin is tough, containing minute **placoid scales**. Teeth are modified placoid scales which are backwardly directed. Their jaws are very powerful. These animals are predaceous. Due to the absence of air bladder, they have to swim constantly to avoid sinking.

**Figure 4.18** A jawless vertebrate - *Petromyzon*

**Figure 4.19** Example of Cartilaginous fishes :  
(a) *Scoliodon* (b) *Pristis*

A detailed black and white illustration of a seahorse (Hippocampus) in a vertical orientation, facing left. It has a segmented body, a long curled tail at the bottom, and several small fins along its back and sides.

**Figure 4.20** Examples of Bony fishes :  
(a) *Hippocampus* (b) *Catla*

Two illustrations of amphibians. (a) shows a salamander, which is elongated with a long tail, four legs, and a mottled pattern of dark spots on a lighter background. (b) shows a frog, which is squat with a short body, four legs, and a mottled green and yellow pattern.

**Figure 4.21** Examples of Amphibia :  
(a) *Salamandra*  
(b) *Rana*

Heart is two-chambered (one auricle and one ventricle). Some of them have **electric organs** (e.g., *Torpedo*) and some possess **poison sting** (e.g., *Trygon*). They are cold-blooded (**poikilothermous**) animals, i.e., they lack the capacity to regulate their body temperature. Sexes are separate. In males pelvic fins bear claspers. They have internal fertilisation and many of them are viviparous.

Examples: *Scoliodon* (Dog fish), *Pristis* (Saw fish), *Carcharodon* (Great white shark), *Trygon* (Sting ray).

kebo104-s22.content:
It includes both marine and fresh water fishes with bony endoskeleton. Their body is streamlined. Mouth is mostly terminal (Figure 4.20). They have four pairs of gills which are covered by an **operculum** on each side. Skin is covered with cycloid/ctenoid scales. **Air bladder** is present which regulates buoyancy. Heart is two-chambered (one auricle and one ventricle). They are cold-blooded animals. Sexes are separate. Fertilisation is usually external. They are mostly oviparous and development is direct.

Examples: Marine – *Exocoetus* (Flying fish), *Hippocampus* (Sea horse); Freshwater – *Labeo* (Rohu), *Catla* (Katla), *Clarias* (Magur); Aquarium – *Betta* (Fighting fish), *Pterophyllum* (Angel fish).

kebo104-s23.content:
As the name indicates (Gr., *Amphi* : dual, *bios*, life), amphibians can live in aquatic as well as terrestrial habitats (Figure 4.21). Most of them have two pairs of limbs. Body is divisible into **head** and **trunk**. Tail may be present in some. The amphibian skin is moist (without scales). The eyes have eyelids. A **tympanum** represents the ear. Alimentary canal, urinary and reproductive tracts open into a common chamber called **cloaca** which opens to the exterior. Respiration is by gills, lungs and through skin. The heart is three-chambered (two auricles and one ventricle). These are cold-blooded animals. Sexes are separate. Fertilisation is external. They are oviparous and development is indirect.

Examples: *Bufo* (Toad), *Rana* (Frog), *Hyla* (Tree frog), *Salamandra* (Salamander), *Ichthyophis* (Limbless amphibia).

The image contains four separate illustrations of reptiles, labeled (a) through (d). (a) shows a green chameleon clinging to a brown branch. (b) shows a green crocodile from a top-down perspective. (c) shows a green turtle from a top-down perspective, highlighting its shell. (d) shows a black cobra snake coiled and raised, with its hood flared.

**Figure 4.22** Reptiles: (a) *Chameleon* (b) *Crocodilus* (c) *Chelone* (d) *Naja*

kebo104-s24.content:
The class name refers to their creeping or crawling mode of locomotion (*Latin, repere* or *reptum*, to creep or crawl). They are mostly terrestrial animals and their body is covered by dry and cornified skin, epidermal **scales** or **scutes** (Fig. 4.22). They do not have external ear openings. Tympanum represents ear. Limbs, when present, are two pairs. Heart is usually three-chambered, but four-chambered in crocodiles. Reptiles are poikilotherms. Snakes and lizards shed their scales as skin cast. Sexes are separate. Fertilisation is internal. They are oviparous and development is direct.

Examples: *Chelone* (Turtle), *Testudo* (Tortoise), *Chameleon* (Tree lizard), *Calotes* (Garden lizard), *Crocodilus* (Crocodile), *Alligator* (Alligator). *Hemidactylus* (Wall lizard), Poisonous snakes – *Naja* (Cobra), *Bangarus* (Krait), *Vipera* (Viper).

kebo104-s25.content:
The characteristic features of Aves (birds) are the presence of **feathers** and most of them can fly except flightless birds (e.g., Ostrich). They possess **beak** (Figure 4.23). The forelimbs are modified into **wings**. The hind limbs generally have scales and are modified for walking, swimming or clasping the tree branches. Skin is dry without glands except the oil gland at the base of the tail. Endoskeleton is fully ossified (bony) and the long bones are hollow with **air cavities** (pneumatic). The digestive tract of birds has additional chambers, the crop and gizzard. Heart is completely four-chambered. They are warm-blooded (**homioiothermous**) animals, i.e., they are able to maintain a constant body temperature. Respiration is by

Figure 4.23 shows four different birds. (a) Neophron (Vulture) is a large bird with dark plumage and a white head. (b) Struthio (Ostrich) is a large, flightless bird with a long neck and long legs. (c) Psittacula (Parrot) is a small, colorful bird with green feathers and a red beak. (d) Pavo (Peacock) is a bird with a long, colorful tail.

**Figure 4.23** Some birds : (a) *Neophron* (b) *Struthio* (c) *Psittacula* (d) *Pavo*

lungs. Air sacs connected to lungs supplement respiration. Sexes are separate. Fertilisation is internal. They are oviparous and development is direct.

Examples : *Corvus* (Crow), *Columba* (Pigeon), *Psittacula* (Parrot), *Struthio* (Ostrich), *Pavo* (Peacock), *Aptenodytes* (Penguin), *Neophron* (Vulture).

kebo104-s26.content:
They are found in a variety of habitats – polar ice caps, deserts, mountains, forests, grasslands and dark caves. Some of them have adapted to fly or live in water. The most unique mammalian characteristic is the presence of milk producing glands (**mammary glands**) by which the young ones are nourished. They have two pairs of limbs, adapted for walking, running, climbing, burrowing, swimming or flying (Figure 4.24). The skin of

Figure 4.24 shows four different mammals. (a) Ornithorhynchus (Platypus) is a small, brown, furry animal with a duck-like bill. (b) Macropus (Kangaroo) is a large, brown, furry animal with a long tail and a pouch. (c) Pteropus (Flying Fox) is a bat with large, dark, leathery wings. (d) Balaenoptera (Whale) is a large, light blue, streamlined animal with a small dorsal fin.

**Figure 4.24** Some mammals : (a) *Ornithorhynchus* (b) *Macropus* (c) *Pteropus* (d) *Balaenoptera*

mammals is unique in possessing **hair**. External ears or **pinnae** are present. Different types of teeth are present in the jaw. Heart is four-chambered. They are homoiothermous. Respiration is by lungs. Sexes are separate and fertilisation is internal. They are viviparous with few exceptions and development is direct.

Examples: Oviparous-*Ornithorhynchus* (Platypus); Viviparous - *Macropus* (Kangaroo), *Pteropus* (Flying fox), *Camelus* (Camel), *Macaca* (Monkey), *Rattus* (Rat), *Canis* (Dog), *Felis* (Cat), *Elephas* (Elephant), *Equus* (Horse), *Delphinus* (Common dolphin), *Balaenoptera* (Blue whale), *Panthera tigris* (Tiger), *Panthera leo* (Lion).

The salient distinguishing features of all phyla under animal kingdom is comprehensively given in the Table 4.2.

**TABLE 4.2 Salient Features of Different Phyla in the Animal Kingdom**

kebo104-s27.content:
The basic fundamental features such as level of organisation, symmetry, cell organisation, coelom, segmentation, notochord, etc., have enabled us to broadly classify the animal kingdom. Besides the fundamental features, there are many other distinctive characters which are specific for each phyla or class.

Porifera includes multicellular animals which exhibit cellular level of organisation and have characteristic flagellated choanocytes. The coelenterates have tentacles and bear cnidoblasts. They are mostly aquatic, sessile or free-floating. The ctenophores are marine animals with comb plates. The platyhelminths have flat body and exhibit bilateral symmetry. The parasitic forms show distinct suckers and hooks. Aschelminthes are pseudocoelomates and include parasitic as well as non-parasitic roundworms.

Annelids are metamERICALLY segmented animals with a true coelom. The arthropods are the most abundant group of animals characterised by the presence of jointed appendages. The molluscs have a soft body surrounded by an external calcareous shell. The body is covered with external skeleton made of chitin. The echinoderms possess a spiny skin. Their most distinctive feature is the presence of water vascular system. The hemichordates are a small group of worm-like marine animals. They have a cylindrical body with proboscis, collar and trunk.

Phylum Chordata includes animals which possess a notochord either throughout or during early embryonic life. Other common features observed in the chordates are the dorsal, hollow nerve cord and paired pharyngeal gill slits. Some of the vertebrates do not possess jaws (Agnatha) whereas most of them possess jaws (Gnathostomata). Agnatha is represented by the class, Cyclostomata. They are the most primitive chordates and are ectoparasites on fishes. Gnathostomata has two super classes, Pisces and Tetrapoda. Classes Chondrichthyes and Osteichthyes bear fins for locomotion and are grouped under Pisces. The Chondrichthyes are fishes with cartilaginous endoskeleton and are marine. Classes, Amphibia, Reptilia, Aves and Mammalia have two pairs of limbs and are thus grouped under Tetrapoda. The amphibians have adapted to live both on land and water. Reptiles are characterised by the presence

of dry and cornified skin. Limbs are absent in snakes. Fishes, amphibians and reptiles are poikilothermous (cold-blooded). Aves are warm-blooded animals with feathers on their bodies and forelimbs modified into wings for flying. Hind limbs are adapted for walking, swimming, perching or clasping. The unique features of mammals are the presence of mammary glands and hairs on the skin. They commonly exhibit viviparity.

kebo104-d000.content: Diagram: Examples of arthropods: a locust, a butterfly, a scorpion, and a prawn (diagram). Caption: Figure 4.12: Examples of Arthropoda. (a) Locust, a green insect with long legs and antennae. (b) Butterfly, a yellow and blue insect with large wings. (c) Scorpion, a dark, segmented arachnid with pincers and a stinging tail. (d) Prawn, a shrimp-like crustacean with a segmented body and multiple legs. Description: This diagram represents four representative members of phylum Arthropoda, illustrating their external morphology and major diagnostic features. The locust is an insect with a distinctly segmented body divided into head, thorax, and abdomen, one pair of antennae, compound eyes, three pairs of jointed legs, and two pairs of wings; the enlarged hind legs are adapted for jumping. The butterfly is also an insect with the same basic body plan—head, thorax, and abdomen—but with two pairs of large, often brightly colored wings, clubbed antennae, compound eyes, and three pairs of jointed legs. The scorpion is an arachnid, not an insect; it has a cephalothorax and abdomen, four pairs of walking legs, pedipalps modified into pincers (chelae), and a segmented tail ending in a venomous stinger. The prawn is a crustacean with a cephalothorax covered by a carapace, a segmented abdomen, two pairs of antennae, multiple pairs of appendages, and a tail fan formed by the uropods and telson. Together, these examples show the diversity of arthropod body organization, jointed appendages, segmentation, and exoskeleton-based form. Labels: Locust, Butterfly, Scorpion, Prawn. NEET relevance: Identification of arthropod examples and their taxonomic grouping; Distinguishing insects, arachnids, and crustaceans by body segments and appendages; Recognizing key arthropod features such as jointed appendages, segmentation, and exoskeleton. Board relevance: Draw and label examples of Arthropoda; Differentiate locust, butterfly, scorpion, and prawn based on external morphology; State diagnostic features of Arthropoda and classify the given examples. Exam-critical labels: Locust, Butterfly, Scorpion, Prawn, (a), (b), (c), (d). Common mistakes: Calling the scorpion an insect (correct: It is an arachnid); Labeling the prawn as a fish or insect (correct: It is a crustacean); Treating the butterfly wings as one pair (correct: Butterflies have two pairs of wings).

kebo104-d001.content: Diagram: external anatomy of Balanoglossus (diagram). Caption: The diagram illustrates the external anatomy of a Balanoglossus. It is an elongated, worm-like organism. The anterior end is divided into a large, bulbous proboscis and a narrower collar region. The rest of the body is a long, tapering trunk. Labels with leader lines point to each of these three regions: Proboscis, Collar, and Trunk. Description: Balanoglossus is a marine, soft-bodied hemichordate commonly called an acorn worm. Its external body is characteristically tripartite, consisting of an anterior proboscis, a short collar immediately behind it, and a long trunk forming the major posterior body region. The proboscis is a muscular, glandular, often conical or bulbous anterior lobe used in burrowing, locomotion, and feeding-related activities. The collar is a short cylindrical segment that surrounds the mouth region and separates the proboscis from the trunk. The trunk is the longest part of the body and is typically subdivided externally into branchial, hepatic, and abdominal regions in many textbook depictions; it bears the gill slits in the branchial region and contains the major visceral organs. The diagram should represent a single elongated animal with the proboscis at the anterior end, the collar directly posterior to it, and the trunk extending posteriorly as the longest region. Labels: Proboscis, Collar, Trunk. NEET relevance: Tripartite body plan of hemichordates; Identification of proboscis, collar, and trunk in Balanoglossus; Classification of Balanoglossus as a hemichordate/acorn worm. Board relevance: Draw and label the external anatomy of Balanoglossus; Mention the three body regions in order; State the function of proboscis, collar, and trunk. Exam-critical labels: Proboscis, Collar, Trunk. Common mistakes: Calling the collar a neck or head (correct: The collar is a distinct short body region of hemichordates); Treating the proboscis as a head (correct: The proboscis is an anterior locomotory and feeding structure, not a head); Labeling the entire posterior body simply as abdomen (correct: The correct label is trunk, which may be subdivided into branchial, hepatic, and abdominal regions).

kebo104-d002.content: Diagram: Examples of Coelenterata/Cnidaria showing medusa and polyp body forms (diagram). Caption: Figure 4.6: Examples of Coelenterata indicating outline of their body form. (a) Aurelia (Medusa) showing a bell-shaped body with tentacles and internal structures. (b) Adamsia (Polyp) showing a cylindrical body with tentacles at the top and a basal disc. Below each illustration is a simplified magenta outline diagram: (a) shows the umbrella-like shape of a medusa, and (b) shows the cylindrical shape of a polyp. Description: This diagram represents the two fundamental body forms of cnidarians (Coelenterata): the medusa and the polyp. The medusa is the free-swimming, umbrella- or bell-shaped form, exemplified by Aurelia (jellyfish), with the mouth located on a central manubrium on the underside, tentacles hanging from the bell margin, and internal gastrovascular structures visible within the bell. The polyp is the sessile, cylindrical form, exemplified by Adamsia (sea anemone), with the oral end bearing a ring of tentacles surrounding the mouth, and the opposite aboral end attached to a substratum by a basal disc. The diagram is meant to show the contrast in body organization: medusa adapted for locomotion and dispersal, polyp adapted for attachment and feeding. The simplified outlines below each organism emphasize the characteristic overall shape of each body form. Labels: Aurelia, Medusa, Bell, Tentacles, Manubrium, Gastrovascular cavity, Adamsia, Polyp, Oral disc, Mouth, Basal disc. NEET relevance: Identify medusa and polyp body forms in cnidarians; Differentiate Aurelia (medusa) and Adamsia (polyp); State key features of medusa vs polyp. Board relevance: Draw and label the medusa and polyp forms of cnidarians; Differentiate between Aurelia and Adamsia with diagrams; Label tentacles, mouth, basal disc, bell, and oral disc. Exam-critical labels: Aurelia, Adamsia, Medusa, Polyp, Tentacles, Mouth, Basal disc, Bell. Common mistakes: Calling the medusa a polyp (correct: The left figure is a medusa (Aurelia), not a polyp); Calling the polyp a medusa (correct: The right figure is a polyp (Adamsia), not a medusa); Labeling the top of the polyp as a bell (correct: It is the oral disc with tentacles).

kebo104-d003.content: Diagram: Examples of Porifera (sponges) (diagram). Caption: Figure 4.5 shows three examples of Porifera: (a) Sycon, a vase-shaped sponge with two long, finger-like projections; (b) Euspongia, a large, rounded, sac-like sponge; and (c) Spongilla, a branching, finger-like sponge. All three are shown in shades of pink and purple, with small dots representing pores (ostia) and larger openings (oscula). Description: This diagram represents three common examples of Porifera (sponges), which are simple multicellular aquatic animals characterized by a porous body wall, a canal system, and a central cavity (spongocoel) that opens to the exterior through an osculum. In the textbook figure, (a) is Sycon (Scypha), a small marine sponge with a vase-like body and a folded body wall that forms radial canals; (b) is Euspongia (bath sponge), a large marine sponge with a rounded, irregular, sac-like body and numerous pores on the surface; and (c) is Spongilla, a freshwater sponge that is typically branched and finger-like. All sponges lack true tissues and organs, and their body wall contains ostia for water entry, choanocyte-lined canals/chambers for filtration, and an osculum for water exit. The diagram is meant to show the external body forms of these representative poriferans rather than internal histology in detail. Labels: Sycon (Scypha), Euspongia, Spongilla, Ostia, Osculum, Body wall. NEET relevance: Identify examples of Porifera shown in the figure; Differentiate marine and freshwater sponges; Recall that Sycon, Euspongia, and Spongilla are examples of Porifera. Board relevance: Draw and label examples of Porifera; Write short notes on Sycon, Euspongia, and Spongilla; Differentiate sponge body forms. Exam-critical labels: Sycon, Euspongia, Spongilla, ostia, osculum, Porifera. Common mistakes: Calling the surface dots 'cells' or 'spicules' without context (correct: They represent ostia or surface pores in a simplified textbook diagram); Labeling the top opening as 'mouth' (correct: It is the osculum); Treating Sycon, Euspongia, and Spongilla as parts of one organism (correct: They are three separate examples of Porifera).

kebo104-d004.content: Diagram: Two cartilaginous fishes, a shark-like fish and a ray/sawfish-like fish (diagram). Caption: Two illustrations of cartilaginous fishes. (a) shows a shark, identified as Scoliodon, with a streamlined body and visible fins. (b) shows a ray, identified as Pristis, with a flattened body and a long, pointed snout. Description: This diagram represents two examples of class Chondrichthyes (cartilaginous fishes): a shark and a ray/sawfish. The upper figure should depict a shark, specifically Scoliodon, in lateral view with a streamlined, fusiform body adapted for active swimming. It should show a pointed snout, terminal mouth on the ventral side, five pairs of gill slits on the lateral head region, paired pectoral and pelvic fins, two dorsal fins, and a heterocercal tail in which the upper lobe is larger than the lower lobe. The body is covered with placoid scales, though these are usually not individually drawn in textbook diagrams. The lower figure should depict a ray/sawfish-like chondrichthyan, specifically Pristis in NCERT context, with a dorsoventrally flattened body adapted for benthic life, enlarged pectoral fins fused with the head region, eyes and spiracles on the dorsal surface, gill slits on the ventral surface, and a long saw-like rostrum bearing lateral tooth-like projections. Together, the figures illustrate the contrasting body forms within cartilaginous fishes: one adapted for pelagic swimming and the other for bottom-dwelling or benthic locomotion. Labels: Scoliodon, Pristis, rostrum, gill slits, pectoral fin, dorsal fin, pelvic fin, heterocercal tail, spiracle, eye. NEET relevance: Identification of class Chondrichthyes from external morphology; Difference between shark and ray/sawfish body form; Heterocercal tail in sharks. Board relevance: Draw and label a shark and a ray/sawfish; Differentiate between shark and ray based on body shape, fin arrangement, and tail; Write characteristics of class Chondrichthyes. Exam-critical labels: Scoliodon, Pristis, gill slits, pectoral fin, dorsal fin, heterocercal tail, rostrum. Common mistakes: Calling the lower figure a ray only, without noting that NCERT specifically uses Pristis (sawfish). (correct: It should be identified as Pristis, a sawfish-like cartilaginous fish.); Labeling the shark tail as a homocercal tail. (correct: It is a heterocercal tail.); Placing gill slits on the dorsal surface of the ray/sawfish. (correct: Gill slits are ventral in rays and sawfish.).

kebo104-d005.content: Diagram: classification of Vertebrata (diagram). Caption: ``` Description: This diagram represents the standard NCERT hierarchical classification of Vertebrata, a subphylum of Chordata characterized by a vertebral column, cranium, dorsal hollow nerve cord, and an endoskeleton of cartilage or bone. Vertebrata is divided into two major divisions based on the presence or absence of jaws: Agnatha (jawless vertebrates) and Gnathostomata (jawed vertebrates). Agnatha includes Cyclostomata, the living jawless vertebrates such as lampreys and hagfishes. Gnathostomata includes all jawed vertebrates and is further divided into two superclasses: Pisces and Tetrapoda. Pisces are aquatic vertebrates with fins and gills; in NCERT they include Chondrichthyes (cartilaginous fishes) and Osteichthyes (bony fishes). Tetrapoda are vertebrates with two pairs of limbs or limb derivatives and include Amphibia, Reptilia, Aves, and Mammalia. The diagram is a branching tree showing evolutionary and taxonomic relationships from the broad group Vertebrata down to progressively more specific classes. Labels: Vertebrata, Division, Agnatha, (lacks jaws), Cyclostomata, Gnathostomata, (bears jaws), Superclass, Pisces, (bear fins), Chondrichthyes, Osteichthyes, Tetrapoda, (bear limbs), Amphibia. NEET relevance: Classification of Vertebrata based on jaws and appendages; Difference between Agnatha and Gnathostomata; Examples of Pisces and Tetrapoda. Board relevance: Draw and label the classification of Vertebrata; Differentiate Agnatha and Gnathostomata; Write examples of classes under Pisces and Tetrapoda. Exam-critical labels: Vertebrata, Agnatha, Gnathostomata, Cyclostomata, Pisces, Tetrapoda, Chondrichthyes, Osteichthyes. Common mistakes: Cyclostomata is placed under Pisces (correct: Cyclostomata belongs under Agnatha); Pisces is treated as a class (correct: Pisces is shown here as a superclass in NCERT classification); Tetrapoda is placed under Pisces (correct: Tetrapoda is a separate superclass under Gnathostomata).

kebo104-d006.content: Diagram: radial symmetry in a sea anemone-like animal (diagram). Caption: Figure 4.1 (a) Radial symmetry: A diagram of a sea anemone showing multiple planes of symmetry passing through its central axis. Description: The diagram represents a cnidarian polyp, specifically a sea anemone, showing radial symmetry. In a true sea anemone, the body is organized around a central oral-aboral axis, with the mouth at the oral end surrounded by a crown of tentacles. Any longitudinal plane passing through this central axis divides the animal into two similar halves, which is the defining feature of radial symmetry. The body is typically cylindrical and attached at the aboral end by a basal disc, while the oral end bears the mouth and tentacles. The tentacles are arranged in a circular or whorled manner around the mouth and are used for prey capture and defense, containing cnidocytes. The diagram should emphasize that multiple planes can pass through the central axis and produce equivalent halves, unlike bilateral symmetry where only one plane does so. Labels: Tentacles, Mouth, Oral disc, Body column, Basal disc, Central axis, Planes of symmetry. NEET relevance: Definition of radial symmetry and identification of sea anemone as an example; Difference between radial and bilateral symmetry; Recognition that multiple planes of symmetry pass through the central axis. Board relevance: Draw and label a sea anemone showing radial symmetry; Explain why sea anemone is radially symmetrical; Mark the planes of symmetry and central axis. Exam-critical labels: Mouth, Tentacles, Body column, Basal disc, Central axis, Planes of symmetry. Common mistakes: Calling the central opening the anus (correct: It is the mouth); Treating the black lines as body parts (correct: They are planes of symmetry); Labeling the lower end as a foot without context (correct: Basal disc).

(b) Struthio (Ostrich) is a large, flightless bird with a long neck and long legs. (c) Psittacula (Parrot) is a small, colorful bird with green feathers and a red beak. (d) Pavo (Peacock) is a bird with a long, colorful tail. Exam-critical labels: Neophron (Vulture), Struthio (Ostrich), Psittacula (Parrot), Pavo (Peacock). kebo104-d008.content: Diagram: Two amphibians: a salamander and a frog (diagram). Caption: Two illustrations of amphibians. (a) shows a salamander, which is elongated with a long tail, four legs, and a mottled pattern of dark spots on a lighter background. (b) shows a frog, which is squat with a short body, four legs, and a mottled green and yellow pattern. Description: The diagram should show two representative amphibians: a salamander and a frog. The salamander (Salamandra) is an elongated, lizard-like amphibian with a distinct head, trunk, four limbs, and a long tail; it is adapted for a terrestrial or semi-aquatic life and retains a tail throughout life. The frog (Rana) is a tailless amphibian with a short, compact body, a broad head, four limbs, and especially long, muscular hind limbs adapted for jumping and swimming. In a correct NCERT-style comparison, the salamander is typically shown above and the frog below, each in lateral view facing the same direction, to emphasize the contrast between a tailed amphibian and a tailless amphibian. The key biological point is that both belong to class Amphibia, but they differ markedly in body form, tail presence, and limb specialization. Labels: (a) Salamandra (salamander), (b) Rana (frog). NEET relevance: Identification of amphibian examples: salamander and frog; Difference between tailed amphibians and tailless amphibians; Recognition that frogs belong to order Anura and salamanders to Urodela/Caudata. Board relevance: Draw and label the diagram of two amphibians: salamander and frog; Differentiate between salamander and frog based on body form and tail; State examples of class Amphibia. Exam-critical labels: Salamandra, Rana, salamander, frog, tail present in salamander, tail absent in frog. Common mistakes: Calling the salamander a lizard (correct: It is an amphibian, specifically Salamandra); Calling the frog tailed (correct: Adult frogs are tailless); Labeling the lower animal as toad instead of frog (correct: The NCERT figure specifically shows Rana (frog)).

kebo104-d009.content: Diagram: Diagrammatic sectional views of three types of body cavities: coelomate, pseudocoelomate, and acoelomate (cross_section). Caption: Figure 4.3: Diagrammatic sectional view of three types of body cavities. (a) Coelomate: Shows a cross-section of an animal with a true coelom, which is a fluid-filled cavity completely lined by mesoderm (red layer). (b) Pseudocoelomate: Shows a cross-section with a pseudocoelom, where the cavity is not fully lined by mesoderm. (c) Acoelomate: Shows a cross-section with no body cavity, where the space between the gut and body wall is filled with mesoderm (red layer). Labels 'Coelom' and 'Pseudocoelom' point to the respective cavities in (a) and (b). Description: This diagram represents transverse sectional views of three fundamental body-cavity conditions in triploblastic animals: coelomate, pseudocoelomate, and acoelomate. In a coelomate, the body wall and gut are separated by a true coelom, a fluid-filled cavity completely lined by mesoderm on both the body-wall side and the gut side; the mesoderm forms the parietal layer lining the body wall and the visceral layer covering the gut, with mesenteries suspending the gut in the cavity. In a pseudocoelomate, the cavity between the body wall and gut is a pseudocoelom, derived from the embryonic blastocoel and not completely lined by mesoderm; mesoderm lines only the outer body wall, while the gut is not fully surrounded by mesoderm. In an acoelomate, there is no body cavity at all; the space between the body wall and gut is filled with solid mesodermal tissue, so the gut lies embedded in mesenchyme/parenchyma rather than in a fluid-filled cavity. Functionally, a coelom provides space for organ development, movement, and cushioning; a pseudocoelom also provides internal space but lacks the complete mesodermal lining; an acoelomate lacks such a cavity entirely. Labels: Coelomate, Pseudocoelomate, Acoelomate, Body wall, Mesoderm, Coelom, Pseudocoelom, Gut, Mesentery. NEET relevance: Differentiate coelomate, pseudocoelomate, and acoelomate based on mesodermal lining.; Identify which animals are coelomate, pseudocoelomate, or acoelomate.; Explain the origin of coelom versus pseudocoelom.. Board relevance: Draw and label sectional views of coelomate, pseudocoelomate, and acoelomate body organization.; Write differences between coelom, pseudocoelom, and no coelom.; State examples of each body-cavity type.. Exam-critical labels: Coelomate, Pseudocoelomate, Acoelomate, Coelom, Pseudocoelom, Body wall, Mesoderm, Gut. Common mistakes: Calling the pseudocoelom a true coelom (correct: It is a pseudocoelom, not fully lined by mesoderm); Labeling the solid mesoderm in an acoelomate as a cavity (correct: There is no cavity in an acoelomate; the space is filled with mesoderm); Thinking the gut is the cavity (correct: The gut is the central digestive tube; the cavity is the space around it).

kebo104-d010.content: Diagram: Illustrations of annelid worms, showing two worm-like organisms side by side (diagram). Caption: Figure 4.11: Illustrations of Annelida. (a) Nereis, a segmented worm with many lateral appendages (parapodia). (b) Hirudinaria, a segmented, elongated worm with a distinct head region. Description: This diagram represents two annelid worms from NCERT Figure 4.11: Nereis and Hirudinaria. Nereis is a marine polychaete annelid with a clearly segmented body, a distinct anterior head region bearing sensory appendages, and paired parapodia on most segments; these parapodia carry chaetae (bristles) and function in locomotion and respiration. Hirudinaria is a leech, a freshwater hirudinean annelid with a dorsoventrally flattened, elongated, externally annulated body, a distinct anterior sucker-bearing head region, and a posterior sucker for attachment and movement; unlike Nereis, it lacks parapodia and chaetae. The diagram should emphasize the annelid body segmentation, the parapodia and bristles in Nereis, and the suckers and annulated body of Hirudinaria, showing their contrasting external morphology within the same phylum. Labels: Nereis, Hirudinaria, Parapodia, Chaetae (bristles), Anterior sucker, Posterior sucker, Segmentation / annuli, Head region. NEET relevance: Identify examples of phylum Annelida.; Differentiate Nereis and Hirudinaria based on parapodia, chaetae, and suckers.; Recognize that annelids are segmented worms.. Board relevance: Draw and label Nereis and Hirudinaria.; Label parapodia, chaetae, anterior sucker, posterior sucker, and segmentation.; Differentiate polychaetes and leeches using external features.. Exam-critical labels: Nereis, Hirudinaria, Parapodia, Chaetae, Anterior sucker, Posterior sucker, Segmentation / annuli. Common mistakes: Roundworm (correct: Annelid worm (Nereis and Hirudinaria)); Both are the same type of worm (correct: Left is Nereis; right is Hirudinaria); Legs or fins (correct: Parapodia).

kebo104-d011.content: Diagram: Ctenophora (Pleurobrachia) (diagram). Caption: Figure 4.8: Illustration of a Ctenophora (Pleurobrachia), showing its characteristic eight rows of ciliated comb plates and two long, branched tentacles. Description: The diagram should represent Pleurobrachia, a marine ctenophore (comb jelly), with a transparent, gelatinous, usually spherical to ovoid body bearing eight longitudinal rows of ciliary comb plates (ctenes) arranged meridionally from the aboral pole toward the oral end. These comb rows are locomotory structures and may appear as iridescent bands. At the oral end, the mouth opens into the gastrovascular cavity, and two long retractile tentacles arise from tentacular sheaths or tentacle bulbs located laterally near the oral region. Each tentacle is branched and bears colloblasts, adhesive cells used for prey capture; unlike cnidarians, ctenophores do not possess cnidoblasts or nematocysts. The aboral pole bears the statocyst, the balance organ that coordinates ciliary beating. The overall organization should show radial/biradial symmetry, with the comb rows running longitudinally and the tentacles hanging downward from the oral side, often extending well beyond the body. Labels: Statocyst, Comb plates (ctenes), Body wall / gelatinous mesoglea, Tentacle sheath, Tentacle, Tentilla / branched tentacular filaments, Colloblasts, Mouth, Gastrovascular cavity. NEET relevance: Identify Pleurobrachia as a ctenophore.; Distinguish ctenophores from cnidarians: comb plates and colloblasts vs cnidoblasts/nematocysts.; Recall that ctenophores are marine, diploblastic, radially/biradially symmetrical, and have eight rows of comb plates.. Board relevance: Draw and label Pleurobrachia.; Label eight comb rows, statocyst, tentacles, and tentacle sheaths.; Differentiate ctenophore tentacles from cnidarian tentacles..
