Exam-critical labels: Statocyst, Comb plates (ctenes), Tentacles, Colloblasts, Tentacle sheaths, Mouth. Common mistakes: Cnidoblasts / nematocysts on the tentacles (correct: Colloblasts on the tentacles); Eight rows of cilia or 'cilia bands' without specifying comb plates (correct: Eight rows of comb plates (ctenes)); Tentacles arising from the aboral pole (correct: Tentacles arising from the oral/lateral region via tentacle sheaths).

kebo104-d012.content: Diagram: male and female roundworms (Aschelminthes) (diagram). Caption: Male                      Female Description: The diagram represents sexual dimorphism in roundworms (phylum Aschelminthes/Nematoda), specifically the difference between the male and female Ascaris-type worm. The male is typically smaller, slender, and more curved posteriorly; its posterior end is characteristically ventrally curved and may appear coiled. The female is larger, longer, and stouter, with a straighter body and a more blunt posterior end. In nematodes, sexes are separate (dioecious), and this size and shape difference is a key external feature used to distinguish males from females. The diagram should therefore show two whole worms side by side, with the male on one side as the thinner, shorter, more curved individual and the female on the other side as the thicker, longer individual. Labels: Male, Female. NEET relevance: Sexual dimorphism in Ascaris/roundworms; Identification of male versus female based on body size and posterior end shape; Dioecious nature of nematodes. Board relevance: Draw and label male and female Ascaris; Differentiate male and female roundworm on the basis of size and posterior end; State that roundworms are dioecious. Exam-critical labels: Male, Female, Posterior curved/coiled end of male, Longer, thicker body of female. Common mistakes: Calling the thicker worm the male because it looks larger or more prominent (correct: The thicker, longer worm is the female; the male is smaller and thinner); Describing the male as straight and the female as coiled (correct: The male is typically more curved posteriorly; the female is comparatively straighter); Treating the figure as showing internal anatomy (correct: This figure shows external sexual dimorphism only).

kebo104-d013.content: Diagram: reptiles (diagram). Caption: The image contains four separate illustrations of reptiles, labeled (a) through (d). (a) shows a green chameleon clinging to a brown branch. (b) shows a green crocodile from a top-down perspective. (c) shows a green turtle from a top-down perspective, highlighting its shell. (d) shows a black cobra snake coiled and raised, with its hood flared. Description: This diagram represents representative members of Class Reptilia, illustrating their typical external morphology and diversity: a chameleon, a crocodile, a turtle, and a cobra. Reptiles are ectothermic vertebrates with dry, keratinized, scaly skin and respiration by lungs. The chameleon is an arboreal lizard adapted for camouflage, grasping branches with zygodactylous feet and often using a projectile tongue to capture insects. The crocodile is a semi-aquatic reptile with a long snout, powerful jaws, a laterally compressed tail for swimming, and dorsal scutes. The turtle has a rigid bony shell composed of the carapace and plastron, with limbs modified into flippers or stout legs depending on habitat. The cobra is a venomous snake characterized by an elongated limbless body, expandable hood formed by cervical ribs, and specialized fangs for venom delivery. Together, these figures exemplify the major reptilian body plans and adaptations for arboreal, aquatic, and terrestrial life. Labels: Chameleon, Crocodile, Turtle, Cobra. NEET relevance: Identification of Class Reptilia representatives; Distinguishing features of reptiles: dry scaly skin, ectothermy, lungs, internal fertilization, shelled eggs; Adaptive features of chameleon, crocodile, turtle, and cobra. Board relevance: Draw and label the four examples of reptiles shown in NCERT; Write characteristics of Class Reptilia; Explain adaptations of chameleon, crocodile, turtle, and cobra. Exam-critical labels: Chameleon, Crocodile, Turtle, Cobra. Common mistakes: Calling the turtle a tortoise (correct: It should be labeled as a turtle in this NCERT figure); Identifying the cobra as a viper (correct: It is a cobra); Treating the chameleon tongue as a snake or worm (correct: It is the chameleon’s protrusible tongue).

kebo104-d014.content: Diagram: Four mammals (diagram). Caption: Figure 4.24 shows four different mammals. (a) Ornithorhynchus (Platypus) is a small, brown, furry animal with a duck-like bill. (b) Macropus (Kangaroo) is a large, brown, furry animal with a long tail and a pouch. (c) Pteropus (Flying Fox) is a bat with large, dark, leathery wings. (d) Balaenoptera (Whale) is a large, light blue, streamlined animal with a small dorsal fin. Description: The diagram represents four representative mammals from different groups, illustrating key mammalian diversity rather than a single anatomical structure. It should show (a) Ornithorhynchus anatinus (platypus), a monotreme mammal with a duck-like bill, webbed feet, dense fur, and a broad flattened tail; (b) Macropus (kangaroo), a marsupial mammal characterized by powerful hind limbs, a long balancing tail, and a ventral pouch for carrying the young; (c) Pteropus (flying fox), a chiropteran mammal with forelimbs modified into wings formed by a skin membrane stretched between elongated fingers, adapted for flight; and (d) Balaenoptera (whale), a large aquatic placental mammal with a streamlined body, forelimbs modified into flippers, reduced external hind limbs, and a tail fluke for propulsion. The scientific point of the figure is to compare major mammalian adaptations across monotremes, marsupials, bats, and whales, all of which are mammals despite their very different body forms. Labels: Ornithorhynchus (Platypus), Macropus (Kangaroo), Pteropus (Flying Fox), Balaenoptera (Whale). NEET relevance: Identification of mammalian groups: monotreme, marsupial, bat, and whale; Examples of mammals showing adaptive radiation and convergent evolution; Distinguishing mammalian features despite diverse body forms. Board relevance: Draw and label the four mammals shown in NCERT Figure 4.24; Name the group each mammal belongs to; State one adaptive feature of each mammal. Exam-critical labels: Ornithorhynchus (Platypus), Macropus (Kangaroo), Pteropus (Flying Fox), Balaenoptera (Whale). Common mistakes: Calling the platypus a bird or reptile because it lays eggs (correct: It is a mammal, specifically a monotreme); Calling the kangaroo a rodent or ordinary deer-like mammal (correct: It is a marsupial mammal of the genus Macropus); Calling the flying fox a bird because it flies (correct: It is a bat, a mammal with membranous wings).

kebo104-d015.content: Diagram: germinal layers in diploblastic and triploblastic body plans (diagram). Caption: Figure 4.2: Diagrams showing germinal layers. (a) Diploblastic: shows two layers, Ectoderm (outer) and Endoderm (inner), with Mesoglea in between. (b) Triploblastic: shows three layers, Ectoderm (outer), Mesoderm (middle), and Endoderm (inner). Description: This diagram represents the germ layers in diploblastic and triploblastic animals in transverse section. In the diploblastic condition, the body wall is formed by two true cellular layers: an outer ectoderm and an inner endoderm, with a non-cellular or loosely cellular gelatinous layer called mesoglea lying between them; this mesoglea is not a true germ layer. In the triploblastic condition, a third true germ layer, the mesoderm, is present between ectoderm and endoderm, making the body wall three-layered. The ectoderm forms the outer protective and sensory covering, the endoderm lines the gut cavity and gives rise to the gastrodermis, and the mesoderm contributes to muscles, connective tissues, and many internal organs in triploblastic animals. The diagram is meant to contrast the two-layered diploblastic body plan with the three-layered triploblastic body plan, emphasizing that mesoglea is present only in diploblastic forms and mesoderm only in triploblastic forms. Labels: Ectoderm, Endoderm, Mesoglea, Mesoderm, (a) Diploblastic, (b) Triploblastic. NEET relevance: Difference between diploblastic and triploblastic animals; Identification of germ layers from a diagram; Recognition that mesoglea is not a true germ layer. Board relevance: Draw and label the germinal layers in diploblastic and triploblastic body plans; Differentiate diploblastic and triploblastic organization; Explain the role of mesoglea versus mesoderm. Exam-critical labels: Ectoderm, Endoderm, Mesoglea, Mesoderm, Diploblastic, Triploblastic. Common mistakes: Calling mesoglea a germ layer (correct: Mesoglea is not a true germ layer; it is a gelatinous layer between ectoderm and endoderm in diploblastic animals.); Placing mesoderm in the diploblastic panel (correct: Mesoderm belongs only to the triploblastic panel.); Swapping ectoderm and endoderm positions (correct: Ectoderm is outermost and endoderm is innermost.).

kebo104-d016.content: Diagram: A schematic showing chordate characteristics/labels: nerve cord, notochord, gill slits, and post-anal part (diagram). Caption: ``` Description: This diagram represents the four fundamental diagnostic characters of phylum Chordata, which are present at least at some stage of development in all chordates. The dorsal, hollow nerve cord lies above the notochord and develops into the central nervous system; the notochord is a flexible, rod-like axial support located just beneath the nerve cord and provides structural support in the embryo and in some adult chordates; pharyngeal gill slits are paired openings in the pharyngeal region that communicate with the exterior and are involved in filter feeding and/or respiration depending on the chordate group; and the post-anal tail is an extension of the body beyond the anus that contains musculature and often aids in locomotion. In a correct schematic, these features are shown in a linear body plan with the nerve cord dorsal-most, the notochord immediately ventral to it, the pharyngeal gill slits in the anterior pharyngeal region, and the post-anal tail extending posteriorly beyond the anus. Labels: Dorsal hollow nerve cord, Notochord, Pharyngeal gill slits, Post-anal tail. NEET relevance: Identify the four diagnostic characters of Chordata.; Differentiate chordates from non-chordates based on dorsal hollow nerve cord, notochord, pharyngeal gill slits, and post-anal tail.; Recognize that these characters may be present only in embryonic stages in some chordates.. Board relevance: Draw and label the four chordate characteristics.; Explain the significance of notochord and dorsal hollow nerve cord.; State the defining features of phylum Chordata.. Exam-critical labels: Dorsal hollow nerve cord, Notochord, Pharyngeal gill slits, Post-anal tail. Common mistakes: Gill slits as 'gills' or 'branchial arches' (correct: Pharyngeal gill slits); Post-anal part as 'tail' without specifying its position beyond the anus (correct: Post-anal tail); Nerve cord as a ventral nerve cord (correct: Dorsal hollow nerve cord).

kebo104-d017.content: Diagram: bilateral symmetry in a crab (diagram). Caption: Figure 4.1 (b) Bilateral symmetry: A diagram of a crab showing a single vertical plane of symmetry dividing it into two identical halves. Description: The diagram should represent a crab as an example of bilateral symmetry in animals: a single median vertical plane passes through the longitudinal axis of the body and divides the organism into two mirror-image halves, a left and a right side. In a crab, the cephalothorax (fused head and thorax) forms the central body region, with paired appendages arranged symmetrically on either side. The two chelipeds (claws) are positioned laterally at the front, and the walking legs extend from both sides in corresponding pairs. The key biological point is not that every tiny detail is perfectly identical, but that the overall body plan is symmetrical about one plane, so the left and right halves correspond in form and arrangement. This symmetry is important for directional movement and coordinated locomotion in the animal. Labels: Median plane of symmetry, Cephalothorax, Chelipeds, Walking legs, Left half, Right half. NEET relevance: Identify the type of symmetry shown in crab.; State the definition of bilateral symmetry.; Explain why crab is an example of bilateral symmetry.. Board relevance: Draw and label a crab showing bilateral symmetry.; Mark the plane of symmetry in the crab.; Write one example of an animal with bilateral symmetry.. Exam-critical labels: Median plane of symmetry, Cephalothorax, Chelipeds, Walking legs. Common mistakes: Radial symmetry (correct: Bilateral symmetry); Body axis as a horizontal line (correct: Median vertical plane); Head and thorax as separate regions (correct: Cephalothorax as a fused region).

kebo104-d018.content: Diagram: Ascidia (diagram). Caption: Illustration of an Ascidia, a marine chordate, showing its characteristic sac-like body and internal structures. Description: Ascidia is a sessile marine urochordate (tunicate) with a sac-like body enclosed by a tough tunic or test made of tunicin. The adult body is typically attached to a substratum by a basal region and bears two siphons at the free end: an oral/incurrent siphon through which water enters, and an atrial/excurrent siphon through which water leaves. Water passes from the oral siphon into the branchial sac (pharynx), which is perforated by numerous gill slits (stigmata) and lined by ciliated epithelium for filter feeding. Mucus secreted by the endostyle traps food particles, which are conveyed to the oesophagus, stomach, intestine, and anus opening into the atrial cavity. The atrial cavity surrounds the pharynx and receives water from the gill slits before expulsion through the atrial siphon. In the adult, the notochord and dorsal nerve cord are absent, while the larval stage shows typical chordate features. The diagram should represent the external sac-like tunicate body, the two siphons, the basal attachment, and, if internal anatomy is shown, the pharyngeal basket with gill slits, endostyle, digestive tract, and atrial cavity. Labels: tunic (test), oral/incurrent siphon, atrial/excurrent siphon, pharynx (branchial sac), gill slits (stigmata), endostyle, atrial cavity, oesophagus, stomach, intestine, anus, attachment base / holdfast. NEET relevance: Identify Ascidia as a urochordate/tunicate and a marine chordate; Adult chordate characters absent or reduced in Ascidia; Presence of pharyngeal gill slits and endostyle. Board relevance: Draw and label Ascidia; Explain why Ascidia is placed in Urochordata/Tunicata; Label oral siphon, atrial siphon, tunic, pharynx, and gill slits. Exam-critical labels: tunic (test), oral/incurrent siphon, atrial/excurrent siphon, pharynx (branchial sac), gill slits (stigmata), endostyle, attachment base / holdfast. Common mistakes: Gill slits absent (correct: Gill slits (stigmata) are present in the pharynx of adult Ascidia); Notochord present in adult (correct: Notochord is absent in adult Ascidia; it is present only in the larval tail); Single opening at the top (correct: Two siphons are present: oral/incurrent and atrial/excurrent).

kebo104-d019.content: Diagram: Examples of Mollusca: a snail shell and an octopus (diagram). Caption: Figure 4.13: Examples of Mollusca. (a) Pila, a large, conical snail shell with a smooth, brownish surface. (b) Octopus, a marine mollusk with a soft body, large eyes, and several long tentacles. Description: This diagram represents two classic examples of phylum Mollusca: (a) the shell of Pila, a freshwater gastropod, and (b) an octopus, a cephalopod mollusc. In Pila, the body is protected by a single, univalve, spirally coiled shell secreted by the mantle; the shell is typically conical and brownish, with a pointed apex at the top and a large aperture at the base through which the soft body can extend. In octopus, the body is soft and unsegmented, with a distinct mantle enclosing the visceral mass, a prominent head with large eyes, and eight muscular arms radiating from the head region. The arms bear suckers on the inner surface and are used for locomotion, prey capture, and manipulation. The figure is meant to illustrate the diversity of molluscan body forms: a hard external shell in gastropods versus a shell-reduced, highly mobile cephalopod body plan. Labels: Pila (snail shell), Octopus, Apex, Aperture, Mantle, Arms, Suckers, Eye. NEET relevance: Identify examples of phylum Mollusca; Differentiate gastropods and cephalopods; Recognize that Pila is a gastropod with a coiled shell and octopus is a cephalopod with eight arms and suckers. Board relevance: Draw and label examples of Mollusca: Pila and Octopus; Write two distinguishing features of Pila and octopus; Explain why octopus is placed in Mollusca despite lacking an external shell. Exam-critical labels: Pila, Octopus, Apex, Aperture, Mantle, Arms, Suckers, Eye. Common mistakes: Calling the snail shell a 'snail' or 'gastropod body' (correct: It is specifically the shell of Pila, a gastropod mollusc); Labeling the octopus arms as 'tentacles' only (correct: Octopus has eight arms; in strict zoological usage, octopuses have arms rather than tentacles); Placing suckers on the outer surface of the octopus arms (correct: Suckers are on the inner/ventral surface of the arms).

kebo104-d020.content: Diagram: Examples of Platyhelminthes: a tapeworm and a liver fluke (diagram). Caption: Figure 4.9: Illustrations of two examples of Platyhelminthes. (a) shows a long, segmented, ribbon-like tapeworm. (b) shows a flattened, leaf-like liver fluke with a prominent sucker at its anterior end. Description: This diagram represents two classic examples of phylum Platyhelminthes: a tapeworm and a liver fluke. The tapeworm is a dorsoventrally flattened, ribbon-like, endoparasitic flatworm with a body divided into many repeated segments called proglottids; the anterior end bears a scolex, which is the attachment organ and typically carries suckers and sometimes hooks for anchorage in the host intestine. The liver fluke is a leaf-shaped, dorsoventrally flattened parasitic flatworm with an anterior oral sucker and a ventral sucker (acetabulum) used for attachment and feeding; its body is unsegmented and adapted for parasitism in the bile ducts or liver of the host. Both organisms lack a true body cavity and show bilateral symmetry, but the tapeworm is elongated and segmented whereas the liver fluke is broad, leaf-like, and unsegmented. Labels: Tapeworm (Taenia), Scolex, Proglottids, Liver fluke (Fasciola), Oral sucker, Ventral sucker (acetabulum). NEET relevance: Identify the examples of Platyhelminthes shown in the figure.; Differentiate cestodes (tapeworms) from trematodes (liver flukes).; Recognize the scolex and proglottids in tapeworm and the suckers in liver fluke.. Board relevance: Draw and label a tapeworm and a liver fluke.; Write distinguishing features of cestodes and trematodes.; Explain parasitic adaptations such as suckers, hooks, segmentation in tapeworm, and leaf-like body in liver fluke.. Exam-critical labels: Tapeworm, Scolex, Proglottids, Liver fluke, Oral sucker, Ventral sucker (acetabulum). Common mistakes: Calling the tapeworm a roundworm or simply a 'worm' without segmentation (correct: It is a cestode (tapeworm) with a segmented body made of proglottids); Labeling the anterior attachment organ of the tapeworm as a mouth (correct: It is the scolex, the attachment organ); Treating the liver fluke as segmented like the tapeworm (correct: The liver fluke is unsegmented and leaf-shaped).

kebo104-d021.content: Diagram: an echinoderm with a small central disc and five long slender arms, corresponding to a brittle star (diagram). Caption: The image shows two examples of echinoderms. (a) is a starfish, specifically Asterias, with five arms radiating from a central disk. (b) is a brittle star, specifically Ophiura, which has a central disk and five long, thin, whip-like arms. Description: The diagram should represent a brittle star (class Ophiuroidea), an echinoderm characterized by a small, distinct central disc and five long, slender, highly flexible arms arising sharply from the disc. Unlike starfish, the arms are clearly demarcated from the central disc and are used mainly for locomotion by rowing or snake-like movements rather than by tube feet. The body shows pentaradial symmetry in the adult stage, with the mouth located on the oral (ventral) surface of the central disc and the anus absent or reduced in many brittle stars. The arms contain articulated ossicles and ambulacral structures, giving them a segmented appearance. Tube feet are present but typically lack suckers and are not the primary locomotory organs. The diagram should therefore depict a brittle star with a compact central disc and five radiating, whip-like arms, accurately reflecting the external morphology of Ophiura/Ophiothrix-type ophiuroids. Labels: Central disc, Arms, Mouth, Madreporite. NEET relevance: Identification of brittle star as an echinoderm of class Ophiuroidea; Difference between starfish and brittle star; Key echinoderm features: pentaradial symmetry, calcareous endoskeleton, water vascular system. Board relevance: Draw and label brittle star; Differentiate Asterias and Ophiura; Write characteristics of Phylum Echinodermata. Exam-critical labels: Central disc, Arms, Mouth, Ophiura / Brittle star, Class Ophiuroidea. Common mistakes: Starfish (Asterias) (correct: Brittle star (Ophiura/Ophiothrix-type ophiuroid)); Tentacles (correct: Arms); Segmented worm-like body (correct: Articulated ophiuroid arm with ossicles).

kebo105-s00.section_title: Introduction + STRUCTURAL ORGANISATION IN PLANTS AND ANIMALS + Chapter 5 + Chapter 6 + Chapter 7
kebo105-s00.content:
Morphology of  
Flowering Plants

Anatomy of Flowering  
Plants

Structural Organisation in  
Animals

The description of the diverse forms of life on earth was made only by observation – through naked eyes or later through magnifying lenses and microscopes. This description is mainly of gross structural features, both external and internal. In addition, observable and perceivable living phenomena were also recorded as part of this description. Before experimental biology or more specifically, physiology, was established as a part of biology, naturalists described only biology. Hence, biology remained as a natural history for a long time. The description, by itself, was amazing in terms of detail. While the initial reaction of a student could be boredom, one should keep in mind that the detailed description, was utilised in the later day reductionist biology where living processes drew more attention from scientists than the description of life forms and their structure. Hence, this description became meaningful and helpful in framing research questions in physiology or evolutionary biology. In the following chapters of this unit, the structural organisation of plants and animals, including the structural basis of physiological or behavioural phenomena, is described. For convenience, this description of morphological and anatomical features is presented separately for plants and animals.

KATHERINE ESAU was born in Ukraine in 1898. She studied agriculture in Russia and Germany and received her doctorate in 1931 in United States. She reported in her early publications that the curly top virus spreads through a plant via the food-conducting or phloem tissue. Dr Esau's *Plant Anatomy* published in 1954 took a dynamic, developmental approach designed to enhance one's understanding of plant structure and an enormous impact worldwide, literally bringing about a revival of the discipline. The *Anatomy of Seed Plants* by Katherine Esau was published in 1960. It was referred to as Webster's of plant biology – it is encyclopediac. In 1957 she was elected to the National Academy of Sciences, becoming the sixth woman to receive that honour. In addition to this prestigious award, she received the National Medal of Science from President George Bush in 1989.

When Katherine Esau died in the year 1997, Peter Raven, director of Anatomy and Morphology, Missouri Botanical Garden, remembered that she 'absolutely dominated' the field of plant biology even at the age of 99.

kebo105-s05.section_title: CHAPTER 5 + MORPHOLOGY OF FLOWERING PLANTS + 5.1 The Root + 5.2 The Stem + 5.3 The Leaf + 5.4 The Inflorescence + 5.5 The Flower + 5.6 The Fruit + 5.7 The Seed + 5.8 Semi-technical Description of a Typical Flowering Plant + 5.9 Description of Some Important Families
kebo105-s05.content:
The wide range in the structure of higher plants will never fail to fascinate us. Even though the angiosperms show such a large diversity in external structure or **morphology**, they are all characterised by presence of roots, stems, leaves, flowers and fruits.

In chapters 2 and 3, we talked about classification of plants based on morphological and other characteristics. For any successful attempt at classification and at understanding any higher plant (or for that matter any living organism) we need to know standard technical terms and standard definitions. We also need to know about the possible variations in different parts, found as adaptations of the plants to their environment, e.g., adaptions to various habitats, for protection, climbing, storage, etc.

If you pull out any weed you will see that all of them have roots, stems and leaves. They may be bearing flowers and fruits. The underground part of the flowering plant is the root system while the portion above the ground forms the shoot system (Figure 5.1).

kebo105-s16.content:
In majority of the dicotyledonous plants, the direct elongation of the radicle leads to the formation of **primary root** which grows inside the soil. It bears lateral roots of several orders that are referred to as **secondary, tertiary**, etc. **roots**. The primary roots and its branches constitute the

The diagram illustrates the structure of a flowering plant, divided into the shoot system and the root system. The shoot system, above ground, consists of a central stem with nodes and internodes. At the nodes, leaves, buds, and branches (bearing flowers and fruits) emerge. The root system, below ground, consists of a primary root that branches into secondary roots.

**Figure 5.1** Parts of a flowering plant

**tap root system**, as seen in the mustard plant (Figure 5.2a). In monocotyledonous plants, the primary root is short lived and is replaced by a large number of roots. These roots originate from the base of the stem and constitute the **fibrous root system**, as seen in the wheat plant (Figure 5.2b). In some plants, like grass, *Monstera* and the banyan tree, roots arise from parts of the plant other than the radicle and are called **adventitious roots** (Figure 5.2c). The main functions of the root system are absorption of water and minerals from the soil, providing a proper anchorage to the plant parts, storing reserve food material and synthesis of plant growth regulators.

The image displays three distinct root systems: (a) Tap root system, characterized by a single, thick, vertical main root with several lateral roots branching out; (b) Fibrous root system, showing a dense, tangled mass of many thin roots; (c) Adventitious roots, which are roots that grow from non-root tissues, such as the stem or leaves, as seen in a plant with a creeping stem.

**Figure 5.2** Different types of roots : (a) Tap (b) Fibrous (c) Adventitious

kebo105-s17.content:
The root is covered at the apex by a thimble-like structure called the **root cap** (Figure 5.3). It protects the tender apex of the root as it makes its way through the soil. A few millimetres above the root cap is the **region of meristematic activity**. The cells of this region are very small, thin-walled and with dense protoplasm. They divide repeatedly. The cells proximal to this region undergo rapid elongation and enlargement and are responsible for the growth of the root in length. This region is called the **region of elongation**. The cells of the elongation zone gradually differentiate and mature. Hence, this zone, proximal to region of elongation, is called the **region of maturation**. From this region some of the epidermal cells form very fine and delicate, thread-like structures called **root hairs**. These root hairs absorb water and minerals from the soil.

**Figure 5.3** The regions of the root-tip

kebo105-s18.content:
What are the features that distinguish a stem from a root? The stem is the ascending part of the axis bearing branches, leaves, flowers and fruits. It develops from the plumule of the embryo of a germinating seed. The stem bears **nodes** and **internodes**. The region of the stem where leaves are born are called nodes while internodes are the portions between two nodes. The stem bears buds, which may be terminal or axillary. Stem is generally green when young and later often become woody and dark brown.

The main function of the stem is spreading out branches bearing leaves, flowers and fruits. It conducts water, minerals and photosynthates. Some stems perform the function of storage of food, support, protection and of vegetative propagation.

kebo105-s19.content:
The leaf is a lateral, generally flattened structure borne on the stem. It develops at the node and bears a bud in its axil. The **axillary bud** later develops into a branch. Leaves originate from shoot apical meristems and are arranged in an acropetal order. They are the most important vegetative organs for photosynthesis.

Figure 5.4 consists of three parts: (a) shows a leaf with its parts labeled: Lamina, Stipule, Petiole, Leaf base, and Axillary bud. (b) shows a leaf with reticulate venation, characterized by a network of veins. (c) shows a leaf with parallel venation, characterized by veins running parallel to each other.

**Figure 5.4** Structure of a leaf :  
 (a) Parts of a leaf  
 (b) Reticulate venation  
 (c) Parallel venation

Figure 5.5 shows two types of compound leaves: (a) a pinnately compound leaf (Neem) and (b) a palmately compound leaf (Silk Cotton). The Rachis is labeled in the pinnately compound leaf.

**Figure 5.5** Compound leaves :  
 (a) pinnately compound leaf  
 (b) palmately compound leaf

A typical leaf consists of three main parts: leaf base, petiole and lamina (Figure 5.4 a). The leaf is attached to the stem by the **leaf base** and may bear two lateral small leaf like structures called stipules. In monocotyledons, the leaf base expands into a sheath covering the stem partially or wholly. In some leguminous plants the leafbase may become swollen, which is called the **pulvinus**. The **petiole** help hold the blade to light. Long thin flexible petioles allow leaf blades to flutter in wind, thereby cooling the leaf and bringing fresh air to leaf surface. The **lamina** or the **leaf blade** is the green expanded part of the leaf with veins and veinlets. There is, usually, a middle prominent vein, which is known as the midrib. Veins provide rigidity to the leaf blade and act as channels of transport for water, minerals and food materials. The shape, margin, apex, surface and extent of incision of lamina varies in different leaves.

kebo105-s20.content: The arrangement of veins and the veinlets in the lamina of leaf is termed as **venation**. When the veinlets form a network, the venation is termed as **reticulate** (Figure 5.4 b). When the veins run parallel to each other within a lamina, the venation is termed as **parallel** (Figure 5.4 c). Leaves of dicotyledonous plants generally possess reticulate venation, while parallel venation is the characteristic of most monocotyledons.

kebo105-s21.content:
A leaf is said to be **simple**, when its lamina is entire or when incised, the incisions do not touch the midrib. When the incisions of the lamina reach up to the midrib breaking it into a number of leaflets, the leaf is called **compound**. A bud is present in the axil of petiole in both simple and compound leaves, but not in the axil of leaflets of the compound leaf.

The compound leaves may be of two types (Figure 5.5). In a **pinnately compound leaf** a

number of leaflets are present on a common axis, the **rachis**, which represents the midrib of the leaf as in neem.

In **palmately compound leaves**, the leaflets are attached at a common point, i.e., at the tip of petiole, as in silk cotton.

kebo105-s22.content:
Phyllotaxy is the pattern of arrangement of leaves on the stem or branch. This is usually of three types – alternate, opposite and whorled (Figure 5.6). In **alternate** type of phyllotaxy, a single leaf arises at each node in alternate manner, as in china rose, mustard and sun flower plants. In **opposite** type, a pair of leaves arise at each node and lie opposite to each other as in *Calotropis* and guava plants. If more than two leaves arise at a node and form a whorl, it is called **whorled**, as in *Alstonia*.

Figure 5.6 Different types of phyllotaxy :  
(a) Alternate (b) Opposite  
(c) Whorled

kebo105-s23.content:
A flower is a modified shoot wherein the shoot apical meristem changes to floral meristem. Internodes do not elongate and the axis gets condensed. The apex produces different kinds of floral appendages laterally at successive nodes instead of leaves. When a shoot tip transforms into a flower, it is always solitary. The arrangement of flowers on the floral axis is termed as **inflorescence**. Depending on whether the apex gets developed into a flower or continues to grow, two major types of inflorescences are defined – racemose and cymose. In **racemose** type of inflorescences the main axis continues to grow, the flowers are borne laterally in an acropetal succession (Figure 5.7).

In **cymose** type of inflorescence the main axis terminates in a flower, hence is limited in growth. The flowers are borne in a basipetal order (Figure 5.8).

Figure 5.7 Racemose inflorescence

**Figure 5.8** Cymose inflorescence

kebo105-s24.content:
The flower is the reproductive unit in the angiosperms. It is meant for sexual reproduction. A typical flower has four different kinds of whorls arranged successively on the swollen end of the stalk or pedicel, called **thalamus or receptacle**. These are calyx, corolla, androecium and gynoecium. Calyx and corolla are accessory organs, while androecium and gynoecium are reproductive organs. In some flowers like lily, the calyx and corolla are not distinct and are termed as perianth. When a flower has both androecium and gynoecium, it is **bisexual**. A flower having either only stamens or only carpels is **unisexual**.

In symmetry, the flower may be **actinomorphic** (radial symmetry) or **zygomorphic** (bilateral symmetry). When a flower can be divided into two equal radial halves in any radial plane passing through the centre, it is said to be **actinomorphic**, e.g., mustard, *datura*, chilli. When it can be divided into two similar halves only in one particular vertical plane, it is **zygomorphic**, e.g., pea, gulmohur, bean, *Cassia*. A flower is **asymmetric** (irregular) if it cannot be divided into two similar halves by any vertical plane passing through the centre, as in canna.

A flower may be **trimerous**, **tetramerous** or **pentamerous** when the floral appendages are in multiple of 3, 4 or 5, respectively. Flowers with bracts-reduced leaf found at the base of the pedicel-are called **bracteate** and those without bracts, **ebracteate**.

**Figure 5.9** Position of floral parts on thalamus : (a) Hypogynous (b) and (c) Perigynous (d) Epigynous

Based on the position of calyx, corolla and androecium in respect of the ovary on thalamus, the flowers are described as hypogynous, perigynous and epigynous (Figure 5.9). In the **hypogynous** flower the gynoecium occupies the highest position while the other parts are situated below it. The ovary in such flowers is said to be **superior**, e.g., mustard, china rose and brinjal. If gynoecium is situated in the centre and other parts of the flower are located on the rim of the thalamus almost at the same level, it is called **perigynous**. The ovary here is said to be **half inferior**, e.g., plum, rose, peach. In **epigynous flowers**, the margin of thalamus grows upward enclosing the ovary completely and getting fused with it, the other parts of flower arise above the ovary. Hence, the ovary is said to be **inferior** as in flowers of guava and cucumber, and the ray florets of sunflower.

kebo105-s25.section_title: 5.5.1 Parts of a Flower + 5.5.1.1 Calyx + 5.5.1.2 Corolla
kebo105-s25.content:
Each flower normally has four floral whorls, viz., calyx, corolla, androecium and gynoecium (Figure 5.10).

The calyx is the outermost whorl of the flower and the members are called sepals. Generally, sepals are green, leaf like and protect the flower in the bud stage. The calyx may be **gamosepalous** (sepals united) or **polysepalous** (sepals free).

Corolla is composed of petals. Petals are usually brightly coloured to attract insects for pollination. Like calyx, corolla may also be **gamopetalous** (petals united) or **polypetalous** (petals free). The shape and colour of corolla vary greatly in plants. Corolla may be tubular, bell-shaped, funnel-shaped or wheel-shaped.

**Aestivation:** The mode of arrangement of sepals or petals in floral bud with respect to the other members of the same whorl is known as aestivation. The main types of aestivation are valvate, twisted, imbricate

The diagram illustrates the vertical arrangement of floral parts. At the top is 'Androecium', followed by 'Gynoecium', then 'Corolla', and 'Calyx'. Below 'Calyx' is 'Pedicel'. A horizontal line extends from the 'Calyx' label to the left, with a diagonal line branching downwards from it to the 'Pedicel' label, indicating the relative positions of these parts in a flower.

**Figure 5.10** Parts of a flower

**Figure 5.11** Types of aestivation in corolla : (a) Valvate (b) Twisted (c) Imbricate (d) Vexillary

and vexillary (Figure 5.11). When sepals or petals in a whorl just touch one another at the margin, without overlapping, as in *Calotropis*, it is said to be **valvate**. If one margin of the appendage overlaps that of the next one and so on as in china rose, lady's finger and cotton, it is called **twisted**. If the margins of sepals or petals overlap one another but not in any particular direction as in *Cassia* and gulmohur, the aestivation is called **imbricate**. In pea and bean flowers, there are five petals, the largest (standard) overlaps the two lateral petals (wings) which in turn overlap the two smallest anterior petals (keel); this type of aestivation is known as **vexillary** or papilionaceous.

kebo105-s28.content:
Androecium is composed of stamens. Each stamen which represents the male reproductive organ consists of a stalk or a filament and an anther. Each anther is usually bilobed and each lobe has two chambers, the pollen-sacs. The pollen grains are produced in pollen-sacs. A sterile stamen is called **staminode**.

Stamens of flower may be united with other members such as petals or among themselves. When stamens are attached to the petals, they are **epipetalous** as in brinjal, or **epiphyllous** when attached to the perianth as in the flowers of lily. The stamens in a flower may either remain free (polyandrous) or may be united in varying degrees. The stamens may be united into one bunch or one bundle (**monoadelphous**) as in china rose, or two bundles (**diadelphous**) as in pea, or into more than two bundles (**polyadelphous**) as in citrus. There may be a variation in the length of filaments within a flower, as in *Salvia* and mustard.

kebo105-s29.content:
Gynoecium is the female reproductive part of the flower and is made up of one or more carpels. A carpel consists of three parts namely stigma, style and ovary. **Ovary** is the enlarged basal part, on which lies the elongated tube, the style. The style connects the ovary to the stigma. The **stigma** is usually at the tip of the **style** and is the receptive surface for pollen grains. Each ovary bears one or more ovules attached to a flattened, cushion-like **placenta**. When more than one carpel is present, they may be free (as in lotus and rose) and are called **apocarpous**. They are termed **syncarpous** when carpels are fused, as in mustard and tomato. After fertilisation, the ovules develop into seeds and the ovary matures into a fruit.

**Placentation:** The arrangement of ovules within the ovary is known as placentation. The placentation are of different types namely, marginal, axile, parietal, basal, central and free central (Figure 5.12). In **marginal** placentation the placenta forms a ridge along the ventral suture of the ovary and the ovules are borne on this ridge forming two rows, as in pea. When the placenta is axial and the ovules are attached to it in a multilocular ovary, the placentaion is said to be **axile**, as in china rose, tomato and lemon. In **parietal** placentation, the ovules develop on the inner wall of the ovary or on peripheral part. Ovary is one-chambered but it becomes two-chambered due to the formation of the false septum, e.g., mustard and *Argemone*. When the ovules are borne on central axis and septa are absent, as in *Dianthus* and *Primrose* the placentation is called **free central**. In **basal** placentation, the placenta develops at the base of ovary and a single ovule is attached to it, as in sunflower, marigold.

kebo105-s30.content:
The fruit is a characteristic feature of the flowering plants. It is a mature or ripened ovary, developed after fertilisation. If a fruit is formed without fertilisation of the ovary, it is called a **parthenocarpic** fruit.

Generally, the fruit consists of a wall or **pericarp** and seeds. The pericarp may be dry or fleshy. When pericarp is thick and fleshy, it is differentiated into the outer **epicarp**, the middle **mesocarp** and the inner **endocarp**.

In mango and coconut, the fruit is known as a drupe (Figure 5.13). They develop from monocarpellary superior ovaries and are one seeded. In mango the pericarp is well differentiated into an

**Figure 5.12** Types of placentation :  
 (a) Marginal  
 (b) Axile  
 (c) Parietal  
 (d) Free central  
 (e) Basal

**Figure 5.13** Parts of a fruit : (a) Mango (b) Coconut

outer thin epicarp, a middle fleshy edible mesocarp and an inner stony hard endocarp. In coconut which is also a drupe, the mesocarp is fibrous.

kebo105-s31.section_title: 5.7 THE SEED + 5.7.1 Structure of a Dicotyledonous Seed
kebo105-s31.content:
The ovules after fertilisation, develop into seeds. A seed is made up of a seed coat and an embryo. The embryo is made up of a radicle, an embryonal axis and one (as in wheat, maize) or two cotyledons (as in gram and pea).

The outermost covering of a seed is the seed coat. The seed coat has two layers, the outer **testa** and the inner **tegmen**. The **hilum** is a scar on the seed coat through which the developing seeds were attached to the fruit.

Above the hilum is a small pore called the **micropyle**. Within the seed coat is the embryo, consisting of an embryonal axis and two cotyledons. The cotyledons are often fleshy and full of reserve food materials. At the two ends of the embryonal axis are present the radicle and the plumule (Figure 5.14). In some seeds such as castor the **endosperm** formed as a result of double fertilisation, is a food storing tissue and called endospermic seeds. In plants such as bean, gram and pea, the endosperm is not present in mature seeds and such seeds are called non-endospermous.

**Figure 5.14** Structure of dicotyledonous seed

kebo105-s33.content:
Generally, monocotyledonous seeds are endospermic but some as in orchids are non-endospermic. In the seeds of cereals such as maize the

**Figure 5.15** Structure of a monocotyledonous seed

seed coat is membranous and generally fused with the fruit wall. The endosperm is bulky and stores food. The outer covering of endosperm separates the embryo by a proteinous layer called **aleurone layer**. The embryo is small and situated in a groove at one end of the endosperm. It consists of one large and shield shaped cotyledon known as **scutellum** and a short axis with a **plumule** and a **radicle**. The plumule and radicle are enclosed in sheaths which are called **coleoptile** and **coleorhiza** respectively (Figure 5.15).

kebo105-s34.section_title: 5.8 SEMI-TECHNICAL DESCRIPTION OF A TYPICAL FLOWERING PLANT
kebo105-s34.content:
Various morphological features are used to describe a flowering plant. The description has to be brief, in a simple and scientific language and presented in a proper sequence. The plant is described beginning with its habit, vegetative characters – roots, stem and leaves and then floral characters inflorescence and flower parts. After describing various parts of plant, a floral diagram and a floral formula are presented. The floral formula is represented by some symbols. In the floral formula, **Br** stands for bracteate **K** stands for calyx, **C** for corolla, **P** for perianth, **A** for androecium and **G** for Gynoecium, **G** for superior ovary and **G** for inferior ovary,  $\sigma$  for male,  $\rho$  for female,  $\sigma\rho$  for bisexual plants,  $\oplus$  for actinomorphic

Figure 5.16: Floral diagram with floral formula. The diagram shows a cross-section of a flower with five sepals (K), five petals (C), ten stamens (A), and a superior ovary (G) with two locules. The floral formula is: \oplus \sigma\rho K\_{2+2} C\_4 A\_{2+4} \underline{G}\_{(2)}.

**Figure 5.16** Floral diagram with floral formula

and % for zygomorphic nature of flower. Fusion is indicated by enclosing the figure within bracket and adhesion by a line drawn above the symbols of the floral parts. A floral diagram provides information about the number of parts of a flower, their arrangement and the relation they have with one another (Figure 5.16). The position of the mother axis with respect to the flower is represented by a dot on the top of the floral diagram. Calyx, corolla, androecium and gynoecium are drawn in successive whorls, calyx being the outermost and the gynoecium being in the centre. Floral formula also shows cohesion and adhesion within parts of whorls and between whorls. The floral diagram and floral formula in Figure 5.16 represents the mustard plant (Family: Brassicaceae).

kebo105-s35.content:
It is a large family, commonly called as the 'potato family'. It is widely distributed in tropics, subtropics and even temperate zones (Figure 5.17).

Plants mostly herbs, shrubs and rarely small trees

**Stem:** herbaceous rarely woody, aerial; erect, cylindrical, branched, solid or hollow, hairy or glabrous, underground stem in potato (*Solanum tuberosum*)

**Leaves:** alternate, simple, rarely pinnately compound, exstipulate; venation reticulate

**Figure 5.17** *Solanum nigrum* (makoi) plant : (a) Flowering twig (b) Flower (c) L.S. of flower (d) Stamens (e) Carpel (f) Floral diagram

kebo105-s37.content:
**Inflorescence :** Solitary, axillary or cymose as in *Solanum*

**Flower:** bisexual, actinomorphic

**Calyx:** sepals five, united, persistent, valvate aestivation

**Corolla:** petals five, united; valvate aestivation

**Androecium:** stamens five, epipetalous

**Gynoecium:** bicarpellary obligately placed, syncarpous; ovary superior, bilocular, placenta swollen with many ovules, axile

**Fruits:** berry or capsule

**Seeds:** many, endospermous

**Floral Formula:**  $\oplus \overset{\curvearrowright}{\text{Q}} K_{(5)} \overbrace{C_{(5)}} A_5 \underline{G}_{(2)}$

kebo105-s38.content:
Many plants belonging to this family are source of food (tomato, brinjal, potato), spice (chilli); medicine (belladonna, *ashwagandha*); fumigatory (tobacco); ornamentals (petunia).

Flowering plants exhibit enormous variation in shape, size, structure, mode of nutrition, life span, habit and habitat. They have well developed root and shoot systems. Root system is either tap root or fibrous. Generally, dicotyledonous plants have tap roots while monocotyledonous plants have fibrous roots. The roots in some plants get modified for storage of food, mechanical support and respiration. The shoot system is differentiated into stem, leaves, flowers and fruits. The morphological features of stems like the presence of nodes and internodes, multicellular hair and positively phototropic nature help to differentiate the stems from roots. Leaf is a lateral outgrowth of stem developed exogeneously at the node. These are green in colour to perform the function of photosynthesis. Leaves exhibit marked variations in their shape, size, margin, apex and extent of incisions of leaf blade (lamina).

The flower is a modified shoot, meant for sexual reproduction. The flowers are arranged in different types of inflorescences. They exhibit enormous variation in structure, symmetry, position of ovary in relation to other parts, arrangement of petals, sepals, ovules etc. After fertilisation, the ovary is modified into fruits and ovules into seeds. Seeds either may be monocotyledonous or dicotyledonous. They vary in shape, size and period of viability. The floral characteristics form the basis of classification

and identification of flowering plants. This can be illustrated through semi-technical descriptions of families. Hence, a flowering plant is described in a definite sequence by using scientific terms. The floral features are represented in the summarised form as floral diagrams and floral formula.

kebo105-d000.section_title: STRUCTURAL ORGANISATION IN PLANTS AND ANIMALS
kebo105-d000.content: Diagram: structure of a dicotyledonous seed (diagram). Caption: Figure 5.14: Structure of a dicotyledonous seed. The left diagram shows the external view of a seed with labels for the Seed coat, Hilum, and Micropyle. The right diagram shows a longitudinal section of the seed, revealing the internal structure: Cotyledon, Plumule, and Radicle. Description: A dicotyledonous seed is typically shown as a seed of a dicot plant such as bean or pea, with an outer protective seed coat and an internal embryo consisting of two large cotyledons. In external view, the seed coat forms the tough outer covering and bears the hilum, which is the scar marking the point of attachment of the seed to the funiculus of the fruit; just adjacent to it is the micropyle, a tiny pore through which the pollen tube originally entered the ovule and which later may permit water entry during germination. In longitudinal section, the embryo lies between the two cotyledons and includes the embryonal axis with the plumule at the upper end, which develops into the shoot, and the radicle at the lower end, which develops into the primary root. The cotyledons serve mainly as food-storage organs and nourish the embryo during germination. The diagram should therefore represent both the external morphology and the internal embryonic organization of a typical dicot seed, with the seed coat enclosing the embryo and cotyledons. Labels: Seed coat, Hilum, Micropyle, Cotyledon, Plumule, Radicle. NEET relevance: Identification of parts of a dicot seed in external view and longitudinal section; Difference between dicot and monocot seeds; Functions of hilum, micropyle, cotyledons, plumule, and radicle. Board relevance: Draw and label the structure of a dicotyledonous seed; Explain the function of seed coat, hilum, micropyle, cotyledons, plumule, and radicle; Differentiate external and internal features of a dicot seed. Exam-critical labels: Seed coat, Hilum, Micropyle, Cotyledon, Plumule, Radicle. Common mistakes: Calling the hilum the micropyle or vice versa (correct: Hilum is the scar of attachment; micropyle is the tiny pore near it); Labeling the cotyledons as seed coat (correct: Cotyledons are the two internal seed leaves; the seed coat is the outer covering); Placing plumule and radicle outside the cotyledons (correct: They are parts of the embryonal axis located between the cotyledons).

kebo105-d001.section_title: STRUCTURAL ORGANISATION IN PLANTS AND ANIMALS
kebo105-d001.content: Diagram: Different types of phyllotaxy in plants shown using China rose, Guava, and Alstonia (diagram). Caption: Figure 5.6: Three diagrams illustrating different types of phyllotaxy. (a) China rose: shows a single leaf at a node, with the next leaf at a lower node on the opposite side (alternate). (b) Guava: shows two leaves at a node, directly opposite to each other (opposite). (c) Alstonia: shows three or more leaves at a single node, arranged in a circle (whorled). Description: This diagram represents phyllotaxy, the arrangement of leaves on a stem or branch at the nodes. In China rose, the phyllotaxy is alternate: a single leaf arises at each node, and successive leaves appear at alternate sides of the stem, often with a spiral arrangement along the axis. In Guava, the phyllotaxy is opposite: two leaves arise at the same node, positioned directly opposite each other. In Alstonia, the phyllotaxy is whorled: three or more leaves arise from a single node and are arranged in a circle around the stem. The diagram is meant to compare these three leaf-arrangement patterns on a stem, emphasizing the number of leaves per node and their relative positions, which are important for plant identification and classification. Labels: Alternate phyllotaxy, Opposite phyllotaxy, Whorled phyllotaxy, China rose, Guava, Alstonia. NEET relevance: Identify the type of phyllotaxy shown by each plant example.; Match China rose with alternate, Guava with opposite, and Alstonia with whorled phyllotaxy.; Differentiate alternate, opposite, and whorled leaf arrangements based on number of leaves per node.. Board relevance: Draw and label the three types of phyllotaxy.; Write examples of alternate, opposite, and whorled phyllotaxy.; Explain the difference between the three arrangements in one or two lines.. Exam-critical labels: Alternate phyllotaxy, Opposite phyllotaxy, Whorled phyllotaxy, China rose, Guava, Alstonia, Node, Stem. Common mistakes: Calling China rose opposite phyllotaxy (correct: China rose shows alternate phyllotaxy); Calling Guava whorled phyllotaxy (correct: Guava shows opposite phyllotaxy); Calling Alstonia opposite phyllotaxy (correct: Alstonia shows whorled phyllotaxy).

kebo105-d002.section_title: STRUCTURAL ORGANISATION IN PLANTS AND ANIMALS
kebo105-d002.content: Diagram: cymose inflorescence of a flowering plant (diagram). Caption: Figure 5.8: Cymose inflorescence. The diagram shows a central axis with several flowers and buds. The flowers are arranged in a cymose pattern, where the terminal flower is the oldest and the buds are younger, developing from the axils of the leaves below the terminal flower. Description: Cymose inflorescence is a determinate inflorescence in which the main axis terminates in a flower, so further growth of that axis stops. The terminal flower is the oldest flower, and younger flowers are produced successively from lateral buds below it, usually in a basipetal and centrifugal sequence. In a typical cymose pattern, the central or terminal flower opens first, and the lateral branches below it may themselves end in flowers, producing a branched, often sympodial arrangement. The diagram should represent this determinate growth clearly: the apex is occupied by a flower rather than a continuing vegetative shoot, and the lateral floral branches arise from below the terminal flower. This arrangement contrasts with racemose inflorescence, where the main axis continues to grow and the youngest flowers are at the top. Labels: terminal flower, lateral flower buds, pedicel, inflorescence axis, lateral branches, bracts. NEET relevance: Definition of cymose inflorescence as a determinate inflorescence; Difference between cymose and racemose inflorescence; Identification of terminal flower as the oldest flower. Board relevance: Draw and label cymose inflorescence; Differentiate cymose and racemose inflorescence; Explain why cymose inflorescence is determinate. Exam-critical labels: terminal flower, lateral flower buds, inflorescence axis, lateral branches, bracts. Common mistakes: Main axis continues beyond the top flower (correct: The top flower is terminal and ends the main axis); Youngest flower at the top (correct: Oldest flower at the top/center in cymose inflorescence); Leaf-like structures are petals or sepals of the flower (correct: They may be bracts or bracteoles at the nodes).

kebo105-d003.section_title: STRUCTURAL ORGANISATION IN PLANTS AND ANIMALS
kebo105-d003.content: Diagram: floral diagram with floral formula of a flower (diagram). Caption: Figure 5.16: Floral diagram with floral formula. The diagram shows a cross-section of a flower with five sepals (K), five petals (C), ten stamens (A), and a superior ovary (G) with two locules. The floral formula is: \oplus \sigma\rho K\_{2+2} C\_4 A\_{2+4} \underline{G}\_{(2)}. Description: This diagram represents the floral diagram and floral formula of a zygomorphic, bisexual flower of the Fabaceae (papilionaceous type), classically exemplified by pea/bean-type flowers in NCERT. The flower is actinomorphic? No—the correct floral symmetry is zygomorphic, indicated by the symbol % or ⚥ depending on convention, and the flower is bisexual, with both androecium and gynoecium present. The calyx is gamosepalous with five sepals arranged as K2+2, meaning the sepals are in two whorls of two and two, typically showing bilabiate arrangement. The corolla consists of five petals arranged as C1+2+(2), i.e., one standard (vexillum), two wings, and two fused keel petals; in many textbook diagrams this is represented as a papilionaceous corolla. The androecium is diadelphous with ten stamens arranged as A(9)+1 or A2+4 depending on the textbook convention used in the figure; in NCERT Class 11 the floral formula shown is A2+4, indicating ten stamens in two groups. The gynoecium is monocarpellary with a superior ovary, shown as G1 or G(1) in many Fabaceae flowers, but the NCERT figure specifically labels G(2) in the provided formula context, which would indicate a bicarpellary syncarpous ovary; however, for the standard NCERT papilionaceous floral formula, the ovary is superior and monocarpellary. The diagram must therefore show concentric whorls from outside to inside: sepals, petals, stamens, and a central ovary, with the floral formula summarizing symmetry, sexuality, calyx, corolla, androecium, and gynoecium. The functional significance is protection by sepals, attraction by petals, pollen production by stamens, and seed formation by the ovary after fertilization. Labels: zygomorphic symmetry, bisexual flower, K2+2, C4, A2+4, G(2), floral diagram, floral formula. NEET relevance: Identify the family from the floral formula and floral diagram; Interpret symbols such as zygomorphic symmetry, bisexuality, fusion of carpels, and superior ovary; Differentiate papilionaceous corolla and diadelphous androecium. Board relevance: Draw and label the floral diagram of Fabaceae; Write the floral formula for a papilionaceous flower; Explain the significance of K, C, A, and G notation. Exam-critical labels: zygomorphic symmetry symbol, bisexual symbol, K2+2, C4 or papilionaceous corolla, A2+4 or diadelphous stamens, G(2) or superior ovary with fused carpels, floral diagram, floral formula. Common mistakes: Calling the flower actinomorphic (correct: It is zygomorphic); Calling the corolla five separate equal petals (correct: Papilionaceous corolla with differentiated petals); Calling the androecium polyandrous (correct: Diadelphous or grouped stamens).

kebo105-d004.section_title: STRUCTURAL ORGANISATION IN PLANTS AND ANIMALS
kebo105-d004.content: Diagram: root tip showing regions of the root and associated structures (diagram). Caption: A detailed diagram of a root tip. At the very bottom is the root cap, a thimble-shaped structure protecting the apex. Just above it is the region of meristematic activity, indicated by a bracket. Above this is the region of elongation, also indicated by a bracket. The top part of the root is the region of maturation, where numerous fine, thread-like root hairs extend outwards. Labels with arrows point to the root hair and the root cap. Brackets on the right side group the upper parts of the root into the 'Region of maturation', 'Region of elongation', and 'Region of meristematic activity'. Description: This diagram represents a longitudinal view of a young root tip, showing the root cap at the extreme apex and three successive zones above it: the region of meristematic activity, the region of elongation, and the region of maturation (differentiation). The root cap is a thimble-shaped protective structure covering the root apex and helps the root push through soil while protecting the apical meristem. Immediately above it lies the meristematic zone, composed of small, thin-walled, densely cytoplasmic cells that divide actively to produce new cells. Above this is the elongation zone, where the newly formed cells rapidly increase in length, causing the root to grow longitudinally. The uppermost zone is the maturation zone, where cells differentiate into specialized tissues such as epidermis, cortex, xylem, phloem, and root hairs. Root hairs are unicellular tubular outgrowths of epidermal cells in the maturation zone; they greatly increase surface area for absorption of water and mineral salts. The diagram should therefore show a tapering root apex with the root cap at the tip, the meristematic region just above it, the elongation region above that, and the maturation region at the top bearing root hairs. Labels: Root cap, Region of meristematic activity, Region of elongation, Region of maturation, Root hair. NEET relevance: Order and function of root tip zones; Identification of root cap, meristematic zone, elongation zone, and maturation zone; Location and function of root hairs. Board relevance: Draw and label the root tip showing regions of the root; Explain the function of root cap and root hairs; Differentiate between meristematic, elongation, and maturation zones. Exam-critical labels: Root cap, Region of meristematic activity, Region of elongation, Region of maturation, Root hair. Common mistakes: Root hairs shown in the elongation zone (correct: Root hairs are present in the region of maturation only); Region of meristematic activity placed above the elongation zone (correct: Meristematic activity is immediately above the root cap and below the elongation zone); Root cap labeled as a lateral structure or root hair cluster (correct: Root cap is the terminal protective cap at the very tip).

kebo105-d005.section_title: STRUCTURAL ORGANISATION IN PLANTS AND ANIMALS
kebo105-d005.content: Diagram: structure of a leaf and types of venation (diagram). Caption: Figure 5.4 consists of three parts: (a) shows a leaf with its parts labeled: Lamina, Stipule, Petiole, Leaf base, and Axillary bud. (b) shows a leaf with reticulate venation, characterized by a network of veins. (c) shows a leaf with parallel venation, characterized by veins running parallel to each other. Description: This diagram represents the external morphology of a typical leaf and two major patterns of venation. In part (a), the leaf is attached to the stem by the petiole, which connects the leaf blade to the stem at the leaf base. The leaf base is the lowermost part of the leaf where it joins the stem; in many dicot plants it may bear small stipules on either side. The lamina (leaf blade) is the broad, flat, green photosynthetic part of the leaf, containing the vascular network and stomata. The axillary bud is located in the axil, i.e., the angle between the leaf base/petiole and the stem, and can develop into a branch or flower. Part (b) shows reticulate venation, in which veins and veinlets branch repeatedly to form a net-like pattern, typical of most dicot leaves. Part (c) shows parallel venation, in which veins run more or less parallel from base to apex, typical of most monocot leaves. The diagram should clearly distinguish the structural parts of a leaf from the venation patterns, and the venation panels should show only the vein arrangement within the lamina, not additional leaf parts. Labels: Lamina, Petiole, Leaf base, Stipule, Axillary bud, Reticulate venation, Parallel venation. NEET relevance: Identify parts of a leaf and their positions; Differentiate reticulate and parallel venation; Relate reticulate venation to dicots and parallel venation to monocots. Board relevance: Draw and label the structure of a leaf; Draw and label reticulate venation and parallel venation; Differentiate lamina, petiole, leaf base, stipule, and axillary bud. Exam-critical labels: Lamina, Petiole, Leaf base, Stipule, Axillary bud, Reticulate venation, Parallel venation. Common mistakes: Calling the entire leaf blade a 'leaf' without labeling the lamina (correct: The broad blade is the lamina); Placing the axillary bud on the leaf blade (correct: The axillary bud is in the axil at the junction of leaf and stem); Labeling stipules as part of the petiole (correct: Stipules are separate appendages at the leaf base).

kebo105-d006.section_title: STRUCTURAL ORGANISATION IN PLANTS AND ANIMALS
kebo105-d006.content: Diagram: parts of a flower (diagram). Caption: The diagram illustrates the vertical arrangement of floral parts. At the top is 'Androecium', followed by 'Gynoecium', then 'Corolla', and 'Calyx'. Below 'Calyx' is 'Pedicel'. A horizontal line extends from the 'Calyx' label to the left, with a diagonal line branching downwards from it to the 'Pedicel' label, indicating the relative positions of these parts in a flower. Description: A complete flower diagram should represent the four concentric floral whorls arranged from outside to inside on the receptacle (thalamus): calyx, corolla, androecium, and gynoecium. The calyx is the outermost whorl made of sepals, usually green, and it protects the flower in the bud stage. Inside it lies the corolla, made of petals, typically colored and often involved in attracting pollinators. The androecium is the male reproductive whorl, composed of stamens; each stamen has a filament and anther, and the anther produces pollen grains. The gynoecium is the innermost female reproductive whorl, composed of one or more carpels/pistils; it includes stigma, style, and ovary, and the ovary contains ovules. The pedicel is the stalk that supports the flower and connects it to the stem. In a scientifically correct diagram, these parts should be shown in their true positional relationship: pedicel at the base, receptacle above it, and the floral whorls arranged successively outward to inward around the receptacle, with the gynoecium at the center. Labels: Pedicel, Calyx, Corolla, Androecium, Gynoecium. NEET relevance: Identify the floral whorls and their order from outside to inside.; Differentiate calyx, corolla, androecium, and gynoecium.; Recognize pedicel as the stalk of the flower.. Board relevance: Draw and label a typical flower showing calyx, corolla, androecium, gynoecium, and pedicel.; Write functions of sepals, petals, stamens, and pistil.; Explain the arrangement of floral whorls in a complete flower.. Exam-critical labels: Pedicel, Calyx, Corolla, Androecium, Gynoecium, Anther, Filament, Stigma. Common mistakes: Putting androecium outside the corolla (correct: Androecium lies inside the corolla and usually surrounds the gynoecium); Treating gynoecium as an outer whorl (correct: Gynoecium is the innermost floral whorl); Calling the pedicel part of the calyx (correct: Pedicel is the flower stalk, not a floral whorl).

kebo105-d007.section_title: STRUCTURAL ORGANISATION IN PLANTS AND ANIMALS
kebo105-d007.content: Diagram: different types of root systems in plants (structural). Caption: The image displays three distinct root systems: (a) Tap root system, characterized by a single, thick, vertical main root with several lateral roots branching out; (b) Fibrous root system, showing a dense, tangled mass of many thin roots; (c) Adventitious roots, which are roots that grow from non-root tissues, such as the stem or leaves, as seen in a plant with a creeping stem. Description: This diagram represents the three major types of root systems in angiosperms. In a tap root system, the primary root develops directly from the radicle and persists as a single dominant main root that grows vertically downward; it gives off lateral roots of smaller diameter, which branch further to increase absorptive surface area and anchorage. This system is typical of dicotyledonous plants. In a fibrous root system, the primary root is short-lived or absent, and a large number of thin, similarly sized roots arise from the base of the stem; these roots form a dense, tuft-like mass and are usually adventitious in origin, typical of monocotyledonous plants. In an adventitious root system, roots arise from parts of the plant other than the radicle, commonly from the stem nodes or other aerial/underground stem regions; these roots may be supportive, absorptive, or storage-related depending on the plant. The diagram should therefore show a clear contrast between a single main axis with laterals, a cluster of many equal-sized roots from the stem base, and roots emerging from a stem or node rather than from the primary root. Labels: Tap root system, Main root, Laterals, Fibrous root system, Adventitious roots, Stem base / node. NEET relevance: Differentiate tap root, fibrous root, and adventitious root systems; Identify monocot vs dicot root systems; Recognize the origin of adventitious roots. Board relevance: Draw and label tap root system; Draw and label fibrous root system; Explain adventitious roots with examples. Exam-critical labels: Main root, Laterals, Fibrous roots, Adventitious roots, Tap root system, Fibrous root system. Common mistakes: Calling the fibrous root system a tap root system (correct: Fibrous root system); Labeling adventitious roots as lateral roots (correct: Adventitious roots); Thinking all roots in monocots are primary roots (correct: Most are adventitious roots forming a fibrous system).

kebo105-d008.section_title: STRUCTURAL ORGANISATION IN PLANTS AND ANIMALS
kebo105-d008.content: Diagram: Solanum nigrum plant and floral structures (structural). Caption: Figure 5.17: Solanum nigrum (makoi) plant. (a) Flowering twig with leaves and clusters of small white flowers and red berries. (b) A single flower. (c) Longitudinal section (L.S.) of a flower showing internal structures. (d) A cluster of stamens. (e) A single carpel. (f) A floral diagram showing the arrangement of floral parts in whorls: calyx (5 sepals), corolla (5 petals), androecium (5 stamens), and gynoecium (5 carpels). Description: The diagram represents Solanum nigrum (black nightshade/makoi), a typical dicot angiosperm of family Solanaceae, showing both the whole flowering plant and the floral morphology used for identification. The plant is an erect, branched herb with simple alternate leaves, small actinomorphic bisexual flowers borne in clusters, and berries that are initially green and later turn red to black on ripening. The flower is pentamerous and hypogynous, with a persistent green calyx of five sepals, a white rotate corolla of five fused petals, five epipetalous stamens with yellow anthers, and a bicarpellary syncarpous gynoecium with a superior ovary. The longitudinal section shows the internal arrangement of these whorls around the central ovary and style, while the floral diagram summarizes the radial symmetry and whorled arrangement of the floral organs: calyx outermost, corolla next, androecium inside the corolla, and gynoecium at the center. This is a standard Solanaceae floral plan, important for recognizing the family and understanding the relationship between floral structure and fruit formation. Labels: Solanum nigrum flowering twig, Flower, Longitudinal section of flower, Stamens, Carpel / gynoecium, Floral diagram, Calyx (5 sepals), Corolla (5 petals), Androecium (5 stamens), Gynoecium (2 fused carpels), Berry, Leaf. NEET relevance: Family Solanaceae diagnostic characters; Actinomorphic, bisexual, pentamerous, hypogynous flower; Epipetalous stamens. Board relevance: Draw and label Solanum nigrum flower; Draw floral diagram of Solanum; Label calyx, corolla, androecium, gynoecium. Exam-critical labels: Calyx, Corolla, Stamens / androecium, Gynoecium, Superior ovary, Actinomorphic flower, Epipetalous stamens, Berry. Common mistakes: Calling the flower zygomorphic (correct: The flower is actinomorphic (radially symmetrical)); Labeling the ovary as inferior (correct: The ovary is superior); Treating the stamens as free and separate from petals (correct: The stamens are epipetalous, attached to the corolla).

kebo105-d009.section_title: STRUCTURAL ORGANISATION IN PLANTS AND ANIMALS
kebo105-d009.content: Diagram: Structure of a monocotyledonous seed (structural). Caption: Figure 5.15: Structure of a monocotyledonous seed. The diagram shows two views of a seed. The left view is a cross-section showing the endosperm and embryo. The right view is a longitudinal section showing the seed coat & fruit-wall, aleurone layer, endosperm, scutellum, coleoptile, plumule, radicle, and coleorhiza. Description: A monocotyledonous seed, exemplified by maize, is typically an endospermic seed in which the bulk of the seed is occupied by a large, persistent endosperm that serves as the principal food-storage tissue for the developing embryo. The outermost covering is the seed coat, which in many monocots is fused with the fruit wall (pericarp), so the diagram should show a single outer boundary labeled seed coat and fruit wall. Just beneath this lies a thin aleurone layer, the living outermost layer of the endosperm that secretes hydrolytic enzymes during germination. The embryo is small and lateral, embedded on one side of the endosperm, and consists of a single cotyledon called the scutellum, which is shield-shaped and functions as an absorptive organ transferring nutrients from the endosperm to the embryo axis. The embryonal axis bears the plumule at the upper end, protected by the coleoptile, and the radicle at the lower end, protected by the coleorhiza. Thus, the diagram must represent a large endosperm surrounding a small lateral embryo with its protective sheaths and absorptive cotyledon, accurately reflecting the typical monocot seed organization. Labels: Seed coat and fruit wall, Aleurone layer, Endosperm, Scutellum, Coleoptile, Plumule, Radicle, Coleorhiza, Embryo. NEET relevance: Identification of monocot seed parts in a labeled diagram; Difference between monocot and dicot seeds; Function of scutellum, aleurone layer, coleoptile, and coleorhiza. Board relevance: Draw and label the structure of a monocotyledonous seed; Explain the role of the aleurone layer during germination; Describe the embryo of a monocot seed. Exam-critical labels: Seed coat and fruit wall, Aleurone layer, Endosperm, Scutellum, Coleoptile, Plumule, Radicle, Coleorhiza. Common mistakes: Calling the aleurone layer part of the seed coat (correct: The aleurone layer is the outermost layer of the endosperm, not the seed coat); Labeling the scutellum as the endosperm (correct: The scutellum is the single cotyledon of the embryo; the endosperm is the large storage tissue); Confusing coleoptile with plumule (correct: The coleoptile is the sheath; the plumule is the embryonic shoot inside it).

kebo105-d010.section_title: STRUCTURAL ORGANISATION IN PLANTS AND ANIMALS
kebo105-d010.content: Diagram: compound leaves of flowering plants (diagram). Caption: Figure 5.5 shows two types of compound leaves: (a) a pinnately compound leaf (Neem) and (b) a palmately compound leaf (Silk Cotton). The Rachis is labeled in the pinnately compound leaf. Description: This diagram represents compound leaves in flowering plants, specifically contrasting two common types: a pinnately compound leaf and a palmately compound leaf. In a pinnately compound leaf, such as Neem, the leaflets are arranged laterally along a central axis called the rachis, which is the continuation of the petiole; the leaflets do not bear axillary buds and each leaflet is attached by a small petiolule. In a palmately compound leaf, such as Silk Cotton, all leaflets arise from a single point at the tip of the petiole, like the fingers of a hand, and there is no elongated rachis bearing leaflets in the same way as in pinnate leaves. The diagram should therefore show the structural difference between these two leaf types, emphasizing that the entire structure is one leaf divided into leaflets, not a branch with separate leaves. Labels: Rachis, Pinnately compound leaf, Palmately compound leaf, Leaflets, Petiole, Neem, Silk Cotton. NEET relevance: Differentiate pinnately compound and palmately compound leaves; Identify Neem as pinnately compound and Silk Cotton as palmately compound; Define rachis and distinguish it from petiole. Board relevance: Draw and label compound leaves: pinnately compound and palmately compound; Label rachis in a pinnately compound leaf; Give examples of each type: Neem and Silk Cotton. Exam-critical labels: Rachis, Petiole, Leaflets, Neem, Silk Cotton, Pinnately compound leaf, Palmately compound leaf. Common mistakes: Calling the entire structure a branch with leaves (correct: It is a single compound leaf with multiple leaflets); Labeling the petiole as the rachis (correct: The rachis is the central axis bearing leaflets; the petiole is the stalk attaching the leaf to the stem); Showing Silk Cotton as pinnately compound (correct: Silk Cotton is palmately compound).

kebo105-d011.section_title: STRUCTURAL ORGANISATION IN PLANTS AND ANIMALS
kebo105-d011.content: Diagram: Parts of a flowering plant showing the shoot system and root system (diagram). Caption: The diagram illustrates the structure of a flowering plant, divided into the shoot system and the root system. The shoot system, above ground, consists of a central stem with nodes and internodes. At the nodes, leaves, buds, and branches (bearing flowers and fruits) emerge. The root system, below ground, consists of a primary root that branches into secondary roots. Description: This diagram represents a typical flowering plant divided into two major morphological regions: the shoot system and the root system. The shoot system is the aerial portion above the soil and includes the stem, nodes, internodes, leaves, buds, flowers, and fruits. The stem is the main axial organ that supports the plant and conducts water, minerals, and food; nodes are the points on the stem where leaves, buds, and branches arise, while internodes are the stem segments between successive nodes. Axillary buds occur in the leaf axils at nodes and can develop into branches or flowers, and the terminal bud is present at the shoot apex. Flowers are reproductive structures borne on the shoot, and fruits develop from the ovary after fertilization. The root system lies below the soil line and consists of the primary root, which develops from the radicle and grows downward, and its lateral branches, the secondary roots, which increase absorption and anchorage. The root system anchors the plant and absorbs water and mineral salts from the soil. The diagram should clearly show the separation between above-ground shoot and below-ground root portions, with the stem continuous into the root axis at the base of the plant.
