Varied number of cisternae are present in a Golgi complex. The Golgi cisternae are concentrically arranged near the nucleus with distinct convex *cis* or the forming

**Figure 8.5** Endoplasmic reticulum

**Figure 8.6** Golgi apparatus

face and concave *trans* or the maturing face. The *cis* and the *trans* faces of the organelle are entirely different, but interconnected.

The golgi apparatus principally performs the function of packaging materials, to be delivered either to the intra-cellular targets or secreted outside the cell. Materials to be packaged in the form of vesicles from the ER fuse with the *cis* face of the golgi apparatus and move towards the maturing face. This explains, why the golgi apparatus remains in close association with the endoplasmic reticulum. A number of proteins synthesised by ribosomes on the endoplasmic reticulum are modified in the cisternae of the golgi apparatus before they are released from its *trans* face. Golgi apparatus is the important site of formation of glycoproteins and glycolipids.

kebo108-s14.content:
These are membrane bound vesicular structures formed by the process of packaging in the golgi apparatus. The isolated lysosomal vesicles have been found to be very rich in almost all types of hydrolytic enzymes (hydrolases – lipases, proteases, carbohydrases) optimally active at the acidic pH. These enzymes are capable of digesting carbohydrates, proteins, lipids and nucleic acids.

The vacuole is the membrane-bound space found in the cytoplasm. It contains water, sap, excretory product and other materials not useful for the cell. The vacuole is bound by a single membrane called tonoplast. In plant cells the vacuoles can occupy up to 90 per cent of the volume of the cell.

In plants, the tonoplast facilitates the transport of a number of ions and other materials against concentration gradients into the vacuole, hence their concentration is significantly higher in the vacuole than in the cytoplasm.

In *Amoeba*, the **contractile vacuole** is important for osmoregulation and excretion. In many cells, as in protists, **food vacuoles** are formed by engulfing the food particles.

kebo108-s16.content:
Mitochondria (sing.: mitochondrion), unless specifically stained, are not easily visible under the microscope. The number of mitochondria per cell is variable depending on the physiological activity of the cells. In terms of shape and size also, considerable degree of variability is observed. Typically it is sausage-shaped or cylindrical having a diameter of 0.2-1.0 $\mu\text{m}$  (average 0.5 $\mu\text{m}$ ) and length 1.0-4.1 $\mu\text{m}$ . Each mitochondrion is a double

Figure 8.7 Structure of mitochondrion (Longitudinal section)

membrane-bound structure with the outer membrane and the inner membrane dividing its lumen distinctly into two aqueous compartments, i.e., the outer compartment and the inner compartment. The inner compartment is filled with a dense homogeneous substance called the **matrix**. The outer membrane forms the continuous limiting boundary of the organelle. The inner membrane forms a number of infoldings called the cristae (sing.: crista) towards the matrix (Figure 8.7). The cristae increase the surface area. The two membranes have their own specific enzymes associated with the mitochondrial function. Mitochondria are the sites of aerobic respiration. They produce cellular energy in the form of ATP, hence they are called 'power houses' of the cell. The matrix also possesses single circular DNA molecule, a few RNA molecules, ribosomes (70S) and the components required for the synthesis of proteins. The mitochondria divide by fission.

kebo108-s17.content:
Plastids are found in all plant cells and in euglenoides. These are easily observed under the microscope as they are large. They bear some specific pigments, thus imparting specific colours to the plants. Based on the type of pigments plastids can be classified into **chloroplasts**, **chromoplasts** and **leucoplasts**.

The chloroplasts contain **chlorophyll** and carotenoid pigments which are responsible for trapping light energy essential for photosynthesis. In the chromoplasts, fat soluble **carotenoid** pigments like carotene, xanthophylls and others are present. This gives the part of the plant a yellow, orange or red colour. The leucoplasts are the colourless plastids of varied shapes and sizes with stored nutrients: **Amyloplasts** store carbohydrates (starch), e.g., potato; **elaioplasts** store oils and fats whereas

**Figure 8.8** Sectional view of chloroplast

the **aleuroplasts** store proteins.

Majority of the chloroplasts of the green plants are found in the mesophyll cells of the leaves. These are lens-shaped, oval, spherical, discoid or even ribbon-like organelles having variable length (5-10 $\mu\text{m}$ ) and width (2-4 $\mu\text{m}$ ). Their number varies from 1 per cell of the *Chlamydomonas*, a green alga to 20-40 per cell in the mesophyll.

Like mitochondria, the chloroplasts are also double membrane bound. Of the two, the inner chloroplast membrane is relatively less permeable. The space limited by the

inner membrane of the chloroplast is called the stroma. A number of organised flattened membranous sacs called the **thylakoids**, are present in the stroma (Figure 8.8). Thylakoids are arranged in stacks like the piles of coins called grana (singular: granum) or the intergranular thylakoids. In addition, there are flat membranous tubules called the stroma lamellae connecting the thylakoids of the different grana. The membrane of the thylakoids enclose a space called a lumen. The stroma of the chloroplast contains enzymes required for the synthesis of carbohydrates and proteins. It also contains small, double-stranded circular DNA molecules and ribosomes. Chlorophyll pigments are present in the thylakoids. The ribosomes of the chloroplasts are smaller (70S) than the cytoplasmic ribosomes (80S).

kebo108-s18.content:
Ribosomes are the granular structures first observed under the electron microscope as dense particles by George Palade (1953). They are composed of ribonucleic acid (RNA) and proteins and are not surrounded by any membrane.

The eukaryotic ribosomes are 80S while the prokaryotic ribosomes are 70S. Each ribosome has two subunits, larger and smaller subunits (Fig 8.9). The two subunits of 80S ribosomes are 60S and 40S while that of 70S ribosomes are 50S and 30S. Here 'S' (Svedberg's Unit) stands for the sedimentation coefficient; it is indirectly a measure of density and size. Both 70S and 80S ribosomes are composed of two subunits.

**Figure 8.9** Ribosome

kebo108-s19.content:
An elaborate network of filamentous proteinaceous structures consisting of microtubules, microfilaments and intermediate filaments present in the cytoplasm is collectively referred to as the **cytoskeleton**. The cytoskeleton in a cell are involved in many functions such as mechanical support, motility, maintenance of the shape of the cell.

**Figure 8.10** Section of cilia/flagella showing different parts : (a) Electron micrograph (b) Diagrammatic representation of internal structure

kebo108-s20.content:
Cilia (sing.: cilium) and flagella (sing.: flagellum) are hair-like outgrowths of the cell membrane. Cilia are small structures which work like oars, causing the movement of either the cell or the surrounding fluid. Flagella are comparatively longer and responsible for cell movement. The prokaryotic bacteria also possess flagella but these are structurally different from that of the eukaryotic flagella.

The electron microscopic study of a cilium or the flagellum show that they are covered with plasma membrane. Their core called the **axoneme**, possesses a number of microtubules running parallel to the long axis. The axoneme usually has nine doublets of radially arranged peripheral microtubules, and a pair of centrally located microtubules. Such an arrangement of axonemal microtubules is referred to as the 9+2 array (Figure 8.10). The central tubules are connected by bridges and is also enclosed by a central sheath, which is connected to one of the tubules of each peripheral doublets by a radial spoke. Thus, there are nine radial spokes. The peripheral doublets are also interconnected by linkers. Both the cilium and flagellum emerge from centriole-like structure called the basal bodies.

kebo108-s21.content:
**Centrosome** is an organelle usually containing two cylindrical structures called centrioles. They are surrounded by amorphous pericentriolar materials. Both the centrioles in a centrosome lie perpendicular to each other in which each has an organisation like the cartwheel. They are

made up of nine evenly spaced peripheral fibrils of tubulin protein. Each of the peripheral fibril is a triplet. The adjacent triplets are also linked. The central part of the proximal region of the centriole is also proteinaceous and called the **hub**, which is connected with tubules of the peripheral triplets by radial **spokes** made of protein. The centrioles form the basal body of cilia or flagella, and spindle fibres that give rise to spindle apparatus during cell division in animal cells.

kebo108-s22.content:
Nucleus as a cell organelle was first described by Robert Brown as early as 1831. Later the material of the nucleus stained by the basic dyes was given the name **chromatin** by Flemming.

An electron micrograph of a nucleus in cross-section. The interior is filled with a dense, granular material labeled 'Nucleoplasm'. A distinct, darker, and more compact spherical region is labeled 'Nucleolus'. The outer boundary is a double-membrane structure labeled 'Nuclear membrane'. A gap in this membrane, through which internal structures are visible, is labeled 'Nuclear pore'.

**Figure 8.11** Structure of nucleus

The interphase nucleus (nucleus of a cell when it is not dividing) has highly extended and elaborate nucleoprotein fibres called chromatin, nuclear matrix and one or more spherical bodies called **nucleoli** (sing.: nucleolus) (Figure 8.11). Electron microscopy has revealed that the nuclear envelope, which consists of two parallel membranes with a space between (10 to 50 nm) called the **perinuclear space**, forms a barrier between the materials present inside the nucleus and that of the cytoplasm. The outer membrane usually remains continuous with the endoplasmic reticulum and also bears ribosomes on it. At a number of

places the nuclear envelope is interrupted by minute pores, which are formed by the fusion of its two membranes. These nuclear pores are the passages through which movement of RNA and protein molecules takes place in both directions between the nucleus and the cytoplasm. Normally, there is only one nucleus per cell, variations in the number of nuclei are also frequently observed. *Can you recollect names of organisms that have more than one nucleus per cell?* Some mature cells even lack nucleus, e.g., erythrocytes of many mammals and sieve tube cells of vascular plants. *Would you consider these cells as 'living'?*

The nuclear matrix or the **nucleoplasm** contains nucleolus and chromatin. The nucleoli are spherical structures present in the nucleoplasm. The content of nucleolus is continuous with the rest of the nucleoplasm as it is not a membrane bound structure. It is a site for active ribosomal RNA synthesis. Larger and more numerous nucleoli are present in cells actively carrying out protein synthesis.

You may recall that the interphase nucleus has a loose and indistinct network of nucleoprotein fibres called chromatin. But during different stages of cell division, cells show structured **chromosomes** in place of the nucleus. Chromatin contains DNA and some basic proteins called **histones**, some non-histone proteins and also RNA. A single human cell has approximately two metre long thread of DNA distributed among its forty six (twenty three pairs) chromosomes. You will study the details of DNA packaging in the form of a chromosome in class XII.

Every chromosome (visible only in dividing cells) essentially has a primary constriction or the **centromere** on the sides of which disc shaped structures called **kinetochores** are present (Figure 8.12). Centromere holds two chromatids of a chromosome. Based on the position of the centromere, the chromosomes can be classified into four types (Figure 8.13). The **metacentric** chromosome has middle centromere forming two equal arms of the chromosome. The **sub-metacentric** chromosome has centromere slightly away from the middle of the chromosome resulting into one shorter arm and one longer arm. In case of **acrocentric** chromosome the centromere is situated close to its end forming one extremely short and one very long arm, whereas the **telocentric** chromosome has a terminal centromere.

**Figure 8.12** Chromosome with kinetochore

**Figure 8.13** Types of chromosomes based on the position of centromere

Sometimes a few chromosomes have non-staining secondary constrictions at a constant location. This gives the appearance of a small fragment called the **satellite**.

kebo108-s23.content:
Many membrane bound minute vesicles called microbodies that contain various enzymes, are present in both plant and animal cells.

All organisms are made of cells or aggregates of cells. Cells vary in their shape, size and activities/functions. Based on the presence or absence of a membrane bound nucleus and other organelles, cells and hence organisms can be named as eukaryotic or prokaryotic.

A typical eukaryotic cell consists of a cell membrane, nucleus and cytoplasm. Plant cells have a cell wall outside the cell membrane. The plasma membrane is selectively permeable and facilitates transport of several molecules. The endomembrane system includes ER, golgi complex, lysosomes and vacuoles. All the cell organelles perform different but specific functions. Centrosome and centriole form the basal body of cilia and flagella that facilitate locomotion. In animal cells, centrioles also form spindle apparatus during cell division. Nucleus contains nucleoli and chromatin network. It not only controls the activities of organelles but also plays a major role in heredity.

Endoplasmic reticulum contains tubules or cisternae. They are of two types: rough and smooth. ER helps in the transport of substances, synthesis of proteins, lipoproteins and glycogen. The golgi body is a membranous organelle composed of flattened sacs. The secretions of cells are packed in them and transported from the cell. Lysosomes are single membrane structures containing enzymes for digestion of all types of macromolecules. Ribosomes are involved in protein synthesis. These occur freely in the cytoplasm or are associated with ER. Mitochondria help in oxidative phosphorylation and generation of adenosine triphosphate. They are bound by double membrane; the outer membrane is smooth and inner one folds into several cristae. Plastids are pigment containing organelles found in plant cells only. In plant cells, chloroplasts are responsible for trapping light energy essential for photosynthesis. The grana, in the plastid, is the site of light reactions and the stroma of dark reactions. The green coloured plastids are chloroplasts, which contain chlorophyll, whereas the other coloured plastids are chromoplasts, which may contain pigments like carotene and xanthophyll. The nucleus is enclosed by nuclear envelope, a double membrane structure with nuclear pores. The inner membrane encloses the nucleoplasm and the chromatin material. Thus, cell is the structural and functional unit of life.

kebo108-d000.content: Diagram: plant cell and animal cell with labeled organelles (diagram). Caption: The image contains two detailed diagrams of cells. Diagram (a) represents a plant cell, characterized by a rigid, rectangular shape. It features a large central vacuole, a thick cell wall with a middle lamella, and plasmodesmata connecting to adjacent cells. Other labeled organelles include the nucleus with a nucleolus and nuclear envelope, rough and smooth endoplasmic reticulum, Golgi apparatus, mitochondrion, chloroplast, peroxisome, ribosomes, microtubules, and cytoplasm. Diagram (b) represents an animal cell, which has a more irregular, rounded shape. It lacks a cell wall and large central vacuole. Labeled organelles include microvilli on the surface, centrioles, peroxisome, lysosome, ribosomes, mitochondrion, rough endoplasmic reticulum, cytoplasm, nucleus with nucleolus and nuclear envelope, smooth endoplasmic reticulum, and Golgi apparatus. Description: This diagram should represent the standard comparison between a plant cell and an animal cell as seen in NCERT Class 11 Biology. The plant cell is typically polygonal or rectangular because of the rigid cell wall, and it contains an outer cell wall made mainly of cellulose, with a middle lamella between adjacent cells and an inner plasma membrane. Plasmodesmata traverse the cell wall to connect the cytoplasm of neighboring plant cells. Inside the plant cell, the cytoplasm contains a large central vacuole bounded by the tonoplast, a nucleus with a nuclear envelope and nucleolus, rough and smooth endoplasmic reticulum, Golgi apparatus, mitochondria, ribosomes, chloroplasts, peroxisomes, and microtubules. The animal cell is usually more rounded or irregular in outline and lacks a cell wall, chloroplasts, and a large central vacuole. It has only a plasma membrane enclosing cytoplasm, a nucleus with nucleolus and nuclear envelope, rough and smooth endoplasmic reticulum, Golgi apparatus, mitochondria, ribosomes, lysosomes, peroxisomes, and centrioles; microvilli may be shown on the surface in some textbook diagrams. The key biological distinction is that plant cells have a rigid wall, plastids, plasmodesmata, and a large vacuole, whereas animal cells have centrioles and prominent lysosomes and no cell wall or chloroplasts. Labels: Cell wall, Middle lamella, Plasma membrane, Plasmodesmata, Cytoplasm, Nucleus, Nuclear envelope, Nucleolus, Rough endoplasmic reticulum, Smooth endoplasmic reticulum, Golgi apparatus, Mitochondrion, Ribosomes, Chloroplast, Vacuole. NEET relevance: Differences between plant and animal cells; Functions of cell wall, plasmodesmata, chloroplast, vacuole, centrioles, lysosomes; Identification of organelles from labeled diagrams. Board relevance: Draw and label plant cell and animal cell; Differentiate plant cell from animal cell; Label nucleus, vacuole, cell wall, plasma membrane, chloroplast, centriole, and Golgi apparatus. Exam-critical labels: Cell wall, Plasma membrane, Middle lamella, Plasmodesmata, Nucleus, Nucleolus, Nuclear envelope, Vacuole. Common mistakes: Calling the middle lamella part of the plasma membrane (correct: The middle lamella is the pectin-rich layer between adjacent plant cell walls); Labeling the vacuole as a vesicle or cytoplasm (correct: It should be the large central vacuole in the plant cell); Showing chloroplasts in the animal cell (correct: Chloroplasts occur only in plant cells and other photosynthetic eukaryotes, not animal cells).

kebo108-d001.content: Diagram: Golgi apparatus showing stacked cisternae (diagram). Caption: A diagram of the Golgi apparatus, showing a stack of several flattened, disc-shaped sacs called cisternae. The cisternae are stacked parallel to each other, with slight offsets. The entire structure is shown in a 3D perspective, with a small vesicle budding off from one of the edges. A label 'Cisternae' with a leader line points to one of the flattened sacs. Description: The diagram should represent the Golgi apparatus as a polarized stack of smooth, flattened membrane-bound sacs called cisternae, arranged in parallel and slightly curved, with a distinct cis face (forming or receiving face) oriented toward the endoplasmic reticulum and a trans face (maturing or shipping face) oriented toward the plasma membrane or secretory pathway. In a typical textbook schematic, the cisternae appear as several stacked, disc-like sacs with small transport vesicles budding from the margins, especially near the trans side. The Golgi apparatus functions in modification, sorting, packaging, and dispatch of proteins and lipids received from the ER; it also participates in glycosylation, sulfation, and formation of secretory vesicles, lysosomes, and in plants, cell wall polysaccharide processing. The essential concept is a dynamic, polarized membrane system with vesicular traffic entering at the cis face and leaving at the trans face. Labels: cisternae, cis face, trans face, transport vesicles. NEET relevance: Identification of Golgi apparatus from a diagram; Function of Golgi: modification, packaging, and secretion; Meaning of cisternae. Board relevance: Draw and label the Golgi apparatus showing stacked cisternae; Explain why Golgi is called a polar organelle; Label cisternae and vesicles in the diagram. Exam-critical labels: cisternae, cis face, trans face, transport vesicles, Golgi apparatus. Common mistakes: Mitochondrion or endoplasmic reticulum (correct: Golgi apparatus); Thylakoid grana (correct: Golgi cisternae); Vacuole or lysosome (correct: Transport/secretory vesicle).

kebo108-d002.content: Diagram: A chromosome with kinetochore (diagram). Caption: Figure 8.12: A detailed diagram of a single chromosome consisting of two sister chromatids joined at a central centromere. Two green oval structures, labeled 'Kinetochore', are attached to the centromere region. Description: The diagram should represent a replicated metaphase chromosome consisting of two sister chromatids joined at a primary constriction called the centromere. The centromere is the specialized chromosomal region where the kinetochore assembles on each sister chromatid. Thus, there are two kinetochores, one on each chromatid, positioned on opposite sides of the centromere and oriented toward opposite spindle poles. The kinetochore is a proteinaceous disc-like complex, not a separate chromosomal body, and it serves as the attachment site for spindle microtubules during cell division. Its function is to mediate chromosome movement, alignment at the metaphase plate, and accurate segregation of sister chromatids during anaphase. The chromosome arms extend above and below the centromere, and the kinetochore is located at the centromeric region rather than elsewhere on the chromatid. Labels: Sister chromatids, Centromere, Kinetochore, Chromosome arms. NEET relevance: Identification of centromere versus kinetochore; Function of kinetochore in spindle attachment and chromosome segregation; Difference between sister chromatids and homologous chromosomes. Board relevance: Draw and label a replicated chromosome showing centromere and kinetochore; Explain the function of kinetochore in mitosis/meiosis; Differentiate centromere and kinetochore. Exam-critical labels: Sister chromatids, Centromere, Kinetochore, Chromosome arms. Common mistakes: Labeling the green oval as the centromere (correct: The green oval should represent the kinetochore, while the narrowed middle region is the centromere); Showing only one kinetochore for the whole chromosome (correct: There are two kinetochores, one on each sister chromatid at the centromere); Placing the kinetochore on the chromosome arm (correct: The kinetochore must be placed at the centromeric region).

kebo108-d003.content: Diagram: Endoplasmic reticulum (rough and smooth) in relation to the nucleus (diagram). Caption: A detailed diagram of the endoplasmic reticulum (ER) and its relationship to the nucleus. On the left, a large, circular nucleus is shown with a distinct nuclear pore. The outer membrane of the nucleus is continuous with the rough endoplasmic reticulum (RER). The RER is depicted as a series of interconnected, flattened sacs (cisternae) studded with numerous small green dots representing ribosomes. To the right and below the RER, the smooth endoplasmic reticulum (SER) is shown as a network of tubular structures without ribosomes. Labels with leader lines point to the Nucleus, Nuclear pore, Rough endoplasmic reticulum, Ribosome, and Smooth Endoplasmic reticulum. Description: The diagram should show the endoplasmic reticulum as a continuous membranous network closely associated with the nuclear envelope. The nucleus is bounded by a double membrane, the nuclear envelope, which is continuous with the membrane of the rough endoplasmic reticulum (RER). The outer nuclear membrane is typically studded with ribosomes and is continuous with the RER cisternae, while the inner nuclear membrane faces the nucleoplasm. Nuclear pores perforate the nuclear envelope and regulate exchange of RNA, proteins, and other molecules between nucleus and cytoplasm. The rough ER consists of flattened membrane-bound cisternae with ribosomes attached to the cytosolic surface, giving it a rough appearance; it is primarily involved in synthesis, folding, and initial processing of secretory, membrane, and lysosomal proteins. The smooth ER lacks ribosomes and appears as a tubular membrane network; it functions in lipid and steroid synthesis, detoxification, carbohydrate metabolism, and calcium storage. The key concept is continuity: the nuclear envelope, especially the outer nuclear membrane, is continuous with the ER membrane system, and the RER and SER are parts of the same interconnected endomembrane system. Labels: Nucleus, Nuclear envelope, Nuclear pore, Rough endoplasmic reticulum (RER), Ribosome, Smooth endoplasmic reticulum (SER). NEET relevance: Continuity of outer nuclear membrane with rough ER; Difference between RER and SER based on ribosomes and function; Function of nuclear pores in transport. Board relevance: Draw and label the nucleus with ER; Differentiate rough and smooth ER; Explain the relationship between nucleus and ER. Exam-critical labels: Nucleus, Nuclear envelope, Nuclear pore, Rough endoplasmic reticulum, Smooth endoplasmic reticulum, Ribosome. Common mistakes: Ribosomes inside the nucleus (correct: Ribosomes attached to the cytosolic surface of the rough ER and outer nuclear membrane); Nuclear pore as a separate organelle (correct: Nuclear pore as an opening/complex in the nuclear envelope); Smooth ER as a different organelle unrelated to rough ER (correct: Smooth ER as a continuous part of the same endoplasmic reticulum network).

kebo108-d004.content: Diagram: Sectional view of a chloroplast (cross_section). Caption: Figure 8.8: Sectional view of a chloroplast. The diagram shows a cross-section of a chloroplast with a double membrane (outer and inner). Inside, there are stacks of green, disc-like structures called grana (singular: granum). These grana are connected by flat, tubular structures called stroma lamellae. The space between the membranes is filled with a green, granular substance called stroma. Labels with leader lines point to the outer membrane, inner membrane, granum, thylakoid, stroma lamella, and stroma. Description: A chloroplast is a double-membrane-bound plastid found in photosynthetic eukaryotic cells, especially in plant leaves. In sectional view, it appears as an oval or lens-shaped organelle enclosed by an outer membrane and an inner membrane, with a narrow intermembrane space between them. The interior is filled with the stroma, a protein-rich matrix containing enzymes for the Calvin cycle, circular chloroplast DNA, 70S ribosomes, and often starch grains and plastoglobuli. Embedded within the stroma is an extensive internal membrane system made of flattened sacs called thylakoids. Thylakoids are arranged in stacks called grana (singular: granum), and adjacent grana are interconnected by stroma lamellae, also called intergranal lamellae. The thylakoid membrane contains chlorophyll, photosystems, electron transport components, and ATP synthase, making it the site of the light reactions of photosynthesis. Thus, the diagram must show the chloroplast envelope, the stroma, grana made of stacked thylakoids, and connecting stroma lamellae in correct spatial relation. Labels: Outer membrane, Inner membrane, Stroma, Granum, Thylakoid, Stroma lamella. NEET relevance: Identification of chloroplast parts in a sectional view; Difference between granum and thylakoid; Function of thylakoid membrane in light reactions. Board relevance: Draw and label sectional view of chloroplast; Label outer membrane, inner membrane, stroma, granum, thylakoid, and stroma lamella; Short note on structure and function of chloroplast. Exam-critical labels: Outer membrane, Inner membrane, Stroma, Granum, Thylakoid, Stroma lamella. Common mistakes: Calling the granum a single thylakoid (correct: A granum is a stack of thylakoids); Labeling stroma lamellae as the membrane envelope (correct: Stroma lamellae are internal connecting thylakoid membranes); Putting stroma outside the chloroplast (correct: Stroma is the internal matrix inside the inner membrane).

kebo108-d005.content: Diagram: Comparison of relative sizes of a typical eukaryotic cell, typical bacteria, PPLO, and viruses (diagram). Caption: The diagram illustrates the relative sizes of various biological structures. On the left is a large, detailed drawing of a eukaryotic cell, showing internal organelles like the nucleus, endoplasmic reticulum, and mitochondria. To its right are smaller drawings: a cluster of rod-shaped bacteria, a group of small circles representing PPLO, and a cluster of even smaller dots representing viruses. Each drawing is accompanied by a label and its typical size range in micrometers (μm). Description: This diagram is a size-comparison chart showing the relative dimensions of a typical eukaryotic cell, a typical bacterium, PPLO (Mycoplasma), and viruses. A typical eukaryotic cell is the largest of the four, usually about 10–20 µm in diameter, and contains a true nucleus bounded by a nuclear envelope plus membrane-bound organelles such as mitochondria, endoplasmic reticulum, Golgi apparatus, and ribosomes. Typical bacteria are much smaller, usually about 1–2 µm, and are prokaryotic cells lacking a true nucleus and membrane-bound organelles; they possess a cell wall, plasma membrane, cytoplasm, ribosomes, and a nucleoid region. PPLO, now commonly referred to as Mycoplasma, are even smaller, about 0.1 µm, and are the smallest free-living cells; they lack a cell wall and are pleomorphic, bounded only by a plasma membrane. Viruses are the smallest entities shown, ranging roughly from 0.02–0.2 µm, and are acellular particles composed of nucleic acid enclosed in a protein coat, sometimes with an outer envelope; they are obligate intracellular parasites and are not true cells. The diagram’s purpose is to emphasize the progressive decrease in size from eukaryotic cell to bacterium to PPLO to virus. Labels: A typical eukaryotic cell, Typical bacteria, PPLO (Mycoplasma), Viruses, 10–20 µm, 1–2 µm, about 0.1 µm, 0.02–0.2 µm. NEET relevance: Relative size order of eukaryotic cell > bacterium > PPLO/Mycoplasma > virus; Difference between prokaryotic cells, eukaryotic cells, and viruses; Why Mycoplasma is considered the smallest free-living cell. Board relevance: Draw and label the comparison of sizes of eukaryotic cell, bacteria, PPLO, and viruses; Write the size range of each component; Differentiate bacteria, PPLO, and viruses in one or two points. Exam-critical labels: A typical eukaryotic cell, Typical bacteria, PPLO (Mycoplasma), Viruses, 10–20 µm, 1–2 µm, about 0.1 µm, 0.02–0.2 µm. Common mistakes: Calling PPLO a virus (correct: PPLO are Mycoplasma, the smallest free-living cells, not viruses); Showing bacteria with a true nucleus and organelles (correct: Bacteria are prokaryotes and lack a true nucleus and membrane-bound organelles); Depicting viruses as cells with cytoplasm and membrane-bound organelles (correct: Viruses are acellular and consist of nucleic acid plus capsid, sometimes envelope).

kebo108-d006.content: Diagram: Electron micrograph of a cell nucleus showing its internal structure (photograph). Caption: An electron micrograph of a nucleus in cross-section. The interior is filled with a dense, granular material labeled 'Nucleoplasm'. A distinct, darker, and more compact spherical region is labeled 'Nucleolus'. The outer boundary is a double-membrane structure labeled 'Nuclear membrane'. A gap in this membrane, through which internal structures are visible, is labeled 'Nuclear pore'. Description: The diagram should represent a eukaryotic cell nucleus in cross-section as seen in electron microscopy. The nucleus is bounded by a double-layered nuclear envelope (nuclear membrane), which separates the nucleoplasm from the surrounding cytoplasm and is continuous with the endoplasmic reticulum at some points. The nucleoplasm is the granular internal matrix containing chromatin and nuclear components; in electron micrographs it often appears dense and uneven because of dispersed chromatin. A nucleolus is present as a distinct, darker, non-membrane-bound, usually spherical or irregular body within the nucleoplasm; it is the site of rRNA synthesis and ribosomal subunit assembly. Nuclear pores are small openings or pore complexes in the nuclear envelope that regulate exchange of RNA, proteins, and ribonucleoprotein particles between nucleus and cytoplasm. The diagram must show the nucleus as an enclosed compartment with the nucleolus inside the nucleoplasm, and the nuclear pores located in the nuclear envelope, not as separate structures outside the nucleus. Labels: Nucleoplasm, Nucleolus, Nuclear pore, Nuclear membrane. NEET relevance: Identification of nucleus parts from an electron micrograph; Function of nucleolus in ribosome biogenesis; Role of nuclear pores in selective transport. Board relevance: Draw and label the nucleus from an electron micrograph; Label nucleoplasm, nucleolus, nuclear membrane, and nuclear pore; Short note on functions of nucleolus and nuclear pores. Exam-critical labels: Nuclear membrane, Nuclear pore, Nucleoplasm, Nucleolus. Common mistakes: Labeling the nuclear pore as a hole in the nucleolus or as a structure inside the nucleus (correct: The nuclear pore is a channel/opening in the nuclear envelope); Calling the nuclear membrane a single membrane (correct: It is a double membrane, the nuclear envelope); Treating the nucleolus as membrane-bound (correct: The nucleolus is non-membrane-bound).

kebo108-d007.content: Diagram: mitochondrion (diagram). Caption: A detailed longitudinal section diagram of a mitochondrion. The outer membrane is a smooth, continuous outer boundary. The inner membrane is folded into several inward-pointing folds called cristae. The space between the two membranes is the inter-membrane space. The interior of the mitochondrion is filled with a dense, granular substance called the matrix. Labels with leader lines point to each of these structures: Outer membrane, Inner membrane, Inter-membrane space, Matrix, and Crista. Description: A mitochondrion is a double-membrane-bound organelle found in eukaryotic cells and is the principal site of aerobic respiration and ATP synthesis. It is typically oval to rod-shaped in longitudinal section. The outer membrane forms the smooth external boundary and encloses the organelle. Beneath it lies the inner membrane, which is highly selective and extensively folded inward to form cristae; these folds greatly increase the surface area available for the electron transport chain and ATP synthase. The narrow intermembrane space lies between the outer and inner membranes and is important in establishing the proton gradient during oxidative phosphorylation. The innermost compartment is the matrix, a dense, enzyme-rich region containing enzymes of the Krebs cycle, mitochondrial DNA, ribosomes, tRNAs, and other components needed for partial autonomous protein synthesis and replication. A correct diagram should clearly show the outer membrane, inner membrane, cristae as folds of the inner membrane, the intermembrane space, and the matrix inside the inner membrane. Labels: Outer membrane, Inner membrane, Intermembrane space, Matrix, Crista. NEET relevance: Identify the parts of a mitochondrion in a labeled diagram.; State the function of cristae in increasing surface area for ATP synthesis.; Differentiate between matrix and intermembrane space.. Board relevance: Draw and label a mitochondrion in longitudinal section.; Label outer membrane, inner membrane, cristae, matrix, and intermembrane space.; Write short notes on the structure and function of mitochondria.. Exam-critical labels: Outer membrane, Inner membrane, Intermembrane space, Matrix, Cristae. Common mistakes: Cristae labeled as the intermembrane space (correct: Cristae are folds of the inner membrane); Matrix labeled as the entire interior including the intermembrane space (correct: Matrix is only the compartment inside the inner membrane); Inner membrane and outer membrane swapped (correct: Outer membrane is the external smooth boundary; inner membrane is the folded membrane inside it).

kebo108-d008.content: Diagram: Ribosome showing large and small subunits and their assembled form (diagram). Caption: Figure 8.9: Ribosome. The diagram shows two views of a ribosome. On the left, the two subunits are shown separately: a larger, purple 'Large subunit' and a smaller, pink 'Small subunit'. An arrow points from the small subunit to the right. On the right, the two subunits are shown joined together to form a complete ribosome, with the large subunit on top and the small subunit at the bottom. Description: The diagram represents a ribosome, a non-membranous ribonucleoprotein particle responsible for protein synthesis (translation). A ribosome is composed of two unequal subunits: a large subunit and a small subunit. These subunits are shown separately on one side and assembled together on the other side to form the functional ribosome. In the assembled form, the small subunit typically lies beneath the large subunit, creating a groove for mRNA binding and tRNA interaction. Ribosomes are the sites where amino acids are linked by peptide bonds according to the codon sequence of mRNA. In prokaryotes, ribosomes are 70S (50S large subunit + 30S small subunit), while in eukaryotes they are 80S (60S large subunit + 40S small subunit). The diagram should emphasize the structural association of the two subunits and their role in translation, not any membrane-bound organization. Labels: Large subunit, Small subunit, Ribosome, mRNA, tRNA. NEET relevance: Identification of ribosome as the site of protein synthesis; Difference between large and small subunits; 70S vs 80S ribosomes and their subunit composition. Board relevance: Draw and label the ribosome showing large and small subunits; Explain the function of ribosomes in protein synthesis; Differentiate between prokaryotic and eukaryotic ribosomes. Exam-critical labels: Large subunit, Small subunit, Ribosome, mRNA, tRNA. Common mistakes: Calling the ribosome a membrane-bound organelle (correct: Ribosome is a non-membranous ribonucleoprotein particle); Labeling the large subunit as the whole ribosome (correct: The large subunit is only one component of the ribosome); Placing the small subunit above the large subunit in the assembled form (correct: The small subunit is typically shown below the large subunit in standard textbook diagrams).

kebo108-d009.content: Diagram: cell cycle (diagram). Caption: The diagram illustrates the cell cycle as a circular process. The main circle is divided into four primary phases: **G<sub>1</sub>** (yellow), **S** (yellow), **G<sub>2</sub>** (yellow), and **M Phase (Mitosis)** (black). The M Phase is further subdivided into five segments: **Cytokinesis** (pink), **Telophase** (light purple), **Anaphase** (medium purple), **Metaphase** (dark purple), and **Prophase** (dark purple). A red arrow indicates the clockwise progression of the cycle. A branch from the G<sub>1</sub> phase leads to a **G<sub>0</sub>** phase, represented by a smaller circle with three blue dots. The background is split diagonally into a white left side and a light green right side. Description: This diagram represents the eukaryotic cell cycle as a continuous, clockwise sequence of phases in which a cell grows, replicates its DNA, and divides. The main interphase portion consists of G1 phase, S phase, and G2 phase: in G1 the cell grows and carries out normal metabolic activities; in S phase DNA replication occurs so each chromosome is duplicated; and in G2 the cell continues growth and prepares the machinery needed for mitosis. The M phase follows and includes mitosis plus cytokinesis. Mitosis is subdivided into prophase, metaphase, anaphase, and telophase, during which duplicated chromosomes condense, align, separate, and re-form daughter nuclei. Cytokinesis then divides the cytoplasm to produce two daughter cells. A side branch from G1 leads to G0, a quiescent, non-dividing state in which cells exit the cycle temporarily or permanently while retaining metabolic activity. The diagram should emphasize that interphase occupies most of the cycle, M phase is comparatively brief, and progression is directional and cyclic. Labels: G1 phase, S phase, G2 phase, M phase (mitosis), Prophase, Metaphase, Anaphase, Telophase, Cytokinesis, G0 phase. NEET relevance: Order of phases in the cell cycle; Difference between interphase and M phase; DNA replication occurs in S phase. Board relevance: Draw and label the cell cycle; Explain the events of G1, S, G2, and M phases; Differentiate mitosis from cytokinesis. Exam-critical labels: G1, S, G2, M phase (mitosis), Prophase, Metaphase, Anaphase, Telophase. Common mistakes: Calling G0 a phase inside the main cycle (correct: G0 is a side branch or exit state from G1, not part of the main cycling loop); Putting cytokinesis before telophase (correct: Cytokinesis occurs after telophase and completes cell division); Treating interphase as only G1 (correct: Interphase includes G1, S, and G2).

kebo108-d010.content: Diagram: Types of chromosomes based on the position of centromere (diagram). Caption: Figure 8.13: A diagram illustrating four types of chromosomes based on centromere position. From left to right: 1. A metacentric chromosome with a central centromere and a 'Satellite' on one arm. 2. A sub-metacentric chromosome with a centromere slightly off-center, showing a 'Short arm' and a 'Long arm'. 3. An acrocentric chromosome with a centromere near one end, also showing 'Short arm' and 'Long arm'. 4. A telocentric chromosome with a terminal centromere at the very end. Labels include 'Satellite', 'Secondary constriction', 'Centromere', 'Short arm', and 'Long arm'. Description: This diagram represents the four major types of chromosomes classified on the basis of centromere position: metacentric, submetacentric, acrocentric, and telocentric. In a metacentric chromosome, the centromere lies approximately in the middle, producing two nearly equal arms (p and q arms); such chromosomes are typically V-shaped during anaphase. In a submetacentric chromosome, the centromere is slightly displaced from the center, so one arm is shorter and the other longer, giving an L- or J-like appearance during movement. In an acrocentric chromosome, the centromere is located very close to one end, resulting in a very short p arm and a long q arm; a secondary constriction may be present on the short arm in some chromosomes, and the distal segment beyond it is called a satellite. In a telocentric chromosome, the centromere is terminal, effectively at the end of the chromosome, so only one arm is visible. The diagram should emphasize that centromere position determines arm length and chromosome shape, and that satellites and secondary constrictions are special structural features seen in some chromosomes, not in all types. Labels: Metacentric chromosome, Submetacentric chromosome, Acrocentric chromosome, Telocentric chromosome, Centromere, Short arm (p arm), Long arm (q arm), Secondary constriction, Satellite. NEET relevance: Identify chromosome types based on centromere position.; Differentiate metacentric, submetacentric, acrocentric, and telocentric chromosomes.; Recognize the roles of centromere, secondary constriction, and satellite.. Board relevance: Draw and label the four types of chromosomes based on centromere position.; Explain the difference between primary and secondary constriction.; Define satellite and mention its association with secondary constriction.. Exam-critical labels: Metacentric chromosome, Submetacentric chromosome, Acrocentric chromosome, Telocentric chromosome, Centromere, Short arm (p arm), Long arm (q arm), Secondary constriction. Common mistakes: Calling the satellite a centromere or part of the centromere (correct: The satellite is a small distal segment separated by a secondary constriction; the centromere is the primary constriction.); Labeling the chromosome types only by arm length without naming metacentric/submetacentric/acrocentric/telocentric (correct: The correct classification is based on centromere position and the resulting arm lengths.); Treating secondary constriction as the same as centromere (correct: Secondary constriction is a non-centromeric narrowing, often linked to NORs.).

kebo108-d011.content: Diagram: Cross-section of cilia/flagella showing the 9+2 microtubule arrangement (cross_section). Caption: Figure 8.10: Section of cilia/flagella. (a) Electron micrograph showing a cross-section of multiple cilia or flagella. (b) Diagrammatic representation of the internal structure of a single cilium or flagellum, showing the 9+2 microtubule arrangement. Labels include: Plasma membrane, Peripheral microtubules (doublets), Interdoublet bridge, Central microtubule, Central sheath, and Radial spoke. Description: This diagram represents the axoneme, the core motile apparatus of a eukaryotic cilium or flagellum. In cross-section, the axoneme shows the classic 9+2 arrangement: nine peripheral microtubule doublets arranged in a ring around a central pair of singlet microtubules. Each peripheral doublet consists of an A tubule (complete microtubule) and a B tubule (incomplete microtubule) and is enclosed externally by the plasma membrane. The central pair is surrounded by a central sheath. Radial spokes extend inward from each peripheral doublet toward the central sheath, helping coordinate bending. Adjacent peripheral doublets are linked by nexin/interdoublet links (often called interdoublet bridges), which provide elastic resistance and help convert sliding into bending. Dynein arms, though not always clearly drawn in simplified textbook figures, are functionally crucial motor proteins attached to the A tubule of each doublet and generate ATP-dependent sliding between adjacent doublets. Together, these components produce the whip-like bending movements of cilia and flagella. Labels: Plasma membrane, Peripheral microtubule doublets, Interdoublet bridge (nexin link), Central pair of microtubules, Central sheath, Radial spoke, A tubule, B tubule, Dynein arms. NEET relevance: Identify the 9+2 arrangement in cilia and flagella; Differentiate cilia/flagella axoneme from centrioles (9 triplets, no central pair); State the role of dynein in microtubule sliding and bending. Board relevance: Draw and label the cross-section of cilium/flagellum showing 9+2 arrangement; Label plasma membrane, peripheral doublets, central pair, radial spokes, and interdoublet bridges; Write short notes on structure and function of cilia and flagella. Exam-critical labels: Plasma membrane, Peripheral microtubule doublets, Central pair of microtubules, Central sheath, Radial spokes, Interdoublet bridges (nexin links). Common mistakes: Calling the peripheral doublets '9 microtubules' instead of 9 doublets (correct: Nine peripheral microtubule doublets, each made of an A and B tubule); Labeling the central pair as 'central microtubule' singular (correct: Central pair of microtubules); Putting radial spokes between peripheral doublets (correct: Radial spokes extend from peripheral doublets toward the central sheath).

kebo108-d012.content: Diagram: Fluid mosaic model of the plasma membrane (diagram). Caption: A detailed diagram of the fluid mosaic model of the plasma membrane. It shows a cross-section of the membrane with a phospholipid bilayer as the base. The bilayer consists of two layers of phospholipids, with their hydrophilic heads (represented by blue spheres) facing the aqueous environment and their hydrophobic tails (represented by wavy lines) facing each other. Various proteins are embedded in or attached to the membrane. Integral proteins are shown as large, complex structures that span the entire bilayer, while peripheral proteins are smaller, attached to the surface. Carbohydrate chains, represented by yellow and green branched structures, are attached to some of the proteins and lipids on the outer surface. Cholesterol molecules, represented by small yellow spheres, are interspersed within the bilayer. Labels with leader lines point to each of these components: 'Sugar' for the carbohydrate chains, 'Peripheral Protein' for a surface protein, 'Integral protein' for a transmembrane protein, 'Phospholipid bilayer' for the main structure, and 'Cholesterol' for the small spheres. Description: The fluid mosaic model of the plasma membrane describes the cell membrane as a dynamic, selectively permeable phospholipid bilayer in which proteins are embedded or associated in a mosaic-like arrangement. The bilayer consists of two layers of amphipathic phospholipids: hydrophilic phosphate heads face the aqueous extracellular fluid and cytosol, while hydrophobic fatty acid tails face inward toward each other, forming a nonpolar core. Integral proteins are embedded within the bilayer, and some span it completely as transmembrane proteins; these function in transport, receptors, enzymes, and cell signaling. Peripheral proteins are attached loosely to the membrane surface, either on the cytoplasmic side or extracellular side, and often participate in signaling, structural support, or anchoring to the cytoskeleton. Cholesterol molecules are interspersed among phospholipids in animal cell membranes, where they regulate membrane fluidity, stability, and permeability. Carbohydrate chains project from the outer surface only, attached to proteins or lipids as glycoproteins and glycolipids, forming the glycocalyx involved in cell recognition, adhesion, and receptor interactions. The membrane is asymmetric, fluid, and dynamic, with lateral movement of lipids and many proteins within the plane of the membrane. Labels: Carbohydrate chain / Glycocalyx, Peripheral protein, Integral protein, Phospholipid bilayer, Cholesterol. NEET relevance: Identify and label components of the plasma membrane; Differentiate integral and peripheral proteins; State the role of cholesterol in membrane fluidity. Board relevance: Draw and label the fluid mosaic model of plasma membrane; Explain the arrangement of phospholipids, proteins, cholesterol, and carbohydrates; Write short notes on membrane fluidity and membrane asymmetry. Exam-critical labels: Phospholipid bilayer, Integral protein, Peripheral protein, Cholesterol, Carbohydrate chain / Glycocalyx, Extracellular side, Cytoplasmic side. Common mistakes: Sugar attached to the inner/cytoplasmic surface (correct: Carbohydrate chains are on the extracellular outer surface only); Peripheral protein spanning the membrane (correct: Peripheral proteins are surface-associated and do not cross the bilayer); Cholesterol shown outside the bilayer (correct: Cholesterol is embedded among the hydrophobic tails inside the bilayer).

kebo108-d013.content: Diagram: Different shapes of cells (diagram). Caption: The diagram illustrates various cell shapes. At the top left, four red blood cells are shown as pink, disc-shaped structures, two of which are biconcave. To their right, several white blood cells are depicted as irregular, amoeboid shapes with internal nuclei. Further right, a group of columnar epithelial cells is shown as tall, narrow, rectangular cells. Below the red blood cells is a large, complex nerve cell with a central body and multiple long, branching axons. To the right of the nerve cell is a single, elongated, spindle-shaped tracheid with internal pits. At the bottom right, a cluster of mesophyll cells is shown as small, rounded, oval cells packed together. Description: This diagram represents the diversity of cell shapes in living organisms, emphasizing that cell form is closely related to function. Red blood cells are small, circular, and biconcave in mammals, a shape that increases surface area for efficient gas exchange and allows flexibility in capillaries. White blood cells are irregular and amoeboid, enabling them to change shape and move through tissues for defense and phagocytosis. Columnar epithelial cells are tall, narrow cells arranged in a continuous layer, specialized for absorption and secretion. A nerve cell (neuron) is highly elongated and branched, with a cell body and long processes adapted for rapid transmission of impulses over long distances. A tracheid is an elongated, tapering xylem cell with thick lignified walls and pits, adapted for water conduction and support in plants. Mesophyll cells are generally rounded or oval, thin-walled, and rich in chloroplasts, forming the photosynthetic tissue of leaves. The diagram should show these as separate examples of shape diversity rather than as connected parts of one tissue. Labels: Red blood cells, White blood cells, Columnar epithelial cells, Nerve cell, Tracheid, Mesophyll cells. NEET relevance: Identify cell types from shape and relate shape to function; Differentiate RBC, WBC, neuron, tracheid, and epithelial cells; Questions on why RBCs are biconcave and why WBCs are amoeboid. Board relevance: Draw and label different shapes of cells; Explain the relationship between cell shape and function; Short notes on RBC, WBC, neuron, tracheid, and epithelial cells. Exam-critical labels: Red blood cells, White blood cells, Columnar epithelial cells, Nerve cell, Tracheid, Mesophyll cells. Common mistakes: Red blood cells shown as round, spherical cells (correct: Red blood cells are biconcave disc-shaped in mammals); White blood cells labeled as round cells (correct: White blood cells are amoeboid and irregular in shape); Columnar epithelial cells labeled as cuboidal cells (correct: Columnar epithelial cells are tall and narrow).

kebo109-s00.content:
A square QR code with a black and white pixelated pattern, used for identification or linking to digital content.

**TABLE 9.1** A Comparison of Elements Present in Non-living and Living Matter\*

\* Adapted from CNR Rao, *Understanding Chemistry*, Universities Press, Hyderabad.

**TABLE 9.2** A List of Representative Inorganic Constituents of Living Tissues

the kind of organic (Figure 9.1) and inorganic constituents (Table 9.2) present in living tissues. From a chemistry point of view, one can identify functional groups like aldehydes, ketones, aromatic compounds, etc. But from a biological point of view, we shall classify them into amino acids, nucleotide bases, fatty acids etc.

Amino acids are organic compounds containing an amino group and an acidic group as substituents on the same carbon i.e., the  $\alpha$ -carbon. Hence, they are called  $\alpha$ -amino acids. They are substituted methanes. There are four substituent groups occupying the four valency positions. These are hydrogen, carboxyl group, amino group and a variable group designated as R group. Based on the nature of R group there are many amino acids. However, those which occur in proteins are only of twenty

types. The R group in these proteinaceous amino acids could be a hydrogen (the amino acid is called glycine), a methyl group (alanine), hydroxy methyl (serine), etc. Three of the twenty are shown in Figure 9.1.

The chemical and physical properties of amino acids are essentially of the amino, carboxyl and the R functional groups. Based on number of amino and carboxyl groups, there are acidic (e.g., glutamic acid), basic (lysine) and neutral (valine) amino acids. Similarly, there are aromatic amino acids (tyrosine, phenylalanine, tryptophan). A particular property of amino acids is the ionizable nature of  $\text{--NH}_2$  and  $\text{--COOH}$  groups. Hence in solutions of different pH, the structure of amino acids changes.

$$\begin{array}{ccccc} \text{R} & & \text{R} & & \text{R} \\ | & & | & & | \\ \text{H}_3\text{N}^+-\text{CH}-\text{COOH} & \rightleftharpoons & \text{H}_3\text{N}^+-\text{CH}-\text{COO}^- & \rightleftharpoons & \text{H}_2\text{N}-\text{CH}-\text{COO}^- \\ \text{(A)} & & \text{(B)} & & \text{(C)} \end{array}$$

B is called zwitterionic form.

Lipids are generally water insoluble. They could be simple fatty acids. A fatty acid has a carboxyl group attached to an R group. The R group could be a methyl ( $\text{--CH}_3$ ), or ethyl ( $\text{--C}_2\text{H}_5$ ) or higher number of  $\text{--CH}_2$  groups (1 carbon to 19 carbons). For example, palmitic acid has 16 carbons including carboxyl carbon. Arachidonic acid has 20 carbon atoms including the carboxyl carbon. Fatty acids could be saturated (without double bond) or unsaturated (with one or more  $\text{C=C}$  double bonds). Another simple lipid is glycerol which is trihydroxy propane. Many lipids have both glycerol and fatty acids. Here the fatty acids are found esterified with glycerol. They can be then monoglycerides, diglycerides and triglycerides. These are also called fats and oils based on melting point. Oils have lower melting point (e.g., gingelly oil) and hence remain as oil in winters. Can you identify a fat from the market? Some lipids have phosphorous and a phosphorylated organic compound in them. These are phospholipids. They are found in cell membrane. Lecithin is one example. Some tissues especially the neural tissues have lipids with more complex structures.

Living organisms have a number of carbon compounds in which heterocyclic rings can be found. Some of these are nitrogen bases – adenine, guanine, cytosine, uracil, and thymine. When found attached to a sugar, they are called nucleosides. If a phosphate group is also found esterified to the sugar they are called nucleotides. Adenosine, guanosine, thymidine, uridine and cytidine are nucleosides. Adenylic acid, thymidylic acid, guanylic acid, uridylic acid and cytidylic acid are nucleotides. Nucleic acids like DNA and RNA consist of nucleotides only. DNA and RNA function as genetic material.

C<sub>6</sub>H<sub>12</sub>O<sub>6</sub> (Glucose)

C<sub>5</sub>H<sub>10</sub>O<sub>5</sub> (Ribose)

kebo109-s01.section_title: Sugars (Carbohydrates) + Amino acids + Fats and oils (lipids) + Nitrogen bases + Nucleosides + Nucleotide + 9.2 PRIMARY AND SECONDARY METABOLITES

**Figure 9.1** Diagrammatic representation of small molecular weight organic compounds in living tissues

The most exciting aspect of chemistry deals with isolating thousands of compounds, small and big, from living organisms, determining their structure and if possible synthesising them.

If one were to make a list of biomolecules, such a list would have thousands of organic compounds including amino acids, sugars, etc. For reasons that are given in section 9.10, we can call these biomolecules as 'metabolites'. In animal tissues, one notices the presence of all such categories of compounds shown in Figure 9.1. These are primary metabolites. However, when one analyses plant, fungal and microbial cells, one would see thousands of compounds other than these primary metabolites, e.g. alkaloids, flavonoids, rubber, essential oils, antibiotics,

coloured pigments, scents, gums, spices. These are called **secondary metabolites** (Table 9.3). While primary metabolites have identifiable functions and play known roles in normal physiological processes, we do not at the moment, understand the role or functions of all the 'secondary metabolites' in host organisms. However, many of them are useful to 'human welfare' (e.g., rubber, drugs, spices, scents and pigments). Some secondary metabolites have ecological importance. In the later chapters and years you will learn more about this.

**TABLE 9.3 Some Secondary Metabolites**

kebo109-s08.content:
There is one feature common to all those compounds found in the acid soluble pool. They have molecular weights ranging from 18 to around 800 daltons (Da) approximately.

The acid insoluble fraction, has only four types of organic compounds i.e., proteins, nucleic acids, polysaccharides and lipids. These classes of compounds with the exception of lipids, have molecular weights in the range of ten thousand daltons and above. For this very reason, biomolecules, i.e., chemical compounds found in living organisms are of two types. One, those which have molecular weights less than one thousand dalton and are usually referred to as micromolecules or simply biomolecules while those which are found in the acid insoluble fraction are called macromolecules or **biomacromolecules**.

The molecules in the insoluble fraction with the exception of lipids are polymeric substances. Then why do lipids, whose molecular weights do not exceed 800 Da, come under acid insoluble fraction, i.e., macromolecular fraction? Lipids are indeed small molecular weight

compounds and are present not only as such but also arranged into structures like cell membrane and other membranes. When we grind a tissue, we are disrupting the cell structure. Cell membrane and other membranes are broken into pieces, and form vesicles which are not water soluble. Therefore, these membrane fragments in the form of vesicles get separated along with the acid insoluble pool and hence in the macromolecular fraction. Lipids are not strictly macromolecules.

The acid soluble pool represents roughly the cytoplasmic composition. The macromolecules from cytoplasm and organelles become the acid insoluble fraction. Together they represent the entire chemical composition of living tissues or organisms.

In summary if we represent the chemical composition of living tissue from abundance point of view and arrange them class-wise, we observe that water is the most abundant chemical in living organisms (Table 9.4).

kebo109-s09.content:
Proteins are polypeptides. They are linear chains of amino acids linked by peptide bonds as shown in Figure 9.3.

Each protein is a polymer of amino acids. As there are 20 types of amino acids (e.g., alanine, cysteine, proline, tryptophan, lysine, etc.), a protein is a heteropolymer and not a homopolymer. A homopolymer has only one type of monomer repeating 'n' number of times. This information about the amino acid content is important as later in your nutrition lessons, you will learn that certain amino acids are essential for our health and they have to be supplied through our diet. Hence, dietary proteins are the source of essential amino acids. Therefore, amino acids can be essential or non-essential. The latter are those which our body can make, while we get essential amino acids through our diet/food. Proteins carry out many functions in living organisms, some transport nutrients across cell membrane, some fight infectious organisms, some are hormones, some are enzymes,

**TABLE 9.4 Average Composition of Cells**

**TABLE 9.5 Some Proteins and their Functions**

etc. (Table 9.5). Collagen is the most abundant protein in animal world and Ribulose bisphosphate Carboxylase-Oxygenase (RuBisCO) is the most abundant protein in the whole of the biosphere.

kebo109-s10.content:
The acid insoluble pellet also has polysaccharides (carbohydrates) as another class of macromolecules. Polysaccharides are long chains of sugars. They are threads (literally a cotton thread) containing different monosaccharides as building blocks. For example, cellulose is a polymeric polysaccharide consisting of only one type of monosaccharide i.e., glucose. Cellulose is a homopolymer. Starch is a variant of this but present as a store house of energy in plant tissues. Animals have another variant called glycogen. Inulin is a polymer of fructose. In a polysaccharide chain (say glycogen), the right end is called the reducing end and the left end is called the non-reducing end. It has branches as shown in the form of a cartoon (Figure 9.2). Starch forms helical secondary structures. In fact, starch can hold  $I_2$  molecules in the helical portion. The starch- $I_2$  is blue in colour. Cellulose does not contain complex helices and hence cannot hold  $I_2$ .

**Figure 9.2** Diagrammatic representation of a portion of glycogen

Plant cell walls are made of cellulose. Paper made from plant pulp and cotton fibre is cellulosic. There are more complex polysaccharides in nature. They have as building blocks, amino-sugars and chemically modified sugars (e.g., glucosamine, N-acetyl galactosamine, etc.). Exoskeletons of arthropods, for example, have a complex polysaccharide called chitin. These complex polysaccharides are mostly homopolymers.

kebo109-s11.content:
The other type of macromolecule that one would find in the acid insoluble fraction of any living tissue is the nucleic acid. These are polynucleotides. Together with polysaccharides and polypeptides these comprise the true macromolecular fraction of any living tissue or cell. For nucleic acids, the building block is a nucleotide. A nucleotide has three chemically distinct components. One is a heterocyclic compound, the second is a monosaccharide and the third a phosphoric acid or phosphate.

As you notice in Figure 9.1, the heterocyclic compounds in nucleic acids are the nitrogenous bases named adenine, guanine, uracil, cytosine, and thymine. Adenine and Guanine are substituted purines while the rest are substituted pyrimidines. The skeletal heterocyclic ring is called as purine and pyrimidine respectively. The sugar found in polynucleotides is either ribose (a monosaccharide pentose) or 2' deoxyribose. A nucleic acid containing deoxyribose is called deoxyribonucleic acid (DNA) while that which contains ribose is called ribonucleic acid (RNA).

kebo109-s12.content:
Proteins, as mentioned earlier, are heteropolymers containing strings of amino acids. Structure of molecules means different things in different contexts. In inorganic chemistry, the structure invariably refers to the molecular formulae (e.g., NaCl, MgCl<sub>2</sub>, etc.). Organic chemists always write a two dimensional view of the molecules while representing the structure of the molecules (e.g., benzene, naphthalene, etc.). Physicists conjure up the three dimensional views of molecular structures while biologists describe the protein structure at four levels. The sequence of amino acids i.e., the positional information in a protein – which is the first amino acid, which is second, and so on – is called the **primary structure** (Figure 9.3 a) of a protein. A protein is imagined as a line, the left end represented by the first amino acid and the right end represented by the last amino

Figure 9.3 Various levels of Protein Structure

acid. The first amino acid is also called as N-terminal amino acid. The last amino acid is called the C-terminal amino acid. A protein thread does not exist throughout as an extended rigid rod. The thread is folded in the form of a helix (similar to a revolving staircase). Of course, only some portions of the protein thread are arranged in the form of a helix. In proteins, only right handed helices are observed. Other regions of the protein thread are folded into other forms in what is called the **secondary structure** (Fig. 9.3 b). In addition, the long protein chain is also folded upon itself like a hollow woolen ball, giving rise to the **tertiary structure** (Fig. 9.3 c). This gives us a 3-dimensional view of a protein. Tertiary structure is absolutely necessary for the many biological activities of proteins.

Some proteins are an assembly of more than one polypeptide or subunits. The manner in which these individual folded polypeptides or subunits are arranged with respect to each other (e.g. linear string of spheres, spheres arranged one upon each other in the form of a cube or plate etc.) is the architecture of a protein otherwise called the **quaternary structure** of a protein (Fig. 9.3 d). Adult human haemoglobin consists of 4 subunits. Two of these are identical to each other. Hence, two subunits of  $\alpha$  type and two subunits of  $\beta$  type together constitute the human haemoglobin (Hb).

kebo109-s13.content:
Almost all enzymes are proteins. There are some nucleic acids that behave like enzymes. These are called ribozymes. One can depict an enzyme by a line diagram. An enzyme like any protein has a primary structure, i.e., amino acid sequence of the protein. An enzyme like any protein has the secondary and the tertiary structure. When you look at a tertiary structure

upon itself, the chain criss-crosses itself and hence, many crevices or pockets are made. One such pocket is the 'active site'. An active site of an enzyme is a crevice or pocket into which the substrate fits. Thus enzymes, through their active site, catalyse reactions at a high rate. Enzyme catalysts differ from inorganic catalysts in many ways, but one major difference needs mention. Inorganic catalysts work efficiently at high temperatures and high pressures, while enzymes get damaged at high temperatures (say above 40°C). However, enzymes isolated from organisms who normally live under extremely high temperatures (e.g., hot vents and sulphur springs), are stable and retain their catalytic power even at high temperatures (upto 80°-90°C). Thermal stability is thus an important quality of such enzymes isolated from thermophilic organisms.

kebo109-s14.content:
How do we understand these enzymes? Let us first understand a chemical reaction. Chemical compounds undergo two types of changes. A physical change simply refers to a change in shape without breaking of bonds. This is a physical process. Another physical process is a change in state of matter: when ice melts into water, or when water becomes a vapour. These are also physical processes. However, when bonds are broken and new bonds are formed during transformation, this will be called a chemical reaction. For example:

$$\text{Ba(OH)}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{SO}_4 \rightarrow \text{BaSO}_4 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$$

is an inorganic chemical reaction. Similarly, hydrolysis of starch into glucose is an organic chemical reaction. Rate of a physical or chemical process refers to the amount of product formed per unit time. It can be expressed as:

$$\text{rate} = \frac{\delta P}{\delta t}$$

Rate can also be called velocity if the direction is specified. Rates of physical and chemical processes are influenced by temperature among other factors. A general rule of thumb is that rate doubles or decreases by half for every 10°C change in either direction. Catalysed reactions proceed at rates vastly higher than that of uncatalysed ones. When enzyme catalysed reactions are observed, the rate would be vastly higher than the same but uncatalysed reaction. For example

$$\begin{array}{ccccccc} \text{CO}_2 & + & \text{H}_2\text{O} & \xrightleftharpoons{\text{Carbonic anhydrase}} & \text{H}_2\text{CO}_3 \\ \text{carbon dioxide} & & \text{water} & & \text{carbonic acid} \end{array}$$

In the absence of any enzyme this reaction is very slow, with about 200 molecules of  $\text{H}_2\text{CO}_3$  being formed in an hour. However, by using the enzyme present within the cytoplasm called carbonic anhydrase, the reaction speeds dramatically with about 600,000 molecules being formed every second. The enzyme has accelerated the reaction rate by about 10 million times. The power of enzymes is incredible indeed!

There are thousands of types of enzymes each catalysing a unique chemical or metabolic reaction. A multistep chemical reaction, when each of the steps is catalysed by the same enzyme complex or different enzymes, is called a metabolic pathway. For example,

$$\text{Glucose} \rightarrow 2 \text{ Pyruvic acid}$$

is actually a metabolic pathway in which glucose becomes pyruvic acid through ten different enzyme catalysed metabolic reactions. When you study respiration in Chapter 12, you will study these reactions. At this stage you should know that this very metabolic pathway with one or two additional reactions gives rise to a variety of metabolic end products. In our skeletal muscle, under anaerobic conditions, lactic acid is formed. Under normal aerobic conditions, pyruvic acid is formed. In yeast, during fermentation, the same pathway leads to the production of ethanol (alcohol). Hence, in different conditions different products are possible.

kebo109-s15.section_title: 9.8.2 How do Enzymes bring about such High Rates of Chemical Conversions?
kebo109-s15.content:
To understand this we should study enzymes a little more. We have already understood the idea of an 'active site'. The chemical or metabolic conversion refers to a reaction. The chemical which is converted into a product is called a 'substrate'. Hence enzymes, i.e. proteins with three dimensional structures including an 'active site', convert a substrate (S) into a product (P). Symbolically, this can be depicted as:

$$\text{S} \rightarrow \text{P}$$

It is now understood that the substrate 'S' has to bind the enzyme at its 'active site' within a given cleft or pocket. The substrate has to diffuse towards the 'active site'. There is thus, an obligatory formation of an 'ES' complex. E stands for enzyme. This complex formation is a transient phenomenon. During the state where substrate is bound to the enzyme active site, a new structure of the substrate called transition state structure is formed. Very soon, after the expected bond breaking/making is completed, the product is released from the active site. In other words, the structure of substrate gets transformed into the structure of product(s). The pathway of this transformation must go through the so-called

transition state structure. There could be many more 'altered structural states' between the stable substrate and the product. Implicit in this statement is the fact that all other intermediate structural states are unstable. Stability is something related to energy status of the molecule or the structure. Hence, when we look at this pictorially through a graph it looks like something as in Figure 9.4.

The y-axis represents the potential energy content. The x-axis represents the progression of the structural transformation or states through the 'transition state'. You would notice two things. The energy level difference between S and P. If 'P' is at a lower level than 'S', the reaction is an exothermic reaction. One need not supply energy (by heating) in order to form the product. However, whether it is an exothermic or spontaneous reaction or an endothermic or energy requiring reaction, the 'S' has to go through a much higher energy state or transition state. The difference in average energy content of 'S' from that of this transition state is called 'activation energy'.

Enzymes eventually bring down this energy barrier making the transition of 'S' to 'P' more easy.

**Figure 9.4** Concept of activation energy

kebo109-s16.content:
Each enzyme (E) has a substrate (S) binding site in its molecule so that a highly reactive enzyme-substrate complex (ES) is produced. This complex is short-lived and dissociates into its product(s) P and the unchanged enzyme with an intermediate formation of the enzyme-product complex (EP).

The formation of the ES complex is essential for catalysis.

$$\text{E} + \text{S} \longrightarrow \text{ES} \longrightarrow \text{EP} \longrightarrow \text{E} + \text{P}$$

The catalytic cycle of an enzyme action can be described in the following steps:

1. First, the substrate binds to the active site of the enzyme, fitting into the active site.
2. The binding of the substrate induces the enzyme to alter its shape, fitting more tightly around the substrate.
3. The active site of the enzyme, now in close proximity of the substrate breaks the chemical bonds of the substrate and the new enzyme- product complex is formed.

4. The enzyme releases the products of the reaction and the free enzyme is ready to bind to another molecule of the substrate and run through the catalytic cycle once again.

kebo109-s17.section_title: 9.8.4 Factors Affecting Enzyme Activity + Temperature and pH
kebo109-s17.content:
The activity of an enzyme can be affected by a change in the conditions which can alter the tertiary structure of the protein. These include temperature, pH, change in substrate concentration or binding of specific chemicals that regulate its activity.

Enzymes generally function in a narrow range of temperature and pH (Figure 9.5). Each enzyme shows its highest activity at a particular temperature and pH called the optimum temperature and optimum pH. Activity declines both below and above the optimum value. Low temperature preserves the enzyme in a temporarily inactive state whereas high temperature destroys enzymatic activity because proteins are denatured by heat.

kebo109-s19.content:
With the increase in substrate concentration, the velocity of the enzymatic reaction rises at first. The reaction ultimately reaches a maximum velocity ( $V_{max}$ ) which is not exceeded by any further rise in concentration of the substrate. This is because the enzyme molecules are fewer than the substrate molecules and after saturation of these molecules, there are no free enzyme molecules to bind with the additional substrate molecules (Figure 9.5).

The activity of an enzyme is also sensitive to the presence of specific chemicals that bind to the enzyme. When the binding of the chemical

![Figure 9.5: Three graphs showing factors affecting enzyme activity. (a) Enzyme activity vs pH, showing a bell-shaped curve. (b) Enzyme activity vs Temperature, showing a bell-shaped curve. (c) Velocity of reaction (V) vs Substrate concentration [S], showing a hyperbolic curve reaching a maximum velocity Vmax. The Km is indicated on the x-axis at half Vmax.](ae05c5a58b4f9ae5751c216b280b0f72_img.jpg)

Figure 9.5 consists of three separate graphs labeled (a), (b), and (c). Graph (a) plots Enzyme activity on the y-axis against pH on the x-axis, showing a bell-shaped curve that peaks at an optimum pH. Graph (b) plots Enzyme activity on the y-axis against Temperature on the x-axis, also showing a bell-shaped curve that peaks at an optimum temperature. Graph (c) plots Velocity of reaction (V) on the y-axis against Substrate concentration [S] on the x-axis. This graph shows a hyperbolic curve that starts at the origin and levels off at a maximum velocity labeled  $V_{max}$ . A horizontal dashed line indicates the  $V_{max}$  level. Another horizontal dashed line at  $\frac{V_{max}}{2}$  intersects the curve, and a vertical dashed line from this point drops to the x-axis at a point labeled  $K_m$ .

Figure 9.5: Three graphs showing factors affecting enzyme activity. (a) Enzyme activity vs pH, showing a bell-shaped curve. (b) Enzyme activity vs Temperature, showing a bell-shaped curve. (c) Velocity of reaction (V) vs Substrate concentration [S], showing a hyperbolic curve reaching a maximum velocity Vmax. The Km is indicated on the x-axis at half Vmax.

**Figure 9.5** Effect of change in : (a) pH (b) Temperature and (c) Concentration of substrate on enzyme activity

shuts off enzyme activity, the process is called **inhibition** and the chemical is called an **inhibitor**.

When the inhibitor closely resembles the substrate in its molecular structure and inhibits the activity of the enzyme, it is known as **competitive inhibitor**.
