Due to its close structural similarity with the substrate, the inhibitor competes with the substrate for the substrate-binding site of the enzyme. Consequently, the substrate cannot bind and as a result, the enzyme action declines, e.g., inhibition of succinic dehydrogenase by malonate which closely resembles the substrate succinate in structure. Such competitive inhibitors are often used in the control of bacterial pathogens.

kebo109-s20.content:
Thousands of enzymes have been discovered, isolated and studied. Most of these enzymes have been classified into different groups based on the type of reactions they catalyse. Enzymes are divided into 6 classes each with 4-13 subclasses and named accordingly by a four-digit number.

**Oxidoreductases/dehydrogenases:** Enzymes which catalyse oxidoreduction between two substrates S and S' e.g.,

$$\text{S reduced} + \text{S' oxidised} \longrightarrow \text{S oxidised} + \text{S' reduced.}$$

**Transferases:** Enzymes catalysing a transfer of a group, G (other than hydrogen) between a pair of substrate S and S' e.g.,

$$\text{S} - \text{G} + \text{S'} \longrightarrow \text{S} + \text{S'} - \text{G}$$

**Hydrolases:** Enzymes catalysing hydrolysis of ester, ether, peptide, glycosidic, C-C, C-halide or P-N bonds.

**Lyases:** Enzymes that catalyse removal of groups from substrates by mechanisms other than hydrolysis leaving double bonds.

$$\begin{array}{c} \text{X} \quad \text{Y} \\ | \quad | \\ \text{C} - \text{C} \end{array} \longrightarrow \text{X} - \text{Y} + \text{C} = \text{C}$$

**Isomerases:** Includes all enzymes catalysing inter-conversion of optical, geometric or positional isomers.

**Ligases:** Enzymes catalysing the linking together of 2 compounds, e.g., enzymes which catalyse joining of C-O, C-S, C-N, P-O etc. bonds.

kebo109-s21.content:
Enzymes are composed of one or several polypeptide chains. However, there are a number of cases in which non-protein constituents called co-factors are bound to the the enzyme to make the enzyme catalytically

active. In these instances, the protein portion of the enzymes is called the apoenzyme. Three kinds of cofactors may be identified: prosthetic groups, co-enzymes and metal ions.

Prosthetic groups are organic compounds and are distinguished from other cofactors in that they are tightly bound to the apoenzyme. For example, in peroxidase and catalase, which catalyze the breakdown of hydrogen peroxide to water and oxygen, haem is the prosthetic group and it is a part of the active site of the enzyme.

Co-enzymes are also organic compounds but their association with the apoenzyme is only transient, usually occurring during the course of catalysis. Furthermore, co-enzymes serve as co-factors in a number of different enzyme catalyzed reactions. The essential chemical components of many coenzymes are vitamins, e.g., coenzyme nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide (NAD) and NADP contain the vitamin niacin.

A number of enzymes require metal ions for their activity which form coordination bonds with side chains at the active site and at the same time form one or more coordination bonds with the substrate, e.g., zinc is a cofactor for the proteolytic enzyme carboxypeptidase.

Catalytic activity is lost when the co-factor is removed from the enzyme which testifies that they play a crucial role in the catalytic activity of the enzyme.

kebo109-s22.content:
Although there is a bewildering diversity of living organisms, their chemical composition and metabolic reactions appear to be remarkably similar. The elemental composition of living tissues and non-living matter appear also to be similar when analysed qualitatively. However, a closer examination reveals that the relative abundance of carbon, hydrogen and oxygen is higher in living systems when compared to inanimate matter. The most abundant chemical in living organisms is water. There are thousands of small molecular weight (<1000 Da) biomolecules. Amino acids, monosaccharide and disaccharide sugars, fatty acids, glycerol, nucleotides, nucleosides and nitrogen bases are some of the organic compounds seen in living organisms. There are 20 types of amino acids and 5 types of nucleotides. Fats and oils are glycerides in which fatty acids are esterified to glycerol. Phospholipids contain, in addition, a phosphorylated nitrogenous compound.

Only three types of macromolecules, i.e., proteins, nucleic acids and polysaccharides are found in living systems. Lipids, because of their association with membranes separate in the macromolecular fraction. Biomacromolecules are polymers. They are made of building blocks which are different. Proteins are heteropolymers made of amino acids. Nucleic acids (RNA and DNA) are composed of nucleotides. Biomacromolecules have a hierarchy of structures –

primary, secondary, tertiary and quaternary. Nucleic acids serve as genetic material. Polysaccharides are components of cell wall in plants, fungi and also of the exoskeleton of arthropods. They also are storage forms of energy (e.g., starch and glycogen). Proteins serve a variety of cellular functions. Many of them are enzymes, some are antibodies, some are receptors, some are hormones and some others are structural proteins. Collagen is the most abundant protein in animal world and Ribulose bisphosphate Carboxylase-Oxygenase (RuBisCO) is the most abundant protein in the whole of the biosphere.

Enzymes are proteins which catalyse biochemical reactions in the cells. Ribozymes are nucleic acids with catalytic power. Proteinaceous enzymes exhibit substrate specificity, require optimum temperature and pH for maximal activity. They are denatured at high temperatures. Enzymes lower activation energy of reactions and enhance greatly the rate of the reactions. Nucleic acids carry hereditary information and are passed on from parental generation to progeny.

kebo109-d000.content: Diagram: oxidoreduction reaction catalysed by oxidoreductases/dehydrogenases (diagram). Caption: $$\text{S reduced} + \text{S' oxidised} \longrightarrow \text{S oxidised} + \text{S' reduced.}$$ Description: This diagram represents a generic oxidoreduction reaction catalysed by an oxidoreductase, specifically a dehydrogenase. In the reaction, one substrate (S) is oxidised while another substrate (S') is reduced, showing coupled electron transfer between two molecules. The correct biochemical meaning is: reduced substrate S donates hydrogen/electrons and becomes oxidised, whereas oxidised substrate S' accepts hydrogen/electrons and becomes reduced. The enzyme does not appear as a visible structural object in this simplified NCERT scheme, but it is implied as the catalyst that facilitates the transfer of reducing equivalents. The diagram is meant to illustrate the general principle of redox coupling in metabolism, where oxidation of one compound is always linked to reduction of another. Labels: S reduced, S' oxidised, S oxidised, S' reduced, →. NEET relevance: Definition and function of oxidoreductases/dehydrogenases; Meaning of oxidation and reduction in enzyme-catalysed reactions; Identification of donor and acceptor substrates in a redox reaction. Board relevance: Draw and label the general reaction catalysed by oxidoreductases/dehydrogenases; Explain how one substrate is oxidised while the other is reduced; State the role of dehydrogenases in removing hydrogen/electrons from a substrate. Exam-critical labels: S reduced, S' oxidised, S oxidised, S' reduced, reaction arrow. Common mistakes: S oxidised + S' reduced on the left side (correct: S reduced + S' oxidised on the left side); The enzyme is shown as a separate molecule in the diagram (correct: This NCERT diagram is a simplified reaction scheme; the enzyme is implied, not drawn); Oxidation means gain of hydrogen (correct: Oxidation means loss of hydrogen/electrons; reduction means gain of hydrogen/electrons).

kebo109-d001.content: Diagram: cyclic structure of glucose (structural). Caption: Chemical structure of Glucose (C6H12O6) in its cyclic hemiacetal form. It is a six-membered pyranose ring with hydroxyl groups at C2, C3, and C4, and a CH2OH group at C5. Description: The diagram should represent the cyclic hemiacetal form of D-glucose, specifically the six-membered pyranose ring (commonly drawn as glucopyranose) formed when the aldehyde group at C1 of open-chain glucose reacts intramolecularly with the hydroxyl group at C5. This cyclization creates a ring containing one oxygen atom and five carbon atoms, with the ring oxygen positioned between C5 and C1. In the standard Haworth projection for D-glucose, the CH2OH group attached to C5 lies above the plane of the ring, while the hydroxyl groups are arranged stereochemically: at C2 the OH is down, at C3 the OH is up, and at C4 the OH is down. The anomeric carbon (C1) bears a hydroxyl group whose orientation distinguishes the alpha and beta anomers: in alpha-D-glucopyranose the anomeric OH is down, whereas in beta-D-glucopyranose it is up. The diagram in NCERT typically emphasizes the pyranose ring, the ring oxygen, the CH2OH substituent, and the hydroxyl substituents at the correct stereochemical positions, illustrating the stable cyclic form of glucose that predominates in solution and is biologically important as the principal monosaccharide used in energy metabolism and as a building block for polysaccharides. Labels: D-glucose, Pyranose ring, Ring oxygen, CH2OH group, Anomeric hydroxyl group (at C1), Hydroxyl group at C2, Hydroxyl group at C3, Hydroxyl group at C4, Alpha-D-glucopyranose or beta-D-glucopyranose. NEET relevance: Identify the cyclic form of glucose as a pyranose hemiacetal.; Locate the anomeric carbon and explain alpha versus beta anomers.; Differentiate glucose from fructose based on ring size and functional group origin.. Board relevance: Draw and label the cyclic structure of glucose.; Show the Haworth projection of D-glucose.; Label the ring oxygen, CH2OH group, and hydroxyl groups at C2, C3, and C4.. Exam-critical labels: Pyranose ring, Ring oxygen, CH2OH group, Anomeric carbon (C1), Hydroxyl groups at C2, C3, and C4, Alpha-D-glucopyranose / Beta-D-glucopyranose. Common mistakes: Fructose (correct: Glucose); Furanose ring (correct: Pyranose ring); Ring oxygen as a substituent outside the ring (correct: Ring oxygen as part of the ring).

kebo109-d002.content: Diagram: Chemical structure of adenine (a purine base) (structural). Caption: Chemical structure of Adenine, a purine base consisting of a fused pyrimidine and imidazole ring system. Description: Adenine is a purine nitrogenous base with the molecular formula C5H5N5. Structurally, it consists of a fused bicyclic heteroaromatic ring system made of a six-membered pyrimidine ring fused to a five-membered imidazole ring, together forming the purine skeleton. In the standard structural representation, the amino group (–NH2) is attached at the 6-position of the purine ring, and the ring contains four nitrogens within the fused system. Adenine is one of the two purine bases in nucleic acids; it pairs specifically with thymine in DNA and with uracil in RNA through hydrogen bonding. In nucleotides, adenine is linked to ribose or deoxyribose via a β-N-glycosidic bond at the N9 position. Labels: Adenine, Amino group (–NH2), Purine ring system, Pyrimidine ring, Imidazole ring, Nitrogen atoms. NEET relevance: Identify adenine as a purine base; Differentiate purines from pyrimidines; Recall that adenine pairs with thymine in DNA and uracil in RNA. Board relevance: Draw and label the chemical structure of adenine; State that adenine is a purine base; Explain the fused ring nature of purines. Exam-critical labels: Adenine, Purine, Pyrimidine ring, Imidazole ring, NH2 (amino group), Nitrogen atoms. Common mistakes: Calling the fused ring system a steroid or cholesterol-like structure (correct: It is a purine base with fused pyrimidine and imidazole rings); Labeling the exocyclic amino group as NH or OH (correct: It should be labeled NH2); Treating the five-membered ring as a separate imine or side chain (correct: It is the imidazole portion of the purine nucleus).

kebo109-d003.content: Diagram: Chemical structure of adenylic acid showing a phosphate group, a ribose sugar, and adenine (structural). Caption: Chemical structure of Adenylic acid, consisting of an Adenine base, a Ribose sugar, and a Phosphate group. Description: The diagram represents adenylic acid, more precisely adenosine monophosphate (AMP), a nucleotide composed of three essential parts: the nitrogenous base adenine, the pentose sugar ribose, and a single phosphate group. Adenine is a purine base attached to the 1' carbon of ribose by a beta-N-glycosidic bond, forming adenosine; the phosphate group is esterified to the 5' carbon of ribose through a phosphoester bond. The ribose is shown as a five-membered furanose ring with a ring oxygen and hydroxyl groups on the 2' and 3' carbons, distinguishing it from deoxyribose. This molecule is biologically important as a monomer of RNA and as a component of energy-transfer and signaling molecules such as ATP, ADP, and cyclic AMP. The correct diagram should therefore show adenine linked to ribose, and ribose linked to one phosphate group at the 5' position. Labels: Adenine, Ribose, Phosphate group, 5' carbon, 1' carbon, 2' hydroxyl (OH), 3' hydroxyl (OH), Ring oxygen, Adenylic acid (adenosine monophosphate, AMP). NEET relevance: Identify the components of a nucleotide: base, sugar, phosphate; Differentiate nucleoside from nucleotide; Recognize adenine as a purine and ribose as the sugar in RNA nucleotides. Board relevance: Draw and label the structure of adenylic acid/AMP; Differentiate adenosine from adenylic acid; Label adenine, ribose, and phosphate group. Exam-critical labels: Adenine, Ribose, Phosphate group, 5' carbon, 1' carbon, Adenylic acid / AMP. Common mistakes: Calling the molecule adenosine instead of adenylic acid/AMP (correct: Adenylic acid, also called adenosine monophosphate (AMP)); Labeling the sugar as deoxyribose (correct: Ribose); Treating the phosphate as attached directly to adenine (correct: The phosphate is attached to the 5' carbon of ribose).

kebo109-d004.content: Diagram: A diagrammatic representation of a portion of glycogen showing a branched polysaccharide structure (structural). Caption: Diagrammatic representation of a portion of glycogen. The diagram shows a branched structure of glucose units. A main horizontal chain of four glucose units is shown at the bottom, connected by alpha-1,4-glycosidic bonds. A branch is attached to the second glucose unit via an alpha-1,6-glycosidic bond. The top part of the diagram shows a cartoon representation of the glycogen molecule as a central thread with multiple branches. Two large green arrows point from the cartoon to the detailed chemical structures. The detailed structures show glucose units as hexagons with hydroxyl groups and hydrogen atoms. The branch point is clearly visible, showing the alpha-1,6-glycosidic linkage. Description: This diagram represents a portion of glycogen, the major storage polysaccharide of animals. Glycogen is a highly branched homopolymer of alpha-D-glucose units. The linear segments are formed by alpha-1,4-glycosidic bonds between successive glucose residues, while branch points are formed by alpha-1,6-glycosidic bonds. In the detailed structural view, a short main chain of glucose rings lies horizontally, and one glucose residue serves as the branching point from which another short chain extends. The upper schematic view depicts glycogen as a compact, tree-like molecule with many branches radiating from a central core, illustrating its dense packing. This branched architecture increases solubility and provides many non-reducing ends, allowing rapid enzymatic release or addition of glucose during glycogenesis and glycogenolysis. The diagram should therefore show glucose monomers, alpha-1,4 linkages along the chain, and a single alpha-1,6 linkage at the branch point, with the overall molecule appearing compact and highly branched rather than linear. Labels: Glycogen, Glucose unit, alpha-1,4-glycosidic bond, alpha-1,6-glycosidic bond, Branch chain, Main chain, Non-reducing ends. NEET relevance: Identify glycogen as the animal storage polysaccharide.; Distinguish alpha-1,4 and alpha-1,6 glycosidic linkages.; Explain why glycogen is highly branched and how that aids rapid glucose release.. Board relevance: Draw and label a branched structure of glycogen.; Mark the alpha-1,4 backbone and alpha-1,6 branch point.; State the monomer unit of glycogen and its biological role.. Exam-critical labels: Glycogen, Glucose unit, alpha-1,4-glycosidic bond, alpha-1,6-glycosidic bond, Branch point, Main chain. Common mistakes: Starch (correct: Glycogen); Cellulose (correct: Glycogen); Peptide bond (correct: Glycosidic bond).

kebo109-d005.content: Diagram: General structure of a triglyceride (structural). Caption: General structure of a Triglyceride: A central glycerol backbone esterified with three fatty acids (R1, R2, and R3). Description: A triglyceride, also called a triacylglycerol, is an ester formed when all three hydroxyl groups of a glycerol molecule are esterified with three fatty acid molecules. The central backbone is glycerol, a three-carbon alcohol arranged vertically in the diagram as CH2–CH–CH2. Each hydroxyl group of glycerol forms an ester linkage (–O–C(=O)–) with the carboxyl group of a fatty acid, producing three fatty acyl residues designated R1, R2, and R3. The fatty acid chains may be identical or different and are represented by hydrocarbon side chains extending outward from the carbonyl carbons. This molecule is the principal storage form of fat in animals and many seeds, serving as a highly energy-dense, hydrophobic reserve molecule. The diagram should therefore represent a glycerol core in the center with three ester bonds connecting it to three variable fatty acid chains. Labels: glycerol, CH2, CH, ester linkage, fatty acid residue, R1, R2, R3, carbonyl group. NEET relevance: Identify the backbone of a triglyceride as glycerol.; Recognize that triglycerides are triesters of glycerol and fatty acids.; Understand that R groups represent variable fatty acid chains.. Board relevance: Draw and label the general structure of a triglyceride.; Differentiate glycerol from fatty acid residues.; Explain esterification between glycerol and fatty acids.. Exam-critical labels: glycerol, ester linkage, R1, R2, R3, CH2, CH, carbonyl group, fatty acid residue. Common mistakes: Calling R1, R2, and R3 the glycerol carbons (correct: R1, R2, and R3 are variable fatty acid side chains, not part of glycerol); Labeling the central structure as triglyceride instead of glycerol (correct: The central three-carbon backbone is glycerol; the whole esterified molecule is the triglyceride); Treating the O atoms as separate substituents rather than ester bridges (correct: The O atoms are part of ester linkages connecting glycerol to fatty acids).

kebo109-d006.content: Diagram: activation energy in an enzyme-catalysed versus uncatalysed reaction (graph). Caption: Figure 9.4: Concept of activation energy. A potential energy diagram showing the energy changes during a chemical reaction. The y-axis is labeled 'Potential Energy' and the x-axis is labeled 'Progress of reaction'. A dashed horizontal line represents the energy level of the 'Substrate (s)'. A solid red curve represents the reaction path without an enzyme, showing a single high activation energy barrier. A dashed pink curve represents the reaction path with an enzyme, showing two lower activation energy barriers. The peak of the red curve is labeled 'Transition state'. The energy difference between the substrate level and the peak of the red curve is labeled 'Activation energy without enzyme'. The energy difference between the substrate level and the peak of the pink curve is labeled 'Activation energy with enzyme'. The final energy level is labeled 'Product (P)' and is lower than the substrate level, indicating an exothermic reaction. Description: This diagram is a potential energy profile for a chemical reaction, comparing the uncatalysed pathway with the enzyme-catalysed pathway. The vertical axis represents potential energy and the horizontal axis represents the progress of reaction (reaction coordinate). The reactants/substrate begin at a defined initial energy level. In the uncatalysed reaction, the pathway rises to a much higher transition-state peak, indicating a large activation energy barrier, and then falls to the product energy level. In the enzyme-catalysed reaction, the enzyme provides an alternative pathway with a lower activation energy, so the peak is much lower; the reactants still convert to the same products, and the overall energy difference between substrate and product remains unchanged. Thus, the enzyme lowers activation energy by stabilizing the transition state and increasing reaction rate, but it does not change the free-energy difference between substrate and product or the equilibrium position of the reaction. Labels: Potential energy, Progress of reaction, Substrate (reactant), Product, Transition state, Activation energy without enzyme, Activation energy with enzyme, Uncatalysed reaction pathway, Enzyme-catalysed reaction pathway. NEET relevance: Definition of activation energy; How enzymes lower activation energy; Difference between catalysed and uncatalysed reaction pathways. Board relevance: Draw and label the activation energy diagram; Differentiate enzyme-catalysed and uncatalysed reactions using the graph; Explain the role of enzymes in lowering activation energy. Exam-critical labels: Potential energy, Progress of reaction, Substrate/Reactant, Product, Transition state, Activation energy without enzyme, Activation energy with enzyme. Common mistakes: Calling the lower curve 'enzyme-substrate complex' or 'intermediate' (correct: It is the enzyme-catalysed reaction pathway showing reduced activation energy); Labeling the peak as 'product' (correct: The peak is the transition state); Saying the enzyme changes the product energy level (correct: The enzyme lowers activation energy but does not change the overall energy difference between substrate and product).

kebo109-d007.content: Diagram: An enzyme-catalysed chemical reaction showing carbon dioxide and water converting reversibly to carbonic acid under carbonic anhydrase (diagram). Caption: $$\begin{array}{ccccccc} \text{CO}_2 & + & \text{H}_2\text{O} & \xrightleftharpoons{\text{Carbonic anhydrase}} & \text{H}_2\text{CO}_3 \\ \text{carbon dioxide} & & \text{water} & & \text{carbonic acid} \end{array}$$ Description: This diagram represents a reversible, enzyme-catalysed hydration reaction in which carbon dioxide (CO2) combines with water (H2O) to form carbonic acid (H2CO3), and the reaction can proceed in the reverse direction as well. The enzyme carbonic anhydrase catalyses this interconversion by lowering the activation energy and greatly increasing the rate of the reaction without being consumed. In physiology, this reaction is especially important in red blood cells and body fluids because it helps in CO2 transport, acid-base balance, and the formation of bicarbonate in blood. The correct chemical relationship is CO2 + H2O ⇌ H2CO3, with carbonic anhydrase written above the reversible arrow to indicate catalysis of both forward and reverse directions. Labels: CO2, H2O, H2CO3, Carbonic anhydrase, reversible arrow (⇌), carbon dioxide, water, carbonic acid. NEET relevance: Identification of the enzyme carbonic anhydrase and its role in CO2 transport.; Recognition of the reversible hydration of CO2 to carbonic acid.; Questions on acid-base balance and formation of bicarbonate in blood.. Board relevance: Draw and label the reaction CO2 + H2O ⇌ H2CO3 with carbonic anhydrase.; State the role of carbonic anhydrase in red blood cells.; Explain why the reaction is reversible.. Exam-critical labels: CO2, H2O, H2CO3, Carbonic anhydrase, ⇌, carbon dioxide, water, carbonic acid. Common mistakes: Carbonic acid is shown as a separate enzyme or catalyst. (correct: Carbonic acid is the product; carbonic anhydrase is the enzyme catalyst.); The reaction is drawn as irreversible with a single arrow. (correct: It must be shown as reversible with a double-headed equilibrium arrow (⇌).); The enzyme is placed below the arrow or beside the reactants. (correct: Carbonic anhydrase should be placed above the reversible arrow.).

kebo109-d008.content: Diagram: Chemical structure of adenosine showing a ribose sugar ring with attached adenine label (structural). Caption: Chemical structure of Adenosine, consisting of an Adenine base attached to a Ribose sugar. Description: This diagram represents adenosine, a nucleoside composed of the purine nitrogenous base adenine linked to the pentose sugar ribose. In the correct structure, adenine is a fused bicyclic purine ring system attached through its N9 atom to the C1′ carbon of β-D-ribofuranose via a β-N-glycosidic bond. The ribose is shown as a five-membered furanose ring containing one ring oxygen and four carbon atoms, with hydroxyl groups at the 2′ and 3′ positions and a CH2OH group at the 5′ position. Adenosine is biologically important as a constituent of RNA and as a precursor of adenosine nucleotides such as AMP, ADP, and ATP; it also functions in cellular signaling and metabolism. The diagram should therefore depict a ribose sugar ring with adenine attached at the anomeric carbon, not a free base or a phosphate-containing nucleotide. Labels: Adenine, Ribose, Ring oxygen, 5′-CH2OH, 2′-OH, 3′-OH, β-N-glycosidic bond. NEET relevance: Identify adenosine as a nucleoside made of adenine + ribose.; Differentiate nucleoside from nucleotide by absence of phosphate.; Recognize adenine as a purine and ribose as a pentose sugar.. Board relevance: Draw and label the structure of adenosine.; Label adenine, ribose, ring oxygen, and hydroxyl groups.; Explain the difference between nucleosides and nucleotides.. Exam-critical labels: Adenine, Ribose, Ring oxygen, 2′-OH, 3′-OH, 5′-CH2OH, β-N-glycosidic bond. Common mistakes: Calling the molecule ATP, AMP, or adenosine monophosphate (correct: Adenosine); Labeling the sugar as deoxyribose (correct: Ribose); Treating adenine as a pyrimidine (correct: Adenine is a purine).

kebo109-d009.content: Diagram: chemical reaction equation (structural). Caption: $$\text{Ba(OH)}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{SO}_4 \rightarrow \text{BaSO}_4 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$$ Description: The diagram should represent a chemical reaction equation, not a biological structure. Specifically, it shows the double displacement reaction between barium hydroxide and sulfuric acid, in which aqueous barium hydroxide reacts with aqueous sulfuric acid to form insoluble barium sulfate as a precipitate and water. The balanced equation is Ba(OH)2 + H2SO4 → BaSO4 + 2H2O. In this reaction, Ba2+ combines with SO4^2− to form BaSO4, while H+ from the acid combines with OH− from the base to form water. The equation must be balanced in terms of atoms and charge, and the arrow indicates the direction from reactants on the left to products on the right. Labels: Ba(OH)2, H2SO4, →, BaSO4, 2H2O. NEET relevance: Identification of reaction type: double displacement and precipitation reaction; Balancing of chemical equations; Products formed when barium hydroxide reacts with sulfuric acid. Board relevance: Write and balance the chemical equation for the reaction between Ba(OH)2 and H2SO4; Identify the type of reaction; State the products formed. Exam-critical labels: Ba(OH)2, H2SO4, BaSO4, 2H2O, →. Common mistakes: Treating the figure as an enzyme or protein diagram (correct: It is a chemical reaction equation showing a precipitation/neutralization reaction); Writing BaSO4 as a reactant (correct: BaSO4 is a product); Omitting the coefficient 2 before H2O (correct: The equation must include 2H2O).

kebo109-d010.content: Diagram: Effect of pH, temperature, and substrate concentration on enzyme activity (graph). Caption: Figure 9.5 consists of three separate graphs labeled (a), (b), and (c). Graph (a) plots Enzyme activity on the y-axis against pH on the x-axis, showing a bell-shaped curve that peaks at an optimum pH. Graph (b) plots Enzyme activity on the y-axis against Temperature on the x-axis, also showing a bell-shaped curve that peaks at an optimum temperature. Graph (c) plots Velocity of reaction (V) on the y-axis against Substrate concentration [S] on the x-axis. This graph shows a hyperbolic curve that starts at the origin and levels off at a maximum velocity labeled  $V_{max}$ . A horizontal dashed line indicates the  $V_{max}$  level. Another horizontal dashed line at  $\frac{V_{max}}{2}$  intersects the curve, and a vertical dashed line from this point drops to the x-axis at a point labeled  $K_m$ . Description: The diagram represents three classic enzyme-kinetics relationships. In panel (a), enzyme activity is plotted against pH and shows a bell-shaped curve, indicating that each enzyme has an optimum pH at which catalytic activity is maximal; activity falls on either side because ionizable groups at the active site and/or substrate are altered by changes in proton concentration, affecting enzyme conformation and catalysis. In panel (b), enzyme activity is plotted against temperature and also shows a bell-shaped curve: activity increases with temperature up to an optimum because molecular motion and collision frequency rise, but beyond the optimum the enzyme denatures and activity rapidly declines. In panel (c), reaction velocity (V) is plotted against substrate concentration ([S]) and shows a hyperbolic saturation curve typical of Michaelis-Menten kinetics: velocity rises steeply at low substrate concentration and approaches a maximum velocity (Vmax) when enzyme active sites become saturated. The substrate concentration at which velocity is half-maximal (Vmax/2) is Km, the Michaelis constant, which is inversely related to enzyme-substrate affinity in the usual interpretation. The figure as a whole illustrates how enzyme activity depends on environmental conditions and substrate availability. Labels: Enzyme activity, pH, Temperature, Velocity of reaction (V), [S], Vmax, Vmax/2, Km, Optimum pH, Optimum temperature. NEET relevance: Identify the effect of pH on enzyme activity and explain optimum pH.; Explain the effect of temperature on enzyme activity and why activity falls after the optimum.; Interpret the Michaelis-Menten curve and define Vmax and Km.. Board relevance: Draw and label the graph of enzyme activity vs pH.; Draw and label the graph of enzyme activity vs temperature.; Draw and label the graph of velocity vs substrate concentration showing Vmax and Km.. Exam-critical labels: Enzyme activity, pH, Temperature, Velocity of reaction (V), [S], Vmax, Vmax/2, Km. Common mistakes: Treating the pH and temperature graphs as linear or sigmoidal curves (correct: They should be bell-shaped curves with a clear optimum); Labeling Km as the maximum velocity or as a point on the y-axis (correct: Km is the substrate concentration on the x-axis at which V = Vmax/2); Placing Vmax at the midpoint of the curve (correct: Vmax is the upper asymptote/plateau of the reaction velocity curve).

kebo109-d011.content: Diagram: levels of protein structure (diagram). Caption: Diagram illustrating the four levels of protein structure: (a) Primary structure shows a linear polypeptide chain with amino acids represented by colored spheres (R groups) attached to a backbone. (b) Secondary structure shows two types of folding: an Alpha-Helix (a coiled spring) and a Beta-pleated sheet (a zigzag pattern). (c) Tertiary structure shows a single polypeptide chain folded into a complex 3D shape, stabilized by Hydrogen bonds and Disulphide bonds. (d) Quaternary structure shows multiple folded polypeptide subunits assembled together. Description: This diagram represents the four hierarchical levels of protein structure. The primary structure is the linear sequence of amino acids in a polypeptide chain, linked by peptide bonds from the N-terminus to the C-terminus; the side chains (R groups) project outward from the backbone. The secondary structure is the local folding of the polypeptide backbone into regular patterns such as the alpha-helix and beta-pleated sheet, stabilized mainly by hydrogen bonds between backbone carbonyl oxygen and amide hydrogen atoms. The tertiary structure is the overall three-dimensional folding of a single polypeptide chain into a compact functional conformation, stabilized by interactions among side chains, including hydrophobic interactions, ionic bonds, hydrogen bonds, van der Waals forces, and disulfide bonds. The quaternary structure is the association of two or more separately folded polypeptide subunits into a functional protein complex, held together by the same types of noncovalent interactions and sometimes disulfide bonds. The diagram should therefore show a progression from a simple amino-acid sequence to local folding motifs, then to a fully folded single chain, and finally to a multi-subunit protein assembly. Labels: Primary structure, Polypeptide chain, Amino acids, Peptide bond, Secondary structure, Alpha-helix, Beta-pleated sheet, Hydrogen bond, Tertiary structure, Disulfide bond, Quaternary structure, Polypeptide subunits. NEET relevance: Identify and differentiate primary, secondary, tertiary, and quaternary protein structures.; State the bonds/interactions stabilizing each level.; Distinguish alpha-helix from beta-pleated sheet.. Board relevance: Draw and label the four levels of protein structure.; Write short notes on primary, secondary, tertiary, and quaternary structure.; Explain the role of hydrogen bonds and disulfide bonds in protein folding.. Exam-critical labels: Primary structure, Secondary structure, Alpha-helix, Beta-pleated sheet, Tertiary structure, Quaternary structure, Hydrogen bond, Disulfide bond. Common mistakes: Calling the alpha-helix a beta-sheet or vice versa (correct: Alpha-helix is the coiled spring-like structure; beta-pleated sheet is the extended sheet-like arrangement); Labeling tertiary structure as multiple chains (correct: Tertiary structure is the folding of a single polypeptide chain); Labeling quaternary structure as just a more folded tertiary structure (correct: Quaternary structure requires two or more polypeptide subunits).

kebo109-d012.content: Diagram: Chemical structure of uridine showing a ribose sugar ring with uracil attached (structural). Caption: Chemical structure of Uridine, consisting of a Uracil base attached to a Ribose sugar. Description: Uridine is a nucleoside composed of the pyrimidine base uracil linked to the pentose sugar ribose. In the correct structural diagram, ribose is shown as a five-membered furanose ring with one ring oxygen and four carbon atoms, and uracil is attached to the anomeric carbon (C1') of ribose through a beta-N-glycosidic bond from N1 of uracil. The ribose ring carries hydroxyl groups at the 2' and 3' positions, and the 5' carbon is present as an exocyclic CH2OH group. This molecule is biologically important as a component of RNA after phosphorylation to UMP, UDP, and UTP, and it serves as the nucleoside form of uracil in nucleic acid metabolism. Labels: Uracil, Ribose, O, CH2OH, OH, OH. NEET relevance: Identify uridine as a nucleoside made of uracil + ribose.; Differentiate nucleoside from nucleotide.; Recognize that uridine contains ribose, not deoxyribose.. Board relevance: Draw and label the chemical structure of uridine.; Label the ribose ring, uracil base, CH2OH group, and hydroxyl groups.; Explain why uridine is a nucleoside and not a nucleotide.. Exam-critical labels: Uracil, Ribose, CH2OH, OH, OH, Ring oxygen, N-glycosidic bond. Common mistakes: Calling the sugar 'deoxyribose' instead of ribose (correct: It should be ribose); Labeling the attached base as 'thymine' (correct: It should be uracil); Treating uridine as a nucleotide (correct: It is a nucleoside).

kebo109-d013.content: Diagram: different ionic forms of an amino acid (structural). Caption: $$\begin{array}{ccccc} \text{R} & & \text{R} & & \text{R} \\ | & & | & & | \\ \text{H}_3\text{N}^+-\text{CH}-\text{COOH} & \rightleftharpoons & \text{H}_3\text{N}^+-\text{CH}-\text{COO}^- & \rightleftharpoons & \text{H}_2\text{N}-\text{CH}-\text{COO}^- \\ \text{(A)} & & \text{(B)} & & \text{(C)} \end{array}$$ Description: This diagram represents the three ionic forms of a generic amino acid, shown as a zwitterionic equilibrium system around the alpha (α) carbon. In the fully protonated form, the amino group is present as H3N+ and the carboxyl group as COOH; this is the cationic form and carries a net positive charge. In the middle form, the amino group remains protonated as H3N+ while the carboxyl group is deprotonated to COO−; this dipolar ion is the zwitterion and is the predominant form of amino acids in aqueous solution near neutral pH. In the fully deprotonated form, the amino group becomes H2N and the carboxyl group remains COO−; this is the anionic form and carries a net negative charge. The side chain R is attached to the α-carbon in all three forms and determines the identity of the amino acid, while the reversible arrows indicate acid-base interconversion depending on pH. Labels: R group (side chain), α-carbon (central carbon), Amino group in protonated form (–NH3+), Carboxyl group in protonated form (–COOH), Carboxylate group (–COO−), Amino group in neutral form (–NH2), Cationic form, Zwitterionic form (dipolar ion), Anionic form, Reversible equilibrium arrows. NEET relevance: Identify the zwitterionic form of amino acids; Explain why amino acids are amphoteric; Predict the predominant ionic form at different pH values. Board relevance: Draw and label the three ionic forms of an amino acid; Explain the effect of pH on amino acid ionization; Define zwitterion and amphoteric nature of amino acids. Exam-critical labels: R group, α-carbon, H3N+, COOH, COO−, H2N, zwitterion, cationic form. Common mistakes: Calling the middle form the neutral form (correct: It is the zwitterionic or dipolar ion form); Labeling the leftmost form as zwitterion (correct: The leftmost form is the cationic form); Labeling the rightmost form as zwitterion (correct: The rightmost form is the anionic form).

kebo109-d014.content: Diagram: Chemical structure of ribose sugar in cyclic ring form (structural). Caption: Chemical structure of Ribose (C5H10O5) in its cyclic furanose form. It is a five-membered ring with hydroxyl groups at C2 and C3, and a CH2OH group at C4. Description: The diagram should represent ribose in its cyclic furanose form, specifically a five-membered ring composed of four carbon atoms and one ring oxygen. In the biologically relevant D-ribofuranose form, the ring is formed by intramolecular cyclization of the open-chain aldopentose ribose, producing a hemiacetal. The ring oxygen is part of the ring and is typically shown at the top or upper position of the pentagon. The anomeric carbon is C1, which bears a hydroxyl group; the remaining ring carbons C2 and C3 each carry hydroxyl groups, and C4 is attached to an exocyclic CH2OH group (C5) extending outside the ring. This sugar is a key component of RNA and of many nucleotides such as ATP, and its cyclic form is the biologically important representation used in nucleic acid chemistry. The diagram should therefore show a pentose sugar ring with the correct hydroxyl substituents and the exocyclic hydroxymethyl side chain, not a generic hexose or an incorrect arrangement of substituents. Labels: Ribose (D-ribofuranose), Ring oxygen, C1 (anomeric carbon), C2 hydroxyl group, C3 hydroxyl group, C4 / CH2OH substituent. NEET relevance: Identify ribose as the sugar component of RNA and nucleotides.; Differentiate ribose from deoxyribose based on the presence of the 2' hydroxyl group.; Recognize the furanose ring form of ribose.. Board relevance: Draw and label the cyclic structure of ribose.; Differentiate ribose and deoxyribose structurally.; State the role of ribose in RNA and nucleotide formation.. Exam-critical labels: Ribose, Ring oxygen, C1 (anomeric carbon), C2 hydroxyl, C3 hydroxyl, CH2OH group. Common mistakes: Deoxyribose (correct: Ribose); Glucose or a six-membered pyranose ring (correct: Ribofuranose, a five-membered ring); CH2OH attached to the wrong carbon or placed as a ring substituent (correct: Exocyclic CH2OH group attached to C4).

kebo109-d015.content: Diagram: Chemical structure of cholesterol (structural). Caption: Chemical structure of Cholesterol, a steroid molecule with four fused rings and a long hydrocarbon tail. Description: The diagram should represent cholesterol, a sterol lipid with the characteristic steroid nucleus: four fused hydrocarbon rings arranged as three six-membered rings (A, B, and C) and one five-membered ring (D). A single hydroxyl group (–OH) is attached at carbon 3 on the A ring, making cholesterol amphipathic, with a small polar head and a largely nonpolar body. A double bond is present between carbon 5 and carbon 6 in the B ring region, and a branched hydrocarbon side chain extends from carbon 17 of the D ring. In addition, methyl groups are attached at carbon 10 and carbon 13. Cholesterol is an important structural component of animal cell membranes, where it modulates membrane fluidity and stability, and it also serves as a precursor for steroid hormones, bile acids, and vitamin D. Labels: Hydroxyl group (–OH), Steroid nucleus, A ring, B ring, C ring, D ring, Double bond (C5=C6), Hydrocarbon side chain, Methyl group at C10, Methyl group at C13. NEET relevance: Identify cholesterol as a sterol lipid.; State that cholesterol has four fused rings and a hydrocarbon side chain.; Explain its role in animal cell membranes in regulating fluidity.. Board relevance: Draw and label the chemical structure of cholesterol.; Differentiate cholesterol from triglycerides and phospholipids.; Write one function of cholesterol in membranes.. Exam-critical labels: Hydroxyl group (–OH), Steroid nucleus, Four fused rings, Hydrocarbon side chain, Double bond (C5=C6). Common mistakes: Triglyceride (correct: Cholesterol); Phospholipid (correct: Cholesterol); Hydroxyl group as 'HO' only (correct: Hydroxyl group (–OH)).

kebo109-d016.content: Diagram: Chemical structure of a phospholipid (lecithin) (structural). Caption: Chemical structure of a Phospholipid (Lecithin): A glycerol backbone esterified with two fatty acids and one phosphate group linked to a choline head group. Description: The diagram represents a phospholipid molecule, specifically lecithin (phosphatidylcholine), which is an amphipathic lipid built on a glycerol backbone. The three-carbon glycerol molecule is esterified at two hydroxyl groups with two fatty acid chains, conventionally shown as R1 and R2, each attached through an ester linkage containing a carbonyl group (C=O) and an oxygen atom. The third hydroxyl group of glycerol is linked to a phosphate group, and the phosphate is further connected to the polar head group choline, which contains a quaternary ammonium nitrogen bearing three methyl groups (N+(CH3)3). This arrangement creates a hydrophilic phosphate-choline head and two hydrophobic fatty acid tails, making phospholipids the principal structural molecules of biological membranes. The molecule is not symmetrical: the head group is polar and charged, while the hydrocarbon tails are nonpolar, allowing phospholipids to self-assemble into bilayers in aqueous environments. Labels: glycerol backbone, fatty acid residue (R1), fatty acid residue (R2), ester linkage, phosphate group, choline, quaternary ammonium nitrogen, methyl groups (CH3), hydrophilic head, hydrophobic tails. NEET relevance: Identify lecithin/phosphatidylcholine as a phospholipid.; Differentiate phospholipids from triglycerides.; Recognize the amphipathic nature of membrane lipids.. Board relevance: Draw and label the chemical structure of lecithin.; Explain why phospholipids are amphipathic.; Compare phospholipid structure with triglyceride structure.. Exam-critical labels: glycerol backbone, two fatty acid tails, phosphate group, choline, quaternary ammonium nitrogen, ester linkage, hydrophilic head, hydrophobic tails. Common mistakes: Calling the molecule a triglyceride or triacylglycerol (correct: It is a phospholipid, specifically lecithin/phosphatidylcholine); Labeling the head group as 'phosphorus' or 'phosphate only' (correct: The head group is phosphocholine: phosphate plus choline); Treating the nitrogen as neutral (correct: The nitrogen in choline is a quaternary ammonium nitrogen with a permanent positive charge).

kebo110-s00.section_title: Introduction + CELL CYCLE AND CELL DIVISION
kebo110-s00.content:
[10.3 Significance of Mitosis](#)

[10.5 Significance of Meiosis](#)

Are you aware that all organisms, even the largest, start their life from a single cell? You may wonder how a single cell then goes on to form such large organisms. Growth and reproduction are characteristics of cells, indeed of all living organisms. All cells reproduce by dividing into two, with each parental cell giving rise to two daughter cells each time they divide. These newly formed daughter cells can themselves grow and divide, giving rise to a new cell population that is formed by the growth and division of a single parental cell and its progeny. In other words, such cycles of growth and division allow a single cell to form a structure consisting of millions of cells.

kebo110-s02.content: Cell division is a very important process in all living organisms. During the division of a cell, DNA replication and cell growth also take place. All these processes, i.e., cell division, DNA replication, and cell growth, hence, have to take place in a coordinated way to ensure correct division and formation of progeny cells containing intact genomes. The sequence of events by which a cell duplicates its genome, synthesises the other constituents of the cell and eventually divides into two daughter cells is termed **cell cycle**. Although cell growth (in terms of cytoplasmic increase) is a continuous process, DNA synthesis occurs only during one specific stage in the cell cycle. The replicated chromosomes (DNA) are then distributed to daughter nuclei by a complex series of events during cell division. These events are themselves under genetic control.

kebo110-s03.content:
A typical eukaryotic cell cycle is illustrated by human cells in culture. These cells divide once in approximately every 24 hours (Figure 10.1). However, this duration of cell cycle can vary from organism to organism and also from cell type to cell type. Yeast for example, can progress through the cell cycle in only about 90 minutes.

The cell cycle is divided into two basic phases:

- Interphase
- M Phase (Mitosis phase)

The M Phase represents the phase when the actual cell division or mitosis occurs and the interphase represents the phase between two successive M phases. It is significant to note that in the 24 hour average duration of cell cycle of a human cell, cell division proper lasts for only about an hour. The interphase lasts more than 95% of the duration of cell cycle.

The M Phase starts with the nuclear division, corresponding to the separation of daughter chromosomes (**karyokinesis**) and usually ends with division of cytoplasm (**cytokinesis**). The interphase, though called the resting phase, is the time during which the cell is preparing for division by undergoing both cell growth and DNA replication in an orderly manner. The interphase is divided into three further phases:

$G_1$  phase corresponds to the interval between mitosis and initiation of DNA replication. During  $G_1$  phase, the cell is metabolically active and continuously grows but does not replicate its DNA. S or **synthesis** phase marks the period during which DNA synthesis or replication takes place. During this time the amount of DNA per cell doubles. If the initial amount of DNA is denoted as  $2C$  then it increases to  $4C$ . However, there is no increase in the chromosome number; if the cell had diploid or  $2n$  number of chromosomes at  $G_1$ , even after S phase the number of chromosomes remains the same, i.e.,  $2n$ .

In animal cells, during the S phase, DNA replication begins in the nucleus, and the centriole duplicates in the cytoplasm. During the  $G_2$  phase, proteins are synthesised in preparation for mitosis while cell growth continues.

Figure 10.1 A diagrammatic view of cell cycle indicating formation of two cells from one cell

How do plants and animals continue to grow all their lives? Do all cells in a plant divide all the time? Do you think all cells continue to divide in all plants and animals? Can you tell the name and the location of tissues having cells that divide all their life in higher plants? Do animals have similar meristematic tissues?

You have studied mitosis in onion root tip cells. It has 16 chromosomes in each cell. Can you tell how many chromosomes will the cell have at  $G_1$  phase, after S phase, and after M phase? Also, what will be the DNA content of the cells at  $G_1$ , after S and at  $G_2$ , if the content after M phase is  $2C$ ?

Some cells in the adult animals do not appear to exhibit division (e.g., heart cells) and many other cells divide only occasionally, as needed to replace cells that have been lost because of injury or cell death. These cells that do not divide further exit  $G_1$  phase to enter an inactive stage called **quiescent stage ( $G_0$ )** of the cell cycle. Cells in this stage remain metabolically active but no longer proliferate unless called on to do so depending on the requirement of the organism.

In animals, mitotic cell division is only seen in the diploid somatic cells. However, there are few exceptions to this where haploid cells divide by mitosis, for example, male honey bees. Against this, the plants can show mitotic divisions in both haploid and diploid cells. From your recollection of examples of alternation of generations in plants (Chapter 3) identify plant species and stages at which mitosis is seen in haploid cells.

kebo110-s04.content:
This is the most dramatic period of the cell cycle, involving a major reorganisation of virtually all components of the cell. Since the number of chromosomes in the parent and progeny cells is the same, it is also called as **equational division**. Though for convenience mitosis has been divided into four stages of nuclear division (karyokinesis), it is very essential to understand that cell division is a progressive process and very clear-cut lines cannot be drawn between various stages. Karyokinesis involves following four stages:

- *Prophase*
- *Metaphase*
- *Anaphase*
- *Telophase*

kebo110-s05.content:
Prophase which is the first stage of karyokinesis of mitosis follows the S and  $G_2$  phases of interphase. In the S and  $G_2$  phases, the new DNA molecules formed are not distinct but intertwined. Prophase is marked by the initiation of condensation of chromosomal material. The chromosomal material becomes untangled during the process of chromatin condensation (Figure 10.2 a). The centrosome, which had undergone duplication during S phase of interphase, now begins to move towards opposite poles of the cell. The completion of prophase can thus be marked by the following characteristic events:

- Chromosomal material condenses to form compact mitotic chromosomes. Chromosomes are seen to be composed of two chromatids attached together at the centromere.
- Centrosome which had undergone duplication during interphase, begins to move towards opposite poles of the cell. Each centrosome radiates out microtubules called asters. The two asters together with spindle fibres forms mitotic apparatus.

Cells at the end of prophase, when viewed under the microscope, do not show golgi complexes, endoplasmic reticulum, nucleolus and the nuclear envelope.

kebo110-s06.content:
The complete disintegration of the nuclear envelope marks the start of the second phase of mitosis, hence the chromosomes are spread through the cytoplasm of the cell. By this stage, condensation of chromosomes is completed and they can be observed clearly under the microscope. This then, is the stage at which morphology of chromosomes is most easily studied. At this stage, metaphase chromosome is made up of two sister chromatids, which are held together by the centromere (Figure 10.2 b). Small disc-shaped structures at the surface of the centromeres are called kinetochores. These structures serve as the sites of attachment of spindle fibres to the chromosomes that are moved into position at the centre of the cell. Hence, the metaphase is characterised by all the chromosomes coming to lie at the equator with one chromatid of each chromosome connected by its kinetochore to spindle fibres from one pole and its sister chromatid connected by its kinetochore to spindle fibres from the opposite pole (Figure 10.2 b). The plane of alignment of the chromosomes at metaphase is referred to as the **metaphase plate**. The key features of metaphase are:

- Spindle fibres attach to kinetochores of chromosomes.
- Chromosomes are moved to spindle equator and get aligned along metaphase plate through spindle fibres to both poles.

kebo110-s07.content:
At the onset of anaphase, each chromosome arranged at the metaphase plate is split simultaneously and the two daughter chromatids, now referred to as daughter chromosomes of the future daughter nuclei, begin their migration towards the two opposite poles. As each chromosome moves away from the equatorial plate, the centromere of each chromosome remains directed towards the pole and hence at the leading edge, with the arms of the chromosome trailing behind (Figure 10.2 c). Thus, anaphase stage is characterised by

The figure consists of four circular diagrams arranged vertically, each showing a cell at a different stage of mitosis. The top two diagrams are labeled (a) and the bottom two are labeled (b).  
1. **Early Prophase**: The top diagram shows a cell with a distinct nucleus containing two tangled chromatin fibers (one blue, one red). The nuclear envelope is represented by a dashed yellow line.  
2. **Late Prophase**: The second diagram shows the nucleus with a more fragmented nuclear envelope (dashed yellow line). Inside, two chromosomes (one blue, one red) are visible.  
3. **Transition to Metaphase**: The third diagram shows the nuclear envelope completely gone. Spindle fibers (red lines) are forming from a central pole (orange dot). Two chromosomes (one blue, one red) are being pulled towards the center.  
4. **Metaphase**: The bottom diagram shows the chromosomes aligned at the metaphase plate (equator). Spindle fibers from both poles (orange dots) are attached to the kinetochores of the chromosomes.

Figure 10.2 a and b : A diagrammatic view of stages in mitosis

The figure shows three stages of mitosis in a plant cell, labeled (c), (d), and (e).  
 (c) Anaphase: The cell is elongated, and sister chromatids (chromosomes) are being pulled towards opposite poles by spindle fibers. There are two distinct groups of chromosomes at each pole.  
 (d) Telophase: Two new nuclei are forming at opposite poles, each containing a set of chromosomes. The cell membrane is beginning to pinch in the middle, indicating the start of cytokinesis.  
 (e) Interphase: Two separate daughter cells are shown, each with a distinct nucleus containing chromatin material.

**Figure 10.2 c to e :** A diagrammatic view of stages in Mitosis

the following key events:

- Centromeres split and chromatids separate.
- Chromatids move to opposite poles.

kebo110-s08.content:
At the beginning of the final stage of karyokinesis, i.e., telophase, the chromosomes that have reached their respective poles decondense and lose their individuality. The individual chromosomes can no longer be seen and each set of chromatin material tends to collect at each of the two poles (Figure 10.2 d). This is the stage which shows the following key events:

- Chromosomes cluster at opposite spindle poles and their identity is lost as discrete elements.
- Nuclear envelope develops around the chromosome clusters at each pole forming two daughter nuclei.
- Nucleolus, golgi complex and ER reform.

kebo110-s09.content: Mitosis accomplishes not only the segregation of duplicated chromosomes into daughter nuclei (karyokinesis), but the cell itself is divided into two daughter cells by the separation of cytoplasm called cytokinesis at the end of which cell division gets completed (Figure 10.2 e). In an animal cell, this is achieved by the appearance of a furrow in the plasma membrane. The furrow gradually deepens and ultimately joins in the centre dividing the cell cytoplasm into two. Plant cells however, are enclosed by a relatively inextensible cell wall, therefore they undergo cytokinesis by a different mechanism. In plant cells, wall formation starts in the centre of the cell and grows outward to meet the existing lateral walls. The formation of the new cell wall begins with the formation of a simple precursor, called the **cell-plate** that represents the middle lamella between the walls of two adjacent cells. At the time of cytoplasmic division, organelles like mitochondria and plastids get distributed between the two daughter cells. In some organisms karyokinesis is not followed by cytokinesis as a result of which multinucleate condition arises leading to the formation of syncytium (e.g., liquid endosperm in coconut).

kebo110-s10.content:
Mitosis or the equational division is usually restricted to the diploid cells only. However, in some lower plants and in some social insects haploid cells also divide by mitosis. It is very essential to understand the significance of this division in the life of an organism. Are you aware of some examples where you have studied about haploid and diploid insects?

Mitosis usually results in the production of diploid daughter cells with identical genetic complement. The growth of multicellular organisms is due to mitosis. Cell growth results in disturbing the ratio between the nucleus and the cytoplasm. It therefore becomes essential for the cell to divide to restore the nucleo-cytoplasmic ratio. A very significant contribution of mitosis is cell repair. The cells of the upper layer of the epidermis, cells of the lining of the gut, and blood cells are being constantly replaced. Mitotic divisions in the meristematic tissues – the apical and the lateral cambium, result in a continuous growth of plants throughout their life.

kebo110-s11.content:
The production of offspring by sexual reproduction includes the fusion of two gametes, each with a complete haploid set of chromosomes. Gametes are formed from specialised diploid cells. This specialised kind of cell division that reduces the chromosome number by half results in the production of haploid daughter cells. This kind of division is called **meiosis**. Meiosis ensures the production of haploid phase in the life cycle of sexually reproducing organisms whereas fertilisation restores the diploid phase. We come across meiosis during gametogenesis in plants and animals. This leads to the formation of haploid gametes. The key features of meiosis are as follows:

- Meiosis involves two sequential cycles of nuclear and cell division called **meiosis I** and **meiosis II** but only a single cycle of DNA replication.
- Meiosis I is initiated after the parental chromosomes have replicated to produce identical sister chromatids at the S phase.
- Meiosis involves pairing of homologous chromosomes and recombination between non-sister chromatids of homologous chromosomes.
- Four haploid cells are formed at the end of meiosis II.

Meiotic events can be grouped under the following phases:

kebo110-s12.content:
**Prophase I:** Prophase of the first meiotic division is typically longer and more complex when compared to prophase of mitosis. It has been further subdivided into the following five phases based on chromosomal behaviour, i.e., Leptotene, Zygotene, Pachytene, Diplotene and Diakinesis.

During **leptotene** stage the chromosomes become gradually visible under the light microscope. The compaction of chromosomes continues throughout leptotene. This is followed by the second stage of prophase I called **zygotene**. During this stage, chromosomes start pairing together and this process of association is called synapsis. Such paired chromosomes are called homologous chromosomes. Electron micrographs of this stage, indicate that chromosome synapsis is accompanied by the formation of complex structure called **synaptonemal complex**. The complex formed by a pair of synapsed homologous chromosomes is called a **bivalent** or a tetrad. However, these are more clearly visible at the next stage. The first two stages of prophase I are relatively short-lived compared to the next stage that is **pachytene**. During this stage, the four chromatids of each bivalent chromosomes becomes distinct and clearly appear as tetrads. This stage is characterised by the appearance of recombination nodules, the sites at which crossing over occurs between non-sister chromatids of the homologous chromosomes. Crossing over is the exchange of genetic material between two homologous chromosomes. Crossing over is also an enzyme-mediated process and the enzyme involved is called recombinase. Crossing over leads to recombination of genetic material on the two chromosomes. Recombination between homologous chromosomes is completed by the end of pachytene, leaving the chromosomes linked at the sites of crossing over.

The beginning of **diplotene** is recognised by the dissolution of the synaptonemal complex and the tendency of the recombined homologous chromosomes of the bivalents to separate from each other except at the sites of crossovers. These X-shaped structures, are called **chiasmata**. In oocytes of some vertebrates, diplotene can last for months or years.

The final stage of meiotic prophase I is **diakinesis**. This is marked by terminalisation of chiasmata. During this phase the chromosomes are fully condensed and the meiotic spindle is assembled to prepare the homologous chromosomes for separation. By the end of diakinesis, the nucleolus disappears and the nuclear envelope also breaks down. Diakinesis represents transition to metaphase.

**Metaphase I:** The bivalent chromosomes align on the equatorial plate (Figure 10.3). The microtubules from the opposite poles of the spindle attach to the kinetochore of homologous chromosomes.

The diagram illustrates the four stages of Meiosis I in a sequential flow from left to right. 
 1. **Prophase I**: A cell containing two pairs of homologous chromosomes (one pair in blue, one in pink) that have exchanged segments (crossing over). 
 2. **Metaphase I**: The homologous chromosome pairs align at the metaphase plate (equator) of the cell. Spindle fibers (yellow lines) connect the centromeres to the poles. 
 3. **Anaphase I**: The homologous chromosomes separate and move toward opposite poles of the cell. Sister chromatids remain joined at their centromeres. 
 4. **Telophase I**: Two new nuclei form at the poles, each containing one pair of homologous chromosomes (a dyad). Cytokinesis follows, resulting in two daughter cells. Arrows indicate the process continues to Meiosis II.

Figure 10.3 Stages of Meiosis I

**Anaphase I:** The homologous chromosomes separate, while sister chromatids remain associated at their centromeres (Figure 10.3).

**Telophase I:** The nuclear membrane and nucleolus reappear, cytokinesis follows and this is called as dyad of cells (Figure 10.3). Although in many cases the chromosomes do undergo some dispersion, they do not reach the extremely extended state of the interphase nucleus. The stage between the two meiotic divisions is called interkinesis and is generally short lived. There is no replication of DNA during interkinesis. Interkinesis is followed by prophase II, a much simpler prophase than prophase I.

kebo110-s13.content:
**Prophase II:** Meiosis II is initiated immediately after cytokinesis, usually before the chromosomes have fully elongated. In contrast to meiosis I, meiosis II resembles a normal mitosis. The nuclear membrane disappears by the end of prophase II (Figure 10.4). The chromosomes again become compact.

**Metaphase II:** At this stage, the chromosomes align at the equator and the microtubules from opposite poles of the spindle get attached to the kinetochores (Figure 10.4) of sister chromatids.

**Anaphase II:** It begins with the simultaneous splitting of the centromere of each chromosome (which was holding the sister chromatids together), allowing them to move toward opposite poles of the cell (Figure 10.4) by shortening of microtubules attached to kinetochores.

The diagram shows the progression of Meiosis II. It begins with two cells from Meiosis I. The first row shows the stages for one cell: Prophase II (chromosomes condense), Metaphase II (chromosomes align at the equator), Anaphase II (sister chromatids separate), and Telophase II (nuclear envelopes form). The second row shows the same stages for the second cell. The final result is four haploid daughter cells, each containing one chromosome of either the original maternal (pink) or paternal (blue) origin.

**Figure 10.4** Stages of Meiosis II

**Telophase II:** Meiosis ends with telophase II, in which the two groups of chromosomes once again get enclosed by a nuclear envelope; cytokinesis follows resulting in the formation of tetrad of cells i.e., four haploid daughter cells (Figure 10.4).

kebo110-s14.content: Meiosis is the mechanism by which conservation of specific chromosome number of each species is achieved across generations in sexually reproducing organisms, even though the process, per se, paradoxically, results in reduction of chromosome number by half. It also increases the genetic variability in the population of organisms from one generation to the next. Variations are very important for the process of evolution.

kebo110-s15.content:
According to the cell theory, cells arise from preexisting cells. The process by which this occurs is called cell division. Any sexually reproducing organism starts its life cycle from a single-celled zygote. Cell division does not stop with the formation of the mature organism but continues throughout its life cycle.

The stages through which a cell passes from one division to the next is called the cell cycle. Cell cycle is divided into two phases called (i) Interphase – a period of preparation for cell division, and (ii) Mitosis (M phase) – the actual period of cell division. Interphase is further subdivided into  $G_1$ , S and  $G_2$ .  $G_1$  phase is the period when the cell grows and carries out normal metabolism. Most of the organelle duplication also occurs during this phase. S phase marks the phase of DNA replication and chromosome duplication.  $G_2$  phase is the period of cytoplasmic growth. Mitosis is also divided into four stages namely prophase, metaphase, anaphase and telophase. Chromosome condensation occurs during prophase. Simultaneously, the centrioles move to the opposite poles. The nuclear envelope and the nucleolus disappear and the spindle fibres start appearing. Metaphase is marked by the alignment of chromosomes at the equatorial plate. During anaphase the centromeres divide and the chromatids start moving towards the two opposite poles. Once the chromatids reach the two poles, the chromosomal elongation starts, nucleolus and the nuclear membrane reappear. This stage is called the telophase. Nuclear division is then followed by the cytoplasmic division and is called cytokinesis. Mitosis thus, is the equational division in which the chromosome number of the parent is conserved in the daughter cell.

In contrast to mitosis, meiosis occurs in the diploid cells, which are destined to form gametes. It is called the reduction division since it reduces the chromosome number by half while making the gametes. In sexual reproduction when the two gametes fuse the chromosome number is restored to the value in the parent. Meiosis is divided into two phases – meiosis I and meiosis II. In the first meiotic division the homologous chromosomes pair to form bivalents, and undergo crossing over. Meiosis I has a long prophase, which is divided further into five phases. These are leptotene, zygotene, pachytene, diplotene and diakinesis. During metaphase I the bivalents arrange on the equatorial plate. This is followed by anaphase I in which homologous chromosomes move to the opposite poles with both their chromatids. Each pole receives half the chromosome number of the parent cell. In telophase I, the nuclear membrane and nucleolus reappear. Meiosis II is similar to mitosis. During anaphase II the sister chromatids separate. Thus at the end of meiosis four haploid cells are formed.

kebo110-d000.content: Diagram: Stages of Meiosis II (diagram). Caption: The diagram shows the progression of Meiosis II. It begins with two cells from Meiosis I. The first row shows the stages for one cell: Prophase II (chromosomes condense), Metaphase II (chromosomes align at the equator), Anaphase II (sister chromatids separate), and Telophase II (nuclear envelopes form). The second row shows the same stages for the second cell. The final result is four haploid daughter cells, each containing one chromosome of either the original maternal (pink) or paternal (blue) origin. Description: This diagram should represent Meiosis II, the equational division that occurs in each of the two haploid cells produced by Meiosis I. Each cell enters Prophase II with chromosomes already duplicated, so each chromosome consists of two sister chromatids joined at a centromere; the nuclear envelope, if present, breaks down and a spindle apparatus forms. In Metaphase II, the chromosomes align singly at the equatorial plate, with spindle fibers from opposite poles attached to the kinetochores of sister chromatids. In Anaphase II, the centromeres divide and sister chromatids separate, moving to opposite poles as daughter chromosomes. In Telophase II, chromosomes reach the poles, decondense, nuclear envelopes re-form around each set, and cytokinesis completes the division. The final outcome is four genetically distinct haploid daughter cells, each containing one set of chromosomes, with each chromosome now consisting of a single chromatid. If the parent cell is diploid with homologous chromosomes of maternal and paternal origin, Meiosis II separates sister chromatids, not homologous chromosomes, and does not reduce chromosome number further. Labels: Prophase II, Metaphase II, Anaphase II, Telophase II, Spindle fibers, Centromere, Sister chromatids, Equatorial plate, Daughter cells. NEET relevance: Identify the stage where sister chromatids separate: Anaphase II.; Differentiate Meiosis I from Meiosis II.; State the ploidy and chromosome number of the final products.. Board relevance: Draw and label the stages of Meiosis II.; Explain Prophase II, Metaphase II, Anaphase II, and Telophase II.; Show the movement of sister chromatids and formation of four haploid cells.. Exam-critical labels: Prophase II, Metaphase II, Anaphase II, Telophase II, Spindle fibers, Centromere, Sister chromatids, Equatorial plate. Common mistakes: Homologous chromosomes separate in Meiosis II (correct: Sister chromatids separate in Meiosis II); Chromosomes align randomly in Metaphase II (correct: Duplicated chromosomes align singly at the equatorial plate in Metaphase II); The final cells are diploid (correct: The final cells are haploid).

kebo110-d001.content: Diagram: cell cycle and cell division (diagram). Caption: A circular diagram of the cell cycle. The circle is divided into four main segments: G1 (yellow), S (yellow), G2 (yellow), and M (black). The M phase is further subdivided into Prophase, Metaphase, Anaphase, Telophase, and Cytokinesis (all in shades of purple). A red arrow points clockwise from the G1 phase. At the top of the circle, there are two small circles representing daughter cells, with a curved arrow pointing from the M phase to them, labeled G0. Description: This diagram represents the eukaryotic cell cycle as a continuous circular sequence of events in which a cell grows, replicates its DNA, and divides. The cycle is conventionally divided into interphase and M phase. Interphase occupies most of the cycle and consists of G1 phase (cell growth, active metabolism, synthesis of RNA, proteins, and organelles), S phase (DNA replication and duplication of chromosomes), and G2 phase (further growth and preparation for mitosis, including synthesis of proteins needed for spindle formation). The M phase is the shortest phase and includes mitosis followed by cytokinesis. Mitosis is subdivided into prophase, metaphase, anaphase, and telophase, during which duplicated chromosomes condense, align at the equator, separate into sister chromatids, and re-form daughter nuclei. Cytokinesis completes cell division by partitioning the cytoplasm into two daughter cells. A side branch from G1 may lead to G0, a quiescent or resting state in which cells exit the cycle temporarily or permanently and do not proceed to DNA replication or division. The diagram should therefore show a clockwise progression from G1 to S to G2 to M, with mitotic subphases arranged sequentially within M phase and a separate offshoot to G0 from G1. Labels: G1 phase, S phase, G2 phase, M phase, Prophase, Metaphase, Anaphase, Telophase, Cytokinesis, G0 phase. NEET relevance: Order of phases in the cell cycle and mitosis; Difference between interphase and M phase; Events of G1, S, and G2. Board relevance: Draw and label the cell cycle diagram; Explain the phases of interphase and mitosis; Differentiate mitosis from cytokinesis. Exam-critical labels: G1 phase, S phase, G2 phase, M phase, Prophase, Metaphase, Anaphase, Telophase. Common mistakes: Putting G0 inside the main circular cycle as if it were a regular phase (correct: G0 is an off-cycle quiescent state branching from G1); Treating cytokinesis as a separate phase outside M phase (correct: Cytokinesis is part of M phase, occurring after nuclear division); Showing interphase as a single phase without G1, S, and G2 (correct: Interphase consists of G1, S, and G2).

kebo110-d002.content: Diagram: stages of meiosis I during cell division (diagram). Caption: The diagram illustrates the four stages of Meiosis I in a sequential flow from left to right. 1. **Prophase I**: A cell containing two pairs of homologous chromosomes (one pair in blue, one in pink) that have exchanged segments (crossing over). 2. **Metaphase I**: The homologous chromosome pairs align at the metaphase plate (equator) of the cell. Spindle fibers (yellow lines) connect the centromeres to the poles. 3. **Anaphase I**: The homologous chromosomes separate and move toward opposite poles of the cell. Sister chromatids remain joined at their centromeres. Description: This diagram should represent Meiosis I, the reductional division in which a diploid germ cell undergoes one round of DNA replication followed by separation of homologous chromosomes, producing two haploid daughter cells whose chromosomes are still duplicated. In Prophase I, homologous chromosomes undergo synapsis to form bivalents or tetrads, and crossing over occurs between non-sister chromatids at chiasmata; the nuclear envelope breaks down and the spindle begins to form. In Metaphase I, the bivalents align at the equatorial plate, with each homolog attached to spindle fibers from opposite poles, while sister chromatids remain joined at their centromeres. In Anaphase I, homologous chromosomes separate and move to opposite poles, but centromeres do not divide and sister chromatids stay together. In Telophase I, chromosomes reach the poles and the cell undergoes cytokinesis to form two haploid daughter cells, each containing one chromosome from each homologous pair, still as dyads. The diagram must emphasize that Meiosis I reduces chromosome number from diploid to haploid, but does not separate sister chromatids. Labels: Prophase I, Metaphase I, Anaphase I, Telophase I, Homologous chromosomes, Bivalent (tetrad), Chiasma, Spindle fibers, Equatorial plate, Pole, Cytokinesis, Haploid daughter cells. NEET relevance: Identify the stage of meiosis I from chromosome arrangement; Differentiate metaphase I from mitotic metaphase; Explain why meiosis I is reductional division. Board relevance: Draw and label stages of meiosis I; Explain synapsis, crossing over, and tetrad formation; Describe metaphase I alignment of bivalents at the equator. Exam-critical labels: Prophase I, Metaphase I, Anaphase I, Telophase I, Homologous chromosomes, Bivalent/Tetrad, Spindle fibers, Equatorial plate. Common mistakes: Metaphase 1 (correct: Metaphase I); Anaphase 1 (correct: Anaphase I); Telophase 1 shows sister chromatids separating (correct: Telophase I shows homologous chromosomes already separated, while sister chromatids remain joined).

kebo110-d003.content: Diagram: stages of mitosis showing early prophase, late prophase, transition to metaphase, and metaphase (diagram). Caption: The figure consists of four circular diagrams arranged vertically, each showing a cell at a different stage of mitosis. The top two diagrams are labeled (a) and the bottom two are labeled (b). 1. **Early Prophase**: The top diagram shows a cell with a distinct nucleus containing two tangled chromatin fibers (one blue, one red). The nuclear envelope is represented by a dashed yellow line. 2. **Late Prophase**: The second diagram shows the nucleus with a more fragmented nuclear envelope (dashed yellow line). Inside, two chromosomes (one blue, one red) are visible. 3. **Transition to Metaphase**: The third diagram shows the nuclear envelope completely gone. Spindle fibers (red lines) are forming from a central pole (orange dot). Two chromosomes (one blue, one red) are being pulled towards the center. Description: This diagram represents successive stages of mitosis in an animal cell, specifically early prophase, late prophase (prometaphase), transition to metaphase, and metaphase. In early prophase, chromatin begins to condense into visible chromosomes while the nucleolus disappears and the nuclear envelope starts to break down; centrosomes have duplicated and are moving apart, initiating spindle formation. In late prophase, chromosomes are more distinctly condensed, each consisting of two sister chromatids joined at a centromere, and the nuclear envelope is fragmented or nearly absent; spindle microtubules continue to organize from opposite poles. In the transition to metaphase, the nuclear envelope is fully disassembled, spindle fibers from opposite poles attach to kinetochores at the centromeres, and chromosomes begin active movement toward the equatorial plane. In metaphase, all chromosomes are maximally condensed and aligned at the metaphase plate (equatorial plane), with sister chromatids oriented toward opposite spindle poles; this alignment ensures accurate segregation in anaphase. The diagram should emphasize the progressive loss of the nucleus, increasing chromosome condensation, formation of the mitotic spindle, and chromosome alignment at the cell equator. Labels: Early Prophase, Late Prophase, Transition to Metaphase, Metaphase, Chromosomes, Sister chromatids, Centromere / kinetochore region, Spindle fibers, Spindle poles / centrosomes, Nuclear envelope, Nucleolus, Metaphase plate.
