NEET relevance: Sequence of mitotic stages and the hallmark events of each stage; Difference between prophase, prometaphase/transition to metaphase, and metaphase; Role of spindle fibers and metaphase plate. Board relevance: Draw and label stages of mitosis; Explain what happens in prophase and metaphase; Differentiate early prophase, late prophase, and metaphase. Exam-critical labels: Early Prophase, Late Prophase, Transition to Metaphase, Metaphase, Chromosomes, Spindle fibers, Centrosomes / spindle poles, Nuclear envelope. Common mistakes: Calling the condensed chromosome rods in late prophase 'chromatin' (correct: They are condensed chromosomes); Labeling the spindle poles as 'centrioles' in all contexts (correct: They are centrosomes or spindle poles); Showing a nuclear envelope intact in metaphase (correct: The nuclear envelope is absent by metaphase).

kebo110-d004.content: Diagram: stages of mitosis/cell division in a cell, showing anaphase, telophase, and interphase (diagram). Caption: The figure shows three stages of mitosis in a plant cell, labeled (c), (d), and (e). (c) Anaphase: The cell is elongated, and sister chromatids (chromosomes) are being pulled towards opposite poles by spindle fibers. There are two distinct groups of chromosomes at each pole. (d) Telophase: Two new nuclei are forming at opposite poles, each containing a set of chromosomes. The cell membrane is beginning to pinch in the middle, indicating the start of cytokinesis. (e) Interphase: Two separate daughter cells are shown, each with a distinct nucleus containing chromatin material. Description: This diagram represents three successive stages of mitosis in a plant cell: anaphase, telophase, and interphase. In anaphase, the sister chromatids have separated at the centromeres and are being pulled by spindle fibres toward opposite poles of the elongated cell; each chromatid is now an individual daughter chromosome, typically appearing V-, J-, or rod-shaped depending on centromere position. In telophase, the separated chromosomes have reached the poles, the spindle apparatus begins to disassemble, and nuclear envelopes re-form around each chromosome set to produce two daughter nuclei; in plant cells, cytokinesis proceeds by cell plate formation in the equatorial region, which later develops into a new cell wall. In interphase, the cell is not actively dividing; each daughter cell contains an intact nucleus with diffuse chromatin rather than distinct chromosomes, a visible nucleolus, and a normal nuclear envelope. Interphase is the preparatory phase in which DNA replication occurs during S phase and the cell grows and synthesizes proteins needed for the next division cycle. Labels: Anaphase, Chromosomes / daughter chromosomes, Spindle fibres, Spindle poles / centrosomal poles, Telophase, Daughter nuclei, Cell plate, Interphase, Nucleus, Chromatin, Nuclear envelope. NEET relevance: Identify the stage from chromosome arrangement and spindle behavior; Differentiate anaphase, telophase, and interphase; Recognize plant cytokinesis by cell plate formation. Board relevance: Draw and label stages of mitosis; Explain anaphase and telophase with diagrams; Differentiate mitosis stages in plant cells. Exam-critical labels: Anaphase, Telophase, Interphase, Chromosomes / daughter chromosomes, Spindle fibres, Daughter nuclei, Cell plate, Nuclear envelope. Common mistakes: Calling the separated chromosome groups in anaphase 'chromatin' (correct: They are daughter chromosomes (formerly sister chromatids) being pulled to opposite poles); Showing cytokinesis as a cleavage furrow in a plant cell (correct: Plant cells form a cell plate that becomes the new cell wall); Depicting interphase as a stage with visible condensed chromosomes (correct: Interphase should show diffuse chromatin and an intact nucleus).

kebo111-s00.section_title: Introduction + PLANT PHYSIOLOGY + Chapter 11 + Chapter 12 + Chapter 13
kebo111-s00.content:
Photosynthesis in Higher Plants

Respiration in Plants

Plant Growth and Development

The description of structure and variation of living organisms over a period of time, ended up as two, apparently irreconcilable perspectives on biology. The two perspectives essentially rested on two levels of organisation of life forms and phenomena. One described at organismic and above level of organisation while the second described at cellular and molecular level of organisation. The first resulted in ecology and related disciplines. The second resulted in physiology and biochemistry. Description of physiological processes, in flowering plants as an example, is what is given in the chapters in this unit. The processes of photosynthesis, respiration and ultimately plant growth and development are described in molecular terms but in the context of cellular activities and even at organism level. Wherever appropriate, the relation of the physiological processes to environment is also discussed.

MELVIN CALVIN born in Minnesota in April, 1911, received his Ph.D. in Chemistry from the University of Minnesota. He served as Professor of Chemistry at the University of California, Berkeley.

Just after world war II, when the world was under shock after the Hiroshima-Nagasaki bombings, and seeing the ill-effects of radio-activity, Calvin and co-workers put radio-activity to beneficial use. He along with J.A. Bassham studied reactions in green plants forming sugar and other substances from raw materials like carbon dioxide, water and minerals by labelling the carbon dioxide with  $C^{14}$ . Calvin proposed that plants change light energy to chemical energy by transferring an electron in an organised array of pigment molecules and other substances. The mapping of the pathway of carbon assimilation in photosynthesis earned him Nobel Prize in 1961.

The principles of photosynthesis as established by Calvin are, at present, being used in studies on renewable resource for energy and materials and basic studies in solar energy research.

kebo111-s05.content:
- 11.1 *What do we Know?*
- 11.2 *Early Experiments*
- 11.3 *Where does Photosynthesis take place?*
- 11.4 *How many Pigments are involved in Photosynthesis?*
- 11.5 *What is Light Reaction?*
- 11.6 *The Electron Transport*
- 11.7 *Where are the ATP and NADPH Used?*
- 11.8 *The C<sub>4</sub> Pathway*
- 11.9 *Photorespiration*
- 11.10 *Factors affecting Photosynthesis*

All animals including human beings depend on plants for their food. Have you ever wondered from where plants get their food? Green plants, in fact, have to make or rather synthesise the food they need and all other organisms depend on them for their needs. The green plants make or rather synthesise the food they need through photosynthesis and are therefore called autotrophs. You have already learnt that the autotrophic nutrition is found only in plants and all other organisms that depend on the green plants for food are heterotrophs. Green plants carry out 'photosynthesis', a physico-chemical process by which they use light energy to drive the synthesis of organic compounds. Ultimately, all living forms on earth depend on sunlight for energy. The use of energy from sunlight by plants doing photosynthesis is the basis of life on earth. Photosynthesis is important due to two reasons: it is the primary source of all food on earth. It is also responsible for the release of oxygen into the atmosphere by green plants. *Have you ever thought what would happen if there were no oxygen to breath?* This chapter focusses on the structure of the photosynthetic machinery and the various reactions that transform light energy into chemical energy.

kebo111-s07.content:
Let us try to find out what we already know about photosynthesis. Some simple experiments you may have done in the earlier classes have shown that chlorophyll (green pigment of the leaf), light and CO<sub>2</sub> are required for photosynthesis to occur.

You may have carried out the experiment to look for starch formation in two leaves – a variegated leaf or a leaf that was partially covered with black paper, and exposed to light. On testing these leaves for the presence of starch it was clear that photosynthesis occurred only in the green parts of the leaves in the presence of light.

Figure 11.1 Priestley's experiment

Another experiment you may have carried out where a part of a leaf is enclosed in a test tube containing some KOH soaked cotton (which absorbs  $\text{CO}_2$ ), while the other half is exposed to air. The setup is then placed in light for some time. On testing for the presence of starch later in the two parts of the leaf, you must have found that the exposed part of the leaf tested positive for starch while the portion that was in the tube, tested negative. This showed that  $\text{CO}_2$  was required for photosynthesis. *Can you explain how this conclusion could be drawn?*

kebo111-s08.content:
It is interesting to learn about those simple experiments that led to a gradual development in our understanding of photosynthesis.

Joseph Priestley (1733-1804) in 1770 performed a series of experiments that revealed the essential role of air in the growth of green plants. Priestley, you may recall, discovered oxygen in 1774. Priestley observed that a candle burning in a closed space – a bell jar, soon gets extinguished (Figure 11.1 a, b, c, d). Similarly, a mouse would soon suffocate in a closed space. He concluded that a burning candle or an animal that breathe the air,

both somehow, damage the air. But when he placed a mint plant in the same bell jar, he found that the mouse stayed alive and the candle continued to burn. Priestley hypothesised as follows: Plants restore to the air whatever breathing animals and burning candles remove.

Can you imagine how Priestley would have conducted the experiment using a candle and a plant? Remember, he would need to rekindle the candle to test whether it burns after a few days. *How many different ways can you think of to light the candle without disturbing the set-up?*

Using a similar setup as the one used by Priestley, but by placing it once in the dark and once in the sunlight, Jan Ingenhousz (1730-1799) showed that sunlight is essential for the plant process that somehow purifies the air fouled by burning candles or breathing animals. Ingenhousz in an elegant experiment with an aquatic plant showed that in bright sunlight, small bubbles were formed around the green parts while in the dark they did not. Later he identified these bubbles to be of oxygen. Hence he showed that it is only the green part of the plants that could release oxygen.

It was not until about 1854 that Julius von Sachs provided evidence for production of glucose when plants grow. Glucose is usually stored as starch. His later studies showed that the green substance in plants (chlorophyll as we know it now) is located in special bodies (later called chloroplasts) within plant cells. He found that the green parts in plants is where glucose is made, and that the glucose is usually stored as starch.

Now consider the interesting experiments done by T.W Engelmann (1843 – 1909). Using a prism he split light into its spectral components and then illuminated a green alga, *Cladophora*, placed in a suspension of aerobic bacteria. The bacteria were used to detect the sites of O<sub>2</sub> evolution. He observed that the bacteria accumulated mainly in the region of blue and red light of the split spectrum. A first action spectrum of photosynthesis was thus described. It resembles roughly the absorption spectra of chlorophyll *a* and *b* (discussed in section 11.4).

By the middle of the nineteenth century the key features of plant photosynthesis were known, namely, that plants could use light energy to make carbohydrates from CO<sub>2</sub> and water. The empirical equation representing the total process of photosynthesis for oxygen evolving organisms was then understood as:

$$\text{CO}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O} \xrightarrow{\text{Light}} [\text{CH}_2\text{O}] + \text{O}_2$$

$$2\text{H}_2\text{A} + \text{CO}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{Light}} 2\text{A} + \text{CH}_2\text{O} + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$

In green plants H<sub>2</sub>O is the hydrogen donor and is oxidised to O<sub>2</sub>. Some organisms do not release O<sub>2</sub> during photosynthesis. When H<sub>2</sub>S, instead is the hydrogen donor for purple and green sulphur bacteria, the 'oxidation' product is sulphur or sulphate depending on the organism and not O<sub>2</sub>. Hence, he inferred that the O<sub>2</sub> evolved by the green plant comes from H<sub>2</sub>O, not from carbon dioxide. This was later proved by using radioisotopic techniques. The correct equation, that would represent the overall process of photosynthesis is therefore:

where C<sub>6</sub> H<sub>12</sub> O<sub>6</sub> represents glucose. The O<sub>2</sub> released is from water; this was proved using radio isotope techniques. Note that this is not a single

reaction but description of a multistep process called photosynthesis. *Can you explain why twelve molecules of water as substrate are used in the equation given above?*

kebo111-s09.content:
You would of course answer: in 'the green leaf' or 'in the chloroplasts', based on what you earlier read in Chapter 8. You are definitely right. Photosynthesis does take place in the green leaves of plants but it does so also in other green parts of the plants. *Can you name some other parts where you think photosynthesis may occur?*

You would recollect from previous unit that the mesophyll cells in the leaves, have a large number of chloroplasts. Usually the chloroplasts align themselves along the walls of the mesophyll cells, such that they get the optimum quantity of the incident light. *When do you think the chloroplasts will be aligned with their flat surfaces parallel to the walls? When would they be perpendicular to the incident light?*

You have studied the structure of chloroplast in Chapter 8. Within the chloroplast there is membranous system consisting of grana, the stroma lamellae, and the matrix stroma (Figure 11.2). There is a clear division of labour within the chloroplast. The membrane system is responsible for trapping the light energy and also for the synthesis of ATP and NADPH. In stroma, enzymatic reactions synthesise sugar, which in turn forms starch. The former set of reactions, since they are directly light driven are called **light reactions** (photochemical reactions). The latter are not directly light driven but are dependent on the products of light reactions (ATP and NADPH). Hence, to distinguish the latter they are called, by convention, as **dark reactions** (carbon reactions). However, this should not be construed to mean that they occur in darkness or that they are not light-dependent.

The diagram shows a cross-section of a chloroplast, represented as an oval with a yellow border. Inside, there are several stacks of green, flattened discs called grana. These grana are interconnected by horizontal green tubes called stromal lamellae. The internal fluid is labeled as stroma. Other structures shown and labeled include:

- Outer membrane**: The outermost boundary of the chloroplast.
- Inner membrane**: The boundary layer just inside the outer membrane.
- Stromal lamella**: Horizontal green structures connecting the grana.
- Grana**: Stacks of green disc-like structures.
- Stroma**: The fluid-filled matrix of the chloroplast.
- Ribosomes**: Small dark dots scattered throughout the stroma.
- Starch granule**: A large, concentric, orange-colored circular structure.
- Lipid droplet**: A small, dark, circular structure.

**Figure 11.2** Diagrammatic representation of an electron micrograph of a section of chloroplast

kebo111-s10.section_title: 11.4 HOW MANY TYPES OF PIGMENTS ARE INVOLVED IN PHOTOSYNTHESIS?
kebo111-s10.content:
Looking at plants have you ever wondered why and how there are so many shades of green in their leaves – even in the same plant? We can look for an answer to this question by trying to separate the leaf pigments of any green plant through paper chromatography. A chromatographic separation of the leaf pigments shows that the colour that we see in leaves is not due to a single pigment but due to four pigments: **Chlorophyll a** (bright or blue green in the chromatogram), **chlorophyll b** (yellow green), **xanthophylls** (yellow) and **carotenoids** (yellow to yellow-orange). Let us now see what roles various pigments play in photosynthesis.

Pigments are substances that have an ability to absorb light, at specific wavelengths. *Can you guess which is the most abundant plant pigment in the world?* Let us study the graph showing the ability of chlorophyll a pigment to absorb lights of different wavelengths (Figure 11.3 a). Of course, you are familiar with the wavelength of the visible spectrum of light as well as the VIBGYOR.

From Figure 11.3a can you determine the wavelength (colour of light) at which chlorophyll a shows the maximum absorption? Does it show another absorption peak at any other wavelengths too? If yes, which one?

Now look at Figure 11.3b showing the wavelengths at which maximum photosynthesis occurs in a plant. Can you see that the wavelengths at which there is maximum absorption by chlorophyll a, i.e., in the blue and the red regions, also shows higher rate of photosynthesis. Hence, we can conclude that chlorophyll a is the chief pigment associated with photosynthesis. But by looking at Figure 11.3c can you say that there is a complete one-to-one overlap between the absorption spectrum of chlorophyll a and the action spectrum of photosynthesis?

Figure 11.3 consists of three vertically stacked graphs, labeled (a), (b), and (c), sharing a common x-axis representing the 'Wavelength of light in nanometres (nm)' ranging from 400 to 700 nm.

- Graph (a):** The y-axis is 'Absorbance of light by chloroplast pigments'. It shows three curves: Chlorophyll a (green), Chlorophyll b (blue), and Carotenoids (yellow). Chlorophyll a has peaks at approximately 430 nm and 660 nm. Chlorophyll b has peaks at approximately 450 nm and 640 nm. Carotenoids show a broad peak between 450 nm and 550 nm.
- Graph (b):** The y-axis is 'Rate of photosynthesis (measured by O<sub>2</sub> release)'. The curve shows two prominent peaks at approximately 430 nm and 660 nm, corresponding to the absorption peaks of chlorophyll a.
- Graph (c):** The y-axis is 'Light absorbed'. It superimposes two curves: 'Rate of photosynthesis' (black line) and 'Absorption' (blue line). The two lines show a very close one-to-one overlap, with major peaks at approximately 430 nm and 660 nm.

**Figure 11.3a** Graph showing the absorption spectrum of chlorophyll a, b and the carotenoids

**Figure 11.3b** Graph showing action spectrum of photosynthesis

**Figure 11.3c** Graph showing action spectrum of photosynthesis superimposed on absorption spectrum of chlorophyll a

These graphs, together, show that most of the photosynthesis takes place in the blue and red regions of the spectrum; some photosynthesis does take place at the other wavelengths of the visible spectrum. Let us see how this happens. Though chlorophyll is the major pigment responsible for trapping light, other thylakoid pigments like chlorophyll *b*, xanthophylls and carotenoids, which are called accessory pigments, also absorb light and transfer the energy to chlorophyll *a*. Indeed, they not only enable a wider range of wavelength of incoming light to be utilised for photosynthesis but also protect chlorophyll *a* from photo-oxidation.

kebo111-s11.content:
The diagram illustrates the structure of a light harvesting complex. It consists of a cluster of pigment molecules, represented by green circles. A red arrow labeled 'Photon' indicates the absorption of light by one of these molecules. Energy is transferred through the pigment molecules to a central 'Reaction centre', which is also a pigment molecule. From the reaction centre, a large blue arrow points upwards to a yellow oval labeled 'Primary acceptor', representing the transfer of energy or electrons to the acceptor.

**Figure 11.4** The light harvesting complex

Light reactions or the 'Photochemical' phase include light absorption, water splitting, oxygen release, and the formation of high-energy chemical intermediates, ATP and NADPH. Several protein complexes are involved in the process. The pigments are organised into two discrete photochemical **light harvesting complexes (LHC)** within the **Photosystem I (PS I)** and **Photosystem II (PS II)**. These are named in the sequence of their discovery, and not in the sequence in which they function during the light reaction. The LHC are made up of hundreds of pigment molecules bound to proteins. Each photosystem has all the pigments (except one molecule of chlorophyll *a*) forming a light harvesting system also called **antennae** (Figure 11.4). These pigments help to make photosynthesis more efficient by absorbing different wavelengths of light. The single chlorophyll *a* molecule forms the **reaction centre**. The reaction centre is different in both the photosystems. In PS I the reaction centre chlorophyll *a* has an absorption peak at 700 nm, hence is called **P700**, while in PS II it has absorption maxima at 680 nm, and is called **P680**.

kebo111-s12.content:
In photosystem II the reaction centre chlorophyll *a* absorbs 680 nm wavelength of red light causing electrons to become excited and jump into an orbit farther from the atomic nucleus. These electrons are picked up by an electron acceptor which passes them to an **electrons transport**

**system consisting of cytochromes** (Figure 11.5). This movement of electrons is downhill, in terms of an oxidation-reduction or redox potential scale. The electrons are not used up as they pass through the electron transport chain, but are passed on to the pigments of photosystem PS I. Simultaneously, electrons in the reaction centre of PS I are also excited when they receive red light of wavelength 700 nm and are transferred to another acceptor molecule that has a greater redox potential. These electrons then are moved downhill again, this time to a molecule of energy-rich NADP<sup>+</sup>. The addition of these electrons reduces NADP<sup>+</sup> to NADPH + H<sup>+</sup>. This whole scheme of transfer of electrons, starting from the PS II, uphill to the acceptor, down the electron transport chain to PS I, excitation of electrons, transfer to another acceptor, and finally down hill to NADP<sup>+</sup> reducing it to NADPH + H<sup>+</sup> is called the **Z scheme**, due to its characteristic shape (Figure 11.5). This shape is formed when all the carriers are placed in a sequence on a redox potential scale.

![Diagram of the Z scheme of light reaction of photosynthesis. The diagram shows the flow of electrons from water splitting at Photosystem II (PS II) to Photosystem I (PS I) and finally to NADP+. Light energy excites electrons in both PS II and PS I. At PS II, water is split into 2H+, [O], and 2e-. Electrons from water replace those lost by PS II. The excited electrons from PS II pass through an electron transport system, which produces ATP from ADP+iP. The electrons then pass to PS I, where they are re-excited by light and transferred to an electron acceptor, which reduces NADP+ to NADPH. The overall process is represented by a zigzag line (Z shape) on a redox potential scale.](b3baf3a29b67c7425d2562ddbc52f0cc_img.jpg)

Diagram of the Z scheme of light reaction of photosynthesis. The diagram shows the flow of electrons from water splitting at Photosystem II (PS II) to Photosystem I (PS I) and finally to NADP+. Light energy excites electrons in both PS II and PS I. At PS II, water is split into 2H+, [O], and 2e-. Electrons from water replace those lost by PS II. The excited electrons from PS II pass through an electron transport system, which produces ATP from ADP+iP. The electrons then pass to PS I, where they are re-excited by light and transferred to an electron acceptor, which reduces NADP+ to NADPH. The overall process is represented by a zigzag line (Z shape) on a redox potential scale.

**Figure 11.5** Z scheme of light reaction

kebo111-s13.content:
You would then ask, *How does PS II supply electrons continuously?* The electrons that were moved from photosystem II must be replaced. This is achieved by electrons available due to splitting of water. The splitting of water is associated with the PS II; water is split into 2H<sup>+</sup>, [O] and electrons. This creates oxygen, one of the net products of photosynthesis. The electrons needed to replace those removed from photosystem I are provided by photosystem II.

$$2\text{H}_2\text{O} \longrightarrow 4\text{H}^+ + \text{O}_2 + 4\text{e}^-$$

We need to emphasise here that the water splitting complex is associated with the PS II, which itself is physically located on the inner side of the membrane of the thylakoid. *Then, where are the protons and O<sub>2</sub> formed likely to be released – in the lumen? or on the outer side of the membrane?*

kebo111-s14.content:
Living organisms have the capability of extracting energy from oxidisable substances and store this in the form of bond energy. Special substances like ATP, carry this energy in their chemical bonds. The process through which

**Figure 11.6** Cyclic photophosphorylation

ATP is synthesised by cells (in mitochondria and chloroplasts) is named phosphorylation. Photo-phosphorylation is the synthesis of ATP from ADP and inorganic phosphate in the presence of light. When the two photosystems work in a series, first PS II and then the PSI, a process called non-cyclic photo-phosphorylation occurs. The two photosystems are connected through an electron transport chain, as seen earlier – in the Z scheme. Both ATP and NADPH + H<sup>+</sup> are synthesised by this kind of electron flow (Figure 11.5).

When only PS I is functional, the electron is circulated within the photosystem and the phosphorylation occurs due to cyclic flow of electrons (Figure 11.6). A possible location where this could be happening is in the stroma

lamellae. While the membrane or lamellae of the grana have both PS I and PS II the stroma lamellae membranes lack PS II as well as NADP reductase enzyme. The excited electron does not pass on to NADP<sup>+</sup> but is cycled back to the PS I complex through the electron transport chain (Figure 11.6). The cyclic flow hence, results only in the synthesis of ATP, but not of NADPH + H<sup>+</sup>. Cyclic photophosphorylation also occurs when only light of wavelengths beyond 680 nm are available for excitation.

kebo111-s15.content:
Let us now try and understand how actually ATP is synthesised in the chloroplast. The chemiosmotic hypothesis has been put forward to explain the mechanism. Like in respiration, in photosynthesis too, ATP synthesis is linked to development of a proton gradient across a membrane. This time these are the membranes of thylakoid. There is one difference though, here the proton accumulation is towards the inside of the membrane, i.e., in the lumen. In respiration, protons accumulate in the intermembrane space of the mitochondria when electrons move through the ETS (Chapter 12).

Let us understand what causes the proton gradient across the membrane. We need to consider again the processes that take place during the activation of electrons and their transport to determine the steps that cause a proton gradient to develop (Figure 11.7).

- Since splitting of the water molecule takes place on the inner side of the membrane, the protons or hydrogen ions that are produced by the splitting of water accumulate within the lumen of the thylakoids.

**Figure 11.7** ATP synthesis through chemiosmosis

- (b) As electrons move through the photosystems, protons are transported across the membrane. This happens because the primary acceptor of electron which is located towards the outer side of the membrane transfers its electron not to an electron carrier but to an H carrier. Hence, this molecule removes a proton from the stroma while transporting an electron. When this molecule passes on its electron to the electron carrier on the inner side of the membrane, the proton is released into the inner side or the lumen side of the membrane.
- (c) The NADP reductase enzyme is located on the stroma side of the membrane. Along with electrons that come from the acceptor of electrons of PS I, protons are necessary for the reduction of NADP<sup>+</sup> to NADPH+ H<sup>+</sup>. These protons are also removed from the stroma.

Hence, within the chloroplast, protons in the stroma decrease in number, while in the lumen there is accumulation of protons. This creates a proton gradient across the thylakoid membrane as well as a measurable decrease in pH in the lumen.

Why are we so interested in the proton gradient? This gradient is important because it is the breakdown of this gradient that leads to the synthesis of ATP. The gradient is broken down due to the movement of protons across the membrane to the stroma through the transmembrane

channel of the  $CF_0$  of the ATP synthase. The ATP synthase enzyme consists of two parts: one called the  $CF_0$  is embedded in the thylakoid membrane and forms a transmembrane channel that carries out facilitated diffusion of protons across the membrane. The other portion is called  $CF_1$  and protrudes on the outer surface of the thylakoid membrane on the side that faces the stroma. The break down of the gradient provides enough energy to cause a conformational change in the  $CF_1$  particle of the ATP synthase, which makes the enzyme synthesise several molecules of energy-packed ATP.

Chemiosmosis requires a membrane, a proton pump, a proton gradient and ATP synthase. Energy is used to pump protons across a membrane, to create a gradient or a high concentration of protons within the thylakoid lumen. ATP synthase has a channel that allows diffusion of protons back across the membrane; this releases enough energy to activate ATP synthase enzyme that catalyses the formation of ATP.

Along with the NADPH produced by the movement of electrons, the ATP will be used immediately in the biosynthetic reaction taking place in the stroma, responsible for fixing  $CO_2$ , and synthesis of sugars.

kebo111-s16.section_title: 11.7 WHERE ARE THE ATP AND NADPH USED?
kebo111-s16.content:
We learnt that the products of light reaction are ATP, NADPH and  $O_2$ . Of these  $O_2$  diffuses out of the chloroplast while ATP and NADPH are used to drive the processes leading to the synthesis of food, more accurately, sugars. This is the **biosynthetic phase** of photosynthesis. This process does not directly depend on the presence of light but is dependent on the products of the light reaction, i.e., ATP and NADPH, besides  $CO_2$  and  $H_2O$ . You may wonder how this could be verified; it is simple: immediately after light becomes unavailable, the biosynthetic process continues for some time, and then stops. If then, light is made available, the synthesis starts again.

*Can we, hence, say that calling the biosynthetic phase as the **dark reaction** is a misnomer? Discuss this amongst yourselves.*

Let us now see how the ATP and NADPH are used in the biosynthetic phase. We saw earlier that  $CO_2$  is combined with  $H_2O$  to produce  $(CH_2O)_n$  or sugars. It was of interest to scientists to find out how this reaction proceeded, or rather what was the first product formed when  $CO_2$  is taken into a reaction or fixed. Just after world war II, among the several efforts to put radioisotopes to beneficial use, the work of Melvin Calvin is exemplary. The use of radioactive  $^{14}C$  by him in algal photosynthesis studies led to the discovery that the first  $CO_2$  fixation product was a 3-carbon organic acid. He also contributed to working out the complete biosynthetic pathway; hence it was called **Calvin cycle** after him. The first product identified was **3-phosphoglyceric acid** or in short **PGA**. How many carbon atoms does it have?

Scientists also tried to know whether all plants have PGA as the first product of  $\text{CO}_2$  fixation, or whether any other product was formed in other plants. Experiments conducted over a wide range of plants led to the discovery of another group of plants, where the first stable product of  $\text{CO}_2$  fixation was again an organic acid, but one which had 4 carbon atoms in it. This acid was identified to be **oxaloacetic acid** or OAA. Since then  $\text{CO}_2$  assimilation during photosynthesis was said to be of two main types: those plants in which the first product of  $\text{CO}_2$  fixation is a  $\text{C}_3$  acid (PGA), i.e., the  **$\text{C}_3$  pathway**, and those in which the first product was a  $\text{C}_4$  acid (OAA), i.e., the  **$\text{C}_4$  pathway**. These two groups of plants showed other associated characteristics that we will discuss later.

kebo111-s17.content:
Let us now ask ourselves a question that was asked by the scientists who were struggling to understand the 'dark reaction'. *How many carbon atoms would a molecule have which after accepting (fixing)  $\text{CO}_2$ , would have 3 carbons (of PGA)?*

The studies very unexpectedly showed that the acceptor molecule was a 5-carbon ketose sugar – ribulose bisphosphate (RuBP). *Did any of you think of this possibility?* Do not worry; the scientists also took a long time and conducted many experiments to reach this conclusion. They also believed that since the first product was a  $\text{C}_3$  acid, the primary acceptor would be a 2-carbon compound; they spent many years trying to identify a 2-carbon compound before they discovered the 5-carbon RuBP.

kebo111-s18.content:
Calvin and his co-workers then worked out the whole pathway and showed that the pathway operated in a cyclic manner; the RuBP was regenerated. Let us now see how the Calvin pathway operates and where the sugar is synthesised. Let us at the outset understand very clearly that the Calvin pathway occurs in **all photosynthetic plants**; it does not matter whether they have  $\text{C}_3$  or  $\text{C}_4$  (or any other) pathways (Figure 11.8).

For ease of understanding, the Calvin cycle can be described under three stages: carboxylation, reduction and regeneration.

- 1. Carboxylation** – Carboxylation is the fixation of  $\text{CO}_2$  into a stable organic intermediate. Carboxylation is the most crucial step of the Calvin cycle where  $\text{CO}_2$  is utilised for the carboxylation of RuBP. This reaction is catalysed by the enzyme RuBP carboxylase which results in the formation of two molecules of 3-PGA. Since this enzyme also has an oxygenation activity it would be more correct to call it RuBP carboxylase-oxygenase or **RuBisCO**.

The diagram illustrates the Calvin cycle as a circular pathway. At the top, an arrow from 'Atmosphere' points to the cycle, labeled with  $\text{CO}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O}$ . The cycle begins at the top with 'Ribulose-1,5-bisphosphate' in a grey box. An arrow labeled '① Carboxylation' (in a blue box) leads to '3-phosphoglycerate' in a grey box. From '3-phosphoglycerate', an arrow labeled '② Reduction' (in a blue box) leads to 'Triose phosphate' in a grey box. This step consumes 'ATP + NADPH' (indicated by an arrow pointing to the step) and produces 'ADP + P<sub>i</sub> + NADP<sup>+</sup>' (indicated by an arrow pointing away). From 'Triose phosphate', an arrow points down to 'Sucrose, starch'. Another arrow from 'Triose phosphate' leads to 'Regeneration' (in a blue box), labeled '③'. This step consumes 'ATP' (indicated by an arrow pointing to the step) and produces 'ADP' (indicated by an arrow pointing away). The final arrow of the cycle returns to 'Ribulose-1,5-bisphosphate'.

**Figure 11.8** The Calvin cycle proceeds in three stages : (1) carboxylation, during which  $\text{CO}_2$  combines with ribulose-1,5-bisphosphate; (2) reduction, during which carbohydrate is formed at the expense of the photochemically made ATP and NADPH; and (3) regeneration during which the  $\text{CO}_2$  acceptor ribulose-1,5-bisphosphate is formed again so that the cycle continues

2. **Reduction** – These are a series of reactions that lead to the formation of glucose. The steps involve utilisation of 2 molecules of ATP for phosphorylation and two of NADPH for reduction per  $\text{CO}_2$  molecule fixed. The fixation of six molecules of  $\text{CO}_2$  and 6 turns of the cycle are required for the formation of one molecule of glucose from the pathway.
3. **Regeneration** – Regeneration of the  $\text{CO}_2$  acceptor molecule RuBP is crucial if the cycle is to continue uninterrupted. The regeneration steps require one ATP for phosphorylation to form RuBP.

Hence for every  $\text{CO}_2$  molecule entering the Calvin cycle, 3 molecules of ATP and 2 of NADPH are required. It is probably to meet this difference in number of ATP and NADPH used in the dark reaction that the cyclic phosphorylation takes place.

To make one molecule of glucose 6 turns of the cycle are required. *Work out how many ATP and NADPH molecules will be required to make one molecule of glucose through the Calvin pathway.*

It might help you to understand all of this if we look at what goes in and what comes out of the Calvin cycle.

kebo111-s19.content:
Plants that are adapted to dry tropical regions have the  $\text{C}_4$  pathway mentioned earlier. Though these plants have the  $\text{C}_4$  oxaloacetic acid as the first  $\text{CO}_2$  fixation product they use the  $\text{C}_3$  pathway or the Calvin cycle as the main biosynthetic pathway. Then, in what way are they different from  $\text{C}_3$  plants? This is a question that you may reasonably ask.

$\text{C}_4$  plants are special: They have a special type of leaf anatomy, they tolerate higher temperatures, they show a response to high light intensities, they lack a process called photorespiration and have greater productivity of biomass. Let us understand these one by one.

Study vertical sections of leaves, one of a  $\text{C}_3$  plant and the other of a  $\text{C}_4$  plant. *Do you notice the differences? Do both have the same types of mesophylls? Do they have similar cells around the vascular bundle sheath?*

The particularly large cells around the vascular bundles of the  $\text{C}_4$  plants are called **bundle sheath cells**, and the leaves which have such anatomy are said to have '**Kranz anatomy**'. 'Kranz' means 'wreath' and is a reflection of the arrangement of cells. The bundle sheath cells may form **several layers** around the vascular bundles; they are characterised by having a large number of chloroplasts, thick walls impervious to gaseous exchange and no intercellular spaces. You may like to cut a section of the leaves of  $\text{C}_4$  plants – maize or sorghum – to observe the Kranz anatomy and the distribution of mesophyll cells.

It would be interesting for you to collect leaves of diverse species of plants around you and cut vertical sections of the leaves. Observe under the microscope – look for the bundle sheath around the vascular bundles. The presence of the bundle sheath would help you identify the  $\text{C}_4$  plants.

Now study the pathway shown in Figure 11.9. This pathway that has been named the Hatch and Slack Pathway, is again a cyclic process. Let us study the pathway by listing the steps.

The primary  $\text{CO}_2$  acceptor is a 3-carbon molecule **phosphoenol pyruvate (PEP)** and is present in the mesophyll cells. The enzyme responsible for this fixation is **PEP carboxylase** or PEPcase. It is important to register that the mesophyll cells lack RuBisCO enzyme. The  $\text{C}_4$  acid OAA is formed in the mesophyll cells.

It then forms other 4-carbon compounds like malic acid or aspartic acid in the mesophyll cells itself, which are transported to the bundle sheath cells. In the bundle sheath cells these  $\text{C}_4$  acids are broken down to release  $\text{CO}_2$  and a 3-carbon molecule.

The 3-carbon molecule is transported back to the mesophyll where it is converted to PEP again, thus, completing the cycle.

The  $\text{CO}_2$  released in the bundle sheath cells enters the  $\text{C}_3$  or the Calvin pathway, a pathway common to all plants. The bundle sheath cells are

The diagram illustrates the Hatch and Slack Pathway (C<sub>4</sub> pathway) involving two types of cells: a **Mesophyll cell** (top, green) and a **Bundle sheath cell** (bottom, grey), connected by **Plasmodesmata**.

- Atmospheric  $\text{CO}_2$**  enters the **Mesophyll cell** and is converted to  $\text{HCO}_3^-$ .
- In the **Mesophyll cell**,  $\text{HCO}_3^-$  and **Phosphoenol-pyruvate** undergo **Fixation** to form a  **$\text{C}_4$  acid**.
- The  **$\text{C}_4$  acid** is then **Transport**ed through the **Plasmodesmata** into the **Bundle sheath cell**.
- In the **Bundle sheath cell**, the  **$\text{C}_4$  acid** undergoes **Decarboxylation**, releasing  **$\text{CO}_2$**  and a  **$\text{C}_3$  acid**.
- The released  **$\text{CO}_2$**  enters the **Fixation by Calvin cycle**.
- The  **$\text{C}_3$  acid** is **Transport**ed back through the **Plasmodesmata** into the **Mesophyll cell**.
- In the **Mesophyll cell**, the  **$\text{C}_3$  acid** undergoes **Regeneration** to reform **Phosphoenol-pyruvate**, completing the cycle.

Labels in the diagram include: **Atmospheric  $\text{CO}_2$** , **Mesophyll cell**, **Plasma membrane**, **Cell wall**,  **$\text{HCO}_3^-$** , **Phosphoenol-pyruvate**, **Fixation**,  **$\text{C}_4$  acid**, **Transport**, **Plasmodesmata**, **Bundle sheath cell**, **Decarboxylation**,  **$\text{CO}_2$** , **Fixation by Calvin cycle**, and  **$\text{C}_3$  acid**.

**Figure 11.9** Diagrammatic representation of the Hatch and Slack Pathway

rich in an enzyme Ribulose bisphosphate carboxylase-oxygenase (**RuBisCO**), but lack PEPcase. Thus, the basic pathway that results in the formation of the sugars, the Calvin pathway, is common to the  $C_3$  and  $C_4$  plants.

Did you note that the Calvin pathway occurs in all the mesophyll cells of the  $C_3$  plants? In the  $C_4$  plants it does not take place in the mesophyll cells but does so only in the bundle sheath cells.

kebo111-s20.content:
Let us try and understand one more process that creates an important difference between  $C_3$  and  $C_4$  plants – **Photorespiration**. To understand photorespiration we have to know a little bit more about the first step of the Calvin pathway – the first  $CO_2$  fixation step. This is the reaction where RuBP combines with  $CO_2$  to form 2 molecules of 3PGA, that is catalysed by RuBisCO.

$$\text{RuBP} + \text{CO}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{RuBisCo}} 2 \times 3\text{PGA}$$

RuBisCO that is the most abundant enzyme in the world (Do you wonder why?) is characterised by the fact that its active site can bind to both  $CO_2$  and  $O_2$  – hence the name. *Can you think how this could be possible?* RuBisCO has a much greater affinity for  $CO_2$  when the  $CO_2 : O_2$  is nearly equal. Imagine what would happen if this were not so! This binding is competitive. It is the relative concentration of  $O_2$  and  $CO_2$  that determines which of the two will bind to the enzyme.

In  $C_3$  plants some  $O_2$  does bind to RuBisCO, and hence  $CO_2$  fixation is decreased. Here the RuBP instead of being converted to 2 molecules of PGA binds with  $O_2$  to form one molecule of phosphoglycerate and phosphoglycolate (2 Carbon) in a pathway called photorespiration. In the photorespiratory pathway, there is neither synthesis of sugars, nor of ATP. Rather it results in the release of  $CO_2$  with the utilisation of ATP. In the photorespiratory pathway there is no synthesis of ATP or NADPH. The biological function of photorespiration is not known yet.

In  $C_4$  plants photorespiration does not occur. This is because they have a mechanism that increases the concentration of  $CO_2$  at the enzyme site. This takes place when the  $C_4$  acid from the mesophyll is broken down in the bundle sheath cells to release  $CO_2$  – this results in increasing the intracellular concentration of  $CO_2$ . In turn, this ensures that the RuBisCO functions as a carboxylase minimising the oxygenase activity.

Now that you know that the  $C_4$  plants lack photorespiration, you probably can understand why productivity and yields are better in these plants. In addition, these plants show tolerance to higher temperatures.

*Based on the above discussion can you compare plants showing the  $C_3$  and the  $C_4$  pathway? Use the table format given in table 11.1 and fill in the information.*

**TABLE 11.1** Fill in the Columns 2 and 3 in this table to highlight the differences between  $C_3$  and  $C_4$  Plants

kebo111-s21.content:
An understanding of the factors that affect photosynthesis is necessary. The rate of photosynthesis is very important in determining the yield of plants including crop plants. Photosynthesis is under the influence of several factors, both internal (plant) and external. The plant factors include the number, size, age and orientation of leaves, mesophyll cells and chloroplasts, internal  $\text{CO}_2$  concentration and the amount of chlorophyll. The plant or internal factors are dependent on the genetic predisposition and the growth of the plant.

The external factors would include the availability of sunlight, temperature,  $\text{CO}_2$  concentration and water. As a plant photosynthesises, all these factors will simultaneously affect its rate. Hence, though several factors interact and simultaneously affect photosynthesis or  $\text{CO}_2$  fixation, usually one factor is the major cause or is the one that limits the rate. Hence, at any point the rate will be determined by the factor available at sub-optimal levels.

When several factors affect any [bio] chemical process, Blackman's (1905) **Law of Limiting Factors** comes into effect. This states the following:

*If a chemical process is affected by more than one factor, then its rate will be determined by the factor which is nearest to its minimal value: it is the factor which directly affects the process if its quantity is changed.*

For example, despite the presence of a green leaf and optimal light and  $\text{CO}_2$  conditions, the plant may not photosynthesise if the temperature is very low. This leaf, if given the optimal temperature, will start photosynthesising.

kebo111-s22.content:
We need to distinguish between light quality, light intensity and the duration of exposure to light, while discussing light as a factor that affects photosynthesis. There is a linear relationship between incident light and  $\text{CO}_2$  fixation rates at low light intensities. At higher light intensities, gradually the rate does not show further increase as other factors become limiting (Figure 11.10). What is interesting to note is that light saturation occurs at 10 per cent of the full sunlight. Hence, except for plants in shade or in dense forests, light is rarely a limiting factor in nature. Increase in

**Figure 11.10** Graph of light intensity on the rate of photosynthesis

incident light beyond a point causes the breakdown of chlorophyll and a decrease in photosynthesis.

kebo111-s23.content:
Carbon dioxide is the major limiting factor for photosynthesis. The concentration of  $\text{CO}_2$  is very low in the atmosphere (between 0.03 and 0.04 per cent). Increase in concentration upto 0.05 per cent can cause an increase in  $\text{CO}_2$  fixation rates; beyond this the levels can become damaging over longer periods.

The  $\text{C}_3$  and  $\text{C}_4$  plants respond differently to  $\text{CO}_2$  concentrations. At low light conditions neither group responds to high  $\text{CO}_2$  conditions. At high light intensities, both  $\text{C}_3$  and  $\text{C}_4$  plants show increase in the rates of photosynthesis. What is important to note is that the  $\text{C}_4$  plants show saturation at about  $360 \mu\text{L}^{-1}$  while  $\text{C}_3$  responds to increased  $\text{CO}_2$  concentration and saturation is seen only beyond  $450 \mu\text{L}^{-1}$ . Thus, current availability of  $\text{CO}_2$  levels is limiting to the  $\text{C}_3$  plants.

The fact that  $\text{C}_3$  plants respond to higher  $\text{CO}_2$  concentration by showing increased rates of photosynthesis leading to higher productivity has been used for some greenhouse crops such as tomatoes and bell pepper. They are allowed to grow in carbon dioxide enriched atmosphere that leads to higher yields.

kebo111-s24.content:
The dark reactions being enzymatic are temperature controlled. Though the light reactions are also temperature sensitive they are affected to a much lesser extent. The  $\text{C}_4$  plants respond to higher temperatures and show higher rate of photosynthesis while  $\text{C}_3$  plants have a much lower temperature optimum.

The temperature optimum for photosynthesis of different plants also depends on the habitat that they are adapted to. Tropical plants have a higher temperature optimum than the plants adapted to temperate climates.

kebo111-s25.content:
Even though water is one of the reactants in the light reaction, the effect of water as a factor is more through its effect on the plant, rather than directly on photosynthesis. Water stress causes the stomata to close hence reducing the  $\text{CO}_2$  availability. Besides, water stress also makes leaves wilt, thus, reducing the surface area of the leaves and their metabolic activity as well.

Green plants make their own food by photosynthesis. During this process carbon dioxide from the atmosphere is taken in by leaves through stomata and used for making carbohydrates, principally glucose and starch. Photosynthesis takes place only in the green parts of the plants, mainly the leaves. Within the leaves, the mesophyll cells have a large number of chloroplasts that are responsible for  $\text{CO}_2$  fixation. Within the chloroplasts, the membranes are sites for the light reaction, while the chemosynthetic pathway occurs in the stroma. Photosynthesis has two stages: the light reaction and the carbon fixing reactions. In the light reaction the light energy is absorbed by the pigments present in the antenna, and funnelled to special chlorophyll *a* molecules called reaction centre chlorophylls. There are two photosystems, PS I and PS II. PS I has a 700 nm absorbing chlorophyll *a* P700 molecule at its reaction centre, while PS II has a P680 reaction centre that absorbs red light at 680 nm. After absorbing light, electrons are excited and transferred through PS II and PS I and finally to NAD forming NADH. During this process a proton gradient is created across the membrane of the thylakoid. The breakdown of the protons gradient due to movement through the  $F_0$  part of the ATPase enzyme releases enough energy for synthesis of ATP. Splitting of water molecules is associated with PS II resulting in the release of  $\text{O}_2$ , protons and transfer of electrons to PS II.

In the carbon fixation cycle,  $\text{CO}_2$  is added by the enzyme, RuBisCO, to a 5-carbon compound RuBP that is converted to 2 molecules of 3-carbon PGA. This is then converted to sugar by the Calvin cycle, and the RuBP is regenerated. During this process ATP and NADPH synthesised in the light reaction are utilised. RuBisCO also catalyses a wasteful oxygenation reaction in  $\text{C}_3$  plants: photorespiration.

Some tropical plants show a special type of photosynthesis called  $\text{C}_4$  pathway. In these plants the first product of  $\text{CO}_2$  fixation that takes place in the mesophyll, is a 4-carbon compound. In the bundle sheath cells the Calvin pathway is carried out for the synthesis of carbohydrates.

kebo111-d000.content: Diagram: stomatal apparatus in leaf epidermis, showing a stoma with two guard cells surrounded by epidermal cells (diagram). Caption: A stylized illustration of a plant cell, showing a large central vacuole, a nucleus with a nucleolus, and other organelles, set against a light green background. The cell wall and membrane are clearly visible, and the internal structures are rendered in various shades of green. Description: The diagram should represent the stomatal apparatus of a leaf epidermis, consisting of a stomatal pore (stoma) flanked by two guard cells and surrounded by epidermal cells. The guard cells are typically kidney-shaped in dicot leaves and are specialized epidermal cells with unevenly thickened walls: the inner wall adjacent to the pore is thicker, while the outer wall is thinner. Each guard cell contains cytoplasm, a nucleus, chloroplasts, and a large vacuole, and the radial arrangement of cellulose microfibrils helps the cells bend during changes in turgor pressure. The stomatal pore lies between the two guard cells and opens into the substomatal chamber beneath the epidermis. Surrounding epidermal cells are ordinary pavement cells that support the guard cells but do not form the pore. The structure functions in gaseous exchange and transpiration, with opening and closing regulated by changes in guard-cell turgidity. Labels: Epidermal cells, Guard cell, Guard cell, Stomatal pore (stoma), Substomatal chamber, Thick inner wall, Thin outer wall. NEET relevance: Identification of stomatal apparatus and its parts; Difference between guard cells and epidermal cells; Mechanism of stomatal opening and closing based on turgor changes. Board relevance: Draw and label stomatal apparatus; Label guard cells, stomatal pore, and epidermal cells; Explain function of stomata in transpiration and gas exchange. Exam-critical labels: Guard cells, Stomatal pore (stoma), Epidermal cells, Thick inner wall, Thin outer wall. Common mistakes: Two kidney-shaped cells are labeled as epidermal cells (correct: They are guard cells); The central opening is labeled as a cell wall or nucleus (correct: It is the stomatal pore (stoma)); The surrounding irregular cells are labeled as guard cells (correct: They are epidermal cells).

kebo111-d001.content: Diagram: effect of light intensity on the rate of photosynthesis (graph). Caption: Graph showing the rate of photosynthesis versus light intensity. The curve starts at the origin (0,0) and increases linearly through point A. It then curves through point B and reaches a plateau at point C. The plateau is marked by a horizontal dashed line labeled E. The point where the curve begins to level off is marked by a vertical dashed line labeled D. The x-axis is labeled 'Light intensity' and the y-axis is labeled 'Rate of photosynthesis'. Description: This diagram represents the typical response of the rate of photosynthesis to increasing light intensity. At very low light intensity, photosynthesis is light-limited, so the rate rises almost linearly as light intensity increases. This initial straight-line region reflects the fact that light is the limiting factor and more photons drive greater photochemical activity. As light intensity continues to increase, the curve begins to bend because another factor, such as carbon dioxide concentration, temperature, or enzyme capacity, starts limiting the process. Eventually the rate reaches a maximum and forms a plateau, showing light saturation: beyond this point, further increase in light intensity does not increase photosynthesis because the photosynthetic apparatus is operating at its maximum capacity under the given conditions. The graph therefore illustrates the law of limiting factors in photosynthesis. Labels: Rate of photosynthesis, Light intensity, Initial linear increase, Limiting-factor transition region, Light saturation plateau, Point of light saturation, Maximum rate of photosynthesis. NEET relevance: Effect of light intensity on photosynthesis rate; Identification of the light-limited region and saturation region; Application of the law of limiting factors. Board relevance: Draw and label the graph of rate of photosynthesis versus light intensity; Explain the initial linear rise and later plateau; State why photosynthesis becomes light-saturated. Exam-critical labels: Rate of photosynthesis, Light intensity, Initial linear increase, Light saturation plateau, Point of saturation, Maximum rate of photosynthesis. Common mistakes: Treating the plateau as a decline in photosynthesis at high light intensity (correct: It is a saturation plateau, not a decline); Labeling the curved region as a separate process or organ (correct: It is the transition from light-limited to saturation conditions); Assuming the dashed line is a biological membrane or boundary (correct: It is only a reference line marking the maximum rate or saturation level).

kebo111-d002.content: Diagram: Calvin cycle in photosynthesis (pathway). Caption: The diagram illustrates the Calvin cycle as a circular pathway. At the top, an arrow from 'Atmosphere' points to the cycle, labeled with  $\text{CO}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O}$ . The cycle begins at the top with 'Ribulose-1,5-bisphosphate' in a grey box. An arrow labeled '① Carboxylation' (in a blue box) leads to '3-phosphoglycerate' in a grey box. From '3-phosphoglycerate', an arrow labeled '② Reduction' (in a blue box) leads to 'Triose phosphate' in a grey box. This step consumes 'ATP + NADPH' (indicated by an arrow pointing to the step) and produces 'ADP + P<sub>i</sub> + NADP<sup>+</sup>' (indicated by an arrow pointing away). From 'Triose phosphate', an arrow points down to 'Sucrose, starch'. Another arrow from 'Triose phosphate' leads to 'Regeneration' (in a blue box), labeled '③'. This step consumes 'ATP' (indicated by an arrow pointing to the step) and produces 'ADP' (indicated by an arrow pointing away). The final arrow of the cycle returns to 'Ribulose-1,5-bisphosphate'. Description: This diagram represents the Calvin cycle (C3 pathway), the light-independent stage of photosynthesis that occurs in the stroma of the chloroplast. Carbon dioxide from the atmosphere enters the cycle and is fixed by the 5-carbon acceptor ribulose-1,5-bisphosphate (RuBP) in the carboxylation step, catalyzed by RuBisCO, producing an unstable 6-carbon intermediate that immediately splits into two molecules of 3-phosphoglycerate (3-PGA). In the reduction phase, 3-PGA is phosphorylated by ATP and reduced by NADPH to form triose phosphate (glyceraldehyde-3-phosphate, G3P). Some triose phosphate exits the cycle to be used in the synthesis of sucrose and starch, while the remainder is used in the regeneration phase to reform RuBP, requiring ATP. Thus, the cycle is a closed circular pathway with three major stages—carboxylation, reduction, and regeneration—and it links atmospheric CO2 fixation to carbohydrate biosynthesis. Labels: Atmosphere, CO2, Ribulose-1,5-bisphosphate (RuBP), Carboxylation, 3-phosphoglycerate (3-PGA), Reduction, ATP, NADPH, ADP, Pi, NADP+, Triose phosphate (G3P), Sucrose, starch, Regeneration. NEET relevance: Identify the first stable product of CO2 fixation in C3 plants; Name the CO2 acceptor molecule in the Calvin cycle; State the role of ATP and NADPH in the Calvin cycle. Board relevance: Draw and label the Calvin cycle; Explain the three phases of the Calvin cycle; Show the role of ATP and NADPH in reduction and regeneration. Exam-critical labels: Ribulose-1,5-bisphosphate (RuBP), CO2, Carboxylation, 3-phosphoglycerate (3-PGA), Reduction, ATP, NADPH, Triose phosphate (G3P). Common mistakes: CO2 + H2O as the input to the Calvin cycle (correct: CO2 is the actual substrate fixed in the Calvin cycle; H2O is not a direct substrate of the cycle); Glucose as the immediate product of the Calvin cycle (correct: Triose phosphate (G3P) is the immediate product; glucose/sucrose/starch are synthesized later); NADH instead of NADPH (correct: NADPH is the reducing agent used in photosynthesis).

kebo111-d003.content: Diagram: RuBisCO-catalysed carbon fixation reaction showing RuBP reacting with CO2 to form 3PGA (pathway). Caption: $$\text{RuBP} + \text{CO}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{RuBisCo}} 2 \times 3\text{PGA}$$ Description: This diagram represents the first carboxylation step of the Calvin cycle (C3 pathway) catalyzed by RuBisCO (ribulose-1,5-bisphosphate carboxylase/oxygenase). In the chloroplast stroma, one molecule of ribulose-1,5-bisphosphate (RuBP), a 5-carbon sugar phosphate, reacts with one molecule of carbon dioxide (CO2). RuBisCO catalyzes the unstable 6-carbon intermediate that is formed, which immediately splits into two molecules of 3-phosphoglycerate (3-PGA), each containing 3 carbons. Thus, the correct stoichiometry is RuBP + CO2 -> 2 x 3-PGA. The diagram should emphasize that RuBisCO is the enzyme above the reaction arrow, RuBP is the substrate on the left, CO2 is the inorganic carbon input, and 3-PGA is the first stable product of carbon fixation in the Calvin cycle. Labels: RuBP, CO2, RuBisCO, 2 × 3-PGA. NEET relevance: Identify the enzyme catalyzing CO2 fixation in the Calvin cycle.; State the substrate and product of RuBisCO-catalyzed carboxylation.; Recall that one RuBP and one CO2 yield two molecules of 3-PGA.. Board relevance: Draw and label the reaction RuBP + CO2 -> 2 x 3-PGA with RuBisCO above the arrow.; Explain the role of RuBisCO in the Calvin cycle.; Write the significance of 3-PGA as the first stable product of carbon fixation in C3 plants.. Exam-critical labels: RuBP, CO2, RuBisCO, 2 x 3-PGA. Common mistakes: RuBisCo written as 'RuBisCo' or 'Rubisco' inconsistently without recognizing it as the enzyme (correct: RuBisCO (ribulose-1,5-bisphosphate carboxylase/oxygenase)); 3PGA shown as a single molecule (correct: 2 x 3-PGA); CO2 omitted or treated as part of RuBP (correct: CO2 is a separate reactant).

kebo111-d004.content: Diagram: Internal structure of a chloroplast (diagram). Caption: The diagram shows a cross-section of a chloroplast, represented as an oval with a yellow border. Inside, there are several stacks of green, flattened discs called grana. These grana are interconnected by horizontal green tubes called stromal lamellae. The internal fluid is labeled as stroma. Other structures shown and labeled include: Description: A chloroplast is a double-membrane-bound plastid found in photosynthetic eukaryotic cells, especially in the mesophyll of green leaves. In a typical textbook section, it appears as an oval or lens-shaped organelle enclosed by an outer membrane and an inner membrane, with a narrow intermembrane space between them. The interior is filled with the stroma, a protein-rich matrix containing circular chloroplast DNA, 70S ribosomes, enzymes of the Calvin cycle, starch grains, and plastoglobuli (lipid droplets). Embedded within the stroma is the thylakoid membrane system: flattened membrane sacs called thylakoids, which are stacked into grana and interconnected by stromal lamellae (also called intergranal lamellae). The thylakoid membranes contain chlorophyll, photosystems, electron transport components, and ATP synthase, and are the site of the light reactions of photosynthesis. The stroma is the site of carbon fixation and synthesis of carbohydrates, while starch grains represent temporary carbohydrate storage. The diagram should therefore represent a chloroplast as a double-membraned organelle with internal thylakoid stacks and connecting lamellae suspended in stroma, not as a simple empty sac or a mitochondrion-like structure. Labels: Outer membrane, Inner membrane, Stroma, Grana, Stromal lamellae, Ribosomes, Starch grain, Plastoglobule. NEET relevance: Identify the double-membrane envelope of chloroplast; Differentiate grana and stromal lamellae; State the site of light reaction (thylakoid/grana) and Calvin cycle (stroma). Board relevance: Draw and label the internal structure of a chloroplast; Explain the role of grana, stroma, and stromal lamellae; Label outer membrane, inner membrane, stroma, grana, and ribosomes. Exam-critical labels: Outer membrane, Inner membrane, Stroma, Grana, Stromal lamellae, Ribosomes, Starch grain, Plastoglobule. Common mistakes: Calling the entire internal membrane system 'grana' or labeling a single thylakoid as a granum (correct: A granum is a stack of thylakoids; a single disc is a thylakoid); Labeling stromal lamellae as 'intergranal thylakoids' without clarifying their connecting role (correct: Stromal lamellae are the unstacked thylakoid membranes connecting grana); Calling starch grains 'lipid droplets' or vice versa (correct: Starch grains are carbohydrate storage bodies; plastoglobules are lipid-containing droplets).

kebo111-d005.content: Diagram: absorption spectra of chloroplast pigments and action spectrum/rate of photosynthesis versus wavelength of light (graph). Caption: Figure 11.3 consists of three vertically stacked graphs, labeled (a), (b), and (c), sharing a common x-axis representing the 'Wavelength of light in nanometres (nm)' ranging from 400 to 700 nm. Description: This figure represents the relationship between wavelength of visible light and two related but distinct biological responses in photosynthesis: pigment absorption and photosynthetic activity. In panel (a), the absorption spectra of the major chloroplast pigments are shown: chlorophyll a, chlorophyll b, and carotenoids. Chlorophyll a shows strong absorption in the blue-violet region and the red region, with poor absorption in the green region; chlorophyll b has a similar but slightly shifted pattern; carotenoids absorb mainly in the blue to blue-green region and contribute accessory absorption. In panel (b), the action spectrum of photosynthesis is shown as the rate of photosynthesis, commonly measured by oxygen evolution, across wavelengths; it generally peaks in the blue and red regions and is low in the green region. In panel (c), the action spectrum is superimposed on the absorption spectrum, demonstrating that the rate of photosynthesis closely follows the combined absorption of chlorophylls and accessory pigments. The key biological message is that photosynthesis is most efficient at wavelengths that are strongly absorbed by photosynthetic pigments, and least efficient in the green region because green light is relatively poorly absorbed and more strongly reflected/transmitted by leaves. Labels: Absorbance of light by chloroplast pigments, Chlorophyll a, Chlorophyll b, Carotenoids, Rate of photosynthesis (measured by O2 release), Rate of photosynthesis, Absorption, Light absorbed, Wavelength of light in nanometres (nm), 400, 500, 600, 700, (a), (b). NEET relevance: Identify the pigments responsible for absorption in different regions of visible light; Explain why photosynthesis shows maximum rate in blue and red light; Differentiate absorption spectrum from action spectrum. Board relevance: Draw and label the absorption spectra of chlorophyll a, chlorophyll b, and carotenoids; Draw the action spectrum of photosynthesis; Compare absorption spectrum with action spectrum. Exam-critical labels: Chlorophyll a, Chlorophyll b, Carotenoids, Rate of photosynthesis (measured by O2 release), Wavelength of light in nanometres (nm), (a), (b), (c), Absorption / Light absorbed. Common mistakes: Treating the middle panel as a different process unrelated to photosynthesis (correct: It is the action spectrum of photosynthesis, usually measured by oxygen evolution or another proxy for photosynthetic rate); Showing chlorophyll a and chlorophyll b as having identical peaks (correct: Chlorophyll b has peaks slightly shifted relative to chlorophyll a); Making carotenoids absorb strongly in the red region (correct: Carotenoids absorb mainly in the blue to blue-green region).

kebo111-d006.content: Diagram: overall photosynthesis equation (pathway). Caption: $$\text{CO}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O} \xrightarrow{\text{Light}} [\text{CH}_2\text{O}] + \text{O}_2$$ Description: This diagram represents the overall photosynthetic reaction, not a cellular organelle or anatomical structure. In its simplest generalized form, carbon dioxide and water are converted into carbohydrate in the presence of light energy, with oxygen released as a by-product: CO2 + H2O 0[CH2O] + O2. The bracketed [CH2O] is a conventional empirical representation of a carbohydrate unit, indicating that the actual product is not a single fixed molecule in all cases but a reduced carbon compound such as glucose, which may later be stored as starch or used in metabolism. In oxygenic photosynthesis, the light-driven reactions occur in chloroplasts, where water is split and oxygen is evolved, while carbon dioxide is reduced during the Calvin cycle to form carbohydrate. Thus, the diagram should convey the input of inorganic substrates, the requirement of light, and the formation of organic matter plus oxygen. Labels: CO2, H2O, Light, [CH2O], O2, Reaction arrow. NEET relevance: Overall equation of photosynthesis and identification of reactants/products; Meaning of [CH2O] as a generalized carbohydrate unit; Source of evolved oxygen in photosynthesis. Board relevance: Write and explain the overall photosynthesis equation; Label reactants, products, and light on the equation; Explain why [CH2O] is used instead of only glucose in the general equation. Exam-critical labels: CO2, H2O, Light, [CH2O], O2, Reaction arrow. Common mistakes: Glucose (C6H12O6) as the only product (correct: [CH2O] as a generalized carbohydrate product); Oxygen as a reactant (correct: Oxygen as a product); Light written below the arrow or inside the products (correct: Light written above the arrow as the energy input).

kebo111-d007.content: Diagram: General photosynthesis equation showing a hydrogen donor reacting with carbon dioxide in light to form an oxidized product, carbohydrate, and water (pathway). Caption: $$2\text{H}_2\text{A} + \text{CO}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{Light}} 2\text{A} + \text{CH}_2\text{O} + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$ Description: This diagram represents the general equation of photosynthesis in which carbon dioxide is reduced to carbohydrate using a hydrogen donor (electron donor) under the influence of light. In the most general form, the reaction is written as 2H2A + CO2  Light  2A + CH2O + H2O, where H2A is the hydrogen donor, A is its oxidized product, CH2O represents a carbohydrate unit, and water is formed as a by-product. The key biological idea is that light energy drives the transfer of hydrogen/electrons from H2A to CO2, resulting in reduction of carbon dioxide and oxidation of the donor. In oxygenic photosynthesis, H2A is water and A is oxygen, but in anoxygenic photosynthesis other donors such as H2S may be used. The diagram should therefore be understood as a generalized chemical equation, not a structural cell diagram. Labels: 2H2A, CO2, Light, 2A, CH2O, H2O, reaction arrow. NEET relevance: General equation of photosynthesis; Meaning of H2A and A in generalized photosynthesis; Difference between oxygenic and anoxygenic photosynthesis. Board relevance: Write and explain the general equation of photosynthesis; Define H2A, A, and CH2O in the equation; Explain why the equation is called generalized. Exam-critical labels: 2H2A, CO2, Light, 2A, CH2O, H2O, reaction arrow. Common mistakes: H2A is always water (correct: H2A is a generalized hydrogen donor; water is only one possible donor in oxygenic photosynthesis); A is oxygen in every case (correct: A is the oxidized form of whatever hydrogen donor is used; oxygen is only the oxidized product when H2A = H2O); CH2O is a specific sugar molecule like glucose (correct: CH2O is a generalized empirical formula representing carbohydrate).

kebo111-d008.content: Diagram: Z scheme of light reaction of photosynthesis showing electron flow through Photosystem II and Photosystem I (pathway). Caption: Diagram of the Z scheme of light reaction of photosynthesis. The diagram shows the flow of electrons from water splitting at Photosystem II (PS II) to Photosystem I (PS I) and finally to NADP+. Light energy excites electrons in both PS II and PS I. At PS II, water is split into 2H+, [O], and 2e-. Electrons from water replace those lost by PS II. The excited electrons from PS II pass through an electron transport system, which produces ATP from ADP+iP. The electrons then pass to PS I, where they are re-excited by light and transferred to an electron acceptor, which reduces NADP+ to NADPH. The overall process is represented by a zigzag line (Z shape) on a redox potential scale. Description: The Z scheme of the light reaction of photosynthesis represents the non-cyclic electron flow through Photosystem II (PSII) and Photosystem I (PSI) in the thylakoid membrane of chloroplasts. Light energy excites chlorophyll in PSII (P680), causing electrons to move to a primary electron acceptor; the lost electrons are replaced by photolysis of water at the oxygen-evolving complex, releasing O2, H+ into the thylakoid lumen, and electrons. The excited electrons pass through an electron transport chain (including plastoquinone, cytochrome b6f, and plastocyanin), during which proton translocation builds a proton gradient used by ATP synthase to convert ADP + Pi into ATP. The electrons then reach PSI (P700), where a second photon excites them again and they are transferred to another primary electron acceptor, then to ferredoxin, and finally to NADP+ reductase, which reduces NADP+ to NADPH. The overall electron flow rises and falls in energy, producing the characteristic Z-shaped pattern. The diagram must show that water is the ultimate electron donor and NADP+ is the final electron acceptor, with ATP and NADPH as the main products of the light reactions. Labels: Photosystem II (PSII), Photosystem I (PSI), Light, Primary electron acceptor, Electron transport chain (ETC), ATP, ADP + Pi, NADP+, NADPH, H2O, Oxygen-evolving complex / photolysis of water, O2, H+, P680, P700. NEET relevance: Identify the site of photolysis of water; Explain why the electron flow is called the Z scheme; Differentiate PSII and PSI functions. Board relevance: Draw and label the Z scheme of light reaction; Show electron flow from water to NADP+; Label PSII, PSI, ETC, ATP, NADPH, H2O, O2, ADP + Pi. Exam-critical labels: Photosystem II, Photosystem I, Primary electron acceptor, Electron transport chain, H2O, O2, ADP + Pi, ATP. Common mistakes: Putting water splitting at Photosystem I (correct: Water is split at Photosystem II by the oxygen-evolving complex); Labeling the electron acceptor as 'e- acceptor' without specifying primary electron acceptor (correct: Primary electron acceptor); Showing ATP as directly produced by PSI (correct: ATP is produced by ATP synthase using the proton gradient generated by electron transport).

kebo111-d009.content: Diagram: overall photosynthesis equation (pathway). Caption: $$6\text{CO}_2 + 12\text{H}_2\text{O} \xrightarrow{\text{Light}} \text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_6 + 6\text{H}_2\text{O} + 6\text{O}_2$$ Description: This diagram represents the overall balanced equation of oxygenic photosynthesis in green plants, showing that carbon dioxide and water are converted into glucose, water, and oxygen in the presence of light energy. The correct stoichiometric form is 6CO2 + 12H2O  Light  C6H12O6 + 6H2O + 6O2. Biologically, this summarizes the net result of photosynthesis in chloroplasts: CO2 is reduced to carbohydrate, while H2O is oxidized to O2. The equation is not meant to imply that all 12 water molecules are directly consumed as reactants in a simple one-step reaction; rather, it reflects the net balance of water used and regenerated during the light and carbon-fixation reactions. In green plants, water serves as the electron and hydrogen donor, and oxygen released comes from water, not from CO2. The diagram should therefore communicate a single overall reaction with a left-to-right arrow labeled light, reactants on the left, products on the right, and no extra structures unless the figure is specifically expanded to show chloroplast compartments or the light and dark reactions separately. Labels: 6CO2, 12H2O, Light, C6H12O6, 6H2O, 6O2. NEET relevance: Overall balanced equation of photosynthesis; Source of evolved oxygen in photosynthesis; Role of water as electron donor. Board relevance: Write and explain the overall equation of photosynthesis; State the source of oxygen released during photosynthesis; Why water appears on both sides of the equation. Exam-critical labels: 6CO2, 12H2O, Light, C6H12O6, 6H2O, 6O2. Common mistakes: Oxygen comes from CO2 (correct: Oxygen released in green plant photosynthesis comes from H2O); The equation should be written as CO2 + H2O -> glucose + O2 without coefficients (correct: The balanced overall equation is 6CO2 + 12H2O -> C6H12O6 + 6H2O + 6O2); Water is only a reactant and not a product (correct: Water appears on both sides in the overall equation because some water is consumed and some is regenerated).

kebo111-d010.content: Diagram: Hatch and Slack pathway (C4 pathway) showing carbon flow between a mesophyll cell and a bundle sheath cell (pathway). Caption: The diagram illustrates the Hatch and Slack Pathway (C<sub>4</sub> pathway) involving two types of cells: a **Mesophyll cell** (top, green) and a **Bundle sheath cell** (bottom, grey), connected by **Plasmodesmata**. Description: The diagram represents the Hatch and Slack pathway, also called the C4 pathway, in which initial CO2 fixation occurs in the mesophyll cell and the Calvin cycle operates in the bundle sheath cell. Atmospheric CO2 enters the mesophyll cell and is rapidly converted to bicarbonate (HCO3-) by carbonic anhydrase. HCO3- combines with phosphoenolpyruvate (PEP) to form a 4-carbon compound, typically oxaloacetate, which is then converted into other C4 acids such as malate or aspartate. These C4 acids are transported through plasmodesmata into the bundle sheath cell. There, decarboxylation releases CO2, which is immediately refixed by RuBisCO through the Calvin cycle to form C3 compounds. The resulting 3-carbon compound returns to the mesophyll cell, where ATP-dependent regeneration of PEP occurs, completing the cycle. This spatial separation of initial CO2 fixation and the Calvin cycle minimizes photorespiration and is characteristic of C4 plants such as maize and sugarcane. Labels: Atmospheric CO2, Mesophyll cell, Cell wall, Plasma membrane, HCO3-, Phosphoenolpyruvate (PEP), Fixation, C4 acid, Transport, Plasmodesmata, Bundle sheath cell, Decarboxylation, CO2, Fixation by Calvin cycle, C3 acid. NEET relevance: Identify the C4 pathway and explain why it reduces photorespiration; Name the first stable product of CO2 fixation in C4 plants; State the site of initial CO2 fixation and the site of Calvin cycle. Board relevance: Draw and label the Hatch and Slack pathway; Explain carbon flow between mesophyll and bundle sheath cells; Write the role of PEP carboxylase, malate/aspartate, and RuBisCO. Exam-critical labels: Mesophyll cell, Bundle sheath cell, Atmospheric CO2, HCO3-, Phosphoenolpyruvate (PEP), C4 acid, Plasmodesmata, Decarboxylation. Common mistakes: Putting the Calvin cycle in the mesophyll cell (correct: The Calvin cycle occurs mainly in the bundle sheath cell in C4 plants); Labeling the first fixation product as oxaloacetate only, without acknowledging C4 acids (correct: The initial product is oxaloacetate, which is then converted to C4 acids such as malate or aspartate); Showing plasmodesmata as a membrane or wall (correct: Plasmodesmata are cytoplasmic channels traversing the cell wall between adjacent cells).

kebo111-d011.content: Diagram: cyclic photophosphorylation in Photosystem I (diagram). Caption: Diagram of cyclic photophosphorylation. A yellow circle labeled 'Light' has a dashed yellow arrow pointing to a green oval labeled 'Chlorophyll P 700'. A red arrow points from 'Chlorophyll P 700' to a pink oval labeled 'e- acceptor' (part of 'Photosystem I').
