kebo116-d000.content: Diagram: Longitudinal section (diagrammatic) of a kidney (cross_section). Caption: Figure 16.2: Longitudinal section (Diagrammatic) of Kidney. The diagram shows a cross-section of a kidney with various internal structures labeled. From the outer edge to the center, labels include: Renal capsule, Cortex, Renal column, Medullary pyramid, Calyx, Renal artery, Renal vein, Renal pelvis, and Ureter. The renal pyramids are shown as conical structures in the medulla, separated by renal columns. Description: A longitudinal section of the human kidney shows a bean-shaped organ enclosed by a thin fibrous renal capsule. Beneath the capsule lies the outer cortex, which is granular and extends inward as renal columns between the medullary pyramids. The inner medulla is organized into conical renal pyramids whose apices, called papillae, project toward small cup-like minor calyces; these may join to form major calyces, which in turn drain into the funnel-shaped renal pelvis. The renal pelvis continues as the ureter at the hilum. The renal artery enters and the renal vein leaves the kidney at the hilum. Functionally, the cortex contains renal corpuscles and convoluted tubules, while the medulla contains loops of Henle and collecting ducts arranged to concentrate urine. The diagram must represent the gross anatomical arrangement of these regions and the urine drainage pathway from papillae to calyces, pelvis, and ureter. Labels: Renal capsule, Cortex, Renal column, Medullary pyramid, Minor calyx, Major calyx, Renal pelvis, Ureter, Renal artery, Renal vein, Hilum. NEET relevance: Identify parts of the kidney in longitudinal section; Trace the path of urine from collecting ducts to ureter; Differentiate cortex and medulla. Board relevance: Draw and label the longitudinal section of kidney; Label outer capsule, cortex, medulla, pyramids, calyx, pelvis, ureter, renal artery, and renal vein; Explain the function of cortex and medulla. Exam-critical labels: Renal capsule, Cortex, Renal column, Medullary pyramid, Calyx, Renal pelvis, Ureter, Renal artery. Common mistakes: Calling the renal pyramid a 'medullary pyramid' but placing it in the cortex (correct: Renal pyramids are part of the medulla, not the cortex); Labeling the cup-like structures as 'calyx' without distinguishing minor and major calyces (correct: Small cups at pyramid tips are minor calyces; larger collecting branches are major calyces); Showing the ureter arising directly from the kidney surface (correct: The ureter continues from the renal pelvis at the hilum).

kebo116-d001.content: Diagram: Nephron and vasa recta showing counter current mechanism in the kidney medulla (diagram). Caption: The diagram shows a nephron and a *vasa recta* in cross-section, divided into three horizontal zones: Cortex, Outer medulla, and Inner medulla. The nephron starts at the Bowman's capsule in the cortex, where filtrate has an osmolarity of  $300 \text{ mOsmolL}^{-1}$ . It passes through the Glomerulus (supplied by an Afferent arteriole and drained by an Efferent arteriole). The filtrate then enters the descending limb of the Loop of Henle, where  $\text{H}_2\text{O}$  is reabsorbed into the interstitium. The osmolarity increases to  $400$ ,  $600$ ,  $800$ ,  $1000$ , and finally  $1200 \text{ mOsmolL}^{-1}$  at the bottom of the loop in the inner medulla. The ascending limb then transports  $\text{NaCl}$  out into the interstitium. The osmolarity decreases back to  $1000$ ,  $800$ ,  $600$ ,  $400$ , and  $300 \text{ mOsmolL}^{-1}$  as it returns to the cortex. The *vasa recta* is a U-shaped capillary loop that follows the nephron. Its descending limb transports  $\text{NaCl}$  out and  $\text{H}_2\text{O}$  in, matching the changes in the nephron's ascending limb. Its ascending limb transports  $\text{NaCl}$  in and  $\text{H}_2\text{O}$  out, matching the changes in the nephron's descending limb. This counter-current exchange maintains the hyperosmotic gradient in the medulla. A collecting tubule is shown on the right, reabsorbing  $\text{H}_2\text{O}$  in the outer medulla and inner medulla, and transporting Urea out in the inner medulla. Horizontal dashed lines separate the Cortex, Outer medulla, and Inner medulla. Vertical osmolarity markers on the left show values of  $300$ ,  $600$ ,  $900$ , and  $1200 \text{ mOsmolL}^{-1}$ . Description: This diagram represents the counter-current mechanism in the renal medulla, involving a juxtamedullary nephron and its associated vasa recta. The nephron begins in the cortex with the renal corpuscle, consisting of Bowman’s capsule enclosing the glomerulus, where ultrafiltration occurs. Filtrate then passes into the proximal convoluted tubule, descends into the loop of Henle, and returns via the ascending limb. The descending limb is permeable to water but relatively impermeable to NaCl, so water leaves the tubule as the filtrate descends into the increasingly hyperosmotic medulla, causing tubular fluid osmolarity to rise. In contrast, the ascending limb is impermeable to water but actively or passively reabsorbs NaCl, so tubular fluid becomes progressively dilute while the medullary interstitium becomes hyperosmotic. The vasa recta, a hairpin-shaped capillary network arising from the efferent arteriole of a juxtamedullary nephron, runs parallel to the loop of Henle and acts as a counter-current exchanger. Blood descending into the medulla gains NaCl and loses water, while blood ascending back toward the cortex loses NaCl and gains water, thereby preserving the corticomedullary osmotic gradient rather than washing it out. The interstitial osmolarity increases from about 300 mOsmol L−1 in the cortex to about 1200 mOsmol L−1 in the inner medulla, with NaCl and urea both contributing to the gradient, especially in the deeper medulla. This gradient is essential for water reabsorption from the collecting ducts and for the concentration of urine. Labels: Bowman’s capsule, Glomerulus, Afferent arteriole, Efferent arteriole, Proximal convoluted tubule, Loop of Henle, Descending limb of loop of Henle, Ascending limb of loop of Henle, Distal convoluted tubule, Collecting duct, Vasa recta, Cortex, Outer medulla, Inner medulla, NaCl. NEET relevance: Counter-current mechanism in the kidney medulla; Difference between counter-current multiplication and counter-current exchange; Permeability of descending vs ascending limb of loop of Henle. Board relevance: Draw and label nephron and vasa recta showing counter-current mechanism; Explain how the medullary osmotic gradient is established and maintained; Label cortex, outer medulla, inner medulla, loop of Henle, and vasa recta. Exam-critical labels: Bowman’s capsule, Glomerulus, Afferent arteriole, Efferent arteriole, Loop of Henle, Descending limb, Ascending limb, Vasa recta. Common mistakes: Calling the vasa recta the loop of Henle or vice versa (correct: The loop of Henle is the nephron tubule; the vasa recta is the surrounding capillary network); Placing the highest osmolarity in the cortex (correct: The highest osmolarity is in the inner medulla, reaching about 1200 mOsmol L−1); Showing water leaving the ascending limb (correct: The ascending limb is impermeable to water; NaCl leaves, not water).

kebo116-d002.content: Diagram: nephron showing proximal convoluted tubule, loop of Henle, distal convoluted tubule, collecting duct, and sites of reabsorption/secretion in cortex and medulla (diagram). Caption: The diagram illustrates the structure of a nephron, the functional unit of the kidney, and the sites of reabsorption and secretion. The nephron is divided into two main regions: the Cortex (top) and the Medulla (bottom). Description: This diagram should represent a nephron, the structural and functional unit of the kidney, with the renal corpuscle and renal tubule arranged across the cortex and medulla. The renal corpuscle (glomerulus within Bowman’s capsule) lies in the cortex and is followed by the proximal convoluted tubule (PCT), which is also in the cortex and is the major site of bulk reabsorption of water, NaCl, bicarbonate, glucose, amino acids, and other nutrients, along with secretion of H+ and NH3. The tubule then continues as the loop of Henle, which descends into the medulla as the descending limb (highly permeable to water, so water is reabsorbed) and returns as the ascending limb (impermeable to water; NaCl is reabsorbed, especially in the thick ascending limb). The thin ascending limb is in the medulla, while the thick ascending limb rises back toward the cortex. The distal convoluted tubule (DCT) lies in the cortex and participates in selective reabsorption and secretion, including NaCl reabsorption, bicarbonate handling, and secretion of K+ and H+. The collecting duct receives filtrate from multiple nephrons, passes from cortex through medulla, and is a major site of water reabsorption under ADH control; it also contributes to NaCl and urea handling, especially in the medulla. The diagram should emphasize that reabsorption and secretion occur at specific segments: PCT in cortex, descending limb in medulla for water, ascending limb for NaCl, DCT in cortex for fine-tuning, and collecting duct spanning cortex and medulla for final concentration of urine. Labels: Renal corpuscle (glomerulus + Bowman’s capsule), Proximal convoluted tubule, Descending limb of loop of Henle, Thin segment of ascending limb of loop of Henle, Thick segment of ascending limb of loop of Henle, Distal convoluted tubule, Collecting duct, Cortex, Medulla, H2O, NaCl, Nutrients, HCO3-, K+, H+. NEET relevance: Identify the nephron parts and their locations in cortex vs medulla; State the main site of reabsorption of glucose, amino acids, water, and bicarbonate; Explain why the descending limb is water-permeable and the ascending limb is water-impermeable. Board relevance: Draw and label a nephron showing cortex and medulla; Mark sites of reabsorption and secretion; Explain the function of PCT, loop of Henle, DCT, and collecting duct. Exam-critical labels: Renal corpuscle, Proximal convoluted tubule, Descending limb of loop of Henle, Thin ascending limb, Thick ascending limb, Distal convoluted tubule, Collecting duct, Cortex. Common mistakes: Calling the renal corpuscle the proximal convoluted tubule (correct: The renal corpuscle is the glomerulus within Bowman’s capsule; the PCT is the coiled tubule that follows it); Labeling the entire ascending limb as only 'thick segment' or only 'thin segment' (correct: The ascending limb has a thin segment in the medulla and a thick segment as it returns toward the cortex); Placing the collecting duct only in the cortex (correct: The collecting duct starts in the cortex and extends through the medulla).

kebo116-d003.content: Diagram: Human urinary system (diagram). Caption: A diagram of the human urinary system. It shows two kidneys positioned on either side of the midline, flanking the dorsal aorta and inferior vena cava. The right kidney is shown in cross-section, revealing internal structures: the outer cortex, the inner medulla, and the renal pelvis. The left kidney is shown externally. Above each kidney sits an adrenal gland. The renal artery and renal vein enter/exit each kidney at the hilum. From each kidney, a ureter extends downwards to the urinary bladder. The urethra is shown exiting the urinary bladder. Labels with leader lines point to: Inferior vena cava, Pelvis, Medulla, Cortex, Adrenal gland, Renal artery, Renal vein, Kidney, Dorsal aorta, Ureter, Urinary bladder, and Urethra. Description: The human urinary system consists of a pair of kidneys, a pair of ureters, a urinary bladder, and a urethra, with the kidneys lying retroperitoneally on either side of the vertebral column in the upper posterior abdominal cavity. Each kidney is bean-shaped and is supplied by a renal artery from the abdominal aorta and drained by a renal vein into the inferior vena cava. The outer renal cortex contains renal corpuscles and convoluted tubules, while the inner renal medulla is arranged into renal pyramids whose papillae drain urine into minor calyces, then major calyces, and finally into the renal pelvis, which continues as the ureter. The ureters are muscular tubes that transport urine to the urinary bladder, a distensible muscular sac in the pelvic cavity that stores urine temporarily. The urethra carries urine from the bladder to the exterior. The adrenal (suprarenal) glands lie superior to the kidneys but are endocrine organs and are not part of the urinary system. A correct diagram should show the kidneys in relation to the dorsal aorta and inferior vena cava, with the renal vessels entering and leaving the hilum, and the collecting system within the kidney leading to the ureter. Labels: Kidney, Cortex, Medulla, Renal pelvis, Ureter, Urinary bladder, Urethra, Renal artery, Renal vein, Dorsal aorta, Inferior vena cava, Adrenal gland. NEET relevance: Identification of kidney internal parts: cortex, medulla, renal pelvis; Blood supply and drainage: renal artery from aorta, renal vein to inferior vena cava; Urine pathway: kidney -> ureter -> urinary bladder -> urethra. Board relevance: Draw and label the human urinary system; Label the internal structure of kidney in a sectional view; Show the route of urine formation and passage. Exam-critical labels: Kidney, Cortex, Medulla, Renal pelvis, Ureter, Urinary bladder, Urethra, Renal artery. Common mistakes: Calling the adrenal gland part of the urinary system (correct: It is an endocrine gland located above the kidney, not part of the urinary tract); Labeling the renal pelvis as the whole inner kidney (correct: The renal pelvis is only the funnel-shaped collecting region, not the medulla); Swapping renal artery and renal vein (correct: Renal artery comes from the aorta to the kidney; renal vein goes from the kidney to the inferior vena cava).

kebo116-d004.content: Diagram: Malpighian body (renal corpuscle) showing afferent arteriole, efferent arteriole, Bowman's capsule, and proximal convoluted tubule (diagram). Caption: A detailed diagram of a Malpighian body, also known as a renal corpuscle. It features a central glomerulus, which is a cluster of capillaries, enclosed within Bowman's capsule. An afferent arteriole (inlet) enters the glomerulus from the top left, and an efferent arteriole (outlet) exits from the top right. The Bowman's capsule is depicted as a double-walled cup-like structure surrounding the glomerulus. The proximal convoluted tubule (PCT) is shown as a coiled tube that extends from the bottom of the Bowman's capsule. Description: The diagram should represent the renal corpuscle (Malpighian body), which is the initial filtering unit of a nephron in the kidney. It consists of a tuft of fenestrated capillaries called the glomerulus, enclosed by a double-walled Bowman’s capsule. The afferent arteriole enters the glomerulus and is usually wider than the efferent arteriole, helping maintain high hydrostatic pressure for ultrafiltration. Blood is filtered across the glomerular capillary wall into the Bowman’s space (capsular space) between the visceral and parietal layers of Bowman’s capsule. The filtrate then passes into the proximal convoluted tubule, which continues from the urinary pole of the capsule. The visceral layer of Bowman’s capsule is formed by podocytes closely covering the glomerular capillaries, while the parietal layer forms the outer wall of the capsule. The efferent arteriole carries blood away from the glomerulus and is narrower than the afferent arteriole. The diagram must show the glomerulus inside Bowman’s capsule, the afferent arteriole entering at the vascular pole, the efferent arteriole leaving from the same region, and the proximal convoluted tubule arising from the urinary pole. Labels: Afferent arteriole, Efferent arteriole, Bowman’s capsule, Glomerulus, Bowman’s space (capsular space), Proximal convoluted tubule. NEET relevance: Identification of the renal corpuscle and its parts; Difference between afferent and efferent arterioles; Site of ultrafiltration in the nephron. Board relevance: Draw and label the Malpighian body/renal corpuscle; Label afferent arteriole, efferent arteriole, Bowman’s capsule, glomerulus, and proximal convoluted tubule; Explain why afferent arteriole is wider than efferent arteriole. Exam-critical labels: Afferent arteriole, Efferent arteriole, Bowman’s capsule, Glomerulus, Proximal convoluted tubule, Bowman’s space. Common mistakes: Calling the glomerulus the Bowman’s capsule (correct: The glomerulus is the capillary tuft inside Bowman’s capsule); Labeling the space inside the capsule as the glomerulus (correct: That space is Bowman’s space; the glomerulus is the capillary network within it); Showing the efferent arteriole larger than the afferent arteriole (correct: The afferent arteriole is usually wider than the efferent arteriole).

kebo116-d005.content: Diagram: Nephron showing blood vessels, tubule, loop of Henle, vasa recta, and collecting duct (diagram). Caption: Figure 16.3: A diagrammatic representation of a nephron showing blood vessels, duct and tubule. The diagram illustrates the structure of a nephron, the functional unit of the kidney. It starts with the Glomerulus (a cluster of capillaries) enclosed in Bowman's capsule. The Afferent arteriole enters the glomerulus, and the Efferent arteriole exits. The tubule continues from Bowman's capsule as the Proximal convoluted tubule, then becomes the Distal convoluted tubule. A loop of Henle (Henle's loop) is shown, consisting of a Descending limb and an Ascending limb. The Vasa recta (a network of capillaries) is shown surrounding the loop of Henle. The tubule finally empties into the Collecting duct. Description: A nephron is the structural and functional unit of the kidney, and this diagram should show both the renal corpuscle and the renal tubule with its associated blood vessels. Blood enters the glomerulus through the afferent arteriole, which is a branch of the renal artery, and leaves through the efferent arteriole. The glomerulus is a tuft of fenestrated capillaries enclosed by Bowman’s capsule; together they form the renal corpuscle, where ultrafiltration of blood occurs. The filtrate then passes into the proximal convoluted tubule (PCT), which is highly coiled and is the main site of selective reabsorption of water, glucose, amino acids, and ions. The tubule then descends into the loop of Henle, which has a descending limb and an ascending limb; the descending limb is permeable mainly to water, while the ascending limb is relatively impermeable to water and helps establish the medullary osmotic gradient. After the loop, the filtrate enters the distal convoluted tubule (DCT), which is also coiled and participates in selective secretion and fine regulation of ions and pH. The DCT opens into a collecting duct, which receives fluid from multiple nephrons and carries urine toward the renal pelvis. Closely associated with the loop of Henle is the vasa recta, a straight capillary network derived from the efferent arteriole of juxtamedullary nephrons; it runs parallel to the loop and helps maintain the medullary concentration gradient by countercurrent exchange. The overall diagram should therefore depict a renal corpuscle at one end, a coiled PCT, a U-shaped loop of Henle descending into the medulla and returning upward, a coiled DCT, and a vertical collecting duct, with afferent and efferent arterioles and the vasa recta shown as the vascular supply around the tubule. Labels: Afferent arteriole, Efferent arteriole, Glomerulus, Bowman’s capsule, Proximal convoluted tubule, Descending limb of loop of Henle, Ascending limb of loop of Henle, Henle's loop, Distal convoluted tubule, Vasa recta, Collecting duct. NEET relevance: Identify the parts of a nephron and trace the path of filtrate.; Differentiate afferent vs efferent arteriole.; State the functions of PCT, loop of Henle, DCT, collecting duct, and vasa recta.. Board relevance: Draw and label a nephron showing blood vessels, tubule, loop of Henle, vasa recta, and collecting duct.; Explain the role of each segment in urine formation.; Differentiate cortical and juxtamedullary nephrons.. Exam-critical labels: Bowman’s capsule, Glomerulus, Afferent arteriole, Efferent arteriole, Proximal convoluted tubule, Descending limb of loop of Henle, Ascending limb of loop of Henle, Distal convoluted tubule. Common mistakes: Calling the glomerulus a capillary network outside Bowman’s capsule (correct: The glomerulus is a capillary tuft enclosed within Bowman’s capsule); Labeling the vasa recta as the loop of Henle or vice versa (correct: The loop of Henle is the U-shaped nephron tubule; the vasa recta are the straight blood capillaries running alongside it); Placing the collecting duct before the distal convoluted tubule (correct: The distal convoluted tubule drains into the collecting duct).

kebo117-s00.section_title: Introduction + LOCOMOTION AND MOVEMENT + 17.1 Types of Movement + 17.2 Muscle + 17.3 Skeletal System + 17.4 Joints + 17.5 Disorders of Muscular and Skeletal System
kebo117-s00.content:
Movement is one of the significant features of living beings. Animals and plants exhibit a wide range of movements. Streaming of protoplasm in the unicellular organisms like *Amoeba* is a simple form of movement. Movement of cilia, flagella and tentacles are shown by many organisms. Human beings can move limbs, jaws, eyelids, tongue, etc. Some of the movements result in a change of place or location. Such voluntary movements are called **locomotion**. Walking, running, climbing, flying and swimming are all some forms of locomotory movements. Locomotory structures need not be different from those affecting other types of movements. For example, in *Paramoecium*, cilia helps in the movement of food through cytopharynx and in locomotion as well. *Hydra* can use its tentacles for capturing its prey and also use them for locomotion. We use limbs for changes in body postures and locomotion as well. The above observations suggest that movements and locomotion cannot be studied separately. The two may be linked by stating that all locomotions are movements but all movements are not locomotions.

Methods of locomotion performed by animals vary with their habitats and the demand of the situation. However, locomotion is generally for search of food, shelter, mate, suitable breeding grounds, favourable climatic conditions or to escape from enemies/predators.

kebo117-s07.content:
Cells of the human body exhibit three main types of movements, namely, amoeboid, ciliary and muscular.

Some specialised cells in our body like macrophages and leucocytes in blood exhibit amoeboid movement. It is effected by pseudopodia formed by the streaming of protoplasm (as in *Amoeba*). Cytoskeletal elements like microfilaments are also involved in amoeboid movement.

Ciliary movement occurs in most of our internal tubular organs which are lined by ciliated epithelium. The coordinated movements of cilia in the trachea help us in removing dust particles and some of the foreign substances inhaled along with the atmospheric air. Passage of ova through the female reproductive tract is also facilitated by the ciliary movement.

Movement of our limbs, jaws, tongue, etc, require muscular movement. The contractile property of muscles are effectively used for locomotion and other movements by human beings and majority of multicellular organisms. Locomotion requires a perfect coordinated activity of muscular, skeletal and neural systems. In this chapter, you will learn about the types of muscles, their structure, mechanism of their contraction and important aspects of the skeletal system.

kebo117-s08.content:
You have studied in Chapter 8 that the cilia and flagella are the outgrowths of the cell membrane. **Flagellar movement** helps in the swimming of spermatozoa, maintenance of water current in the canal system of sponges and in locomotion of Protists like *Euglena*. Muscle is a specialised tissue of mesodermal origin. About 40-50 per cent of the body weight of a human adult is contributed by muscles. They have special properties like excitability, contractility, extensibility and elasticity. Muscles have been classified using different criteria, namely location, appearance and nature of regulation of their activities. Based on their location, three types of muscles are identified : (i) Skeletal (ii) Visceral and (iii) Cardiac.

**Skeletal muscles** are closely associated with the skeletal components of the body. They have a striped appearance under the microscope and hence are called **striated muscles**. As their activities are under the voluntary control of the nervous system, they are known as voluntary muscles too. They are primarily involved in locomotory actions and changes of body postures.

**Visceral muscles** are located in the inner walls of hollow visceral organs of the body like the alimentary canal, reproductive tract, etc. They do not exhibit any striation and are smooth in appearance. Hence, they are called **smooth muscles (nonstriated muscle)**. Their activities are not under the voluntary control of the nervous system and are therefore known as involuntary muscles. They assist, for example, in the transportation of food through the digestive tract and gametes through the genital tract.

As the name suggests, **Cardiac muscles** are the muscles of heart. Many cardiac muscle cells assemble in a branching pattern to form a cardiac muscle. Based on appearance, cardiac muscles are striated. They are involuntary in nature as the nervous system does not control their activities directly.

Let us examine a skeletal muscle in detail to understand the structure and mechanism of contraction. Each organised skeletal muscle in our body is made of a number of **muscle bundles** or **fascicles** held together by a common collagenous connective tissue layer called **fascia**. Each muscle bundle contains a number of muscle fibres (Figure 17.1). Each

**Figure 17.1** Diagrammatic cross sectional view of a muscle showing muscle bundles and muscle fibres

muscle fibre is lined by the plasma membrane called sarcolemma enclosing the sarcoplasm. Muscle fibre is a syncitium as the sarcoplasm contains many nuclei. The endoplasmic reticulum, i.e., sarcoplasmic reticulum of the muscle fibres is the store house of calcium ions. A characteristic feature of the muscle fibre is the presence of a large number of parallelly arranged filaments in the sarcoplasm called myofilaments or **myofibrils**. Each myofibril has alternate dark and light bands on it. A detailed study of the myofibril has established that the striated appearance is due to the distribution pattern of two important proteins – **Actin** and **Myosin**. The light bands contain actin and is called I-band or Isotropic band, whereas the dark band called 'A' or Anisotropic band contains

myosin. Both the proteins are arranged as rod-like structures, parallel to each other and also to the longitudinal axis of the myofibrils. Actin filaments are thinner as compared to the myosin filaments, hence are commonly called thin and thick filaments respectively. In the centre of each 'I' band is an elastic fibre called 'Z' line which bisects it. The thin filaments are firmly attached to the 'Z' line. The thick filaments in the 'A' band are also held together in the middle of this band by a thin fibrous membrane called 'M' line. The 'A' and 'I' bands are arranged alternately throughout the length of the myofibrils. The portion of the myofibril between two successive 'Z' lines is considered as the functional unit of contraction and is called a sarcomere (Figure 17.2). In a resting state, the edges of thin filaments on either side of the thick filaments partially overlap the free ends of the thick filaments leaving the central part of the thick filaments. This central part of thick filament, not overlapped by thin filaments is called the 'H' zone.

Figure 17.2 consists of two parts. Part (a) is an electron micrograph showing the striated pattern of a muscle fibre, with alternating dark and light bands. Part (b) is a schematic diagram of a sarcomere, the basic contractile unit. It shows two vertical black lines representing the Z lines at the ends. Between the Z lines, thick red filaments (myosin) are arranged in the center, forming the A band. Thin red filaments (actin) are attached to the Z lines and extend towards the center, partially overlapping the thick filaments. The central region of the thick filaments that is not overlapped by thin filaments is labeled as the H zone. The I band is the region at the ends of the sarcomere containing only thin filaments. The entire segment between two Z lines is labeled as a Sarcomere.

**Figure 17.2** Diagrammatic representation of (a) anatomy of a muscle fibre showing a sarcomere (b) a sarcomere

kebo117-s09.content:
Each actin (thin) filament is made of two 'F' (filamentous) actins helically wound to each other. Each 'F' actin is a polymer of monomeric 'G' (Globular) actins. Two filaments of another protein, tropomyosin also run close to the 'F' actins throughout its length. A complex protein Troponin is distributed at regular intervals on the tropomyosin. In the resting state a subunit of troponin masks the active binding sites for myosin on the actin filaments (Figure 17.3a).

Each myosin (thick) filament is also a polymerised protein. Many monomeric proteins called Meromyosins (Figure 17.3b) constitute one thick filament. Each meromyosin has two important parts, a globular head with a short arm and a tail, the former being called the heavy meromyosin (HMM) and the latter, the light meromyosin (LMM). The HMM component, i.e., the head and short arm projects outwards at regular distance and angle from each other from the surface of a polymerised myosin filament and is known as cross arm. The globular head is an active ATPase enzyme and has binding sites for ATP and active sites for actin.

Figure 17.3(a) shows an actin (thin) filament, which is a double helix of globular actin subunits (F actin). Two strands of tropomyosin (brown) run along the length of the filament, and troponin complexes (red spheres) are attached at regular intervals. Figure 17.3(b) shows a myosin monomer (Meromyosin), consisting of a globular head (HMM) and a long tail (LMM). The head is labeled 'Head' and 'Cross arm'. It contains 'Actin binding sites' and 'ATP binding sites'.

**Figure 17.3** (a) An actin (thin) filament (b) Myosin monomer (Meromyosin)

kebo117-s10.content:
Mechanism of muscle contraction is best explained by the sliding filament theory which states that contraction of a muscle fibre takes place by the sliding of the thin filaments over the thick filaments.

Muscle contraction is initiated by a signal sent by the central nervous system (CNS) via a motor neuron. A motor neuron along with the muscle fibres connected to it constitute a motor unit. The junction between a motor neuron and the sarcolemma of the muscle fibre is called the neuromuscular junction or motor-end plate. A neural signal reaching this junction releases a neurotransmitter (Acetyl choline) which generates an action potential in the sarcolemma. This spreads through the muscle fibre and causes the release of calcium ions into the sarcoplasm. Increase in  $\text{Ca}^{++}$  level leads to the binding of calcium with a subunit of troponin on actin filaments and thereby remove the masking of active sites for myosin. Utilising the energy from ATP hydrolysis, the myosin head now binds to the exposed active sites on actin to form a cross bridge (Figure 17.4).

Diagram illustrating the four stages of the cross-bridge cycle in muscle contraction. The cycle is shown in a clockwise direction. 1. Formation of cross bridge: A myosin head (part of a myosin filament) binds to an actin filament (part of an actin filament), forming a cross bridge. This process is labeled '(Formation of cross bridge)'. 2. Sliding/rotation: The myosin head undergoes a conformational change, pulling the actin filament. This process is labeled 'Sliding/rotation'. 3. Breaking of cross bridge: The myosin head releases the actin filament, breaking the cross bridge. This process is labeled '(Breaking of cross bridge)'. 4. Reset: The myosin head returns to its initial state, ready to bind again. The diagram shows ATP being hydrolyzed to ADP and P\_i, and ADP and P\_i being released. The labels 'Actin filament', 'Myosin filament', 'ATP', 'ADP', and 'P\_i' are present throughout the cycle.

**Figure 17.4** Stages in cross bridge formation, rotation of head and breaking of cross bridge

This pulls the attached actin filaments towards the centre of 'A' band. The 'Z' line attached to these actins are also pulled inwards thereby causing a shortening of the sarcomere, i.e., contraction. It is clear from the above steps, that during shortening of the muscle, i.e., contraction, the 'I' bands get reduced, whereas the 'A' bands retain the length (Figure 17.5). The myosin, releasing the ADP and  $\text{P}_i$  goes back to its relaxed state. A new ATP binds and the cross-bridge is broken (Figure 17.4). The ATP is again hydrolysed by the myosin head and the cycle of cross bridge formation

The diagram illustrates the sliding-filament theory of muscle contraction across three states: Relaxed, Contracting, and Maximally Contracted. The diagram shows two sarcomeres with labels for H zone, I band, A band, and Z line. In the relaxed state, the H zone and I band are wide. As the muscle contracts, the H zone and I band narrow, and the Z lines move closer together. In the maximally contracted state, the H zone is absent and the I band is at its narrowest.

**Figure 17.5** Sliding-filament theory of muscle contraction (movement of the thin filaments and the relative size of the I band and H zones)

and breakage is repeated causing further sliding. The process continues till the  $\text{Ca}^{++}$  ions are pumped back to the sarcoplasmic cisternae resulting in the masking of actin filaments. This causes the return of 'Z' lines back to their original position, i.e., relaxation. The reaction time of the fibres can vary in different muscles. Repeated activation of the muscles can lead to the accumulation of lactic acid due to anaerobic breakdown of glycogen in them, causing fatigue. Muscle contains a red coloured oxygen storing pigment called myoglobin. Myoglobin content is high in some of the muscles which gives a reddish appearance. Such muscles are called the Red fibres. These muscles also contain plenty of mitochondria which can utilise the large amount of oxygen stored in them for ATP production. These muscles, therefore, can also be called aerobic muscles. On the other hand, some of the muscles possess very less quantity of myoglobin and therefore, appear pale or whitish. These are the White fibres. Number of mitochondria are also few in them, but the amount of sarcoplasmic reticulum is high. They depend on anaerobic process for energy.

kebo117-s11.content:
*Skeletal system consists of a framework of bones and a few cartilages. This system has a significant role in movement shown by the body. Imagine chewing food without jaw bones and walking around without the limb bones. Bone and cartilage are specialised connective tissues. The former has a very hard matrix due to calcium salts in it and the latter has slightly pliable matrix due to chondroitin salts. In human beings, this system is made up of 206 bones and a few cartilages. It is grouped into two principal divisions – the axial and the appendicular skeleton.*

**Axial skeleton** comprises 80 bones distributed along the main axis of the body. The skull, vertebral column, sternum and ribs constitute axial skeleton. The **skull** (Figure 17.6) is composed of two

A sagittal section diagram of a human skull. The diagram is color-coded: the cranium is light orange, the facial bones are light pink, and the mandible and hyoid bone are light green. Labels with leader lines point to the following structures:

- Frontal bone (forehead)
- Sphenoid bone (central base of the skull)
- Ethmoid bone (between the orbits)
- Lacrimal bone (inner corner of the eye)
- Nasal bone (bridge of the nose)
- Zygomatic bone (cheekbone)
- Maxilla (upper jaw)
- Mandible (lower jaw)
- Hyoid bone (U-shaped bone in the neck)
- Occipital condyle (base of the skull)
- Occipital bone (back of the skull)
- Temporal bone (side of the skull)
- Parietal bone (top of the skull)

**Figure 17.6** Diagrammatic view of human skull

*sets of bones – cranial and facial, that totals to 22 bones. Cranial bones are 8 in number. They form the hard protective outer covering, cranium for the brain. The facial region is made up of 14 skeletal elements which form the front part of the skull. A single U-shaped bone called hyoid is present at the base of the buccal cavity. Each middle ear contains three tiny bones – Malleus, Incus and Stapes, collectively called **Ear Ossicles**. The skull region articulates with the superior region of the*

vertebral column with the help of two occipital condyles (dicondylar skull).

Our **vertebral column** (Figure 17.7) is formed by 26 serially arranged units called vertebrae and is dorsally placed. It extends from the base of the skull and constitutes the main framework of the trunk. Each vertebra has a central hollow portion (neural canal) through which the spinal cord passes. First vertebra is the atlas and it articulates with the occipital condyles. The vertebral column is differentiated into cervical (7), thoracic (12), lumbar (5), sacral (1-fused) and coccygeal (1-fused) regions starting from the skull. The number of cervical vertebrae are seven in almost all mammals including human beings. The vertebral column protects the spinal cord, supports the head and serves as the point of attachment for the ribs and musculature of the back. **Sternum** is a flat bone on the ventral midline of thorax.

There are 12 pairs of ribs. Each rib is a thin flat bone connected dorsally to the vertebral column and ventrally to the sternum. It has two articulation surfaces on its dorsal end and is hence called bicephalic. First seven pairs of ribs are called true ribs. Dorsally, they are attached to the thoracic vertebrae and ventrally connected to the sternum with the help of hyaline cartilage. The 8<sup>th</sup>, 9<sup>th</sup> and 10<sup>th</sup> pairs of ribs do not articulate directly with the sternum but join the seventh rib with the help of hyaline cartilage. These are called vertebrochondral (false) ribs. Last 2 pairs (11<sup>th</sup> and 12<sup>th</sup>) of ribs are not connected ventrally and are therefore, called floating ribs. Thoracic vertebrae, ribs and sternum together form the rib cage (Figure 17.8).

The bones of the limbs along with their girdles constitute the **appendicular skeleton**. Each **limb** is made of 30 bones. The bones of the hand (fore limb) are

A diagram showing the human vertebral column from a right lateral perspective. The column is shown with its characteristic curves. Labels with leader lines point to specific regions and structures: 'Cervical vertebra' at the top, 'Thoracic vertebra' in the upper middle section, 'Intervertebral disc' between two vertebrae, 'Lumbar vertebra' in the lower middle section, 'Sacrum' at the base of the lumbar region, and 'Coccyx' at the very bottom.

Figure 17.7 Vertebral column (right lateral view)

Figure 17.8 Ribs and rib cage

A detailed anatomical diagram of the right pectoral girdle and upper arm from a frontal view. The diagram shows the following bones and structures labeled from top to bottom: Clavicle, Scapula, Humerus, Radius, Ulna, Carpals, Metacarpals, and Phalanges. The clavicle is shown horizontally at the top, connecting to the scapula. The humerus is the long bone of the upper arm. The radius and ulna are the two bones of the forearm. The hand is composed of the carpals (wrist), metacarpals (palm), and phalanges (fingers).

**Figure 17.9** Right pectoral girdle and upper arm. (frontal view)

A detailed anatomical diagram of the right pelvic girdle and lower limb bones from a frontal view. The diagram shows the following bones and structures labeled from top to bottom: Ilium, Pubis, Ischium, Coxal bone, Sacrum, Femur, Patella, Tibia, Fibula, Tarsals, Metatarsals, and Phalanges. The pelvic girdle is formed by the fusion of the ilium, pubis, and ischium into a single coxal bone. The femur is the long bone of the thigh, with the patella (knee cap) in front of the knee joint. The tibia and fibula are the two bones of the lower leg. The foot is composed of the tarsals (ankle), metatarsals (midfoot), and phalanges (toes).

**Figure 17.10** Right pelvic girdle and lower limb bones (frontal view)

humerus, radius and ulna, carpals (wrist bones – 8 in number), metacarpals (palm bones – 5 in number) and phalanges (digits – 14 in number) (Figure 17.9). Femur (thigh bone – the longest bone), tibia and fibula, tarsals (ankle bones – 7 in number), metatarsals (5 in number) and phalanges (digits – 14 in number) are the bones of the legs (hind limb) (Figure 17.10). A cup shaped bone called patella cover the knee ventrally (knee cap).

**Pectoral and Pelvic girdle** bones help in the articulation of the upper and the lower limbs respectively with the axial skeleton. Each girdle is formed of two halves. Each half of pectoral girdle consists of a clavicle and a scapula (Figure 17.9). Scapula is a large triangular flat bone situated in the dorsal part of the thorax between the second and the seventh ribs. The dorsal, flat, triangular body of scapula has a slightly elevated ridge called the spine which projects as a flat, expanded process called the acromion. The clavicle articulates with this. Below the acromion is a depression called the glenoid cavity which articulates with the head of the humerus to form the shoulder joint. Each clavicle is a long slender bone with two curvatures. This bone is commonly called the collar bone.

Pelvic girdle consists of two coxal bones (Figure 17.10). Each coxal bone is formed by the fusion of three bones – ilium, ischium and pubis. At the point of fusion of the above bones is a cavity called acetabulum to which the thigh bone articulates. The two halves of the pelvic girdle meet ventrally to form the pubic symphysis containing fibrous cartilage.

kebo117-s12.content:
Joints are essential for all types of movements involving the bony parts of the body. Locomotory movements are no exception to

this. Joints are points of contact between bones, or between bones and cartilages. Force generated by the muscles is used to carry out movement through joints, where the joint acts as a fulcrum. The movability at these joints vary depending on different factors. Joints have been classified into three major structural forms, namely, fibrous, cartilaginous and synovial.

**Fibrous joints** do not allow any movement. This type of joint is shown by the flat skull bones which fuse end-to-end with the help of dense fibrous connective tissues in the form of sutures, to form the cranium.

In **cartilaginous joints**, the bones involved are joined together with the help of cartilages. The joint between the adjacent vertebrae in the vertebral column is of this pattern and it permits limited movements.

**Synovial joints** are characterised by the presence of a fluid filled synovial cavity between the articulating surfaces of the two bones. Such an arrangement allows considerable movement. These joints help in locomotion and many other movements. Ball and socket joint (between humerus and pectoral girdle), hinge joint (knee joint), pivot joint (between atlas and axis), gliding joint (between the carpals) and saddle joint (between carpal and metacarpal of thumb) are some examples.

kebo117-s13.section_title: 17.5 DISORDERS OF MUSCULAR AND SKELETAL SYSTEM
kebo117-s13.content:
**Myasthenia gravis:** Auto immune disorder affecting neuromuscular junction leading to fatigue, weakening and paralysis of skeletal muscle.

**Muscular dystrophy:** Progressive degeneration of skeletal muscle mostly due to genetic disorder.

**Tetany:** Rapid spasms (wild contractions) in muscle due to low  $\text{Ca}^{++}$  in body fluid.

**Arthritis:** Inflammation of joints.

**Osteoporosis:** Age-related disorder characterised by decreased bone mass and increased chances of fractures. Decreased levels of estrogen is a common cause.

**Gout:** Inflammation of joints due to accumulation of uric acid crystals.

kebo117-s14.content:
Movement is an essential feature of all living beings. Protoplasmic streaming, ciliary movements, movements of fins, limbs, wings, etc., are some forms exhibited by animals. A voluntary movement which causes the animal to change its place, is

called locomotion. Animals move generally in search of food, shelter, mate, breeding ground, better climate or to protect themselves.

The cells of the human body exhibit amoeboid, ciliary and muscular movements. Locomotion and many other movements require coordinated muscular activities. Three types of muscles are present in our body. Skeletal muscles are attached to skeletal elements. They appear striated and are voluntary in nature. Visceral muscles, present in the inner walls of visceral organs are nonstriated and involuntary. Cardiac muscles are the muscles of the heart. They are striated, branched and involuntary. Muscles possess excitability, contractility, extensibility and elasticity.

Muscle fibre is the anatomical unit of muscle. Each muscle fibre has many parallelly arranged myofibrils. Each myofibril contains many serially arranged units called sarcomere which are the functional units. Each sarcomere has a central 'A' band made of thick myosin filaments, and two half 'I' bands made of thin actin filaments on either side of it marked by 'Z' lines. Actin and myosin are polymerised proteins with contractility. The active sites for myosin on resting actin filament are masked by a protein-troponin. Myosin head contains ATPase and has ATP binding sites and active sites for actin. A motor neuron carries signal to the muscle fibre which generates an action potential in it. This causes the release of  $\text{Ca}^{++}$  from sarcoplasmic reticulum.  $\text{Ca}^{++}$  activates actin which binds to the myosin head to form a cross bridge. These cross bridges pull the actin filaments causing them to slide over the myosin filaments and thereby causing contraction.  $\text{Ca}^{++}$  are then returned to sarcoplasmic reticulum which inactivate the actin. Cross bridges are broken and the muscles relax.

Repeated stimulation of muscles leads to fatigue. Muscles are classified as Red and White fibres based primarily on the amount of red coloured myoglobin pigment in them.

Bones and cartilages constitute our skeletal system. The skeletal system is divisible into axial and appendicular. Skull, vertebral column, ribs and sternum constitute the axial skeleton. Limb bones and girdles form the appendicular skeleton. Three types of joints are formed between bones or between bone and cartilage – fibrous, cartilaginous and synovial. Synovial joints allow considerable movements and therefore, play a significant role in locomotion.

kebo117-d000.content: Diagram: muscle fibre striations and a sarcomere (structural). Caption: Figure 17.2 consists of two parts. Part (a) is an electron micrograph showing the striated pattern of a muscle fibre, with alternating dark and light bands. Part (b) is a schematic diagram of a sarcomere, the basic contractile unit. It shows two vertical black lines representing the Z lines at the ends. Between the Z lines, thick red filaments (myosin) are arranged in the center, forming the A band. Thin red filaments (actin) are attached to the Z lines and extend towards the center, partially overlapping the thick filaments. The central region of the thick filaments that is not overlapped by thin filaments is labeled as the H zone. The I band is the region at the ends of the sarcomere containing only thin filaments. The entire segment between two Z lines is labeled as a Sarcomere. Description: The diagram should represent the striated organization of skeletal muscle at two levels: an electron micrograph showing alternating dark and light bands along a myofibril, and a schematic sarcomere, the basic structural and functional contractile unit of striated muscle. A sarcomere extends from one Z line (Z disc) to the next Z line. Thin filaments (actin) are anchored to the Z line and project inward toward the center, while thick filaments (myosin) occupy the central region. The A band corresponds to the full length of the thick filaments and includes the regions where thin and thick filaments overlap; it appears darker. The I band is the lighter region on either side of the Z line and contains only thin filaments. The H zone is the lighter central part of the A band where only thick filaments are present and thin filaments do not overlap. During contraction, thin filaments slide inward over thick filaments, shortening the I band and H zone while the A band remains essentially constant in length. The micrograph should therefore show repeating sarcomeres with alternating dark A bands and light I bands, and the schematic should clearly depict Z lines at both ends, thin filaments extending from Z lines toward the center, thick filaments centered in the sarcomere, overlap regions, and the central H zone. Labels: Z line (Z disc), Sarcomere, A band, I band, H zone, Thin filament (actin), Thick filament (myosin). NEET relevance: Identify and label Z line, A band, I band, H zone, and sarcomere.; Explain the sliding filament mechanism and which bands change during contraction.; State that the sarcomere is the functional unit of skeletal muscle.. Board relevance: Draw and label a sarcomere.; Explain striations in skeletal muscle.; Describe the arrangement of actin and myosin filaments.. Exam-critical labels: Z line, Sarcomere, A band, I band, H zone, Thin filament (actin), Thick filament (myosin). Common mistakes: Calling the Z line the center of the sarcomere (correct: The Z line is the boundary at each end of the sarcomere); Labeling the H zone as the entire A band (correct: The H zone is only the central lighter part of the A band); Thinking the I band contains thick filaments (correct: The I band contains only thin filaments).

kebo117-d001.content: Diagram: sliding-filament theory of muscle contraction showing sarcomeres in relaxed, contracting, and maximally contracted states (diagram). Caption: The diagram illustrates the sliding-filament theory of muscle contraction across three states: Relaxed, Contracting, and Maximally Contracted. The diagram shows two sarcomeres with labels for H zone, I band, A band, and Z line. In the relaxed state, the H zone and I band are wide. As the muscle contracts, the H zone and I band narrow, and the Z lines move closer together. In the maximally contracted state, the H zone is absent and the I band is at its narrowest. Description: This diagram should represent the sliding-filament theory of skeletal muscle contraction at the level of sarcomeres, the functional contractile units of myofibrils. A sarcomere extends from one Z line (Z disc) to the next Z line, and contains thin actin filaments anchored to the Z lines and thick myosin filaments centered around the M line within the A band. During contraction, the thin filaments slide inward over the thick filaments without either filament shortening; as a result, the Z lines move closer together, the I band shortens, and the H zone narrows and may disappear at maximal contraction. The A band remains constant in length because it corresponds to the full length of the thick filaments. In the relaxed state, the H zone and I band are widest; in the contracting state, overlap between actin and myosin increases; and in the maximally contracted state, the H zone is absent, the I band is minimal, and the Z lines are closest together. The diagram should show two adjacent sarcomeres across these three states to emphasize that contraction occurs by sliding of filaments, not by shortening of the filaments themselves. Labels: Z line (Z disc), I band, A band, H zone, M line, Thin filament (actin), Thick filament (myosin), Relaxed, Contracting, Maximally contracted. NEET relevance: Identification of sarcomere parts: A band, I band, H zone, Z line, M line; What changes and what remains constant during contraction; Mechanism of sliding-filament theory. Board relevance: Draw and label the sarcomere in relaxed and contracted states; Explain sliding-filament theory with diagram; Differentiate A band, I band, and H zone. Exam-critical labels: Z line, A band, I band, H zone, M line, Thin filament (actin), Thick filament (myosin), Relaxed. Common mistakes: Saying the A band shortens during contraction (correct: The A band remains constant in length); Calling the H zone the same as the I band (correct: The H zone is the central part of the A band; the I band is the region adjacent to the Z line containing only thin filaments); Placing the Z line in the middle of the A band (correct: The Z line forms the boundary of each sarcomere and lies in the center of the I band).

kebo117-d002.content: Diagram: Human right pectoral girdle and upper arm skeleton from a frontal view, including shoulder girdle, arm, forearm, and hand bones (diagram). Caption: A detailed anatomical diagram of the right pectoral girdle and upper arm from a frontal view. The diagram shows the following bones and structures labeled from top to bottom: Clavicle, Scapula, Humerus, Radius, Ulna, Carpals, Metacarpals, and Phalanges. The clavicle is shown horizontally at the top, connecting to the scapula. The humerus is the long bone of the upper arm. The radius and ulna are the two bones of the forearm. The hand is composed of the carpals (wrist), metacarpals (palm), and phalanges (fingers). Description: This diagram represents the right pectoral girdle and upper limb skeleton in frontal view. The pectoral girdle consists of the clavicle and scapula, which attach the upper limb to the axial skeleton and provide mobility at the shoulder. The clavicle is a slender, S-shaped bone placed superiorly and horizontally, articulating medially with the sternum and laterally with the acromion of the scapula. The scapula is a flat, triangular bone lying posterolaterally over the upper ribs; in a frontal view only its general outline and key processes may be indicated. The humerus is the long bone of the arm, extending from the shoulder joint to the elbow. At the elbow, the humerus articulates with the two forearm bones: the radius on the lateral side (thumb side) and the ulna on the medial side (little-finger side). Distally, the radius and ulna articulate with the carpal bones of the wrist. The hand skeleton is composed of carpals in the wrist, metacarpals in the palm, and phalanges in the fingers. Functionally, this arrangement provides a highly mobile shoulder, a stable yet flexible forearm for pronation and supination, and a hand adapted for grasping and fine manipulation. Labels: Clavicle, Scapula, Humerus, Radius, Ulna, Carpals, Metacarpals, Phalanges. NEET relevance: Identification of bones of the pectoral girdle and upper limb; Difference between radius and ulna in anatomical position; Sequence of bones from shoulder to fingers. Board relevance: Draw and label the right pectoral girdle and upper arm skeleton; Label clavicle, scapula, humerus, radius, ulna, carpals, metacarpals, and phalanges; State the role of the pectoral girdle in supporting the upper limb. Exam-critical labels: Clavicle, Scapula, Humerus, Radius, Ulna, Carpals, Metacarpals, Phalanges. Common mistakes: Labeling the scapula as the clavicle or vice versa (correct: The clavicle is the superior horizontal bone; the scapula is the flat triangular shoulder blade below it); Swapping radius and ulna (correct: Radius is lateral (thumb side) and ulna is medial (little-finger side) in anatomical position); Calling the wrist bones 'metacarpals' or the palm bones 'carpals' (correct: Carpals are the wrist bones; metacarpals are the palm bones).

kebo117-d003.content: Diagram: Human vertebral column in right lateral view, with associated rib cage outline (diagram). Caption: A diagram showing the human vertebral column from a right lateral perspective. The column is shown with its characteristic curves. Labels with leader lines point to specific regions and structures: 'Cervical vertebra' at the top, 'Thoracic vertebra' in the upper middle section, 'Intervertebral disc' between two vertebrae, 'Lumbar vertebra' in the lower middle section, 'Sacrum' at the base of the lumbar region, and 'Coccyx' at the very bottom. Description: The diagram should represent the human vertebral column in right lateral view, showing the normal spinal curvatures and the regional division of vertebrae from superior to inferior: 7 cervical vertebrae in the neck region, 12 thoracic vertebrae in the thoracic region articulating with the ribs, 5 lumbar vertebrae in the lower back, followed by the sacrum formed by fusion of five sacral vertebrae, and the coccyx formed by fusion of 3–5 rudimentary coccygeal vertebrae. The vertebral column is a flexible, weight-bearing axial skeleton that protects the spinal cord, supports the head and trunk, provides attachment for ribs and muscles, and transmits body weight to the pelvic girdle. In lateral view, it shows four physiological curvatures: cervical and lumbar lordoses (anteriorly convex) and thoracic and sacral kyphoses (posteriorly convex). Intervertebral discs lie between adjacent vertebral bodies from the axis region down to the sacrum, acting as fibrocartilaginous shock absorbers and permitting limited movement. The thoracic region should be shown associated with the rib cage outline, because ribs articulate with thoracic vertebrae. The diagram must emphasize the continuous stacked vertebral bodies and the regional transitions rather than individual vertebral details. Labels: Cervical vertebrae, Thoracic vertebrae, Intervertebral disc, Lumbar vertebrae, Sacrum, Coccyx, Rib cage outline. NEET relevance: Identification of vertebral regions and their counts; Functions of intervertebral discs; Physiological curvatures of the vertebral column. Board relevance: Draw and label the vertebral column in lateral view; Mark cervical, thoracic, lumbar vertebrae, sacrum, coccyx, and intervertebral disc; Explain the S-shaped curvature and its significance. Exam-critical labels: Cervical vertebrae, Thoracic vertebrae, Lumbar vertebrae, Intervertebral disc, Sacrum, Coccyx, Rib cage outline. Common mistakes: Labeling the entire upper spine as 'cervical vertebra' instead of 'cervical vertebrae'. (correct: Use 'cervical vertebrae' for the cervical region, or specify individual cervical vertebrae only if the diagram shows them.); Calling the rib cage outline part of the vertebral column. (correct: The ribs are separate bones that articulate with thoracic vertebrae; they are not part of the vertebral column.); Placing the sacrum above the lumbar vertebrae. (correct: The sacrum lies below the lumbar vertebrae and above the coccyx.).

kebo117-d004.content: Diagram: Structure of an actin (thin) filament and a myosin monomer (meromyosin) (structural). Caption: Figure 17.3(a) shows an actin (thin) filament, which is a double helix of globular actin subunits (F actin). Two strands of tropomyosin (brown) run along the length of the filament, and troponin complexes (red spheres) are attached at regular intervals. Figure 17.3(b) shows a myosin monomer (Meromyosin), consisting of a globular head (HMM) and a long tail (LMM). The head is labeled 'Head' and 'Cross arm'. It contains 'Actin binding sites' and 'ATP binding sites'. Description: The diagram should represent two distinct structures from the contractile apparatus of skeletal muscle: (a) a thin filament and (b) a myosin monomer (meromyosin). In the thin filament, F-actin is a double-helical polymer of globular G-actin subunits arranged as two intertwined strands. Each G-actin subunit bears a myosin-binding site, and these sites are periodically covered by tropomyosin, a long fibrous protein that lies in the groove of the actin helix. Troponin complexes are attached at regular intervals to tropomyosin and act as the regulatory calcium-sensitive component that shifts tropomyosin away from actin-binding sites during contraction. In the myosin monomer, the molecule consists of a long fibrous tail (light meromyosin, LMM) and a globular head region (heavy meromyosin, HMM). The head contains the actin-binding site and the ATPase/ATP-binding site, which are essential for cross-bridge formation and energy-dependent movement. The tail forms the rod-like portion that contributes to thick filament assembly. The diagram must clearly distinguish the regulatory proteins on the thin filament from the functional domains of myosin on the thick filament precursor. Labels: F-actin, Tropomyosin, Troponin, Actin-binding site, ATP-binding site, Head (HMM), Cross arm, Tail (LMM). NEET relevance: Identify the components of the thin filament: F-actin, tropomyosin, and troponin.; State the role of troponin in calcium-mediated regulation of contraction.; Locate actin-binding and ATPase sites on myosin.. Board relevance: Draw and label the structure of an actin (thin) filament.; Draw and label a myosin monomer (meromyosin).; Explain the arrangement of tropomyosin and troponin on F-actin.. Exam-critical labels: F-actin, Tropomyosin, Troponin, Actin-binding site, Head (HMM), Tail (LMM), ATP-binding site. Common mistakes: Calling tropomyosin the central filament backbone (correct: F-actin is the backbone; tropomyosin lies in its groove); Labeling troponin as part of actin subunits (correct: Troponin is a separate regulatory complex attached periodically to tropomyosin); Putting ATP-binding site on the myosin tail (correct: ATP-binding/ATPase site is on the myosin head).

kebo117-d005.content: Diagram: diagrammatic cross-sectional view of a skeletal muscle showing fascicles, muscle fibres, sarcolemma, and blood capillaries (cross_section). Caption: A detailed diagrammatic cross-section of a skeletal muscle. The muscle is shown as a large, roughly circular structure. It is composed of several fascicles, which are bundles of muscle fibres. Each fascicle is surrounded by a layer of connective tissue. Within each fascicle, individual muscle fibres are visible. Each muscle fibre is a long, cylindrical cell with multiple nuclei. The outer membrane of a muscle fibre is the sarcolemma. Between the fascicles, there are blood capillaries and connective tissue. Labels with leader lines point to a 'Muscle fibre (muscle cell)', 'Sarcolemma', 'Blood capillary', and 'Fascicle (muscle bundle)'. Description: This diagram should represent the hierarchical organization of skeletal muscle in cross-section, with the whole muscle enclosed by an outer connective tissue sheath and subdivided into fascicles (muscle bundles). Each fascicle contains many individual muscle fibres (muscle cells), which are long, cylindrical, multinucleated cells. In cross-section, the fibres appear as polygonal or rounded profiles packed closely together. Each muscle fibre is bounded by the sarcolemma, the plasma membrane of the muscle cell, which lies just inside the thin connective tissue surrounding the fibre. Blood capillaries run in the connective tissue spaces between fibres and fascicles to supply oxygen and nutrients and remove wastes. The diagram should emphasize that fascicles are bundles of fibres, fibres are the cellular units, and capillaries are external to the fibres but within the muscle connective tissue framework. Labels: Fascicle (muscle bundle), Muscle fibre (muscle cell), Sarcolemma, Blood capillary, Whole skeletal muscle. NEET relevance: Hierarchy of skeletal muscle organization: muscle -> fascicle -> muscle fibre; Identification of sarcolemma as the membrane of a muscle fibre; Location and role of blood capillaries in muscle tissue. Board relevance: Draw and label the cross-sectional view of skeletal muscle; Label fascicle, muscle fibre, sarcolemma, and blood capillary; Explain the structural organization of skeletal muscle. Exam-critical labels: Fascicle (muscle bundle), Muscle fibre (muscle cell), Sarcolemma, Blood capillary. Common mistakes: Calling the outer boundary a sarcolemma (correct: The outer boundary of the whole muscle is connective tissue (epimysium), while sarcolemma surrounds only each muscle fibre); Labeling a single muscle fibre as a fascicle (correct: A fascicle is a bundle of many muscle fibres); Placing blood capillaries inside the muscle fibre (correct: Capillaries are in the connective tissue spaces around fibres, not inside the fibre).

kebo117-d006.content: Diagram: stages of cross bridge formation, rotation of myosin head, and breaking of cross bridge during muscle contraction between actin filament and myosin filament (diagram). Caption: Diagram illustrating the four stages of the cross-bridge cycle in muscle contraction. The cycle is shown in a clockwise direction. 1. Formation of cross bridge: A myosin head (part of a myosin filament) binds to an actin filament (part of an actin filament), forming a cross bridge. This process is labeled '(Formation of cross bridge)'. 2. Sliding/rotation: The myosin head undergoes a conformational change, pulling the actin filament. This process is labeled 'Sliding/rotation'. 3. Breaking of cross bridge: The myosin head releases the actin filament, breaking the cross bridge. This process is labeled '(Breaking of cross bridge)'. 4. Reset: The myosin head returns to its initial state, ready to bind again. The diagram shows ATP being hydrolyzed to ADP and P\_i, and ADP and P\_i being released. The labels 'Actin filament', 'Myosin filament', 'ATP', 'ADP', and 'P\_i' are present throughout the cycle. Description: This diagram represents the cross-bridge cycle of skeletal muscle contraction between a thin actin filament and a thick myosin filament. In the resting state, the myosin head is energized by ATP hydrolysis and carries ADP and inorganic phosphate (Pi). When the myosin-binding site on actin becomes exposed, the energized myosin head attaches to actin, forming a cross bridge. Release of Pi and then ADP strengthens the attachment and triggers the power stroke, during which the myosin head pivots/rotates and pulls the actin filament toward the center of the sarcomere, producing sliding of filaments. A new ATP molecule then binds to the myosin head, causing dissociation of the actin-myosin cross bridge. ATP hydrolysis re-cocks the myosin head, restoring the high-energy state so the cycle can repeat as long as Ca2+ and ATP are available. The diagram should therefore show the cyclical sequence: energized myosin head with ADP + Pi, attachment to actin, power stroke/sliding, and ATP-dependent detachment. Labels: Actin filament, Myosin filament, Myosin head, Cross bridge, ATP, ADP, Inorganic phosphate (Pi), Formation of cross bridge, Sliding/rotation, Breaking of cross bridge. NEET relevance: Identify the stages of the cross-bridge cycle; Explain the role of ATP in muscle contraction and relaxation; Differentiate between cross-bridge formation, power stroke, and detachment. Board relevance: Draw and label the cross-bridge cycle; Explain the sequence of events in muscle contraction; Label actin filament, myosin filament, myosin head, ATP, ADP, Pi, and cross bridge. Exam-critical labels: Actin filament, Myosin filament, Myosin head, Cross bridge, ATP, ADP, Inorganic phosphate (Pi), Formation of cross bridge. Common mistakes: Calling Pi simply 'P' without clarifying it is inorganic phosphate (correct: Inorganic phosphate (Pi)); Labeling the entire thick filament as 'myosin head' (correct: Myosin head is only the projecting globular part of the myosin molecule); Saying ATP is present during cross-bridge attachment as the binding molecule (correct: ADP + Pi are associated with the energized head; ATP binding causes detachment).

kebo117-d007.content: Diagram: Human skull bones shown in a sagittal/lateral sectional view (diagram). Caption: A sagittal section diagram of a human skull. The diagram is color-coded: the cranium is light orange, the facial bones are light pink, and the mandible and hyoid bone are light green. Labels with leader lines point to the following structures: Description: This diagram represents a lateral or sagittal sectional view of the human skull, showing the bony framework of the head. The skull is divided into the cranium, which encloses and protects the brain, and the facial skeleton, which forms the framework of the face, orbits, nasal cavity, and jaws. The cranial vault is formed mainly by the frontal, parietal, temporal, occipital, sphenoid, and ethmoid bones, while the facial skeleton includes the nasal, lacrimal, zygomatic, maxilla, and mandible. The occipital bone forms the posterior and inferior part of the cranium and bears the occipital condyles, which articulate with the atlas (first cervical vertebra). The sphenoid and ethmoid are deep cranial bones contributing to the cranial base and orbital/nasal regions. The mandible is the only movable bone of the skull and forms the lower jaw, whereas the hyoid bone lies inferior to the mandible in the neck and does not articulate directly with other bones. The diagram should emphasize the spatial continuity of the cranial bones, the facial projection anteriorly, and the relationship of the skull to the orbit, nasal cavity, jaw, and upper cervical region. Labels: Frontal bone, Parietal bone, Temporal bone, Occipital bone, Occipital condyle, Sphenoid bone, Ethmoid bone, Lacrimal bone, Nasal bone, Zygomatic bone, Maxilla, Mandible, Hyoid bone. NEET relevance: Identification of skull bones in lateral view; Difference between cranium and facial bones; Function of mandible as the only movable skull bone. Board relevance: Draw and label the human skull in lateral view; Mark major cranial and facial bones; Differentiate upper jaw and lower jaw. Exam-critical labels: Frontal bone, Parietal bone, Temporal bone, Occipital bone, Occipital condyle, Sphenoid bone, Ethmoid bone, Nasal bone. Common mistakes: Labeling the temporal bone as the occipital bone (correct: Temporal bone is the lateral-inferior cranial bone; occipital bone is the posterior-inferior bone); Treating the hyoid bone as part of the skull (correct: The hyoid bone is an independent neck bone below the mandible); Calling the maxilla the mandible or vice versa (correct: Maxilla is the upper jaw; mandible is the lower jaw).

kebo117-d008.content: Diagram: Human ribs and rib cage (diagram). Caption: A diagram of the human rib cage from an anterior (front) view. The sternum is in the center, with ribs extending from it to the vertebral column. The ribs are numbered 1 through 12 on the left side. Brackets on the left group the ribs: 'True ribs' (1-7), 'False ribs' (8-10), and 'Floating ribs' (11-12). Labels on the right point to the 'Sternum', 'Ribs', and 'Vertebral column'. Description: The diagram should show the human thoracic cage (rib cage) in anterior view, consisting of 12 pairs of ribs articulating posteriorly with the thoracic vertebrae and anteriorly with the sternum either directly or indirectly through costal cartilages. The sternum lies in the midline and is divided into the manubrium, body, and xiphoid process. The first 7 pairs of ribs are true ribs because each attaches directly to the sternum via its own costal cartilage. Ribs 8, 9, and 10 are false ribs because their cartilages join the cartilage of the rib above, forming the costal margin indirectly attached to the sternum. Ribs 11 and 12 are floating ribs because they have no anterior attachment to the sternum. The ribs are curved, flattened bones that protect the heart, lungs, and upper abdominal organs, and also assist in respiration by changing thoracic volume during breathing. Posteriorly, all ribs articulate with the thoracic vertebral column; anteriorly, the costal cartilages connect the ribs to the sternum or to adjacent cartilages. The diagram should clearly represent the symmetrical arrangement of the rib pairs around the sternum and the classification into true, false, and floating ribs. Labels: Sternum, Ribs, Vertebral column, True ribs (1–7 pairs), False ribs (8–10 pairs), Floating ribs (11–12 pairs), Costal cartilages, Thoracic cage. NEET relevance: Classification of ribs into true, false, and floating ribs; Number of ribs in humans: 12 pairs; Direct vs indirect attachment to sternum. Board relevance: Draw and label the human rib cage; Differentiate true, false, and floating ribs; State the number of ribs and their attachments. Exam-critical labels: Sternum, Ribs, Vertebral column, True ribs (1–7), False ribs (8–10), Floating ribs (11–12), Costal cartilages. Common mistakes: Calling ribs 8–12 all false ribs (correct: Ribs 8–10 are false ribs; ribs 11–12 are floating ribs.); Showing ribs 11 and 12 attached to the sternum (correct: Ribs 11 and 12 have no anterior attachment and are floating ribs.); Labeling the costal cartilages as ribs (correct: The bony curved parts are ribs; the anterior connecting segments are costal cartilages.).

kebo117-d009.content: Diagram: Right pelvic girdle and lower limb bones of the human skeleton in frontal view (diagram). Caption: A detailed anatomical diagram of the right pelvic girdle and lower limb bones from a frontal view. The diagram shows the following bones and structures labeled from top to bottom: Ilium, Pubis, Ischium, Coxal bone, Sacrum, Femur, Patella, Tibia, Fibula, Tarsals, Metatarsals, and Phalanges. The pelvic girdle is formed by the fusion of the ilium, pubis, and ischium into a single coxal bone. The femur is the long bone of the thigh, with the patella (knee cap) in front of the knee joint. The tibia and fibula are the two bones of the lower leg. The foot is composed of the tarsals (ankle), metatarsals (midfoot), and phalanges (toes). Description: This diagram represents the right pelvic girdle and the right lower limb in frontal (anterior) view. The pelvic girdle is formed by the right coxal bone, which is a single fused bone composed of the ilium, ischium, and pubis; these three parts meet at the acetabulum, the socket that articulates with the head of the femur. Medially, the sacrum is shown as the central axial bone articulating with the pelvic girdle at the sacroiliac region. Inferior to the pelvis, the femur is the thigh bone and the longest bone of the body; its proximal head fits into the acetabulum, and its distal end articulates at the knee with the tibia and patella. The patella is a sesamoid bone embedded in the quadriceps tendon and lies anterior to the knee joint. Below the knee, the tibia is the larger, medial weight-bearing bone of the leg, while the fibula is the slender lateral bone that mainly provides muscle attachment and stability. In the foot, the tarsals form the ankle and posterior foot region, the metatarsals form the midfoot/forefoot long bones, and the phalanges are the toe bones. The diagram should show the bones in correct anatomical order from pelvis to foot, with the femur proximal to the tibia and fibula, and the foot bones arranged distal to the leg bones. Labels: Ilium, Ischium, Pubis, Coxal bone, Sacrum, Femur, Patella, Tibia, Fibula, Tarsals, Metatarsals, Phalanges. NEET relevance: Identification of bones of the pelvic girdle and lower limb; Distinguishing coxal bone from pelvis and naming its three fused parts; Recognizing tibia vs fibula and their medial-lateral positions. Board relevance: Draw and label the right pelvic girdle and lower limb bones in frontal view; Label the coxal bone, sacrum, femur, patella, tibia, fibula, tarsals, metatarsals, and phalanges; Explain that the hip bone is formed by fusion of ilium, ischium, and pubis. Exam-critical labels: Coxal bone, Ilium, Ischium, Pubis, Sacrum, Femur, Patella, Tibia. Common mistakes: Labeling the entire hip region as 'pelvis' instead of 'coxal bone'. (correct: The labeled bone in the diagram is the coxal bone (hip bone), not the entire pelvis.); Placing the fibula on the medial side and the tibia on the lateral side. (correct: The tibia is medial and the fibula is lateral.); Showing the patella as part of the femur or tibia. (correct: The patella is a separate sesamoid bone located anterior to the knee joint.).

kebo118-s00.section_title: Introduction + 18.1 Neural System + 18.2 Human Neural System + 18.3 Neuron as Structural and Functional Unit of Neural System + 18.4 Central Neural System
kebo118-s00.content:
# CHAPTER 18NEURAL CONTROL AND COORDINATION

As you know, the functions of the organs/organ systems in our body must be coordinated to maintain homeostasis. **Coordination** is the process through which two or more organs interact and complement the functions of one another. For example, when we do physical exercises, the energy demand is increased for maintaining an increased muscular activity. The supply of oxygen is also increased. The increased supply of oxygen necessitates an increase in the rate of respiration, heart beat and increased blood flow via blood vessels. When physical exercise is stopped, the activities of nerves, lungs, heart and kidney gradually return to their normal conditions. Thus, the functions of muscles, lungs, heart, blood vessels, kidney and other organs are coordinated while performing physical exercises. In our body the neural system and the endocrine system jointly coordinate and integrate all the activities of the organs so that they function in a synchronised fashion.

The neural system provides an organised network of point-to-point connections for a quick coordination. The endocrine system provides chemical integration through hormones. In this chapter, you will learn about the neural system of human, mechanisms of neural coordination like transmission of nerve impulse and impulse conduction across a synapse.

kebo118-s05.content:
The neural system of all animals is composed of highly specialised cells called **neurons** which can detect, receive and transmit different kinds of stimuli.

The neural organisation is very simple in lower invertebrates. For example, in *Hydra* it is composed of a network of neurons. The neural system is better organised in insects, where a brain is present along with a number of ganglia and neural tissues. The vertebrates have a more developed neural system.

kebo118-s06.content:
The human neural system is divided into two parts :

- (i) the **central neural system** (CNS)
- (ii) the **peripheral neural system** (PNS)

The CNS includes the **brain** and the **spinal cord** and is the site of information processing and control. The PNS comprises of all the nerves of the body associated with the CNS (brain and spinal cord). The nerve fibres of the PNS are of two types :

- (a) **afferent fibres**
- (b) **efferent fibres**

The afferent nerve fibres transmit impulses from tissues/organs to the CNS and the efferent fibres transmit regulatory impulses from the CNS to the concerned peripheral tissues/organs.

The PNS is divided into two divisions called **somatic neural system** and **autonomic neural system**. The somatic neural system relays impulses from the CNS to skeletal muscles while the autonomic neural system transmits impulses from the CNS to the involuntary organs and smooth muscles of the body. The autonomic neural system is further classified into **sympathetic neural system** and **parasympathetic neural system**.
