The anther is a four-sided (tetragonal) structure consisting of four **microsporangia** located at the corners, two in each lobe.

The microsporangia develop further and become **pollen sacs**. They extend longitudinally all through the length of an anther and are packed with pollen grains.

**Structure of microsporangium:** In a transverse section, a typical microsporangium appears near circular in outline. It is generally surrounded by four wall layers (Figure 1.3b)– the epidermis, endothecium, middle layers and the tapetum. The outer three wall layers perform the function of protection and help in dehiscence of anther to release the pollen. The innermost wall layer is the **tapetum**. It nourishes the developing pollen grains. Cells of the tapetum possess dense cytoplasm and generally have more than one nucleus. *Can you think of how tapetal cells could become bi-nucleate?*

When the anther is young, a group of compactly arranged homogenous cells called the **sporogenous tissue** occupies the centre of each microsporangium.

**Microsporogenesis :** As the anther develops, the cells of the sporogenous tissue undergo meiotic divisions to form microspore tetrads. *What would be the ploidy of the cells of the tetrad?*

The image contains two diagrams. Diagram (a) shows a single stamen, consisting of a long, thin, green filament (stalk) and a bilobed anther at the top. Diagram (b) is a three-dimensional cutaway view of an anther, showing its internal structure. It has two lobes, each containing two pollen sacs. The sacs are filled with numerous small, round, pinkish-red pollen grains. A vertical line, labeled 'Line of dehiscence', runs along the length of each lobe, indicating where the anther will split open to release the pollen. The filament (stalk) is shown at the base of the anther.

**Figure 1.2** (a) A typical stamen;  
(b) three-dimensional cut section  
of an anther

Figure 1.3 consists of three diagrams: (a) shows a transverse section of a young anther with five lobes, each containing a microsporangium. Labels point to the Connective tissue between lobes, the outer Epidermis, several Middle layers, the Endothecium, the central Sporogenous tissue, and the inner Tapetum. (b) is an enlarged view of one microsporangium, showing its wall layers: Epidermis, Endothecium, Middle layers, and Tapetum, with Microspore mother cells visible within the sporogenous tissue. (c) shows a mature, dehisced anther with four lobes that have opened to release numerous small, yellowish Pollen grains.

**Figure 1.3** (a) Transverse section of a young anther; (b) Enlarged view of one microsporangium showing wall layers; (c) A mature dehisced anther

As each cell of the sporogenous tissue is capable of giving rise to a microspore tetrad. Each one is a potential pollen or microspore mother cell. The process of formation of microspores from a pollen mother cell (PMC) through meiosis is called **microsporogenesis**. The microspores, as they are formed, are arranged in a cluster of four cells—the **microspore tetrad** (Figure 1.3a). As the anthers mature and dehydrate, the microspores dissociate from each other and develop into **pollen grains** (Figure 1.3 b). Inside each microsporangium several thousands of microspores or pollen grains are formed that are released with the dehiscence of anther (Figure 1.3c).

**Pollen grain:** The pollen grains represent the male gametophytes. If you touch the opened anthers of *Hibiscus* or any other flower you would find deposition of yellowish powdery pollen grains on your fingers. Sprinkle these grains on a drop of water taken on a glass slide and observe under

**Figure 1.4** Scanning electron micrographs of a few pollen grains

a microscope. You will really be amazed at the variety of architecture – sizes, shapes, colours, designs – seen on the pollen grains from different species (Figure 1.4).

Pollen grains are generally spherical measuring about 25-50 micrometers in diameter. It has a prominent two-layered wall. The hard outer layer called the **exine** is made up of sporopollenin which is one of the most resistant organic material known. It can withstand high temperatures and strong acids and alkali. No enzyme that degrades sporopollenin is so far known. Pollen grain exine has prominent apertures called **germ pores** where sporopollenin is absent. Pollen grains are well-preserved as fossils because of the presence of sporopollenin. The exine exhibits a fascinating array of patterns and designs. *Why do you think the exine should be hard? What is the function of germ pore?* The inner wall of the pollen grain is called the **intine**. It is a thin and continuous layer made up of cellulose and pectin. The cytoplasm of pollen grain is surrounded by a plasma membrane. When the pollen grain is mature it contains two cells, the **vegetative cell** and **generative cell** (Figure 1.5b). The vegetative cell is bigger, has abundant food reserve and a large irregularly shaped nucleus. The **generative cell** is small and floats in the cytoplasm of the vegetative cell. It is spindle shaped with dense cytoplasm and a nucleus. In over 60 per cent of angiosperms, pollen grains are shed at this 2-celled stage. In the remaining species, the generative cell divides mitotically to give rise to the two male gametes before pollen grains are shed (3-celled stage).

Pollen grains of many species cause severe allergies and bronchial afflictions in some people often leading to chronic respiratory disorders– asthma, bronchitis, etc. It may be mentioned that *Parthenium* or carrot grass that came into India as a contaminant with imported wheat, has become ubiquitous in occurrence and causes pollen allergy.

**Figure 1.5** (a) Enlarged view of a pollen grain tetrad; (b) stages of a microspore maturing into a pollen grain

Pollen grains are rich in nutrients. It has become a fashion in recent years to use pollen tablets as food supplements. In western countries, a large number of pollen products in the form of tablets and syrups are available in the market. Pollen consumption has been claimed to increase the performance of athletes and race horses (Figure 1.6).

Figure 1.6 Pollen products

When once they are shed, pollen grains have to land on the stigma before they lose viability if they have to bring about fertilisation. How long do you think the pollen grains retain viability? The period for which pollen grains remain viable is highly variable and to some extent depends on the prevailing temperature and humidity. In some cereals such as rice and wheat, pollen grains lose viability within 30 minutes of their release, and in some members of Rosaceae, Leguminosae and Solanaceae, they maintain viability for months. You may have heard of storing semen/sperms of many animals including humans for artificial insemination. It is possible to store pollen grains of a large number of species for years in liquid nitrogen ( $-196^{\circ}\text{C}$ ). Such stored pollen can be used as pollen banks, similar to seed banks, in crop breeding programmes.

lebo101-s05.section_title: 1.2.2 The Pistil, Megasporangium (ovule) and Embryo sac
lebo101-s05.content:
The gynoecium represents the female reproductive part of the flower. The gynoecium may consist of a single pistil (**monocarpellary**) or may have more than one pistil (**multicarpellary**). When there are more than one, the pistils may be fused together (**syncarpous**) (Figure 1.7b) or may be free (**apocarpous**) (Figure 1.7c). Each pistil has three parts (Figure 1.7a), **the stigma, style and ovary**. The **stigma** serves as a landing platform for pollen grains. The style is the elongated slender part beneath the stigma. The basal bulged part of the pistil is the **ovary**. Inside the ovary is the **ovarian cavity (locule)**. The **placenta** is located inside the ovarian cavity. Recall the definition and types of placentation that you studied in

Figure 1.7 consists of four diagrams labeled (a) through (d).  
 (a) A dissected flower of *Hibiscus* showing the pistil. Labels include: Stigma, Style, Syncarpous ovary, Ovary, and Thalamus.  
 (b) A multicarpellary, syncarpous pistil of *Papaver*. Labels include: Stigma and Carpels.  
 (c) A multicarpellary, apocarpous gynoecium of *Michelia*. Labels include: Carpels.  
 (d) A diagrammatic view of a typical anatropous ovule. Labels include: Hilum, Funicle, Micropyle, Micropylar pole, Outer integument, Inner integument, Nucellus, Embryo sac, and Chalazal pole.

**Figure 1.7** (a) A dissected flower of *Hibiscus* showing pistil (other floral parts have been removed); (b) Multicarpellary, syncarpous pistil of *Papaver*; (c) A multicarpellary, apocarpous gynoecium of *Michelia*; (d) A diagrammatic view of a typical anatropous ovule

Class XI. Arising from the placenta are the **megasporangia**, commonly called **ovules**. The number of ovules in an ovary may be one (wheat, paddy, mango) to many (papaya, water melon, orchids).

**The Megasporangium (Ovule) :** Let us familiarise ourselves with the structure of a typical angiosperm ovule (Figure 1.7d). The ovule is a small structure attached to the placenta by means of a stalk called **funicle**. The body of the ovule fuses with funicle in the region called **hilum**. Thus, hilum represents the junction between ovule and funicle. Each ovule has one or two protective envelopes called **integuments**. Integuments encircle the nucellus except at the tip where a small opening called the **micropyle** is organised. Opposite the micropylar end, is the **chalaza**, representing the basal part of the ovule.

Enclosed within the integuments is a mass of cells called the **nucellus**. Cells of the nucellus have abundant reserve food materials. Located in the nucellus is the **embryo sac** or **female gametophyte**. An ovule generally has a single embryo sac formed from a megaspore.

**Megasporogenesis :** The process of formation of megaspores from the **megaspore mother cell** is called **megasporogenesis**. Ovules generally differentiate a single megaspore mother cell (MMC) in the micropylar region

Figure 1.8 consists of three parts: (a), (b), and (c).  
 Part (a) shows three diagrams of an ovule at different stages of megaspore development. The first diagram shows the 'Micropylar end', 'Nucellus', and 'Megaspore mother cell'. The second diagram shows the 'Micropylar end', 'Nucellus', and 'Megaspore dyad'. The third diagram shows the 'Micropylar end' and 'Megaspore tetrad'.  
 Part (b) shows four diagrams illustrating the development of the embryo sac. The first three show the '2, 4, and 8-nucleate stages'. The fourth diagram shows a 'mature embryo sac' with labels for 'Micropylar end', 'Synergids', 'Egg', 'Central cell', '2 polar nuclei', and 'Antipodals'.  
 Part (c) is a detailed diagrammatic representation of the mature embryo sac, showing the 'Chalazal end', 'Antipodals', 'Polar nuclei', 'Central cell', 'Egg', 'Synergids', 'Filiform apparatus', and 'Micropylar end'.

**Figure 1.8** (a) Parts of the ovule showing a large megaspore mother cell, a dyad and a tetrad of megaspores; (b) 2, 4, and 8-nucleate stages of embryo sac and a mature embryo sac; (c) A diagrammatic representation of the mature embryo sac.

of the nucellus. It is a large cell containing dense cytoplasm and a prominent nucleus. The MMC undergoes meiotic division. *What is the importance of the MMC undergoing meiosis?* Meiosis results in the production of four **megaspores** (Figure 1.8a).

**Female gametophyte :** In a majority of flowering plants, one of the megaspores is **functional** while the other three degenerate. Only the **functional megaspore** develops into the **female gametophyte (embryo sac)**. This method of embryo sac formation from a single megaspore is termed **monosporic** development. *What will be the ploidy of the cells of the nucellus, MMC, the functional megaspore and female gametophyte?*

Let us study about the formation of the embryo sac in detail. (Figure 1.8b). The nucleus of the functional megaspore divides mitotically to form two nuclei which move to the opposite poles, forming the **2-nucleate** embryo sac. Two more sequential mitotic nuclear divisions result in the formation of the **4-nucleate** and later the **8-nucleate** stages of the embryo sac. It is of interest to note that these mitotic divisions are strictly free nuclear, that is, nuclear divisions are not followed immediately by cell wall formation. After the 8-nucleate stage, cell walls are laid down leading to the organisation of the typical **female gametophyte** or **embryo sac**. Observe the distribution of cells inside the embryo sac (Figure 1.8b, c). Six of the eight nuclei are surrounded by cell walls and organised into cells; the remaining two nuclei, called polar nuclei are situated below the egg apparatus in the large **central cell**.

There is a characteristic distribution of the cells within the embryo sac. Three cells are grouped together at the micropylar end and constitute the **egg apparatus**. The egg apparatus, in turn, consists of two **synergids** and one **egg cell**. The synergids have special cellular thickenings at the micropylar tip called filiform apparatus, which play an important role in guiding the pollen tubes into the synergid. Three cells are at the chalazal end and are called the **antipodals**. The large central cell, as mentioned earlier, has two polar nuclei. Thus, a typical angiosperm embryo sac, at maturity, though **8-nucleate** is **7-celled**.

lebo101-s06.0.content:
In the preceding sections you have learnt that the male and female gametes in flowering plants are produced in the pollen grain and embryo sac, respectively. As both types of gametes are non-motile, they have to be brought together for fertilisation to occur. How is this achieved?

**Pollination** is the mechanism to achieve this objective. Transfer of pollen grains (shed from the anther) to the stigma of a pistil is termed **pollination**. Flowering plants have evolved an amazing array of adaptations to achieve pollination. They make use of external agents to achieve pollination. *Can you list the possible external agents?*

**Kinds of Pollination :** Depending on the source of pollen, pollination can be divided into three types.

- Autogamy :** In this type, pollination is achieved within the same flower. Transfer of pollen grains from the anther to the stigma of the same flower (Figure 1.9a). In a normal flower which opens and exposes the anthers and the stigma, complete autogamy is rather rare. Autogamy in such flowers requires synchrony in pollen release and stigma receptivity and also, the anthers and the stigma should

Cleistogamous flowers

**Figure 1.9** (a) Self-pollinated flowers; (b) Cross pollinated flowers; (c) Cleistogamous flowers

lie close to each other so that self-pollination can occur. Some plants such as *Viola* (common pansy), *Oxalis*, and *Commelina* produce two types of flowers – **chasmogamous** flowers which are similar to flowers of other species with exposed anthers and stigma, and **cleistogamous** flowers which do not open at all (Figure 1.9c). In such flowers, the anthers and stigma lie close to each other. When anthers dehisce in the flower buds, pollen grains come in contact with the stigma to effect pollination. Thus, cleistogamous flowers are invariably autogamous as there is no chance of cross-pollen landing on the stigma. Cleistogamous flowers produce assured seed-set even in the absence of pollinators. Do you think that cleistogamy is advantageous or disadvantageous to the plant? Why?

- (ii) **Geitonogamy** – Transfer of pollen grains from the anther to the stigma of another flower of the same plant. Although geitonogamy is functionally cross-pollination involving a pollinating agent, genetically it is similar to autogamy since the pollen grains come from the same plant.
- (iii) **Xenogamy** – Transfer of pollen grains from anther to the stigma of a different plant (Figure 1.9b). This is the only type of pollination which during pollination brings genetically different types of pollen grains to the stigma.

**Agents of Pollination :** Plants use two abiotic (wind and water) and one biotic (animals) agents to achieve pollination. Majority of plants use biotic agents for pollination. Only a small proportion of plants use abiotic agents. Pollen grains coming in contact with the stigma is a chance factor in both wind and water pollination. To compensate for this uncertainties and associated loss of pollen grains, the flowers produce enormous amount of pollen when compared to the number of ovules available for pollination.

Pollination by wind is more common amongst abiotic pollinations. Wind pollination also requires that the pollen grains are light and non-sticky so that they can be transported in wind currents. They often possess well-exposed stamens (so that the pollens are easily dispersed into wind currents, Figure 1.10) and large often-feathery stigma to easily trap air-borne pollen grains. Wind-pollinated flowers often have a single ovule in each ovary and numerous flowers packed into an inflorescence; a familiar example is the corn cob – the ears you see are nothing but the stigma and style which wave in the wind to trap pollen grains. Wind-pollination is quite common in grasses.

Pollination by water is quite rare in flowering plants and is limited to about 30 genera, mostly monocotyledons. As against this, you would recall that water is a regular mode of transport for the male gametes among the lower plant groups such as algae, bryophytes and pteridophytes. It is believed, particularly for some bryophytes and pteridophytes, that their distribution is limited because of the need for water for the transport of male gametes and fertilisation. Some examples of water pollinated plants are *Vallisneria* and *Hydrilla* which grow in fresh water and several marine sea-grasses such as *Zostera*. Not all aquatic plants use water for pollination. In a majority of aquatic plants such as water hyacinth and water lily, the flowers emerge above the level of water and are pollinated by insects or wind as in most of the land plants. In *Vallisneria*, the female flower reach the surface of water by the long stalk and the male flowers or pollen grains are released on to the surface of water. They are carried passively by water currents (Figure 1.11a); some of them eventually reach the female flowers and the stigma. In another group of water pollinated plants such as seagrasses, female flowers remain submerged in water and the pollen grains are released inside the water. Pollen grains in many such species are long, ribbon like and they are carried passively inside the water; some of them reach the stigma and achieve pollination. In most of the water-pollinated species, pollen grains are protected from wetting by a mucilaginous covering.

Both wind and water pollinated flowers are not very colourful and do not produce nectar. *What would be the reason for this?*

**Figure 1.10** A wind-pollinated plant showing compact inflorescence and well-exposed stamens

Figure 1.11 consists of two parts. Part (a) shows a *Vallisneria* plant with long, narrow leaves and a single female flower at the top. Part (b) shows a male flower with two anthers and a female flower with a stigma. Pollen grains are shown being carried by an insect from the male flower to the stigma of the female flower.

**Figure 1.11** (a) Pollination by water in *Vallisneria*; (b) Insect pollination

Majority of flowering plants use a range of animals as pollinating agents. Bees, butterflies, flies, beetles, wasps, ants, moths, birds (sunbirds and humming birds) and bats are the common pollinating agents. (Figure 1.11b). Among the animals, insects, particularly bees are the dominant biotic pollinating agents. Even larger animals such as some primates (lemurs), arboreal (tree-dwelling) rodents, or even reptiles (gecko lizard and garden lizard) have also been reported as pollinators in some species.

lebo101-s06.1.content:
Often flowers of animal-pollinated plants are specifically adapted for a particular species of animal.

Majority of insect-pollinated flowers are large, colourful, fragrant and rich in nectar. When the flowers are small, a number of flowers are clustered into an inflorescence to make them conspicuous. Animals are attracted to flowers by colour and/or fragrance. The flowers pollinated by flies and beetles secrete foul odours to attract these animals. To sustain animal visits, the flowers have to provide rewards to the animals. Nectar and pollen grains are the usual floral rewards. For harvesting the reward(s) from the flower the animal visitor comes in contact with the anthers and the stigma. The body of the animal gets a coating of pollen grains, which are

generally sticky in animal pollinated flowers. When the animal carrying pollen on its body comes in contact with the stigma, it brings about pollination.

In some species floral rewards are in providing safe places to lay eggs; an example is that of the tallest flower of *Amorphophallus* (the flower itself is about 6 feet in height). A similar relationship exists between a species of moth and the plant *Yucca* where both species – moth and the

plant – cannot complete their life cycles without each other. The moth deposits its eggs in the locule of the ovary and the flower, in turn, gets pollinated by the moth. The larvae of the moth come out of the eggs as the seeds start developing.

*Why don't you observe some flowers of the following plants (or any others available to you): Cucumber, Mango, Peepal, Coriander, Papaya, Onion, Lobia, Cotton, Tobacco, Rose, Lemon, Eucalyptus, Banana? Try to find out which animals visit them and whether they could be pollinators. You'll have to patiently observe the flowers over a few days and at different times of the day. You could also try to see whether there is any correlation in the characteristics of a flower to the animal that visits it. Carefully observe if any of the visitors come in contact with the anthers and the stigma as only such visitors can bring about pollination. Many insects may consume pollen or the nectar without bringing about pollination. Such floral visitors are referred to as pollen/nectar robbers. You may or may not be able to identify the pollinators, but you will surely enjoy your efforts!*

**Outbreeding Devices :** Majority of flowering plants produce hermaphrodite flowers and pollen grains are likely to come in contact with the stigma of the same flower. Continued self-pollination result in inbreeding depression. Flowering plants have developed many devices to discourage self-pollination and to encourage cross-pollination. In some species, pollen release and stigma receptivity are not synchronised. Either the pollen is released before the stigma becomes receptive or stigma becomes receptive much before the release of pollen. In some other species, the anther and stigma are placed at different positions so that the pollen cannot come in contact with the stigma of the same flower. Both these devices prevent autogamy. The third device to prevent inbreeding is self-incompatibility. This is a genetic mechanism and prevents self-pollen (from the same flower or other flowers of the same plant) from fertilising the ovules by inhibiting pollen germination or pollen tube growth in the pistil. Another device to prevent self-pollination is the production of unisexual flowers. If both male and female flowers are present on the same plant such as castor and maize (monoecious), it prevents autogamy but not geitonogamy. In several species such as papaya, male and female flowers are present on different plants, that is each plant is either male or female (dioecy). This condition prevents both autogamy and geitonogamy.

**Pollen-pistil Interaction :** Pollination does not guarantee the transfer of the right type of pollen (compatible pollen of the same species as the stigma). Often, pollen of the wrong type, either from other species or from the same plant (if it is self-incompatible), also land on the stigma. The pistil has the ability to recognise the pollen, whether it is of the right type (compatible) or of the wrong type (incompatible). If it is of the right type, the pistil accepts the pollen and promotes post-pollination events that

Longitudinal section of a flower showing growth of pollen tube

**Figure 1.12** (a) Pollen grains germinating on the stigma; (b) Pollen tubes growing through the style; (c) L.S. of pistil showing path of pollen tube growth; (d) enlarged view of an egg apparatus showing entry of pollen tube into a synergids; (e) Discharge of male gametes into a synergids and the movements of the sperms, one into the egg and the other into the central cell

leads to fertilisation. If the pollen is of the wrong type, the pistil rejects the pollen by preventing pollen germination on the stigma or the pollen tube growth in the style. The ability of the pistil to recognise the pollen followed by its acceptance or rejection is the result of a continuous dialogue between pollen grain and the pistil. This dialogue is mediated by chemical components of the pollen interacting with those of the pistil. It is only in recent years that botanists have been able to identify some of the pollen and pistil components and the interactions leading to the recognition, followed by acceptance or rejection.

As mentioned earlier, following compatible pollination, the pollen grain germinates on the stigma to produce a pollen tube through one of the germ pores (Figure 1.12a). The contents of the pollen grain move into the

pollen tube. Pollen tube grows through the tissues of the stigma and style and reaches the ovary (Figure 1.12b, c). You would recall that in some plants, pollen grains are shed at two-celled condition (a vegetative cell and a generative cell). In such plants, the generative cell divides and forms the two male gametes during the growth of pollen tube in the stigma. In plants which shed pollen in the three-celled condition, pollen tubes carry the two male gametes from the beginning. Pollen tube, after reaching the ovary, enters the ovule through the micropyle and then enters one of the synergids through the filiform apparatus (Figure 1.12d, e). Many recent studies have shown that filiform apparatus present at the micropylar part of the synergids guides the entry of pollen tube. All these events—from pollen deposition on the stigma until pollen tubes enter the ovule—are together referred to as pollen-pistil interaction. As pointed out earlier, pollen-pistil interaction is a dynamic process involving pollen recognition followed by promotion or inhibition of the pollen. The knowledge gained in this area would help the plant breeder in manipulating pollen-pistil interaction, even in incompatible pollinations, to get desired hybrids.

lebo101-s06.2.content:
You can easily study pollen germination by dusting some pollen from flowers such as pea, chickpea, *Crotalaria*, balsam and *Vinca* on a glass slide containing a drop of sugar solution (about 10 per cent). After about 15–30 minutes, observe the slide under the low power lens of the microscope. You are likely to see pollen tubes coming out of the pollen grains.

A breeder is interested in crossing different species and often genera to combine desirable characters to produce commercially ‘superior’ varieties. **Artificial hybridisation** is one of the major approaches of crop improvement programme. In such crossing experiments it is important to make sure that only the desired pollen grains are used for pollination and the stigma is protected from contamination (from unwanted pollen). This is achieved by emasculation and bagging techniques.

If the female parent bears bisexual flowers, removal of anthers from the flower bud before the anther dehisces using a pair of forceps is necessary. This step is referred to as **emasculation**. Emasculated flowers have to be covered with a bag of suitable size, generally made up of butter paper, to prevent contamination of its stigma with unwanted pollen. This process is called **bagging**. When the stigma of bagged flower attains receptivity, mature pollen grains collected from anthers of the male parent are dusted on the stigma, and the flowers are rebagged, and the fruits allowed to develop.

If the female parent produces unisexual flowers, there is no need for emasculation. The female flower buds are bagged before the flowers open. When the stigma becomes receptive, pollination is carried out using the desired pollen and the flower rebagged.

lebo101-s07.content:
After entering one of the synergids, the pollen tube releases the two male gametes into the cytoplasm of the synergid. One of the male gametes moves towards the egg cell and fuses with its nucleus thus completing the **syngamy**. This results in the formation of a diploid cell, the **zygote**. The other male gamete moves towards the two polar nuclei located in the central cell and fuses with them to produce a triploid **primary endosperm nucleus (PEN)** (Figure 1.13a). As this involves the fusion of three haploid nuclei it is termed **triple fusion**. Since two types of fusions, syngamy and triple fusion take place in an embryo sac the phenomenon is termed **double fertilisation**, an event unique to flowering plants. The central cell after triple fusion becomes the **primary endosperm cell (PEC)** and develops into the **endosperm** while the zygote develops into an **embryo**.

Figure 1.13(a) shows a fertilised embryo sac. It is an oval structure containing several cells. At the top are two **Degenerating synergids**. Below them is a **Zygote (2n)**. In the center is a large **Primary endosperm cell (PEC)** containing a **Primary endosperm nucleus (3n) (PEN)**. At the bottom are three **Degenerating antipodal cells**. Figure 1.13(b) shows the stages of embryo development in a dicot. It starts with a **Zygote** which divides into a **Globular Embryo** (a sphere of cells), then a **Heart-Shaped Embryo** (with two lobes), and finally a **Mature** embryo. The mature embryo has a **Suspensor** (a stalk of cells), a **Radicle** (the embryonic root), a **Cotyledon** (seed leaf), and a **Plumule** (the embryonic shoot).

**Figure 1.13** (a) Fertilised embryo sac showing zygote and Primary Endosperm Nucleus (PEN); (b) Stages in embryo development in a dicot [shown in reduced size as compared to (a)]

lebo101-s08.content:
Following double fertilisation, events of endosperm and embryo development, maturation of ovule(s) into seed(s) and ovary into fruit, are collectively termed **post-fertilisation events**.

Endosperm development precedes embryo development. *Why?* The primary endosperm cell divides repeatedly and forms a triploid

endosperm tissue. The cells of this tissue are filled with reserve food materials and are used for the nutrition of the developing embryo. In the most common type of endosperm development, the PEN undergoes successive nuclear divisions to give rise to free nuclei. This stage of endosperm development is called free-nuclear endosperm. Subsequently cell wall formation occurs and the endosperm becomes cellular. The number of free nuclei formed before cellularisation varies greatly. The coconut water from tender coconut that you are familiar with, is nothing but free-nuclear endosperm (made up of thousands of nuclei) and the surrounding white kernel is the cellular endosperm.

Endosperm may either be completely consumed by the developing embryo (e.g., pea, groundnut, beans) before seed maturation or it may persist in the mature seed (e.g. castor and coconut) and be used up during seed germination. *Split open some seeds of castor, peas, beans, groundnut, fruit of coconut and look for the endosperm in each case. Find out whether the endosperm is persistent in cereals – wheat, rice and maize.*

lebo101-s10.content:
Embryo develops at the micropylar end of the embryo sac where the zygote is situated. Most zygotes divide only after certain amount of endosperm is formed. This is an adaptation to provide assured nutrition to the developing embryo. Though the seeds differ greatly, the early stages of embryo development (**embryogeny**) are similar in both monocotyledons and dicotyledons. Figure 1.13 depicts the stages of embryogeny in a dicotyledonous embryo. The zygote gives rise to the **proembryo** and subsequently to the **globular, heart-shaped and mature embryo**.

A typical dicotyledonous embryo (Figure 1.14a), consists of an **embryonal axis** and two **cotyledons**. The portion of embryonal axis above the level of cotyledons is the **epicotyl**, which terminates with the **plumule** or stem tip. The cylindrical portion below the level of cotyledons is **hypocotyl** that terminates at its lower end in the **radicle** or **root tip**. The root tip is covered with a **root cap**.

Embryos of monocotyledons (Figure 1.14 b) possess only one cotyledon. In the grass family the cotyledon is called **scutellum** that is situated towards one side (lateral) of the embryonal axis. At its lower end, the embryonal axis has the

Figure 1.14 consists of two diagrams. Diagram (a) shows a typical dicot embryo with a central embryonal axis and two large cotyledons. Labels from top to bottom are: Plumule, Cotyledons, Hypocotyl, Radicle, and Root cap. Diagram (b) shows a longitudinal section of a grass embryo, which is monocotyledonous. It features a single cotyledon called the scutellum. Other labeled parts include the Coleoptile, Shoot apex, Epiblast, Radicle, Root cap, and Coleorhiza.

**Figure 1.14** (a) A typical dicot embryo; (b) L.S. of an embryo of grass

radical and root cap enclosed in an undifferentiated sheath called **coleorrhiza**. The portion of the embryonal axis above the level of attachment of scutellum is the epicotyl. Epicotyl has a shoot apex and a few leaf primordia enclosed in a hollow foliar structure, the **coleoptile**.

*Soak a few seeds in water (say of wheat, maize, peas, chickpeas, ground nut) overnight. Then split the seeds and observe the various parts of the embryo and the seed.*

lebo101-s11.content:
In angiosperms, the seed is the final product of sexual reproduction. It is often described as a fertilised ovule. Seeds are formed inside fruits. A seed typically consists of seed coat(s), cotyledon(s) and an embryo axis. The cotyledons (Figure 1.15a) of the embryo are simple structures, generally thick and swollen due to storage of food reserves (as in legumes). Mature seeds may be **non-albuminous** or **ex-albuminous**. Non-albuminous seeds have no residual endosperm as it is completely consumed during embryo development (e.g., pea, groundnut). Albuminous seeds retain a part of endosperm as it is not completely used up during embryo development (e.g., wheat, maize, barley, castor). Occasionally, in some seeds such as black pepper and beet, remnants of nucellus are also persistent. This residual, persistent nucellus is the **perisperm**.

Integuments of ovules harden as tough protective seed coats (Figure 1.15a). The micropyle remains as a small pore in the seed coat. This facilitates entry of oxygen and water into the seed during germination. As the seed matures, its water content is reduced and seeds become relatively dry (10-15 per cent moisture by mass). The general metabolic activity of the embryo slows down. The embryo may enter a state of inactivity called **dormancy**, or if favourable conditions are available (adequate moisture, oxygen and suitable temperature), they germinate.

As ovules mature into seeds, the ovary develops into a fruit, i.e., the transformation of ovules into seeds and ovary into fruit proceeds simultaneously. The wall of the ovary develops into the wall of fruit called **pericarp**. The fruits may be fleshy as in guava, orange, mango, etc., or may be dry, as in groundnut, and mustard, etc. Many fruits have evolved mechanisms for dispersal of seeds. Recall the classification of fruits and their dispersal mechanisms that you have studied in an earlier class. *Is there any relationship between number of ovules in an ovary and the number of seeds present in a fruit?*

In most plants, by the time the fruit develops from the ovary, other floral parts degenerate and fall off. However, in a few species such as apple, strawberry, cashew, etc., the thalamus also contributes to fruit formation. Such fruits are called **false fruits** (Figure 1.15b). Most fruits however develop only from the ovary and are called **true fruits**. Although in most of the species, fruits are the results of fertilisation, there are a few species

The image contains detailed anatomical diagrams of seeds and fruits.   
**(a) Structure of some seeds:**  
- Top left: Dicot seed showing two **Cotyledons** and a **Micropyle**.  
- Top middle/right: Longitudinal sections of seeds showing **Seed coat**, **Endosperm**, **Cotyledon**, **Hypocotyl root axis**, **Shoot apical meristem**, and **Root tip**.  
- Middle left: Another seed section with **Seed coat**, **Cotyledon**, **Shoot apical meristem**, **Hypocotyl root axis**, **Root tip**, and **Endosperm**.  
- Middle right: Longitudinal section of a monocot grain (maize) showing **Pericarp**, **Endosperm**, **Scutellum**, **Coleoptile**, **Plumule**, **Radicle**, and **Coleorhiza**.  
**(b) False fruits:**  
- Bottom left: Sections of an apple showing **Thalamus**, **Seed**, **Endocarp**, and **Mesocarp**.  
- Bottom right: Section of a strawberry showing **Thalamus** and **Achene**.

**Figure 1.15** (a) Structure of some seeds. (b) False fruits of apple and strawberry

in which fruits develop without fertilisation. Such fruits are called **parthenocarpic fruits**. Banana is one such example. Parthenocarpy can be induced through the application of growth hormones and such fruits are seedless.

Seeds offer several advantages to angiosperms. Firstly, since reproductive processes such as pollination and fertilisation are independent of water, seed formation is more dependable. Also seeds have better adaptive strategies for dispersal to new habitats and help the species

to colonise in other areas. As they have sufficient food reserves, young seedlings are nourished until they are capable of photosynthesis on their own. The hard seed coat provides protection to the young embryo. Being products of sexual reproduction, they generate new genetic combinations leading to variations.

Seed is the basis of our agriculture. Dehydration and dormancy of mature seeds are crucial for storage of seeds which can be used as food throughout the year and also to raise crop in the next season. Can you imagine agriculture in the absence of seeds, or in the presence of seeds which germinate straight away soon after formation and cannot be stored?

How long do the seeds remain alive after they are dispersed? This period again varies greatly. In a few species the seeds lose viability within a few months. Seeds of a large number of species live for several years. Some seeds can remain alive for hundreds of years. There are several records of very old yet viable seeds. The oldest is that of a lupine, *Lupinus arcticus* excavated from Arctic Tundra. The seed germinated and flowered after an estimated record of 10,000 years of dormancy. A recent record of 2000 years old viable seed is of the date palm, *Phoenix dactylifera* discovered during the archeological excavation at King Herod's palace near the Dead Sea.

*After completing a brief account of sexual reproduction of flowering plants it would be worth attempting to comprehend the enormous reproductive capacity of some flowering plants by asking the following questions: How many eggs are present in an embryo sac? How many embryo sacs are present in an ovule? How many ovules are present in an ovary? How many ovaries are present in a typical flower? How many flowers are present on a tree? And so on...*

*Can you think of some plants in which fruits contain very large number of seeds. Orchid fruits are one such category and each fruit contain thousands of tiny seeds. Similar is the case in fruits of some parasitic species such as Orobanche and Striga. Have you seen a tiny seed of Ficus? How large is the tree of Ficus developed from that tiny seed. How many billions of seeds does each Ficus tree produce? Can you imagine any other example in which such a tiny structure can produce such a large biomass over the years?*

lebo101-s12.content:
Although seeds, in general are the products of fertilisation, a few flowering plants such as some species of *Asteraceae* and grasses, have evolved a special mechanism, to produce seeds without fertilisation, called **apomixis**. *What is fruit production without fertilisation called?* Thus, apomixis is a form of asexual reproduction that mimics sexual reproduction. There are several ways of development of apomictic seeds. In some species, the diploid egg cell is formed without reduction division and develops into the embryo without fertilisation. More often, as in many *Citrus* and *Mango*

varieties some of the nucellar cells surrounding the embryo sac start dividing, protrude into the embryo sac and develop into the embryos. In such species each ovule contains many embryos. Occurrence of more than one embryo in a seed is referred to as **polyembryony**. *Take out some seeds of orange and squeeze them. Observe the many embryos of different sizes and shapes from each seed. Count the number of embryos in each seed. What would be the genetic nature of apomictic embryos? Can they be called clones?*

Hybrid varieties of several of our food and vegetable crops are being extensively cultivated. Cultivation of hybrids has tremendously increased productivity. One of the problems of hybrids is that hybrid seeds have to be produced every year. If the seeds collected from hybrids are sown, the plants in the progeny will segregate and do not maintain hybrid characters. Production of hybrid seeds is costly and hence the cost of hybrid seeds become too expensive for the farmers. If these hybrids are made into apomicts, there is no segregation of characters in the hybrid progeny. Then the farmers can keep on using the hybrid seeds to raise new crop year after year and he does not have to buy hybrid seeds every year. Because of the importance of apomixis in hybrid seed industry, active research is going on in many laboratories around the world to understand the genetics of apomixis and to transfer apomictic genes into hybrid varieties.

lebo101-s13.content:
Flowers are the seat of sexual reproduction in angiosperms. In the flower, androecium consisting of stamens represents the male reproductive organs and gynoecium consisting of pistils represents the female reproductive organs.

A typical anther is bilobed, dithecous and tetrasporangiate. Pollen grains develop inside the microsporangia. Four wall layers, the epidermis, endothecium, middle layers and the tapetum surround the microsporangium. Cells of the sporogenous tissue lying in the centre of the microsporangium, undergo meiosis (microsporogenesis) to form tetrads of microspores. Individual microspores mature into pollen grains.

Pollen grains represents the male gametophytic generation. The pollen grains have a two-layered wall, the outer exine and inner intine. The exine is made up of sporopollenin and has germ pores. Pollen grains may have two cells (a vegetative cell and generative cell) or three cells (a vegetative cell and two male gametes) at the time of shedding.

The pistil has three parts – the stigma, style and the ovary. Ovules are present in the ovary. The ovules have a stalk called funicle, protective integument(s), and an opening called micropyle. The central tissue is the nucellus in which the archesporium differentiates. A cell of the archesporium, the megaspore mother cell divides meiotically and one of the megaspores forms the embryo sac (the female gametophyte). The mature embryo sac is 7-celled and 8-nucleate. At the micropylar end is

the egg apparatus consisting of two synergids and an egg cell. At the chalazal end are three antipodals. At the centre is a large central cell with two polar nuclei.

Pollination is the mechanism to transfer pollen grains from the anther to the stigma. Pollinating agents are either abiotic (wind and water) or biotic (animals).

Pollen-pistil interaction involves all events from the landing of pollen grains on the stigma until the pollen tube enters the embryo sac (when the pollen is compatible) or pollen inhibition (when the pollen is incompatible). Following compatible pollination, pollen grain germinates on the stigma and the resulting pollen tube grow through the style, enter the ovules and finally discharges two male gametes in one of the synergids. Angiosperms exhibit double fertilisation because two fusion events occur in each embryo sac, namely syngamy and triple fusion. The products of these fusions are the diploid zygote and the triploid primary endosperm nucleus (in the primary endosperm cell). Zygote develops into the embryo and the primary endosperm cell forms the endosperm tissue. Formation of endosperm always precedes development of the embryo.

The developing embryo passes through different stages such as the proembryo, globular and heart-shaped stages before maturation. Mature dicotyledonous embryo has two cotyledons and an embryonal axis with epicotyl and hypocotyl. Embryos of monocotyledons have a single cotyledon. After fertilisation, ovary develops into fruit and ovules develop into seeds.

A phenomenon called apomixis is found in some angiosperms, particularly in grasses. It results in the formation of seeds without fertilisation. Apomicts have several advantages in horticulture and agriculture.

Some angiosperms produce more than one embryo in their seed. This phenomenon is called polyembryony.

lebo101-s15.section_title: ~~HUMAN REPRODUCTION~~ SEXUAL REPRODUCTION IN FLOWERING PLANTS
lebo101-s15.content:
7. What are chasmogamous flowers? Can cross-pollination occur in cleistogamous flowers? Give reasons for your answer.
  8. Mention two strategies evolved to prevent self-pollination in flowers.
  9. What is self-incompatibility? Why does self-pollination not lead to seed formation in self-incompatible species?
  10. What is bagging technique? How is it useful in a plant breeding programme?
  11. What is triple fusion? Where and how does it take place? Name the nuclei involved in triple fusion.
  12. Why do you think the zygote is dormant for sometime in a fertilised ovule?
  13. Differentiate between:
    - (a) hypocotyl and epicotyl;
    - (b) coleoptile and coleorhiza;
    - (c) integument and testa;
    - (d) perisperm and pericarp.
  14. Why is apple called a false fruit? Which part(s) of the flower forms the fruit?
  15. What is meant by emasculation? When and why does a plant breeder employ this technique?
  16. If one can induce parthenocarpy through the application of growth substances, which fruits would you select to induce parthenocarpy and why?
  17. Explain the role of tapetum in the formation of pollen-grain wall.
  18. What is apomixis and what is its importance?
-

lebo101-d000.content: Diagram: pollen grain tetrad and stages of a microspore maturing into a pollen grain (diagram). Caption: Figure 1.5: (a) Enlarged view of a pollen grain tetrad showing four pollen grains. (b) Stages of a microspore maturing into a pollen grain, showing the development of the vegetative cell and generative cell. Labels include: Vacuoles, Nucleus, Asymmetric spindle, Vegetative cell, and Generative cell. Description: The diagram represents two related stages in male gametophyte development in angiosperms. In panel (a), a pollen grain tetrad is shown as four haploid microspores formed after meiosis of a pollen mother cell; the four microspores are typically arranged in a tetrahedral or isobilateral cluster and are initially enclosed by a common callose wall before separation. In panel (b), a single microspore undergoes maturation into a pollen grain: first it is a uninucleate microspore with a centrally placed nucleus, then vacuolation occurs and the nucleus shifts to an eccentric position, followed by an asymmetric mitotic division that produces a large vegetative (tube) cell and a small generative cell. The vegetative cell contains abundant cytoplasm and the vegetative nucleus and is responsible for pollen tube growth, while the generative cell is the smaller cell that later divides mitotically to form two male gametes. The mature pollen grain is therefore typically two-celled at the time of shedding in many species, though in some species it may be shed at the three-celled stage. Labels: Pollen grain tetrad, Microspores, Microspore, Vacuoles, Nucleus, Asymmetric mitotic division, Vegetative cell, Generative cell, Vegetative nucleus. NEET relevance: Identification of the stage of microspore development shown in the figure; Difference between tetrad, microspore, and mature pollen grain; Role of vacuolation and asymmetric mitosis in pollen development. Board relevance: Draw and label the tetrad and stages of microspore maturation into pollen grain; Explain how a microspore becomes a mature pollen grain; Label vacuole, nucleus, asymmetric division, vegetative cell, and generative cell. Exam-critical labels: Pollen grain tetrad, Microspore, Vacuoles, Nucleus, Asymmetric mitotic division, Vegetative cell, Generative cell. Common mistakes: Calling the tetrad a pollen grain (correct: It is a pollen grain tetrad containing four microspores); Labeling the large cell as the generative cell (correct: The large cell is the vegetative cell); Thinking the generative cell is outside the pollen grain (correct: The generative cell is inside the pollen grain, enclosed by the vegetative cell cytoplasm).

lebo101-d001.content: Diagram: pollen products / bee pollen products (photograph). Caption: Figure 1.6: Pollen products. The image shows various bee pollen products including 'THE ENERGY SHAKE' (Vanilla), 'Bee Pollen Cream', 'Bee Pollen Extract', and a box of 'Bee Pollen'. A bee is shown on a flower, and a blister pack of 30 tablets is also visible. The text on the box reads: 'Bee Pollen from England', 'Natural Bee-collected Pollen in Tiny Golden Pods', 'A natural food from honey bees', 'NET WEIGHT .45 OZ.' Description: This figure is not a biological structure but a commercial collage illustrating pollen products derived from bee-collected pollen. Bee pollen consists of pollen grains gathered by worker bees from flowers, mixed with nectar and salivary secretions, and packed into pellets that are transported in the corbiculae (pollen baskets) on the hind legs. The diagram shows examples of marketed products made from or containing bee pollen, such as tablets, cream, extract, and a beverage mix. The flower with a bee represents the source of pollen collection, while the packaged items represent processed consumer products. Scientifically, the key concept is that pollen is a male gametophytic product of flowering plants and, when collected by bees, can be used as a nutritional supplement; however, these products are not plant reproductive structures themselves but processed bee-derived pollen preparations. Labels: Pollen products, Bee pollen, Bee pollen cream, Bee pollen extract, The Energy Shake, 30 tablets, Natural bee-collected pollen, In tiny golden pods, A natural food from honey bees, Flower, Honey bee. NEET relevance: Bee pollen is a pollen-based dietary supplement collected by honey bees from flowers.; Pollen grains are the male gametophytes of angiosperms.; Distinguish pollen products from other bee products such as honey, royal jelly, and propolis.. Board relevance: Identify the figure as pollen products/bee pollen products.; Explain that bees collect pollen from flowers and it is marketed as tablets, cream, extract, or nutritional shake.; Short note on the role of pollen as a male reproductive unit in flowering plants.. Exam-critical labels: Pollen products, Bee pollen, Flower, Honey bee, Bee pollen extract, Bee pollen cream, 30 tablets, Natural bee-collected pollen. Common mistakes: Pollen grain structure diagram (correct: Commercial bee pollen products collage); Honeycomb or bee hive product (correct: Bee pollen supplement and cosmetic products); Plant reproductive organ diagram (correct: Bee collecting pollen from a flower).

lebo101-d002.content: Diagram: structure of seeds and false fruits in flowering plants (diagram). Caption: The image contains detailed anatomical diagrams of seeds and fruits. **(a) Structure of some seeds:** - Top left: Dicot seed showing two **Cotyledons** and a **Micropyle**. - Top middle/right: Longitudinal sections of seeds showing **Seed coat**, **Endosperm**, **Cotyledon**, **Hypocotyl root axis**, **Shoot apical meristem**, and **Root tip**. Description: This NCERT figure represents two related concepts in angiosperms: (a) the structure of representative seeds and (b) false fruits, specifically apple and strawberry. In the seed diagrams, the essential embryonic parts are shown in relation to the seed coverings and nutritive tissue. A dicot seed typically has two cotyledons, an embryonal axis with the plumule (future shoot) at the upper end and the radicle (future root) at the lower end, with the hypocotyl region between them; the seed coat surrounds the embryo and may enclose endosperm in endospermic seeds. In monocot seeds such as maize, the single cotyledon is the scutellum, which lies adjacent to the endosperm and absorbs nutrients for the embryo; the plumule is protected by the coleoptile and the radicle by the coleorhiza. The pericarp is the fruit wall derived from the ovary wall and is distinct from the seed coat. In the false fruit diagrams, the edible fleshy part of apple is mainly the thalamus (receptacle), not the ovary wall; the true fruit is the core containing the seeds, while the pericarp forms the core wall. Strawberry is also a false fruit in which the thalamus becomes the fleshy edible part and the true fruits are the small achenes on its surface. Thus, the figure emphasizes the distinction between seed anatomy, true fruit tissues, and accessory (false) fruit tissues derived from non-ovarian parts. Labels: Cotyledons, Micropyle, Seed coat, Endosperm, Hypocotyl, Radicle, Plumule, Shoot apical meristem, Root apical meristem, Scutellum, Coleoptile, Coleorhiza, Pericarp, Thalamus, Endocarp. NEET relevance: Difference between dicot and monocot seed structure; Function of cotyledons, scutellum, coleoptile, and coleorhiza; Identification of false fruits and accessory fruit tissues. Board relevance: Draw and label the structure of a dicot seed; Draw and label the structure of a monocot seed (maize grain); Explain why apple and strawberry are called false fruits. Exam-critical labels: Cotyledons, Micropyle, Seed coat, Endosperm, Plumule, Radicle, Scutellum, Coleoptile. Common mistakes: Calling the fleshy edible part of apple the ovary wall or pericarp alone (correct: In apple, the edible fleshy part is mainly the thalamus (receptacle), while the pericarp forms the core around the seeds); Labeling the small surface structures of strawberry as seeds (correct: They are achenes, which are true fruits containing seeds); Using 'hypocotyl root axis' as a single label (correct: The correct embryonic axis regions are hypocotyl and radicle, with plumule above).

lebo101-d003.content: Diagram: Megaspore development and embryo sac development in the ovule, including a mature embryo sac (diagram). Caption: Figure 1.8 consists of three parts: (a), (b), and (c). Part (a) shows three diagrams of an ovule at different stages of megaspore development. The first diagram shows the 'Micropylar end', 'Nucellus', and 'Megaspore mother cell'. The second diagram shows the 'Micropylar end', 'Nucellus', and 'Megaspore dyad'. The third diagram shows the 'Micropylar end' and 'Megaspore tetrad'. Part (b) shows four diagrams illustrating the development of the embryo sac. The first three show the '2, 4, and 8-nucleate stages'. The fourth diagram shows a 'mature embryo sac' with labels for 'Micropylar end', 'Synergids', 'Egg', 'Central cell', '2 polar nuclei', and 'Antipodals'. Part (c) is a detailed diagrammatic representation of the mature embryo sac, showing the 'Chalazal end', 'Antipodals', 'Polar nuclei', 'Central cell', 'Egg', 'Synergids', 'Filiform apparatus', and 'Micropylar end'. Description: This diagram represents megasporogenesis and embryo sac development in an angiosperm ovule, culminating in the mature Polygonum-type embryo sac. In the ovule, the nucellus surrounds the megaspore mother cell (MMC), which undergoes meiosis to form a linear tetrad of four haploid megaspores; typically, three degenerate and the chalazal-most megaspore remains functional. This functional megaspore undergoes three successive mitotic divisions without cytokinesis to produce an 8-nucleate, 7-celled embryo sac. The mature embryo sac is organized with the micropylar end bearing the egg apparatus, consisting of one egg cell flanked by two synergids with prominent filiform apparatus that helps attract and guide the pollen tube. The central cell occupies the largest portion of the embryo sac and contains two polar nuclei, which may fuse to form the secondary nucleus. The chalazal end contains three antipodal cells, which are usually short-lived and may be nutritive. The diagram should clearly show the spatial polarity of the embryo sac: micropylar end toward the pollen tube entry region and chalazal end opposite it, with the embryo sac embedded within the nucellus of the ovule. Labels: Micropylar end, Chalazal end, Nucellus, Megaspore mother cell, Megaspore dyad, Megaspore tetrad, Functional megaspore, 2-nucleate embryo sac, 4-nucleate embryo sac, 8-nucleate embryo sac, Egg cell, Synergids, Filiform apparatus, Central cell, Polar nuclei. NEET relevance: Megasporogenesis in angiosperms; Polygonum-type embryo sac development; Number of nuclei/cells in mature embryo sac. Board relevance: Draw and label the stages of megaspore development in ovule; Draw and label mature embryo sac; Explain megasporogenesis and embryo sac formation. Exam-critical labels: Megaspore mother cell, Megaspore dyad, Megaspore tetrad, Functional megaspore, Micropylar end, Chalazal end, Nucellus, Synergids. Common mistakes: Calling the megaspore tetrad a pollen tetrad (correct: It is a tetrad of megaspores formed by meiosis of the megaspore mother cell); Labeling the central cell as the egg cell (correct: The egg cell is only one cell at the micropylar end; the central cell is the large middle cell containing polar nuclei); Putting antipodals at the micropylar end (correct: Antipodals are at the chalazal end).

lebo101-d004.content: Diagram: Pollen germination and pollen tube growth in flowering plants, including pollen tube entry into the ovule/embryo sac and discharge of male gametes (multiple views: photograph, fluorescence micrograph, and diagrammatic structural/cross-sectional illustrations). Caption: (a) Description: This diagram represents the sequence of pollen germination, pollen tube growth through the pistil, entry into the ovule, and double fertilization in a flowering plant. After landing on a compatible stigma, a pollen grain germinates and produces a pollen tube that grows through the stigma and style toward the ovary. The generative cell divides to form two male gametes, which travel within the pollen tube along with the vegetative nucleus. The pollen tube enters the ovule usually through the micropyle (porogamy), penetrates one synergid of the egg apparatus via the filiform apparatus, and releases the two male gametes into the embryo sac. One male gamete fuses with the egg cell to form the zygote, while the other fuses with the two polar nuclei in the central cell to form the primary endosperm nucleus, initiating double fertilization. The embryo sac typically contains the egg apparatus at the micropylar end (one egg cell and two synergids), a large central cell with polar nuclei, and antipodal cells at the chalazal end. Labels: Pollen grain, Pollen tube, Stigma, Style, Ovary, Ovule, Embryo sac, Egg apparatus, Egg cell, Synergid, Filiform apparatus, Central cell, Polar nuclei, Antipodal cells, Male gametes. NEET relevance: Path of pollen tube growth from stigma through style to ovule; Entry of pollen tube through micropyle into synergid; Role of filiform apparatus in pollen tube guidance. Board relevance: Draw and label the longitudinal section of pistil showing pollen tube growth; Draw and label the embryo sac showing entry of pollen tube and discharge of male gametes; Explain the route of pollen tube and the fate of the two male gametes. Exam-critical labels: Pollen tube, Stigma, Style, Ovary, Ovule, Embryo sac, Egg cell, Synergid. Common mistakes: Calling the pollen tube a 'style' or 'stigma' (correct: The pollen tube is the tubular outgrowth growing through the style from the stigma); Labeling the synergid as the egg cell (correct: The synergids are the two accessory cells flanking the egg cell); Putting polar nuclei in the egg apparatus (correct: Polar nuclei are in the central cell).

lebo101-d005.content: Diagram: plant embryos: a typical dicot embryo and a longitudinal section of a grass embryo (diagram). Caption: Figure 1.14 consists of two diagrams. Diagram (a) shows a typical dicot embryo with a central embryonal axis and two large cotyledons. Labels from top to bottom are: Plumule, Cotyledons, Hypocotyl, Radicle, and Root cap. Diagram (b) shows a longitudinal section of a grass embryo, which is monocotyledonous. It features a single cotyledon called the scutellum. Other labeled parts include the Coleoptile, Shoot apex, Epiblast, Radicle, Root cap, and Coleorhiza. Description: The diagram represents two embryonic structures in angiosperms: a typical dicot embryo and a longitudinal section of a grass (monocot) embryo. In the dicot embryo, the embryonal axis lies between two cotyledons and is differentiated into the plumule at the upper end, the hypocotyl in the middle region below the cotyledons, and the radicle at the lower end, which is protected by a root cap. The cotyledons are the seed leaves and serve mainly as food-storage and/or food-absorbing organs during germination. In the grass embryo, the single cotyledon is modified into the scutellum, which lies laterally and absorbs nutrients from the endosperm. The embryonal axis bears the shoot apex enclosed by the coleoptile and the radicle enclosed by the coleorhiza. The epiblast, when present, is a small rudimentary scale-like structure opposite the scutellum and is considered a vestigial second cotyledon in many grasses. The diagram must show the protective sheaths around the embryonic shoot and root in grasses, and the clear distinction between dicot and monocot embryo organization. Labels: Plumule, Cotyledons, Hypocotyl, Radicle, Root cap, Scutellum, Coleoptile, Shoot apex, Epiblast, Coleorhiza. NEET relevance: Identify parts of dicot and monocot embryos.; Differentiate dicot embryo from grass embryo.; Functions of cotyledons, scutellum, coleoptile, and coleorhiza.. Board relevance: Draw and label a typical dicot embryo.; Draw and label L.S. of a grass embryo.; Differentiate dicot and monocot embryos in a tabular form.. Exam-critical labels: Plumule, Cotyledons, Hypocotyl, Radicle, Root cap, Scutellum, Coleoptile, Shoot apex. Common mistakes: Calling the scutellum a second cotyledon in grasses without qualification (correct: The scutellum is the single cotyledon of the grass embryo; the epiblast is the rudimentary second cotyledon-like structure, if present.); Labeling the coleoptile as the root sheath (correct: The coleoptile encloses the shoot apex; the coleorhiza encloses the radicle.); Placing the plumule outside the cotyledons in the dicot embryo (correct: The plumule lies at the upper end of the embryonal axis between the cotyledons.).

lebo101-d006.content: Diagram: pollination in flowering plants, showing water pollination in Vallisneria and an insect-pollinated flower (diagram). Caption: Figure 1.11 consists of two parts. Part (a) shows a *Vallisneria* plant with long, narrow leaves and a single female flower at the top. Part (b) shows a male flower with two anthers and a female flower with a stigma. Pollen grains are shown being carried by an insect from the male flower to the stigma of the female flower. Description: The diagram represents two distinct modes of pollination in angiosperms. In part (a), Vallisneria shows hydrophily: the plant is rooted in the bottom of freshwater, with long ribbon-like submerged leaves and a female flower borne on a long coiled stalk that reaches the water surface at anthesis. The male flowers are produced separately, detach from the plant, and float on the water surface; their anthers release pollen grains that are carried by water currents to the stigma of the female flower. In part (b), insect pollination (entomophily) is shown in a typical flower where an insect visits the flower for nectar or pollen, picks up pollen from the anther of one flower, and transfers it to the sticky stigma of another flower. The essential concept is that pollen transfer occurs from anther to stigma, but the vector differs: water in Vallisneria and insects in the second example. The diagram should emphasize the exposed stigma in the female flower, the detached floating male flower in Vallisneria, and the insect-mediated transfer of pollen in the second case. Labels: Vallisneria plant, Female flower, Male flower, Stigma, Anther, Pollen grains, Insect, Flower. NEET relevance: Difference between hydrophily and entomophily; Why Vallisneria is a classic example of water pollination; Role of floating male flowers and surface-reaching female flowers in Vallisneria. Board relevance: Draw and label pollination in Vallisneria; Draw and label insect pollination in a flower; Explain how pollen is transferred in hydrophily and entomophily. Exam-critical labels: Female flower, Male flower, Stigma, Anther, Pollen grains, Insect, Vallisneria plant. Common mistakes: Calling the detached floating Vallisneria male flower a pollen grain or seed (correct: It is a male flower bearing stamens/anthers that release pollen); Labeling the entire enlarged insect-pollinated structure as 'female flower' (correct: It is a flower showing the stigma of the pistil; the stigma is only one part of the female reproductive organ); Thinking water pollination in Vallisneria occurs by pollen sinking through water (correct: Pollen is transferred at or near the water surface, often aided by floating male flowers).

lebo101-d007.content: Diagram: Scanning electron micrographs of pollen grains (photograph). Caption: Two scanning electron micrographs of pollen grains. The left image shows a variety of pollen grains with different surface textures, including smooth, granular, and reticulate patterns. The right image shows a single pollen grain with a highly detailed, spiky surface structure. Description: The diagram should represent scanning electron micrographs of pollen grains, which are the male gametophytes of flowering plants (angiosperms). In the left micrograph, multiple pollen grains are shown together to demonstrate natural variation in size, shape, and surface ornamentation among species or even within a sample. Pollen grains are typically spherical, ellipsoidal, triangular, or sometimes irregular in outline, and their outer wall, the exine, may appear smooth, reticulate, granular, ridged, or spiny depending on the species. In the right micrograph, a single pollen grain is shown at higher magnification to reveal the detailed sculpturing of the exine, including spine-like projections or other ornamentation and one or more apertures (germ pores or furrows) through which the pollen tube emerges during germination. Functionally, the pollen wall protects the male gametophyte from desiccation and mechanical damage, while the aperture provides the site for pollen tube initiation after pollination on the stigma. Labels: pollen grain, exine, germ pore, surface ornamentation. NEET relevance: Identification of pollen grain structure from SEM or diagram; Difference between exine and intine; Role of sporopollenin in making exine highly resistant. Board relevance: Draw and label a pollen grain; Explain the structure of pollen wall; State the function of exine and germ pore. Exam-critical labels: pollen grain, exine, germ pore, surface ornamentation. Common mistakes: pollen sac (correct: pollen grain); spores (correct: pollen grains); intine (correct: exine).

lebo101-d008.content: Diagram: Transverse section of a young anther, enlarged view of one microsporangium, and a mature dehisced anther showing pollen grains (diagram). Caption: Figure 1.3 consists of three diagrams: (a) shows a transverse section of a young anther with five lobes, each containing a microsporangium. Labels point to the Connective tissue between lobes, the outer Epidermis, several Middle layers, the Endothecium, the central Sporogenous tissue, and the inner Tapetum. (b) is an enlarged view of one microsporangium, showing its wall layers: Epidermis, Endothecium, Middle layers, and Tapetum, with Microspore mother cells visible within the sporogenous tissue. (c) shows a mature, dehisced anther with four lobes that have opened to release numerous small, yellowish Pollen grains. Description: This figure represents the development and structure of the anther in angiosperms. In the young anther (transverse section), the anther is typically bilobed and tetrasporangiate, meaning it has four microsporangia (pollen sacs), two in each lobe, separated by a central connective that contains the vascular bundle. Each microsporangium is surrounded by a wall of four layers from outside to inside: epidermis, endothecium, middle layers, and tapetum. The innermost tapetum is nutritive and supports the developing sporogenous tissue, which consists of diploid microspore mother cells (pollen mother cells) that will undergo meiosis to form haploid microspores. As the anther matures, the wall layers except the epidermis and endothecium degenerate; the endothecium develops fibrous thickenings that aid in anther dehiscence. At maturity, the anther splits open along the stomium to release pollen grains from the dehisced anther. The diagram therefore shows the progression from young anther structure to enlarged microsporangium anatomy and finally to a mature dehisced anther with liberated pollen grains. Labels: Epidermis, Endothecium, Middle layers, Tapetum, Sporogenous tissue, Microspore mother cells, Connective, Pollen grains, Anther lobe, Microsporangium, Stomium. NEET relevance: Identify the layers of the anther wall in correct order; Differentiate sporogenous tissue, microspore mother cells, and pollen grains; Explain the role of tapetum in pollen development. Board relevance: Draw and label transverse section of a young anther; Draw and label enlarged microsporangium; Draw and label mature dehisced anther showing pollen grains. Exam-critical labels: Epidermis, Endothecium, Middle layers, Tapetum, Sporogenous tissue, Microspore mother cells, Connective, Pollen grains. Common mistakes: Calling the central tissue in the young anther 'pollen grains' (correct: It should be sporogenous tissue or microspore mother cells, depending on developmental stage); Labeling the innermost wall layer as 'endothecium' (correct: The innermost wall layer is tapetum); Treating the connective as part of the pollen sac wall (correct: The connective is the sterile tissue between the two anther lobes).

lebo101-d009.content: Diagram: stamen and internal structure of anther showing pollen sacs, pollen grains, filament, and line of dehiscence (structural). Caption: The image contains two diagrams. Diagram (a) shows a single stamen, consisting of a long, thin, green filament (stalk) and a bilobed anther at the top. Diagram (b) is a three-dimensional cutaway view of an anther, showing its internal structure. It has two lobes, each containing two pollen sacs. The sacs are filled with numerous small, round, pinkish-red pollen grains. A vertical line, labeled 'Line of dehiscence', runs along the length of each lobe, indicating where the anther will split open to release the pollen. The filament (stalk) is shown at the base of the anther. Description: The diagram represents a typical angiosperm stamen and a three-dimensional cut section of an anther. A stamen consists of a slender filament that supports the anther at its apex. The anther is usually bilobed and dithecous, meaning it has two lobes (thecae), and each lobe contains two pollen sacs, giving a total of four microsporangia. These pollen sacs are the internal chambers where microspore mother cells undergo meiosis to form microspores, which develop into pollen grains. In the cut-open anther, the pollen sacs are seen within the anther wall, and mature pollen grains may be visible inside or spilling out when the anther dehisces. Dehiscence occurs along the line of dehiscence, a specialized region of the anther wall that splits open at maturity to release pollen. The diagram should clearly show the relationship between the filament, the anther lobes, the pollen sacs inside the anther, the pollen grains within the sacs, and the line along which the anther opens. Labels: Anther, Filament, Pollen sacs, Pollen grains, Line of dehiscence. NEET relevance: Identification of stamen parts and anther structure; Number of pollen sacs in a typical anther; Meaning of dithecous and bilobed anther. Board relevance: Draw and label a typical stamen; Draw and label a T.S. or cut section of anther; Explain pollen formation and release. Exam-critical labels: Anther, Filament, Pollen sacs, Pollen grains, Line of dehiscence. Common mistakes: Calling the pollen sacs 'anther lobes' (correct: The anther lobes are the two external halves of the anther; the pollen sacs are the four internal chambers within them); Labeling the line of dehiscence as the filament or as a pollen sac wall (correct: It is the longitudinal opening region on the anther wall where the anther splits open); Treating pollen grains as part of the anther wall (correct: Pollen grains are the contents of the pollen sacs and are released from them).

lebo101-d010.content: Diagram: female reproductive structures of flowering plants: pistil/gynoecium forms and a diagrammatic ovule (diagram). Caption: Figure 1.7 consists of four diagrams labeled (a) through (d). (a) A dissected flower of *Hibiscus* showing the pistil. Labels include: Stigma, Style, Syncarpous ovary, Ovary, and Thalamus. (b) A multicarpellary, syncarpous pistil of *Papaver*. Labels include: Stigma and Carpels. (c) A multicarpellary, apocarpous gynoecium of *Michelia*. Labels include: Carpels. Description: This NCERT figure represents two related aspects of angiosperm female reproductive morphology: the pistil/gynoecium in different floral conditions and the structure of a typical anatropous ovule. In the pistil diagrams, the gynoecium is the female reproductive whorl composed of one or more carpels; each carpel bears a stigma at the apex for pollen reception, a style as the slender transmitting region, and an ovary at the base that encloses ovules. In a syncarpous gynoecium, two or more carpels are fused into a single pistil, as in Papaver, whereas in an apocarpous gynoecium the carpels remain free from one another, as in Michelia. In the ovule diagram, the ovule is anatropous, meaning it is inverted so that the micropyle lies close to the funicle and hilum. The funicle attaches the ovule to the placenta; the point of attachment is the hilum. The ovule body is enclosed by two integuments, outer and inner, which leave a small opening called the micropyle at the apex. Inside the integuments lies the nucellus, and within the nucellus is the embryo sac, the female gametophyte. The opposite end of the ovule is the chalazal pole, where the integuments and nucellus converge. Together, these diagrams illustrate the organization of the female reproductive structures of flowering plants and the orientation and internal anatomy of a typical ovule. Labels: Stigma, Style, Ovary, Thalamus, Carpels, Syncarpous ovary, Hilum, Funicle, Micropyle, Micropylar pole, Outer integument, Inner integument, Nucellus, Embryo sac, Chalazal pole. NEET relevance: Identification of pistil parts and their functions; Difference between syncarpous and apocarpous gynoecium; Recognition of anatropous ovule and its features. Board relevance: Draw and label the pistil of Hibiscus; Draw and label syncarpous and apocarpous gynoecium; Draw and label a typical anatropous ovule. Exam-critical labels: Stigma, Style, Ovary, Thalamus, Carpels, Syncarpous ovary, Funicle, Hilum. Common mistakes: Calling the entire female structure a 'pistil' when the figure specifically shows a syncarpous ovary or a gynoecium with fused carpels. (correct: Use 'pistil' for the whole female organ and 'syncarpous ovary' or 'syncarpous gynoecium' when carpels are fused.); Labeling the ovule opening as the chalaza. (correct: The opening is the micropyle; the chalaza is the opposite end.); Putting the micropyle at the far end of an anatropous ovule. (correct: In an anatropous ovule, the micropyle is near the funicle and hilum due to inversion.).

lebo101-d011.content: Diagram: flowers and flowering plant forms related to self-pollinated and cleistogamous/chasmogamous flowers (diagram). Caption: (a) Description: This diagram represents three reproductive flower types relevant to pollination biology in angiosperms. Panel (a) should show self-pollinated flowers, typically illustrating flowers in which pollen transfer occurs within the same flower or between flowers of the same plant; such flowers often have close proximity of anthers and stigma and may be adapted to ensure autogamy or geitonogamy. Panel (b) should show cross-pollinated flowers, i.e., chasmogamous flowers that open at anthesis and permit pollen transfer between different plants, usually aided by wind, insects, or other agents; these flowers are generally exposed and structurally suited for outcrossing. Panel (c) should show a cleistogamous plant bearing both chasmogamous flowers and cleistogamous flowers: the chasmogamous flowers are open, showy flowers that can cross-pollinate, whereas the cleistogamous flowers are small, closed, non-opening flowers that self-pollinate obligatorily without exposure to pollinators. In cleistogamy, the closed flower ensures seed set even under unfavorable conditions, while the open flower allows genetic recombination when conditions permit. The diagram should therefore emphasize the contrast between open, pollinator-accessible flowers and closed, self-pollinating flowers on the same plant, along with the concept of self-pollination versus cross-pollination. Labels: Self-pollinated flowers, Cross-pollinated flowers, Chasmogamous flower, Cleistogamous flowers. NEET relevance: Definition and distinction between chasmogamous and cleistogamous flowers; Why cleistogamous flowers ensure assured seed set; Difference between self-pollination and cross-pollination. Board relevance: Draw and label the diagram showing chasmogamous and cleistogamous flowers; Differentiate self-pollinated and cross-pollinated flowers; Explain the adaptive significance of cleistogamy. Exam-critical labels: Chasmogamous flower, Cleistogamous flowers, Self-pollinated flowers, Cross-pollinated flowers.
